### AF 2023-03-17 1131
(00:00:30) During the 2016 election, the anonymous Twitter account Ricky Vaughn, operated by [[Douglas Mackey]], was one of the most influential accounts promoting [[Donald Trump]].
(00:00:48) In 2021, the [[United States Department of Justice]] under the [[Biden Administration]] charged [[Douglas Mackey]] with election interference for posting a meme that falsely claimed people could vote via text message.
(00:01:38) The trial of [[Douglas Mackey]] was delayed after an expert witness for his defense, [[George Hawley]], withdrew following alleged intimidation by the [[Southern Poverty Law Center]].
(00:02:00) [[Marjorie Taylor Greene]] wrote a letter to Attorney General [[Merrick Garland]] demanding the [[United States Department of Justice]] drop its charges against [[Douglas Mackey]].
(00:50:51) [[Douglas Mackey]], known as Ricky Vaughn, was a participant in the online "optics war" around 2017-2018, aligning with figures like [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] against others such as [[Richard Spencer]].
(00:52:30) [[Douglas Mackey]] was doxed in 2018 by [[Chris Cantwell]] or [[Paul Nehlen]] before facing federal charges in 2021.
(00:52:52) The [[United States Department of Justice]] charged [[Douglas Mackey]] under the Ku Klux Klan Act, alleging his meme about texting votes deprived people of their civil right to vote.
(00:53:54) Defense attorney [[Andrew Frisch]] told U.S. District Judge [[Nicholas Garaufis]] that planned [[Southern Poverty Law Center]] article by journalist [[Luke O'Brien]] caused defense expert witness [[George Hawley]] to withdraw from [[Douglas Mackey]]'s trial.
(00:55:12) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] warned that a conviction for [[Douglas Mackey]] would set a dangerous precedent allowing prosecution for political "misinformation," potentially making it illegal to claim elections are rigged.
(00:57:31) [[Marjorie Taylor Greene]] called on Attorney General [[Merrick Garland]] to drop the [[United States Department of Justice]]'s charges against [[Douglas Mackey]].
(01:00:30) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] noted that figures like [[Elon Musk]], [[Tucker Carlson]], and [[Derek Beatty]] rallied to the defense of [[Douglas Mackey]].
(01:06:08) [[Nicholas J. Fuentes]] expressed full support for [[Douglas Mackey]], describing him as "a great guy and really right on the issues," and urged donations to his legal defense fund.

Fuentes asserted that a handful of major media and tech conglomerates, working in concert with the intelligence apparatus, [[The Wall Street Journal|Wall Street]], the military-industrial complex, and [[Los Angeles|Hollywood]], controlled national discourse and actively suppressed stories like the [[Hunter Biden]] investigation.

### AF 2020-10-29 0712
(00:53:55) Nicholas J. ### AF 2020-10-29 0712
(01:00:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]]'s confirmation of the [[Hunter Biden]] probe now provided a verified source for fact-checkers and media figures [[who]] had previously demanded evidence of the [[Joe Biden|Biden family]]'s alleged corruption.

### AF 2020-10-29 0712
(01:01:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the extensive confirmed evidence of corruption against [[Joe Biden]] and [[Hunter Biden]] with the years-long investigations into [[Donald Trump]] which, in his view, failed to find equivalent wrongdoing.

(01:02:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes acknowledged the importance of having a factual "paper trail" to substantiate claims of corruption, stating that everything he and his movement believed was backed "historically, statistically, scientifically, in every way."

Fuentes.

### AF 2020-10-28 0711
(00:48:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced a personal "crusade" against mask mandates, encouraging his audience to stop wearing masks and to generate "friction" by refusing to comply with regulations in stores and businesses in order to force an end to COVID-19 restrictions.

### AF 2020-10-28 0711
(01:10:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted entering a [[United States]] Post Office without a mask, refusing repeated demands from a postal worker to wear one, and stating "it's okay" before being threatened with a police call, which he dismissed as a bluff before completing his transaction and leaving.

### AF 2020-10-28 0711
(01:23:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that congressional hearings with [[big tech]] CEOs like [[Mark Zuckerberg]] of [[Facebook]] and [[Twitter|Jack Dorsey]] of [[Twitter]] were not news and meant nothing, comparing them to public press releases, and therefore chose not to cover them on his show.

### AF 2020-10-28 0711
(01:32:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the slow progress of [[Donald Trump]]'s [[United States|America]] First agenda was due to sabotage by a permanent bureaucratic class, or "deep state," citing [[Miles Taylor]] as one example of many administration officials hired from the [[Republican Party|Republican]] establishment [[who]] actively worked against [[the president's]] goals.

### AF 2020-10-28 0711
(01:56:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a segment of the population, including violent repeat offenders like [[Walter Wallace Jr.]], is a "net negative" that cannot be reformed and must be permanently managed through incarceration or institutionalization rather than accommodated by changing societal laws or police practices.

### AF 2020-10-28 0711
(02:03:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Owen Benjamin]] (real name Owen Smith) is [[Jews|Jewish]], anti-[[Christianity|Christian]], has blasphemed the Trinity and Mary, and has made personal threats and slanderous claims against him and his family.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(00:07:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes opened his October 27, 2020 broadcast by announcing the show's focus on a riot in [[Philadelphia]] and new election-related policies from [[Twitter]].

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(00:28:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes described a recurring pattern of civil unrest in [[United States|American]] cities, citing a recent incident in [[Kanye West|West]] [[Philadelphia]] where a man named Walter Wallace was shot by police after charging at them with a knife, which was followed by looting.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(00:50:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Twitter]] was introducing a new prompt to warn users before they tweeted about topics it deemed potential election misinformation, such as [[Hunter Biden]].

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed increasing confidence that [[Donald Trump]] would win the 2020 election, citing tightening polls in swing states like [[State of Florida|Florida]] and [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]], but warned that institutional forces could challenge his ability to hold office.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:12:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s path to victory depended on strong [[Republican Party|Republican]] turnout on Election Day, as early voting numbers appeared to favor [[Democratic Party|Democrats]], and expressed concern that an incident could be orchestrated to depress that turnout.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:16:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that after the 2016 election, companies like [[Google]] and [[Facebook]] held internal meetings vowing to prevent a similar outcome, with [[Facebook]] pioneering "fake news" fact-checking policies targeting outlets like [[Alex Jones|InfoWars]] and [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]].

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:18:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that over the preceding four years, social media platforms had systematically banned or shadow-banned [[Donald Trump]] supporters and conservatives, progressively tightening community guidelines to target specific ideologies.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:20:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that in 2020, [[Twitter]] began regularly fact-checking or blocking tweets from [[Donald Trump]] and his staff, including press secretary [[Kayleigh McEnany]], [[who]] was locked out for sharing a [[The New York Post|New York Post]] article.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:21:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a report from [[Zero Hedge]] stating that [[Twitter]] would begin prompting users with warnings about topics likely to involve election misinformation in the final week before the 2020 election.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:25:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Twitter]] of editorial bias, claiming it promoted unverified stories damaging to [[Donald Trump]] on its front [[page]] while suppressing and blocking links to stories about [[Hunter Biden]].

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:29:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Twitter]]'s new warning system and its prompts assuring users that mail-in voting was safe and that results might be delayed were preparing the public to accept a contested election outcome favorable to [[Joe Biden]].

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:31:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that [[Twitter]]'s stated concern about election integrity was a pretext to censor content harmful to [[Joe Biden]], while simultaneously dismissing concerns about mail-in ballot fraud as misinformation.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:34:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that tests had shown a failure rate for mailed ballots, yet [[Twitter]] banned discussion of such vulnerabilities, demonstrating its partisan intervention.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the control of information by [[Facebook]], [[Google]], and [[Twitter]] as an existential threat more important than any single election, arguing it would make the election of someone like [[Donald Trump]] impossible in the future.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:38:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the business models of [[Apple Inc.|Apple]], [[Google]], [[Facebook]], and [[Amazon Inc.|Amazon]] as creating "wage slavery" and argued that leftists supported their censorship because it currently targeted their political opponents.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:44:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes turned to the shooting of Walter Wallace in [[Philadelphia]], framing it as part of a recurring series of events where a [[black]] criminal is killed by police after resisting arrest, leading to riots.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:48:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes read a report from [[Fox News]] stating the [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] [[U.S. Department of Defense|National Guard]] had been deployed to [[Philadelphia]] after riots injured 30 officers and led to 91 arrests, with businesses including several [[Rite Aid]] pharmacies looted.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:50:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the core issue behind police shootings and riots was disproportionate [[black]] criminality, not systemic racism or police brutality, stating that the solution was for [[black]] people to stop committing crimes.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:55:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed racial disparities in outcomes to behavioral and genetic differences, citing high out-of-wedlock birth rates and disruptive behavior in schools within [[black]] communities.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:57:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a specific business in [[Philadelphia]] that was looted in May 2020 during the [[George Floyd]] riots, reopened three weeks prior, and was looted again on October 26, arguing this pattern deterred investment in [[black]] neighborhoods.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:59:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that videos from the [[Philadelphia]] riots showed [[black]] people looting stores and smashing ATMs, which he said was indicative of a broader pattern of criminal and self-destructive behavior.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the placement of darker-toned beauty products behind locked glass cases in stores was a response to theft, not racism, and was evidence of the behavioral issues causing racial disparities.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:06:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested the [[Philadelphia]] riots might help [[Donald Trump]] win [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] and the election by highlighting lawlessness a week before voters went to the polls.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:07:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the [[United States|American]] response to riots with how he believed [[China]], [[Russia]], or [[Saudi Arabia]] would handle similar unrest, implying those countries would not tolerate such behavior from a minority population.

### AF 2020-10-27 0710
(01:11:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Martin Luther King Jr.]] would have been no different from contemporary race hustlers like [[Al Sharpton]] and criticized baby boomers for clinging to an idealized view of the civil rights leader.

(03:00:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated a philosophical opposition to women serving on the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]], arguing that judgment, not brilliance or knowledge of the law, is critical and that women are biologically less suited for impersonal judgment.

(03:05:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes viewed [[Amy Coney Barrett]]'s confirmation as critically important for potential [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] decisions on ballot legitimacy in swing states like [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]], which could decide the election.

Wade*, would at minimum protect the [[Concept|First Amendment]] and [[U.S. Constitution|Second Amendment]] from potential hate speech laws and gun control under a future [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] administration.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced a "casual Friday" episode of his show, focusing on a drastic reduction in immigration and developments at the [[Federal Communications Commission|FCC]].

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:01:00) [[Patrick Casey]] had previously given Nicholas J. Fuentes a hard [[time]] about projections for reduced legal immigration in fiscal years 2020 and 2021.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:02:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the 97% reduction in immigration was evidence that [[Donald Trump]] was following through on his promises.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:03:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the show would discuss [[Federal Communications Commission|FCC]] Chairman [[Ajit Pai]], [[who]] had previously shut down Net Neutrality.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:03:50) [[Ajit Pai]] announced that the [[Federal Communications Commission|FCC]] would begin to reinterpret Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, which Nicholas J. Fuentes argued could stop major tech companies from censoring conservatives.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:06:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes covered a [[Donald Trump|Trump]] town hall on [[NBC News|NBC]] where [[Savannah Guthrie]] interviewed the former president.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:06:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes also covered about a half-hour of a [[Joe Biden]] town hall hosted by [[George Stephanopoulos]].

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:09:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the setups for the separate [[Donald Trump|Trump]] and [[Joe Biden]] town halls in [[State of Florida|Florida]] and [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] were socially distanced, making the cancellation of an in-[[person]] debate seem arbitrary.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:11:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Savannah Guthrie]]'s interview with [[Donald Trump]] as brutal and lacking impartiality, focusing on scandals like the [[White House]] event being a "super spreader" and accusations of white supremacy.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:12:30) In contrast, Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[George Stephanopoulos]] did not interrupt [[Joe Biden]] during his town hall, which featured questions Nicholas J. Fuentes considered soft, such as "what does it say about the [[country]] if [[Donald Trump|Trump]] wins?"

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:13:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that the [[Joe Biden]] town hall occurred within 24 hours of the [[The New York Post|New York Post]] obtaining emails about [[Hunter Biden]] and [[Ukraine]], yet [[Joe Biden]] faced no questions about it.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:14:27) A second presidential debate was scheduled for October 22nd, which Nicholas J. Fuentes planned to cover.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:15:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]] lost a crucial opportunity to reach a large audience by not having a debate, which he needed as early voting numbers were concerning.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:16:00) [[Ryan Girdusky]] tweeted data showing that in [[State of Florida|Florida]], only 4% of newly registered [[black]] voters had registered as [[Republican Party|Republicans]], which Nicholas J. Fuentes cited as evidence that outreach to [[black]] voters by figures like [[Candace Owens]] had failed.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:16:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes blamed [[Jared Kushner]] and [[Brad Parscale]] for an incompetent campaign strategy that focused on pandering to [[black]] voters through policies like the First Step Act and historically [[black]] college funding.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:19:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted the election would come down to [[Minnesota]], [[Wisconsin]], [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]], and [[State of Florida|Florida]], with particular focus on the latter three.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:20:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that demographic trends showed swing states like [[State of New Mexico|New Mexico]], [[State of Nevada|Nevada]], [[State of Colorado|Colorado]], and [[State of Virginia|Virginia]] becoming solidly [[Democratic Party|Democrat]], while [[Republican Party|Republican]]-leaning states like [[State of Texas|Texas]], [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]], and [[Arizona]] were becoming toss-ups.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:22:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[State of California|California]] would remain a [[Democratic Party|Democrat]] state, while [[State of Texas|Texas]] was at risk of becoming a swing state.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:22:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed the leftward shift of states largely to immigration and other demographic factors.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:23:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that until recently, tech censorship was the only major issue [[Donald Trump]] had not addressed effectively in the final stretch of his reelection campaign.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:24:33) [[Federal Communications Commission|FCC]] Chairman [[Ajit Pai]] moved to revise guidance on Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, which Nicholas J. Fuentes described as the primary tactic to force companies like [[Facebook]] and [[Twitter]] to stop censoring conservatives.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:26:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if platforms like [[Facebook]] and [[Twitter]] make editorial decisions by censoring political opinions, they should be classified as publishers like the [[The New York Times|New York Times]] and lose their special legal immunity.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:31:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes used [[Fox News]] and [[Tucker Carlson]] as an example of a publisher that is held legally accountable for the content it advances, unlike social media platforms with Section 230 immunity.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:32:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes contested free market arguments for protecting tech companies, stating that major corporations like [[Amazon Inc.|Amazon]], [[Twitter]], and [[Facebook]] achieved market dominance through government subsidies, regulations, and legal loopholes.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:37:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that clarifying Section 230 would force companies like [[Twitter]] and [[Facebook]] to choose between being platforms that cannot censor or publishers that are legally liable, creating a financial incentive to stop censoring conservatives.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:38:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that controlling the distribution of information through the internet was the most important issue, as it allowed dissenting voices to reach a mass audience, unlike traditional media controlled by a small group.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:40:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that after the 2016 election, the establishment worked to "button up" the internet by shutting down platforms for dissidents like the [[Daily Stormer]].

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:42:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the trajectory of tech companies was towards totalitarianism, with [[Google]] rewriting history and making dissident content inaccessible, leaving only [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]]-approved information.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:44:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes introduced a report from the [[Cato Institute]] showing that legal immigration in the second half of fiscal year 2020 fell by 92% from the first half, the largest annual decline in [[United States|U.S.]] history.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:50:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the coronavirus-driven reduction in immigration to near zero created a new negotiating starting point, shifting the onus to those [[who]] would argue to increase immigration back up.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:52:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that with immigration near zero, the [[America First]] movement should become prominent in the [[Republican Party]] to argue for keeping immigration low and dictating a new, restrictive policy.

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:55:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Donald Trump]]'s accomplishments as including a 97% reduction in immigration, the [[Federal Communications Commission|FCC]] moving against tech censorship, 450 miles of border wall construction, and troop reductions in [[Iraq]] and [[Afghanistan]].

### AF 2020-10-16 0703
(00:56:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited these accomplishments to [[Donald Trump]] overcoming opposition from [[Democratic Party|Democrats]], the media, intelligence agencies, and subversion from within his own party from figures like [[Paul Ryan]] and [[Mitch McConnell]].

Fuentes began his monologue by reacting to a clip of [[Donald Trump]] being interviewed by [[Savannah Guthrie]] of [[NBC News|NBC]] about his recent COVID-19 diagnosis and treatment at [[Walter Reed National Military Medical Center|Walter Reed]] National Military Medical Center.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:04:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Savannah Guthrie]] for focusing on [[Donald Trump]]'s COVID-19 test timing and mask-wearing habits 20 days before a presidential election.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:27:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the [[Joe Biden]] town hall on [[ABC News|ABC]] would have perfect audio and less tough questions compared to the "cage match" interrogation of [[Donald Trump]] by [[Savannah Guthrie]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:28:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized the [[NBC News|NBC]] town hall's questioning sequence: first on [[Donald Trump]] catching COVID-19, then disavowing white supremacy, then disavowing [[QAnon]], then retweeting conspiracy theories, then committing to a peaceful transfer of power, then an audience question citing [[Bob Woodward]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:29:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that after tough questions, the [[NBC News|NBC]] town hall moved to softer audience questions about healthcare and masks, with constant interjections from [[Savannah Guthrie]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:42:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes critiqued [[Donald Trump]]'s answers on healthcare and taxes, arguing they lacked cogent detail and that discussing terms like "under-levered" or "filing number" was ineffective with a general public unfamiliar with finance.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:43:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the political establishment wants managerial technocrats like [[Joe Biden]] or [[Emmanuel Macron]] [[who]] use boring, meaningless language to maintain stability, unlike [[Donald Trump]]'s bold, emotive, and denotative style.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(00:59:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded his review of the [[NBC News|NBC]] town hall, stating [[Donald Trump]] did very well despite a biased moderator, terrible audio, and a barrage of tough questions on white supremacy, [[QAnon]], taxes, and the election.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(01:00:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes began reacting to the concurrent [[ABC News|ABC]] town hall with [[Joe Biden]], noting the audio was perfect, the moderator did not interrupt, and questions were softer, such as on fracking and climate policy.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(01:00:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Joe Biden]]'s speaking style as sounding sedated, like someone between wakefulness and sleep, and compared his uninterrupted, filibuster-length answers to the confrontational style [[Savannah Guthrie]] used with [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(01:04:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes praised [[Donald Trump]]'s handling of a DACA question at the [[NBC News|NBC]] town hall, specifically his subtle correction that there is a difference between DACA and Dreamers while complimenting the questioner's understanding, calling it a calculated and effective people-[[person]] technique.

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(01:06:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the two town halls, noting [[Donald Trump]] faced a "cage match" with constant interruptions and argumentative questions from [[Savannah Guthrie]], while [[Joe Biden]] was given a platform to speak without interjection by [[George Stephanopoulos]].

### AF 2020-10-15 0702
(01:11:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[George Stephanopoulos]] did not interject once during [[Joe Biden]]'s long answer on transgender rights, a stark contrast to [[Savannah Guthrie]]'s treatment of [[Donald Trump]].

(01:17:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded his review of both town halls, stating the contrast proved every power structure in [[United States|America]]—media, [[The Wall Street Journal|Wall Street]], lobbies, academia—was rigging the election in favor of [[Joe Biden]] and no longer even pretending to be impartial.

(01:21:01) Nicholas J. (01:22:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the foreign policy establishment, including the [[Council on Foreign Relations]], the [[Israel lobby]], and defense contractors like [[Raytheon Technologies Corporation|Lockheed Martin]] and [[Raytheon Technologies Corporation|Raytheon]], does not want peace but permanent war against so-called rogue regimes to justify massive budgets, half of [[United States|U.S]]. discretionary spending.

### AF 2020-10-13 0700

### AF 2020-08-31 0671
(00:02:30) [[Portland]] experienced 90 days of continuous unrest involving [[Antifa]] and [[Black Lives Matter]], including an attempt to explode a federal courthouse with explosives and fireworks. (00:03:06) A caravan of approximately 600 [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporters with trucks and flags entered [[Portland]], leading to a major clash with left-wing protesters. (00:03:19) [[Jay Bishop]], a member of the group [[Patriot Prayer]], was shot and killed by an [[Antifa]] member during the clashes in [[Portland]]. (00:04:02) Major media outlets like the [[Associated Press]] and the [[The New York Times|New York Times]] did not report that an [[Antifa]] member killed a [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporter in [[Portland]]. (00:04:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes covered three days of the [[Republican Party|RNC]], specifically Monday, Wednesday, and Thursday. (00:05:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted [[Patrick]], [[Jaden]], [[Beardson]], and [[Jake Lloyd]] in [[Chicago]] after plans to attend the [[Republican Party|RNC]] in [[Jacksonville]] were canceled. (00:07:29) [[Donald Trump]]'s speech at the [[Republican Party|RNC]] was distinct from the mainstream establishment performances of other speakers. (00:08:54) [[Mike Pence]] delivered a strong performance at the [[Republican Party|RNC]], and [[Donald Trump]]'s speech was also considered strong despite some expected "cringe" elements. (00:09:11) The final night of the [[Republican Party|RNC]] featured a fireworks display, an opera singer, and [[Donald Trump]]'s family in front of the [[White House]], which was considered a great success. (00:09:48) [[Donald Trump]]'s speech at the [[Republican Party|RNC]] drew 23.4 million television viewers, while [[Joe Biden]]'s drew 24.6 million, according to [[Nielsen]]. (00:10:00) The [[DNC]] averaged 21.6 million viewers across four nights, while the [[Republican Party|RNC]] averaged 19.4 million. (00:10:21) [[Donald Trump]] publicly disputed the [[Nielsen]] ratings on [[Twitter]], claiming the [[Republican Party|RNC]] had 147.9 million viewers when digital viewership was included. (00:11:56) Major cable news channels like [[MSNBC]] and [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]] carried wall-to-wall coverage of the [[DNC]] but did not carry the first nights of the [[Republican Party|RNC]]. (00:12:30) The [[Republican Party|RNC]] featured a noticeable number of [[black]] speakers, with 12 appearing over the course of the convention. (00:14:16) [[Tim Scott]] invoked [[George Floyd]] and [[Breonna Taylor]] during his speech at the [[Republican Party|RNC]]. (00:14:28) [[Nikki Haley]] spoke of her [[India|Indian]] roots and alluded to her decision to take down the Confederate flag during her [[Republican Party|RNC]] speech. (00:14:33) [[Herschel Walker]] stated he had seen racism up close and that [[Donald Trump]] was not racist during his [[Republican Party|RNC]] speech. (00:15:14) [[Mark]] and [[Patricia McCloskey]], the couple [[who]] pointed guns at [[Black Lives Matter]] protesters in [[St. Louis]], spoke at the [[Republican Party|RNC]] about suburban safety. (00:16:48) Speeches at the [[Republican Party|RNC]] played to white anxiety about crime, immigration, and demographic change, with [[Donald Trump]] specifically talking about illegal immigration and the wall. (00:17:32) The [[Republican Party|RNC]] included speeches about rioting and looting from figures like the [[McCloskeys]] and a police chief, alongside messages from [[Mike Pence]] and [[Donald Trump]]. (00:19:16) [[Donald Trump]]'s 2016 victory involved flipping [[Wisconsin]], [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], and [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] by tapping into the white working class, a realignment [[Republican Party|Republicans]] needed to double down on. (00:21:00) The [[Republican Party]] was losing or had lost states like [[State of California|California]], [[State of New Mexico|New Mexico]], [[State of Nevada|Nevada]], [[State of Colorado|Colorado]], [[Arizona]], and [[State of Virginia|Virginia]], making the Midwestern states crucial for future elections. (00:23:27) The [[Republican Party|RNC]]'s production value, including a giant fireworks display on the [[National Mall]] and an opera singer, was considered far superior to the [[DNC]]'s remote, audience-less presentation. (00:24:01) [[Joe Biden]] gave a 25-minute speech to an empty convention room during the [[DNC]], followed by a finale featuring video call grids of supporters. (00:27:08) The [[DNC]] featured a musical performance by [[Billy Porter]], while the [[Republican Party|RNC]] featured an Italian opera singer performing "Ave Maria" on a [[White House]] balcony. (00:31:38) [[Kenosha]], [[Wisconsin]], experienced widespread destruction including looting, arson, and a car lot fire causing $2.5 million in damage, following the police shooting of [[Jacob Blake]]. (00:32:11) [[Kyle Rittenhouse]], a 17-year-old [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporter from [[Illinois]], shot and killed two people in [[Kenosha]] during the unrest. (00:36:46) The unrest in [[Kenosha]] began after [[Jacob Blake]] was shot seven times by [[Kenosha]] police officer [[Rustin Shesky]] while being taken into custody. (00:38:18) [[Jacob Blake]] was charged with sexual assault, trespassing, and disorderly conduct in connection with domestic abuse on July 6, and an arrest warrant was issued on July 7. (00:39:02) According to the [[Kenosha Professional Police Association]], [[Jacob Blake]] was armed with a knife, fought with officers, and put one in a headlock before being shot. (00:45:46) Following the looting of the Magnificent Mile in [[Chicago]], the [[city]] deployed a significant police presence, public works trucks, and implemented a curfew. (00:47:51) [[Portland]] and [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] were among the worst-affected cities by prolonged unrest, largely due to the persistent presence of [[Antifa]]. (00:48:51) A caravan of about 600 vehicles carrying [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporters arrived in downtown [[Portland]] and was confronted by protesters, leading to several arrests and a fatal shooting. (00:49:30) [[Donald Trump]] invoked [[Portland]] as a liberal [[city]] overrun with violence in a speech at the [[Republican Party|RNC]] as part of his law and order campaign theme. (00:50:07) [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporters and [[Black Lives Matter]]/[[Antifa]] protesters directly engaged in [[Portland]], using non-lethal weapons like paintball guns, [[mace]], pepper spray, skateboards, and bricks. (00:52:53) [[Jay Bishop]] (also identified as Aaron Danielson), a supporter of the [[Patriot Prayer]] group, was shot and killed on a street in [[Portland]] around 8:45 PM local [[time]]. (00:53:28) [[Donald Trump]] praised [[Jay Bishop]] with a post on [[Twitter]] that read "Rest in Peace Jay." (00:54:48) [[Kyle Rittenhouse]] shot and killed two [[Antifa]] or [[Black Lives Matter]] protesters in [[Kenosha]], which he claimed was in self-defense. (00:57:50) [[James Fields]] was sentenced to life in prison plus 419 years for his actions during the [[Charlottesville]] rally. (01:00:39) In [[Louisville]], [[State of Kentucky|Kentucky]], charges were dropped against 92 protesters [[who]] demonstrated in front of the attorney general's home over the [[Breonna Taylor]] case. (01:01:17) [[Laura Trump]] said [[Donald Trump]] was going to meet with [[Jacob Blake]]'s family to talk about criminal justice reform. (01:01:58) [[Jared Kushner]] said he was going to talk to [[LeBron James]] about issues. (01:05:20) [[Kyle Rittenhouse]] was facing state charges in [[Wisconsin]] and could not receive a presidential pardon. (01:13:49) [[John Podesta]] discussed within the [[Democratic Party]] elite what would happen if [[Donald Trump]] won and [[Joe Biden]] refused to concede the election. (01:14:49) The components for a potential revolution or coup against [[Donald Trump]] existed, including media control, mobilizable protesters, hostile elements within the [[Republican Party]] and [[White House]], and a deep state willing to sabotage [[the president]]. (01:15:50) A scenario was outlined where media and social media could propagate a narrative of election illegitimacy, mobilize protesters, and create a constitutional crisis to install [[Joe Biden]] and [[Kamala Harris]]. (01:19:35) [[Black Lives Matter]]'s approval rating among white people had turned negative after initially being positive following the death of [[George Floyd]], while remaining unchanged for [[black]] and [[Hispanic]] people. (02:00:39) [[Elijah Schaffer]] responded to criticism by Nicholas J. Fuentes, calling him a "[[State of California|California]] conservative" while claiming to be "risking my life and shaping the narrative." (02:01:01) [[Elijah Schaffer]] deleted an interview he conducted with Nicholas J. Fuentes a couple of years prior and later smeared him. (02:06:08) [[Salim]] asked Nicholas J. Fuentes if it was [[time]] for his "[[Washington D.C.|DC]] buddy" to "reign in the QAnon thing," suggesting it could mislead well-meaning [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporters. ### AF 2020-08-27 0670

### AF 2020-08-27 0670

(00:47:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the Cannon Hinnant case as an example of how a decentralized network of dissident voices on social media could raise awareness and funds, an ability that would vanish if [[Concept|Big Tech]] achieved a total monopoly on information dissemination.

### AF 2020-08-12 0660
(00:49:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that if a handful of corporations like [[Google]], [[Facebook]], and [[Twitter]] secure a monopoly over mass communications combined with the federal government, they will control all public perception of reality, including which news and statistics are released.

(00:50:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that under a future regime of total tech and government control, when authorities move to confiscate firearms or violence erupts, no one will be able to report on it or organize resistance, leaving individuals isolated against powerful institutions.

(43:36:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that past riots in [[Ferguson]] (2014), [[Los Angeles]] (1992), and after [[Martin Luther King Jr.]]'s assassination (1968) were predominantly carried out by [[black]] individuals.

(44:09:63) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the [[Minneapolis]] looting, which used [[George Floyd]] as a pretext, with the [[Chicago]] looting, which he said occurred with little pretext following a dubious report about a shooting in [[Englewood]].

(00:25:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Bari Weiss]] had posted critical tweets about [[Nick Cannon]], calling him "brainwashed" and a "pig," yet [[Nick Cannon]] responded by reading her book and seeking reconciliation.

(00:57:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] had successfully reduced troops in [[Syria]] the previous year after several attempts, and viewed the planned withdrawal from [[Afghanistan]] as a major achievement.

Fuentes reported that the [[United States]] planned to pull nearly 12,000 troops out of [[Germany]], with 6,400 returning to the [[United States|U.S]]. and 5,600 being repositioned to other [[North Atlantic Treaty Organization|NATO]] countries like [[Belgium]] and [[Italian Republic|Italy]].

### AF 2020-08-04 0654
(01:01:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed increasing optimism about [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s re-election prospects and his administration's record, citing progress on the border wall, immigration, troop reductions, and trade deals with [[China]] and through [[USMCA]].

### AF 2020-08-04 0654
(01:03:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s recent successes vindicate a strategy of "slow and steady" progress and building day-by-day, countering narratives within dissident circles that there is "no political solution."

### AF 2020-08-04 0654
(01:05:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration policies like the public charge rule, funding the border wall through the [[U.S. Department of Defense|Department of Defense]], and the travel ban as examples of "slow and steady" victories built up over years of litigation and effort.

### AF 2020-08-04 0654
(01:07:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the lesson from [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s administration is that if tens of thousands of people adopt a mindset of steady, daily effort in their professions and families, they can achieve civilizational change over the long term.

(00:03:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that leaked body camera footage from the [[George Floyd]] arrest had been published by the [[The Daily Mail|Daily Mail]] after being made available only under strict conditions in [[Hennepin County]], [[Minnesota]].

(00:06:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the official autopsy report concluded [[George Floyd]] died from a drug overdose involving fentanyl and methamphetamine, not from asphyxiation during his arrest.

(00:07:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the body camera footage showed [[George Floyd]] was committing a crime, resisted arrest, and had a near-lethal amount of fentanyl and methamphetamine in his system at the [[time]] of his death.

(00:08:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump]] announced on [[Air Force One]] that he was looking at banning [[TikTok Inc.|TikTok]], a move later revealed to be a negotiation tactic leading to talks with [[Microsoft Corporation|Microsoft]].

### AF 2020-08-03 0653

(00:15:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Bill Barr|William Barr]]'s testimony, in which [[Bill Barr|William Barr]] said the demonization of police following the death of [[George Floyd]] was "gravely injurious to our inner [[city]] communities."

(00:30:66) The [[Republican Party]]'s proposed relief bill slashed enhanced federal unemployment benefits from a $600 weekly bonus to a system based on 70% of previous wages, capped at a maximum of $500 per week. (01:08:10) The "Gen Z [[Republican Party|GOP]]" group, founded by individuals including [[Sam Garber|Samuel Garber]], officially launched with a video stating their opposition to [[Donald Trump]] and a desire to build a future [[Republican Party]] that stays away from "unproductive discourse." Fuentes stated that the mission of his movement was to make the [[Republican Party]] so hostile and intolerant towards left-wing figures that they would have to join the [[Democratic Party]]. Fuentes argued that reports on domestic terrorism systematically excluded violence committed by non-white or non-right-wing groups, such as [[Black Lives Matter]] or [[Antifa]], from their definitions and statistics. (01:39:66) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]] and the [[Southern Poverty Law Center|SPLC]] as [[American Jewish Committee|Jewish organizations]] that produce and launder propaganda through NGOs and think tanks to be disseminated by left-wing media. (01:53:88) [[Mitch McConnell]] unveiled the [[Republican Party]]'s coronavirus relief plan, which proposed $105 billion to help schools reopen, $16 billion for state testing capacity, $26 billion for vaccine development, and liability shields for businesses, doctors, and schools. (01:56:82) The [[Republican Party]]'s proposed relief bill included $190 billion for additional Paycheck Protection Program loans and allowed small businesses with fewer than 300 employees that saw revenue fall by more than 50% to apply for a second round of aid. (02:00:90) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the [[Republican Party]]'s proposed coronavirus relief as grossly inadequate, noting that only $250 billion of trillions in total bailout spending went to direct payments to [[United States|Americans]]. (02:03:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the economic damage from government-mandated lockdowns was permanent, citing an example from [[China]] where 20% of restaurants were predicted never to reopen. (02:07:72) Nicholas J. Fuentes's "GROIPER meetups" included lawyers, scientists, and doctors, whom he described as "super high quality people."

### AF 2020-07-24 0648

(00:04:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that [[Ben Shapiro]]'s publication, the [[The Daily Wire|Daily Wire]], hosted a live stream on [[Google|YouTube]] featuring [[Ben Shapiro]], [[Jeremy Boreing]], [[Andrew Klavan]], and [[Michael Knowles]], which garnered only 4,000 live viewers despite the channel's 2.1 million subscribers.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:05:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the [[The Daily Wire|Daily Wire]]'s viewership with his own show on [[DLive]], which had nearly 5,000 viewers before going live, claiming his solo broadcast outclassed the professionally produced ensemble show funded by billionaires.

(00:07:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted watching a debate hosted by [[Jesse Lee Peterson]] on [[The Fallen State]] between [[Elijah Schaffer]] (also [[known]] as Slightly Offensive) and [[Hasan Piker|Vaush]] (Ian), which he found repulsive and indicative of a lack of authentic conservative argumentation.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:10:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes positioned his show as "full-throated, unambiguously... doubled down on reaction, on the right wing, on real conservatism," in contrast to figures like [[Elijah Schaffer]] [[who]] he argued were not authentically conservative.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:13:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Jesse Lee Peterson]] from the debate, [[who]] asked [[Hasan Piker|Vaush]] how it felt to hear "a logical white [[person]] [[who]] has no fear make an argument," a comment that caused [[Elijah Schaffer]] to facepalm in apparent embarrassment.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:16:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Elijah Schaffer]] had scrubbed interviews with him from his channel, indicating a shift away from engaging with figures like Nicholas J. Fuentes on the timeline.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:16:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that far-left figures like [[Hasan Piker|Vaush]] were "outing [[who]] is really a conservative and [[who]] isn't" by being able to argue "fake conservatives" like [[Elijah Schaffer]] into being friendly and agreeable.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:17:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes grouped [[Elijah Schaffer]] with figures like [[Hunter Avallone]], suggesting they were conservatives "okay with pedophiles that want to kill right-wingers and want to destroy [[United States|America]]," in contrast to "the real right-wing" represented by his show.

Fuentes, stated that [[Chevron]]'s chief human resources officer [[Rhonda Morris]] announced the company had selected 26% women for global roles and, in the [[United States]], 29% of selected candidates were from ethnically diverse backgrounds.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:21:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes interpreted the [[Chevron]] report as the company firing white male executives and replacing them with [[blacks]], people of color, and women, using the pandemic as a pretext to increase diversity quotas.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:22:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes connected the [[Chevron]] layoffs to a demographic battle in the [[United States]], [[Europe]], [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]], and [[Australia]], arguing that white populations were shrinking while non-white populations were exploding and being brought into the [[country]].

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:24:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the diversification of companies and countries as a zero-sum game where non-whites benefit at the expense of whites, [[who]] lose their jobs, prospects, and future position in society.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648

(00:32:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the [[Washington Examiner]] report again, stating that [[Donald Trump|President Trump]]'s shutdown of the southern border early in the coronavirus crisis kept nearly 190,000 illegal immigrants from 130 nations from entering the [[United States]].

(00:34:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes opined that the coronavirus pandemic had been "the best thing to happen to [[the White House]] on immigration," serving as a pretext to construct the border wall, keep out illegals, and shut down legal immigration.

(00:35:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the success in stopping 190,000 illegal immigrants proved the [[United States]] government could stop all illegal border crossings if it wanted to, suggesting the ongoing crisis was self-inflicted and facilitated by laws designed to enable immigration.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:40:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the government, comprised of representatives and bureaucrats, wanted illegal immigrants in the [[country]] because they owed their jobs to big business interests that benefited from cheap immigrant labor.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648

### AF 2020-07-24 0648

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:49:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Christopher Columbus]] statues were erected in the last century as a cultural celebration for Italian immigrants in industrial cities, making their removal a direct attack on Italian [[heritage]].

(00:50:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended the [[Christopher Columbus]] statues, not as a sanitized historical artifact, but as a celebration of [[Europe|European]] civilization bringing [[Jesus Christ|Christ]] and conquest to the continent, which he considered good and necessary history.

### AF 2020-07-24 0648
(00:54:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the 2017 [[Charlottesville]] rally, recalling that while others said Confederate statues could go because they represented racism, he and [[United States|America]] First argued they were about [[heritage]], [[country]], and people.

(00:34:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that opposing the LGBT agenda does not mean wanting to kill gay people, but supporting reasonable government regulations like fines and broadcasting licenses to protect society.

(00:35:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes introduced the featured story about [[Tucker Carlson]], whom he described as a rival in the same [[time]] slot [[who]] had been threatened by [[The New York Times]].

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:36:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that [[Tucker Carlson]] revealed on his show that [[The New York Times]] was planning to publish his home address, which had previously led to protesters at his home in 2018.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:36:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes read from a [[Forbes]] article detailing [[Tucker Carlson]]'s allegations against [[The New York Times]] and freelance journalist [[Murray Carpenter]].

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:37:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that after [[Tucker Carlson]] named the reporter and photographer, [[Murray Carpenter]] and [[Tristan Spinski]], their contact information and addresses were shared on [[Twitter]].

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:37:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[The New York Times]] responded on [[Twitter]] that it did not plan to publish [[Tucker Carlson]]'s residence, which [[Tucker Carlson|Carlson]] was aware of before his broadcast.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:38:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes said he believed [[Tucker Carlson]]'s account because it was too specific with names and timing to be made up, likely based on a tip.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:39:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Tucker Carlson]] is uniquely hated by the media, with weekly write-ups in [[Vice]] and [[The Atlantic]] accusing him of being a white nationalist.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:39:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the media for framing the journalists [[who]] planned to dox [[Tucker Carlson]] as the victims after their plan was exposed and they were doxed themselves.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:40:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that left-wing activists like [[Jared Holt]] and [[Will Sommer]] are paid to dox and harass conservatives with impunity, facing no consequences from conservative institutions.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:42:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Jared Holt]] is funded by the [[Project for the American Way]], which is funded by the [[Open Society Foundations]] founded by [[George Soros]].

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:42:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Jared Holt]] regularly attends [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]] with a press pass and is friendly with figures like [[Mike Cernovich]] and [[Wilt Chamberlain|Will Chamberlain]].

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:43:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes praised [[Tucker Carlson]] for firing back by naming the journalists on his show, which is watched by four to five million people nightly.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:44:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted his own retaliation against a [[person]] threatening [[Jaden McNeil]] by creating a website to expose her old racist tweets, which he said was legal.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:45:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the only motive for [[The New York Times]] to publish [[Tucker Carlson]]'s address was to intimidate him or have someone harm him, representing the media as a sworn enemy.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:46:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Tucker Carlson]] understands and communicates what is wrong with [[United States|America]] more effectively than [[Donald Trump]], which is why he is targeted.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:49:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[the left]]'s ability to swarm targets, as seen with [[Antifa]], is a tactical advantage, and the right needs comparable, legal, organized responses.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:50:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the "Groypa War" as an example of successfully mobilizing an online army of young supporters to counter opponents.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:51:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed hope that [[The New York Times]] would not publish [[Tucker Carlson]]'s address, recalling that protesters had previously bashed his door in.

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:51:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Tucker Carlson]], himself, [[Michelle Malkin]], and sometimes [[Donald Trump]] as the only people "telling it like it is."

### AF 2020-07-21 0645
(00:52:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared that if he ever got into power, [[The New York Times]] would "go to jail."

### AF 2020-07-20 0644
(01:35:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the deployment of 150 federal police to cities like [[Chicago]] was insufficient to quell widespread riots and violent crime, stating that thousands of officers or military occupation would be necessary to restore order.

(01:38:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump|President Trump]] had failed to deliver on his 2016 campaign promises of law and order, criticizing the administration for tweets and speeches rather than tangible action to secure [[United States|American]] cities.

### AF 2020-07-20 0644
(01:52:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that individual firearm ownership was an inadequate defense against mob violence, citing the case of the [[McCloskeys]], and advocated for discreet community [[organization]] and solidarity as the only viable means of protection.

### AF 2020-07-20 0644
(01:53:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the conflict in [[United States|American]] cities as a war between civilization and barbarism, drawing a parallel to the historical settlement of the continent where settlers had to kill Native [[United States|American]] attackers rather than negotiate.

(01:39:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the nationwide crime wave was caused by the emboldening of criminals and the demoralization of police forces following the [[George Floyd]] protests and calls to defund the police.

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:45:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the foundational principle of conservatism is the establishment of order above all other political virtues, and that this principle had been abandoned in [[the United States]].

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:48:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that demographic change and diversity were leading [[the United States]] toward a future of instability and violence comparable to [[Brazil]].

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:52:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that a conservative or nationalist must prioritize demographic stability to ensure national order, arguing that policies leading to demographic change were antithetical to conservatism.

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:59:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised viewers to protect themselves from both criminal violence and potential state prosecution, emphasizing situational awareness and strategic caution over merely arming themselves.(01:34:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that popular self-help books like *How to Win Friends and Influence People* and *The Power of Positive Thinking* contain simple, common-sense concepts that can be understood by reading only the table of contents and chapter conclusions.

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:36:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized most people as living with dysfunctional behaviors like overspending, making decisions based on short-term emotion, and wasting [[time]] without pursuing meaningful self-improvement.

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:36:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes described society as a hierarchical pyramid where the majority of people occupy the base and only a few can reach the top, a fact he considered an immutable reality of life.

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:37:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the self-help industry for offering a false promise of success to people at the base of the societal pyramid, arguing that factors like genetics, height, and intelligence create uneven ranges of achievable potential from birth.

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:39:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that a rigid, caste-based society might be preferable as it allows people to accept their fate and find mobility within their group, rather than toiling endlessly towards unattainable goals.

(01:40:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he blocked the writer [[known]] as "BAP" on [[Twitter]] after a dispute over race realism and IQ, dismissing BAP's claim of [[Mediterranean Sea|Mediterranean]] ancestry as a "cope" and labeling him a "phony."

(01:41:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed skepticism towards reports of [[China]] conducting atrocities against Uighurs, likening them to "atrocity propaganda" and "Neocon propaganda," while acknowledging the Uighurs were suffering under the [[China|Chinese]] regime.

(01:42:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed high rates of [[black]] fatherlessness to a lack of contraception use, young pregnancies, fathers not sticking around or being incarcerated, and a cultural inability to delay gratification, which he linked to inherent genetic differences.

(01:49:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that in-[[person]] organizing is necessary and that the failures of the [[Charlottesville]] rally were preventable mistakes, such as inviting the [[National Socialist Movement]] and holding an open, outdoor event instead of a controlled, indoor gathering.

### AF 2020-07-17 0643
(01:52:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized "BAP" as having terrible engagement on [[Twitter]], an unchanged brand for five years, a "stupid gimmick" involving baby talk, and a poorly written, unedited book that was reviewed by "retard" [[Michael Anton]].

### AF 2020-07-17 0643

(01:57:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he built the [[United States|America]] First internet movement and space through his own ingenuity and creative vision, contrasting himself with figures [[who]] build clout by tearing down other people.

(01:07:17.520) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused the [[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention|CDC]], [[Anthony Fauci|Fauci]], the [[White House]], and the mainstream media of peddling lies, contrasting the [[United States]] response with [[Sweden]] and [[China]], which had "moved on."

### AF 2020-07-16 0642
(01:07:54.000) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that bullish [[stock]] market investors, [[who]] risk their own money, did not believe in the catastrophic second wave narrative, unlike media and government figures [[who]] face no consequences for lying.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(00:17:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the May 28, 2020, riots in [[Minneapolis]] escalated from peaceful protests into arson, looting, vandalism, and shootings in response to the death of [[George Floyd]].

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:00:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the looting of specific stores like the [[Apple Store]], [[Thurston Jewelers]], and [[High Lake Liquors]] indicated opportunism and greed rather than political outrage over [[George Floyd]]'s death.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:02:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the riots occurred because the participants could not or refused to behave in a way that lives up to a civilized society, citing a pattern of similar events in [[Ferguson]], [[Baltimore]], [[Chicago]], [[City|Detroit]], and [[St. Louis]].

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:03:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the disproportionate frequency of such riots among [[black]] communities, both in the [[United States]] and in countries like [[Haiti]], [[Nigeria]], [[Somalia]], and [[Jamaica]], pointed to a behavioral pattern rather than political or economic causes.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:05:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Donald Trump]] for not commenting on the riots, suggesting it was due to a misguided belief that he could win the [[black]] vote in the 2020 election.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:08:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes proposed imposing indefinite martial law in [[United States|American]] neighborhoods with murder rates as high as those in [[Chicago]]'s [[Englewood]] and [[Humboldt Park]].

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:12:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed claims that the riots were orchestrated by [[George Soros]] or [[Antifa]], attributing them instead to racial animus and [[black]] dysfunction.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:14:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[Minneapolis]] riots as the latest episode in a historical pattern of "[[black]] dysfunction," comparing it to riots that occurred in [[African continent|Africa]] following decolonization in the 1960s.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:19:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that abusive police behavior in some neighborhoods stems from officers facing constant threats, gang activity, and non-compliance over long careers, not from inherent racism.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:19:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the pervasive focus on white racism and white supremacy as [[United States|America]]'s purported "original sin," which he described as the dominant narrative about the [[country]]'s most salient problem.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:21:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a video of a [[black]] man robbing a convenience store, tying up the clerk, dousing her in lighter fluid, and setting her on fire for seven dollars as an example of violent crime that fuels public fear and calls to police.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:21:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that the civil disorder and destruction seen in [[Minneapolis]] could become a future reality for neighborhoods across the [[United States]] if current trajectories of demographic change and lawlessness continue unchecked.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:23:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for an immediate halt to all immigration as the first step to protect [[United States|American]] communities from further destabilization.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:24:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes called for laws granting local communities autonomy to decide [[who]] can live within them, arguing this is necessary to preserve safety and order.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:24:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[U.S. Constitution|Second Amendment]] and [[Concept|First Amendment]] are essential tools for self-defense and open discourse needed to address the root causes of societal problems like the [[Minneapolis]] riots.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:24:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that governments must seriously address criminality by arresting rioters and looters and imposing long jail sentences to prevent a bleak national future.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:25:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized mainstream conservatives for pivoting to [[the left]] or remaining silent in response to the [[Minneapolis]] riots, refusing to honestly discuss the causes of the violence.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:26:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[U.S. Department of Defense|National Guard]] should have been activated immediately to quell the riots in [[Minneapolis]] and should be a permanent presence in high-crime areas like [[Chicago]].

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:26:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that over Memorial Day weekend in [[Chicago]], 10 people were killed and 50 were shot, illustrating the scale of chronic violence in some [[United States|American]] cities.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:27:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes linked ongoing demographic change in the [[United States]] to the exacerbation of social unrest and civil disorder, arguing it does not benefit societal stability.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:27:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that rioters in [[Minneapolis]] targeted and destroyed their own community because they perceived it as "white" or "[[United States|America]]," and therefore not truly theirs.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:27:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that a mob would have lynched the police officer involved in the death of [[George Floyd]] if not for a large police presence protecting his home, illustrating a climate of mob violence and absent due process.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:28:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that within the decade, a police officer [[who]] kills a [[black]] individual would likely be murdered by a [[black]] mob, marking a catastrophic [[turning point]] for the [[country]].

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:29:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the potential consequences of a police disappearance in a white neighborhood—minor loitering or speeding—with the looting, arson, and destruction that occurred in [[Minneapolis]].

### AF 2020-05-28 0615
(01:30:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that "demographics is [[destiny]]," arguing that the behavioral patterns seen in troubled neighborhoods are an inevitable outcome of their population composition.

### AF 2020-05-28 0615

(00:05:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed his show was the only one to cover recent racial incidents with nuance, analyzing each case individually rather than offering dogmatic left-wing or right-wing answers.

(00:06:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[the left]]'s dogmatic answer as always blaming the white [[person]] and portraying the [[black]] [[person]] as an innocent victim, as seen with [[Ahmaud Arbery]] being called an aspiring electrician.

### AF 2020-05-27 0614
(00:08:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that only nationalists and the [[United States|America]] First movement had the answer to the race relations crisis because they were willing to analyze each incident honestly with all facts in mind.

(00:21:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the massive media coverage of the [[Ahmaud Arbery]] shooting with the lack of coverage for the deadly violence in [[Chicago]] over Memorial Day weekend.

(00:47:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that the legislative solution to tech censorship revolves around Section 230 of the [[New York|Communications Decency Act]], which protects platforms from liability if they do not exert editorial control.

(01:13:12) [[Moncef Slaoui]] expressed confidence that a few hundred million doses of a coronavirus vaccine would be delivered by the end of 2020, despite acknowledging the ambitious timeline in an interview with the [[The New York Times|New York Times]]. Pritzker]], and [[Gavin Newsom]], not the federal government, held the authority to reopen their respective states following coronavirus lockdowns. Penney]] and [[J.Crew]] filed for bankruptcy amid the coronavirus pandemic, accelerating a trend of declining brick-and-mortar stores already struggling against online retailers like [[Amazon Inc.|Amazon]]. (01:31:40) The [[United States]] [[U.S. Department of Justice|Department of Justice]] was reportedly aiming to file an antitrust lawsuit against [[Google]] as early as the summer of 2020, with a multi-state probe led by [[State of Texas|Texas]] Attorney General [[Ken Paxton]] likely to file suit in the fall. (01:35:10) The [[United States]] [[Federal Trade Commission]] had closed a previous antitrust investigation into [[Google]]'s search business in 2013, unanimously opting not to bring a lawsuit. (01:35:40) The [[United States]] [[House]] Judiciary Committee was conducting a probe into [[Google]] and three other major tech companies, expected to culminate in legislative proposals rather than enforcement action. Fuentes argued that access to social media platforms like [[Google|YouTube]] and [[Facebook]] was essential for the survival and growth of dissident political movements, as traditional media like print, radio, and television were closed off. (02:48:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that figures like [[Andrew Anglin]], [[Chuck Johnson]], and [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] had been successively banned from major internet platforms, a pattern he believed would eventually extend to commentators like [[Steven Crowder]] and [[Ben Shapiro]]. (02:50:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Donald Trump]] thanking his "keyboard warriors" on [[Twitter]] acknowledged the role of online activists in driving an ideological revolution within the [[Republican Party]]. (02:58:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Democratic Party]], exemplified by [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]], focused on acquiring political power through measures like eliminating the Electoral College, opposing voter ID laws, and letting felons vote, while the [[Republican Party]] failed to prioritize its own political advantage. (03:01:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the administration of [[Barack Obama]] using the [[Internal Revenue Service]] to [[target]] conservative organizations' nonprofit status, the [[Environmental Protection Agency]] to [[target]] gun manufacturers, and [[Department of Housing and Urban Development|Housing and Urban Development]] rules to open Section 8 housing in majority-white neighborhoods as examples of using state power to punish political opponents.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:00:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the return of his show *[[United States|America]] First* on a Thursday evening, framing the broadcast as a response to a slow news cycle.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:00:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the evening's featured story as a new stimulus proposal from [[House Democrats]], a three trillion dollar bill that included a $1,200 cash payment for all individuals, including college students, children, and illegal immigrants.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:02:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the significance of the [[House Democrats]]' stimulus bill was not its contents but the fact that the [[Republican Party]] was offering no alternative relief during what he described as the worst recession in [[United States|American]] history, with 20 million jobs lost and unemployment potentially exceeding 20%.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:03:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced a secondary topic for the show: the exhumation of [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]] and his wife's remains from a state park in [[Memphis]], [[Tennessee]], following the removal of his monument by a private nonprofit, [[Memphis Greenspace]].

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:04:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes revealed his personal attendance at the [[Charlottesville]] rally in August 2017, which he stated was organized to protest the removal of Confederate monuments, a cultural transformation he argued had only accelerated since that event.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:07:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported on a recent webinar hosted by [[Dan Crenshaw]], during which a questioner asked about the 1965 Immigration Act and mass immigration; Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Cassandra Fairbanks]]'s coverage of the event.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:08:34) [[Dan Crenshaw]] responded to a question on immigration during his webinar by stating that white people would not do the jobs immigrants do, a claim Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized as the biggest and most pervasive immigration myth.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:10:23) [[Dan Crenshaw]] stated during his webinar that he was not familiar with the 1965 Hart-Celler Immigration Act, which Nicholas J. Fuentes described as a foundational and significant piece of [[United States|American]] immigration law.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:11:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes highlighted past [[Facebook]] posts from [[Dan Crenshaw]] that criticized [[Donald Trump]] as Islamophobic and called [[Christianity|Christian]] extremists hypocrites, contrasting them with his current image as a conservative [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporter.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:12:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Dan Crenshaw]] of "shameless [[Israel]] shilling" and being an ignorant puppet controlled by donors and lobbyists, citing his lack of knowledge on immigration law as evidence.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:15:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Charlie Kirk]], [[Dan Crenshaw]], [[Ben Shapiro]], and [[Matt Walsh]] as dishonorable, ignorant, and cowardly adversaries, claiming they lacked the respectable character of historical [[United States|American]] conflict.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:17:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes sarcastically requested to see a picture of [[Dan Crenshaw]] "taking the money from [[Israel]]," contrasting it with the congressman's public macho image.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:18:18) [[Dan Crenshaw]] stated publicly that "our morality comes from [[Jerusalem]]," a claim Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected, asserting his own morality came from [[Jesus Christ]], not the [[Israel|Jewish state]].

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:19:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes promoted his new merchandise available at Merch.NicholasJFuentes.com, including an "I'm With Groyper" T-shirt that had generated controversy on [[Twitter]].

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:20:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes promoted his new subscription website, NicholasJFuentes.com, offering access to his entire catalog of *[[United States|America]] First* videos, speeches, debates, and unreleased content for five dollars per month.
