(00:21:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Ben Shapiro]] for performing four [[ad]] reads during a live taping of his podcast at the March for Life rally, characterizing it as a greedy act.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:21:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Charlie Kirk]] of filing a DMCA copyright claim against a video Nicholas J. Fuentes linked under [[Charlie Kirk|Kirk]]'s tweet, which showed [[Charlie Kirk|Kirk]] advocating for 40 million more work visas in October, contradicting his later tweet supporting a shutdown on temporary work visas.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:22:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that [[Dan Crenshaw]] could run for [[the White House]] based solely on his "G.I. Joe with the eyepatch" caricature, despite the congressman's alleged ignorance and shifting political allegiances.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:22:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Rob Smith]], [[Charlie Kirk]], [[Benny Johnson]], [[Dan Crenshaw]], [[Ben Shapiro]], and [[Matt Walsh]] as a collection of dishonorable and unlikeable adversaries, citing [[Benny Johnson]]'s history as a serial plagiarist and [[Rob Smith]]'s past as a [[Democratic Party|Democrat]] [[who]] advertised for gay dating apps.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:23:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes began reading a report stating that the remains of Confederate General [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]] and his wife would be removed from [[Health Sciences Park]] in [[Memphis]], [[Tennessee]], where a monument to him once stood, following an agreement with the [[Sons of Confederate Veterans]].

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:25:38) The mayor of [[Memphis]] stated that the statues of [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]] and [[Jefferson Davis]] "no longer represent [[who]] we are as a modern diverse [[city]] with momentum," a rationale Nicholas J. Fuentes interpreted as a rejection of historical [[United States|America]] in favor of a liberal, non-white, globalist future.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:30:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Abraham Lincoln]] believed freed slaves would return to [[African continent|Africa]], not live in equality with whites, and that all the Founding Fathers were complicit in or directly practiced slavery, drawing a parallel to the targeted removal of [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]]'s remains.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:30:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Stacey Abrams]]'s 2013 hot mic comment about the danger of a white conservative minority government controlling the [[Southern United States|South]] and the *[[The New York Times|New York Times]]*'s 1619 Project as evidence of a broader campaign to rewrite [[United States|American]] history and erase white [[heritage]].

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:32:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the movement to remove monuments like that of [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]] was not fundamentally about racism but about a racial grievance against white [[United States|Americans]] and [[United States|American]] history itself, with the end goal of unseating the white majority.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:33:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]] and [[Jefferson Davis]] monuments in [[Memphis]] were frequently vandalized, which he interpreted as proof that the population had already turned against the [[country]]'s historical myth, making their official removal inevitable.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:34:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that militant [[black]] activists and [[the left]] see no distinction between figures like [[George Washington]], [[Abraham Lincoln]], or [[Thomas Jefferson]] and [[Ku Klux Klan]] leaders like [[Nathan Bedford Forrest]], because all existed outside the current liberal paradigm.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:35:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[United States|America]] in 1776 was a white, [[Christianity|Christian]], [[United Kingdom|British]] republic, not a modern, diverse nation, and that the Founding Fathers were not civil rights activists or social justice warriors but men [[who]] owned slaves and restricted suffrage.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:37:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed a radical revolutionary consensus, forged by mass media, academia, and culture-making institutions over the past few decades, now governs how [[United States|American]] history is viewed, which he described as a lens constructed by liberal and [[Jews|Jewish]] intellectuals.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:38:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes sarcastically imagined the Founding Fathers [[time]]-traveling to modern [[United States|America]] and expressing horror at urban decay, crime, drug addiction, and transgenderism, rather than approval for the progressive transformation of their nation.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:40:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted a symbolic future where monuments to [[Thomas Jefferson]] and [[Abraham Lincoln]] are bulldozed and replaced with monuments to [[Barack Obama]] or museums about racism and Nazi hunters, representing the bulldozing of [[United States|American]] culture.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:41:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that only in white countries is historical criticism of past leaders used as a pretext to "bulldoze a people, a nation, an entire civilization," whereas countries like [[Saudi Arabia]], [[China]], and [[Russia]] continue to revere their foundational figures regardless of modern moral standards.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:43:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes, referencing his own Mexican and [[Ireland|Irish]]/Italian ancestry, stated that immigrants should respect the [[heritage]] and culture of the [[country]] they arrive in, rather than loot, pillage, and vandalize it, framing his defense of [[United States|American]] [[heritage]] as explicitly cultural rather than exclusively racial.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:44:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes outlined the three phases of coronavirus relief passed by [[Congress]], totaling roughly three trillion dollars, with the third phase being a $2.1 trillion stimulus that included $1,200 cash payments and bailouts for airlines, cruise lines, and [[The Wall Street Journal|Wall Street]].

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:45:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Republican Party|House Republicans]] were refusing further stimulus, while [[House Democrats]], led by [[Nancy Pelosi]], had released a new $3 trillion bill for a vote, which included nearly $1 trillion for state/local governments, a second round of $1,200 direct payments, $200 billion in hazard pay, and an extension of federal unemployment benefits.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:48:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the [[House Democrats]]' stimulus bill as a "naked political play" that included money for illegal immigrants and funding for irrelevant items like marijuana dispensaries, but argued the [[Republican Party]]'s refusal to offer any relief was worse, as the government-induced shutdown had caused the economic crisis.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:49:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the economic crisis was government-induced through shutdowns that made business illegal, and therefore the government had a responsibility to provide financial relief, mocking [[Ben Shapiro]]'s description of economic hardship as "the adventure of your lifetime."

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:52:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused the [[Republican Party]] of hypocrisy for claiming another $3 trillion in stimulus was unaffordable while routinely spending trillions on foreign wars, entitlements, cash payments to immigrants, and public sector pensions, framing fiscal conservatism as a racket to prevent the middle class from accessing government money.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:54:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the $1,200 cash payment program cost $250 billion, roughly one-twelfth of the total stimulus spent so far, and questioned why the government couldn't provide another $250 billion or even $5,000 to individuals during the crisis.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:55:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the [[United States|American]] political system as the "biggest racket in the history of mankind," where politicians spend millions to get elected to offices that control trillions in tax revenue and borrowing capacity, with the benefits flowing to contractors and industries rather than the taxpaying middle class.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:57:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the middle class is deliberately kept on a "hamster wheel" and denied benefits like [[Universal Studios|Universal]] Basic Income because once they stop being "tax livestock," the system that feeds "all the important people" and immigrant voters would collapse.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(00:59:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed past support for [[Andrew Yang]]'s "Yang Gang" and [[Universal Studios|Universal]] Basic Income, praising the idea of a politician offering tangible, direct financial help to voters as a refreshing departure from vague promises of economic growth.

(01:00:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the [[Republican Party]] for being willing to spend trillions on corporations like [[Raytheon Technologies Corporation|Raytheon]], [[Raytheon Technologies Corporation|Lockheed Martin]], [[United Airlines]], and [[Southwest Airlines]] while refusing further direct aid to the middle class, suggesting this money often ends up in [[stock]] buybacks rather than wage increases or job creation.

### AF 2020-05-14 0605
(01:01:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to the declining velocity of money over 30 years as evidence that wealth is being hoarded by the rich rather than circulated through the economy, undermining the trickle-down justification for corporate bailouts and tax cuts.

(01:02:04) Nicholas J. (00:52:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[Republican Party]]'s recapture of [[State of California|California]]'s 25th district as a potential "last dying gasp" in a state where demographic trends favor the [[Democratic Party]].

(00:37:50) Nicholas J. (00:39:20) Nicholas J. (00:41:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that the political left, driven by a coalition of non-white groups and self-hating white liberals, is motivated not by a desire for equality but by racial revenge and a goal to displace white people from positions of power in [[United States|America]].

(00:47:00) Nicholas J. (00:52:50) Nicholas J. (50:48:68) Nicholas J. Fuentes urged [[Donald Trump]] to bring all [[United States]] troops home from abroad, seal the border, shut down trade, de-invest from [[China]], and stop politicians from taking money from [[China]], using the crisis capital of the pandemic to implement a sweeping nationalist agenda.

### AF 2020-03-19 0568
(00:01:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the main story for the show would be about the coronavirus pandemic and related economic stimulus proposals. (00:01:40) [[Senate]] [[Republican Party|Republicans]] unveiled a draft proposal to provide $1,200 cash payments to individuals earning under $75,000 and $2,400 to couples earning under $150,000, with amounts tapering for higher incomes and reduced payments for those with low taxable income. (00:03:06) The [[United States Senate]] passed the first phase of a coronavirus relief package, which included free coronavirus testing and paid sick leave. (00:04:18) [[Italian Republic|Italy]] surpassed [[China]] in official confirmed deaths from coronavirus, with [[Italian Republic|Italy]] reporting 41,000 cases compared to [[China]]'s 80,000. (00:05:07) [[Richard Burr]], a [[Senator]] from [[North Carolina]] and member of the [[Senate Intelligence Committee]], sold his [[stock]] holdings in late February while publicly stating the [[United States]] had the coronavirus under control. (00:06:57) [[Tulsi Gabbard]] dropped out of the [[Democratic presidential primary]] and endorsed [[Joe Biden]]. (00:07:37) [[Tulsi Gabbard]]'s presidential campaign was primarily focused on opposing foreign regime change wars. (00:08:38) [[Joe Biden]] voted for the [[Iraq War]] in 2003 and, as [[Vice President]] under [[Barack Obama]], supported the [[NATO intervention in Libya]] that deposed [[Muammar Gaddafi]] and backed funding for the [[Syria|Syrian]] opposition against [[Bashar al-Assad]]. (00:11:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his opposition to regime change in [[Syria]], the wars in [[Iraq]] and [[Afghanistan]], war with [[Iran]], and expressed skepticism of alliances with [[Saudi Arabia]], [[Qatar]], and [[Israel]]. (00:12:27) [[Tulsi Gabbard]] was a member of the [[Council on Foreign Relations]]. (00:16:50) [[Tulsi Gabbard]] was a frequent guest on [[Tucker Carlson]]'s show. (00:20:09) [[Donald Trump]] opposed unnecessary regime change wars. (00:20:57) [[Donald Trump]] worked to reduce illegal immigration, build a wall, restrict asylum seekers, and reduce levels of legal immigration. (00:21:32) [[Donald Trump]] opposed [[China]] and free trade, worked to bring troops home from [[Syria]], and negotiated an end to the war in [[Afghanistan]]. (00:22:19) [[Donald Trump]] authorized three rounds of missile strikes on [[Syria]] in 2017 and 2018. (00:22:52) The [[Donald Trump]] administration abandoned the [[Barack Obama]] administration's goal of regime change in [[Syria]] and announced a near-total withdrawal of [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. forces]] from [[Syria]] in August 2019. (00:27:26) The number of confirmed coronavirus cases in the [[United States]] increased from approximately 9,000 to nearly 14,000 in 24 hours. (00:28:04) Global confirmed coronavirus cases reached 246,328, with [[China]] reporting 80,928, [[Italian Republic|Italy]] at 41,035, [[Iran]] at 18,407, [[Spain]] at 18,077, [[Germany]] at 15,320, the [[United States]] at nearly 14,000, [[France]] at 10,995, and [[South Korea]] at 8,565. (00:30:53) The [[United States]] conducted 22,000 coronavirus tests in a single day, a significant increase from an estimated maximum capacity of 15,000-20,000 tests per day the previous week. (00:32:23) Drive-through coronavirus testing centers were established in [[Washington state]] and [[New York]]. (00:32:50) [[South Korea]] conducted an average of 15,000 coronavirus tests per day, which helped control its outbreak. (00:39:09) The [[The New York Times|New York Times]] reported that [[Senate]] [[Republican Party|Republicans]] unveiled an economic rescue plan providing hundreds of billions in loans to corporations and small businesses, corporate tax cuts, and $1,200 checks for taxpayers earning up to $75,000, with amounts tapering to zero for those earning over $99,000. (00:40:45) [[Mitch McConnell]], the [[Senate Majority Leader]], introduced the economic relief proposal after private talks with [[Republican Party|Republicans]] in the [[White House]]. (00:41:49) The [[Republican Party|Republican]] relief proposal included an additional $500 per child for eligible individuals and couples. (00:45:07) The [[Republican Party|Republican]] relief plan proposed moving the income tax filing deadline from April 15 to July 15. (00:45:56) The [[Republican Party|Republican]] plan authorized over $200 billion in support for hard-hit industries, including $50 billion for commercial airlines and $8 billion for air cargo carriers. (00:55:09) [[ProPublica]] reported that [[Richard Burr]], as chair of the [[Senate Intelligence Committee]], sold between $600,000 and $1.72 million in stocks in 33 transactions a week before the market declined by 30%, after receiving daily coronavirus briefings. (00:56:44) [[National Public Radio|NPR]] obtained a recording from February 27 in which [[Richard Burr]] gave a dire preview of the coronavirus's economic impact to a VIP group at the [[Capitol Hill Club]], whose members paid up to $10,000 for access. (00:57:55) In a February 7 op-ed, [[Richard Burr]] assured the public the [[United States]] was better prepared than ever for public health threats like the coronavirus. (00:58:18) [[Richard Burr]] sold up to $150,000 in shares of [[Wyndham Hotels & Resorts]] and up to $100,000 in shares of [[Extended Stay America]], companies whose values later plummeted.

(01:24:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised viewers to prepare for potential lockdowns by obtaining supplies from multiple stores or online services.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began his broadcast on Monday, March 16, 2020, by announcing the show would focus on the ongoing coronavirus pandemic and the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary elections.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(00:04:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[United States]] was seeing widespread closures of bars, restaurants, and schools, with cities under lockdown and the [[Dow Jones & Company|Dow Jones]] Industrial Average down nearly 13%.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(00:09:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed he had been consistently covering the coronavirus outbreak since January 21st, 2020, when it was a small outbreak in [[Wuhan]], and had accurately predicted the shutdown of restaurants and the spread seen in [[Italian Republic|Italy]] would occur in the [[United States]].

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(00:15:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary contests scheduled for March 17th in [[Arizona]], [[State of Florida|Florida]], and [[State of Illinois|Illinois]] would proceed, while [[State of Ohio|Ohio]] had postponed its primary to June 2nd.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(00:28:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the 11th [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debate had occurred the previous day, hosted by [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]] and [[Telemundo]], featuring only [[Joe Biden]] and [[Bernie Sanders]] with no audience.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(00:35:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the coronavirus pandemic could prolong the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary race by forcing states to delay voting, preventing [[Joe Biden]] from securing a majority of delegates and allowing [[Bernie Sanders]] to remain in the contest.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(00:46:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Harvard University]] was one of the first colleges to tell students not to return from spring break and move classes online, a measure he predicted other colleges would follow.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(00:50:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that he had advised viewers in January to [[stock]] up on essentials like water, food, and ammunition in preparation for a potential pandemic, and noted that grocery stores were now packed and shelves were being cleared.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:01:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes described his recent activities, including streaming the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debate, playing Fortnite with MAGA rapper [[Bryson Gray]], and making daily trips to [[stock]] up on groceries and gas.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:12:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the coronavirus pandemic as a major historical event comparable to World War II, the [[John F. Kennedy|JFK]] assassination, or [[September 11 attacks|9/11]], and expressed fascination at living through it.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:17:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the 11th [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debate as boring and pointless, arguing [[Joe Biden]] was already the certain nominee and [[Bernie Sanders]] delivered the same repetitive stump speech.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:21:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued it was irresponsible for states to hold primary elections during the pandemic, as moving polling stations would not prevent infected voters from spreading the virus to poll workers and other voters.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:25:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes encouraged viewers not to vote in the primary elections due to the health risk, stating the outcome was predetermined and "this [[country]]'s fucked no matter what."

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:26:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented global coronavirus case numbers, reporting 181,853 confirmed cases worldwide with 7,113 deaths, including 80,000 cases in [[China]], 28,000 in [[Italian Republic|Italy]], 15,000 in [[Iran]], and 4,525 in the [[United States]].

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:30:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that for every confirmed case in the [[United States]], there were likely 5 to 10 undetected cases, estimating the true number was between 22,500 and 50,000.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:31:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump]] and his coronavirus task force had issued new guidelines recommending [[United States|Americans]] avoid gatherings of more than 10 people, avoid dining in at bars and restaurants, and engage in schooling from home.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:32:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that young and healthy people must also take precautions to slow the virus's transmission and prevent overburdening the healthcare system, citing reports that a significant percentage of ICU patients in [[Italian Republic|Italy]] were under 50.

(01:39:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that contracting the virus could lead to permanent organ damage, including lung and kidney damage, and potential infertility, even for those [[who]] do not die.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:41:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized individuals and public figures, including libertarians and a journalist on [[Twitter]], for framing defiance of public health guidelines as an act of resilience or freedom, calling it pathetic and a sign of national weakness.

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:44:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States]] lacked the collective identity and ability to make sacrifices needed to handle serious crises, predicting future challenges would wreak havoc on the [[country]].

(01:48:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed states that had ordered all restaurants and bars to close dine-in services, including [[State of Colorado|Colorado]], [[State of Connecticut|Connecticut]], [[State of Illinois|Illinois]], [[New York]], and [[Washington D.C.|Washington]].

### AF 2020-03-16 0565
(01:55:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the entire [[San Francisco Bay Area]], including the [[city]] of [[San Francisco Bay Area|San Francisco]], had been placed under a mandatory "shelter at home" order for three weeks, allowing only essential businesses to remain open.

(01:57:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that similar total lockdowns and curfews would soon be implemented in other major cities like [[Chicago]], [[New York|New York City]], and [[Los Angeles]].

### AF 2020-03-16 0565

(00:19:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that low coronavirus testing rates in the [[United States]]—five tests per million people compared to 3,692 in [[South Korea]] and 826 in [[Italian Republic|Italy]]—meant the true number of cases was exponentially higher and the virus was not contained.

(01:23:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he would never get another vaccine for the rest of his life due to a severe anxiety reaction to needles and physiological procedures.

### AF 2020-03-09 0560
(01:34:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Ben Shapiro]]'s rebuttal that "[[Israel]] can defend herself" during a debate as the one substantive counter-argument, which he also labeled a lie.

### AF 2020-03-09 0560
(01:37:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he and [[Michelle Malkin]] did not grill each other on policy positions during their [[time]] together in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]], as he considered such behavior autistic and not typical of normal friendships.

### AF 2020-03-09 0560
(02:04:07) A Yelp review for Nicholas J. Fuentes's local post office confirmed his experience, with another customer complaining about an extremely rude employee with an attitude, leading them to change their shipping address to avoid her.

### AF 2020-03-09 0560
(02:08:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that students at his middle school, Park, universally hated [[Michelle Obama]] because her school lunch rules took effect when he entered, drastically changing and limiting their meal options.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(00:04:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the featured story for the broadcast would be the spread of the coronavirus, noting the world had surpassed 100,000 confirmed cases and the [[United States]] had over 300.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(00:35:98) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the [[United States]] federal government passed an $8.3 billion aid package in response to the coronavirus.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(00:52:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[New York|New York State]] had tested fewer than 100 people for coronavirus in the preceding months, despite having 41 confirmed cases in [[New York|New York City]].

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(03:45:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the show would also discuss International Women's Day, which he characterized as a recurring celebration.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(04:38:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that International Women's Day originated approximately a hundred years ago with socialists in [[Russia]].

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(13:14:78) Nicholas J. Fuentes read from an article defining International Women's Day as dedicated to celebrating women's achievements and bringing attention to gender parity, which it defined as a statistical measure comparing income, education, and work hours.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(21:25:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes read that the first observance of International Women's Day was in 1909 when the [[Socialist Party of America]] celebrated 15,000 women [[who]] protested in [[New York|New York City]].

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(21:56:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes read that [[Russia]] set the trend for celebrating on March 8th, and that on March 8, 1917, tens of thousands of [[Russian]] women took to the streets demanding change, which paved the way for them to be granted voting rights.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(26:14:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes read that the [[United Nations]] officially recognized International Women's Day in 1975 and began adopting an annual theme in 1996.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(43:31:88) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that worldwide confirmed cases of coronavirus reached 101,919, having just passed the 100,000 mark for the first [[time]] that day.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(44:11:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed the following coronavirus case counts: 80,651 in mainland [[China]], 6,767 in [[South Korea]], 4,747 in [[Iran]], 4,636 in [[Italian Republic|Italy]], 670 in [[Germany]], 613 in [[France]], 350 in [[State of Japan|Japan]], 321 in the [[United States]], and 374 in [[Spain]].

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(44:56:66) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that central banks in various countries were promising economic stimulus in response to the coronavirus.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(48:48:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration was downplaying the coronavirus outbreak and limiting testing to avoid triggering a bad economic effect in an election year.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(50:31:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that coronavirus testing in the [[United States]] required meeting a stringent series of qualifications and was not being administered widely, even in [[known]] transmission areas.

### AF 2020-03-06 0559
(51:29:98) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that coronavirus testing kits in at least two states were found to deliver false negatives, leading to people being released from quarantine only to later test positive.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:05:04) A letter from a writer associated with [[Robert Spencer]]'s website, Jihad Watch, condemned [[Michelle Malkin]] for defending Nicholas J. Fuentes and speaking at the [[AFPAC]] conference.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:09:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes's show was banned from [[Google|YouTube]] on February 14th, prompting him to develop a proprietary streaming service on his website to circumvent platform censorship.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:11:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes planned to implement a subscription paywall on his new streaming website, charging five dollars per month for access to an archive of over 550 past shows.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:15:22) The inaugural [[AFPAC]] conference, held in [[Washington D.C.|Washington DC]] and organized by Nicholas J. Fuentes, was considered an unambiguous victory and a foundational event for his political movement.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:20:29) [[Robert Spencer]] and [[Pamela Geller]], through their website Jihad Watch, published a letter criticizing [[Michelle Malkin]] for defending Nicholas J. Fuentes and questioning historical narratives about World War II and dual loyalty.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:21:54) Columnist [[Bret Stephens]] wrote in [[The New York Times]] that "[[Israel]] cannot be safe in an [[United States|America]] First world," a statement Nicholas J. Fuentes cited as emblematic of the conflict between nationalism and globalism.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:25:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was willing to engage in civil debate with figures like [[Laura Loomer]], [[Jacob Wohl]], [[Charlie Kirk]], and [[Ben Shapiro]], but accused them of demanding submission to specific talking points on [[Israel]] and open borders.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:26:25) [[Robert Spencer]] and [[Pamela Geller]], [[who]] have been labeled Islamophobic by groups like the [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]] and [[Southern Poverty Law Center|SPLC]], were among the first to attack [[Michelle Malkin]] and Nicholas J. Fuentes using similar accusations of bigotry.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:34:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[AIPAC]] policy conference, attended by two-thirds of [[Congress]] and [[the President]], demonstrated a problematic dual loyalty where officials swear support to a foreign nation.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:35:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that the most vocal critics of his movement's stance on dual loyalty, such as [[Pamela Geller]], [[Robert Spencer]], and [[Ben Shapiro]], were themselves [[Jews|Jewish]] Zionists.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:36:23) [[Charlie Kirk]] and [[Ben Shapiro]] used a joke Nicholas J. Fuentes made about "cookie monster ovens" as a central attack against him during the "Groypers war," believing it to be a definitive criticism.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:38:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the 1967 USS Liberty incident, where [[Israel]] attacked a [[United States]] [[U.S. Department of Defense|Navy]] ship, as an example of historical events he believes are minimized while narratives about World War II are enforced.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:42:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that political smears are effective because they isolate targets, citing [[Michelle Malkin]], [[Gavin McInnes]], and himself as examples, but claimed their power diminishes as more people rally behind a cause.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:44:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified that his advocacy for "optics" was never about hiding beliefs but about presenting the [[United States|America]] First message in a way that is persuasive and comfortable for normal people to accept.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:53:13) [[Elizabeth Warren]] remained in the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary race after Super Tuesday, a decision Nicholas J. Fuentes argued acted as a spoiler by preventing the progressive vote from consolidating behind [[Bernie Sanders]].

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(00:57:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes drew a parallel between the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] establishment's treatment of [[Bernie Sanders]] and the [[Republican Party|GOP]] establishment's past opposition to [[Donald Trump]], suggesting both populist outsiders faced similar systemic resistance.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(01:00:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Joe Biden]], as the likely [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] nominee, was a weak candidate due to his age, perceived lack of a compelling platform, and the risk that disillusioned [[Bernie Sanders]] supporters would not turn out for him in the general election.

### AF 2020-03-04 0557
(01:02:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed key upcoming [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary contests, including [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[Washington D.C.|Washington]], [[State of Missouri|Missouri]], [[Idaho]], [[State of Mississippi|Mississippi]], and [[North Dakota]] the following week, and [[State of Florida|Florida]], [[State of Illinois|Illinois]], [[State of Ohio|Ohio]], and [[Arizona]] the week after.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a live broadcast to cover the results of the Super Tuesday [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary elections.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:01:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that [[Patrick Casey]], [[Vince James]], and [[Steven Crowder|Steve Franzen]] would be joining the broadcast later as panelists.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:04:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes recapped the results of the earlier [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primaries, noting [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]]'s technical victory in the [[Iowa]] caucuses, [[Bernie Sanders]]'s win in [[New Hampshire]], and [[Bernie Sanders|Sanders]]'s large victory with [[Hispanic]] voters in [[State of Nevada|Nevada]].

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:05:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Democratic Party]] establishment had orchestrated the consolidation of the moderate lane behind [[Joe Biden]] to prevent [[Bernie Sanders]] from winning a majority of delegates.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:07:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Elizabeth Warren]], despite her progressive platform, was acting as a spoiler candidate by staying in the race and siphoning votes from [[Bernie Sanders]] on Super Tuesday.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:10:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Joe Biden]] and [[Bernie Sanders]] were the only candidates besides [[Michael Bloomberg|Mike Bloomberg]] with the funds and national infrastructure to compete in all 50 states.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:10:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]] lacked overwhelming national support and a national infrastructure, making his early successes in [[Iowa]] and [[New Hampshire]] a fluke.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:12:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary as a battle between [[Joe Biden]] and [[Bernie Sanders]].

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:14:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested the most likely outcome was a contested convention where superdelegates would deny [[Bernie Sanders]] the nomination even if he had a plurality of delegates, potentially causing problems with his supporters' turnout in the general election.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:17:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes brought panelists [[Patrick Casey]] and [[Vince James]] into the broadcast conversation.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:22:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Bernie Sanders]]'s perceived broad coalition from his [[State of Nevada|Nevada]] victory was starting to fall apart in more traditional states, revealing a narrow base of support.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:24:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Joe Biden]]'s strong performance shattered the idea that voters are rational, given [[Joe Biden|Biden's]] poor debate performances and visible cognitive issues.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:25:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[black]] voters as a traditionally irrational and fixed voting bloc that votes [[Democratic Party|Democrat]] regardless of policy alignment.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:28:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes said he had not factored [[Michael Bloomberg|Mike Bloomberg]] into his analysis because Super Tuesday was the first [[time]] [[Michael Bloomberg|Bloomberg]] was on the ballot.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:28:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a poll from a couple of weeks prior that showed [[Michael Bloomberg|Mike Bloomberg]] in the lead in [[Kansas|Arkansas]].

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:29:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Michael Bloomberg|Mike Bloomberg]] had been flooding Super Tuesday states with advertisements for months, spending $400 million on commercials and $150 million on other expenditures.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:31:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes read a quote from [[Michael Bloomberg|Mike Bloomberg]]'s speech in [[State of Florida|Florida]], where [[Michael Bloomberg|Bloomberg]] claimed his campaign had "done something no one else thought was possible."

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:34:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes raised the question of whether [[Elizabeth Warren]] would drop out if she lost her home state of [[Massachusetts]], citing the precedent of [[Marco Rubio]] dropping out after losing [[State of Florida|Florida]] in the 2016 [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:36:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if [[Elizabeth Warren]] lost every contest in February and on Super Tuesday, including her home state, it would be [[time]] for her to drop out.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:37:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes asked [[Vince James]] if he thought [[Elizabeth Warren]] would stay in the race specifically to peel support from [[Bernie Sanders]].

### AF 2020-03-03 0556

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(00:40:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed a photo of the T-posing incident, noting a woman [[who]] appeared to be blocking [[Jared Holt]]'s path was later reported to be deliberately impeding him to help the Groyper's assemble.

(00:41:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes said he did not know [[who]] the woman was but speculated she might have been "one of [[us]]."

(00:44:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he intuitively understood "the femoid question," claiming some people are born simps and some are not, and referenced a "prominent simp" at the [[AFPAC]] hangout.

(00:44:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes addressed a question about Zoomer Clips, whose [[Twitter]] was deleted, saying the account had been posting content that was "getting [[us]] in trouble" and needed "to take a [[time]] out."

(00:45:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that commentator [[Hasan Piker|Vaush]] was also doing coverage of Super Tuesday and typically got about half of Fuentes's viewership.

(00:46:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes said he thought about voting in [[State of California|California]] but did not, and was registered as an independent.

(00:52:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes remarked that [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] candidates like [[Cenk Uygur]] seemed "more based" than [[Republican Party|Republicans]], [[who]] had been weeded out for saying anything controversial.

(00:54:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Joe Biden]] had now won five states and was leading in several more, making it a big night for him.

(00:56:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented that [[Trey Gowdy]] was "kind of obnoxious" and his "look is just bad."

(02:35:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed strong approval for [[Donald Trump]]'s recent policy actions, including the public charge rule, the "Remain in Mexico" policy, crackdowns on birth tourism, and deploying task forces to sanctuary cities.

(02:38:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Tulsi Gabbard]] was not a viable candidate, citing her lack of charisma, poor debate performances, low polling numbers, and standard [[Democratic Party|Democrat]] positions on most issues aside from foreign policy.

(02:39:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Democratic Party]] is no longer an anti-war party, citing [[Elizabeth Warren]]'s support for keeping troops in [[Afghanistan]] without electoral penalty as evidence.

(02:40:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that politicians like [[Tulsi Gabbard]] and [[Andrew Yang]] supported dissonant policies, such as advocating for a [[Universal Studios|Universal]] Basic Income while also supporting mass immigration of low-wage workers.

(02:40:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified that his opposition to the [[Iraq War]] was based on it not being in [[United States|America]]'s national interest, rather than a blanket opposition to all war, which he believes is sometimes necessary for self-defense or state interests.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(02:45:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the majority of the federal budget is mandatory entitlement spending like [[Social Security]] and [[Medicare]], with defense spending being a smaller, discretionary portion that is easier to control and necessary for maintaining superpower status.

(02:46:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out a double standard where anti-war activists criticize [[United States|America]]'s military engagements but praise or ignore similar actions by [[Russia]], [[China]], or [[Iran]].

(02:49:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Super Tuesday]] exit polls showing [[Bernie Sanders]] leading among non-white voters (45%) and young people in [[State of California|California]], attributing this to his strength with the growing [[Hispanic]] demographic.

(02:50:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Bernie Sanders]]'s socialist policies like free healthcare and college are financially unsustainable with mass immigration, as a growing population of welfare recipients would burden the system.

Fuentes contrasted the split in the moderate [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] lane ([[Joe Biden]], [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]], [[Amy Klobuchar]], [[Michael Bloomberg]]) with the narrower split in the progressive lane ([[Bernie Sanders]], [[Elizabeth Warren]]), arguing the former was larger.

(03:35:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Joe Biden]] had reached a 70% chance of winning [[State of Texas|Texas]] according to the [[The New York Times|New York Times]] forecast, leading by just over two points with most votes counted.

### AF 2020-03-03 0556
(03:38:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that [[Bernie Sanders]] had suffered a major defeat on [[Super Tuesday]], was unlikely to avoid a contested convention, and that the nomination would ultimately go to [[Joe Biden]], [[who]] would then lose to [[Donald Trump]] in the general election.

### AF 2020-03-02 0555
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosts the online political commentary program *[[United States|America]] First*.

### AF 2020-03-02 0555

### AF 2020-02-25 0554
(01:19:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed that the main thrust of the debate was about [[Bernie Sanders]] being on [[the verge]] of becoming unstoppable, with other candidates attacking him from different angles to weaken him before the [[State of South Carolina|South Carolina]] primary.

### AF 2020-02-25 0554
(01:19:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Elizabeth Warren]]'s pitch was that she was a more electable and pragmatic progressive than [[Bernie Sanders]], while [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]] labeled [[Bernie Sanders|Sanders]] a "chaos candidate" that [[Vladimir Putin]] and the establishment feared.

### AF 2020-02-25 0554
(01:20:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the debate's importance hinged on whether [[Joe Biden]]'s performance could propel him to do better than expected in the [[State of South Carolina|South Carolina]] primary, which was critical to stopping [[Bernie Sanders]]'s momentum.

### AF 2020-02-25 0554
(01:22:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that [[Bernie Sanders]] maintained a consistent debate performance by sticking to his core talking points about millionaires, healthcare, and education, which he found effective despite occasional weak answers on unexpected questions.

### AF 2020-02-25 0554
(01:55:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the debate's closing segment, where candidates were asked personal questions about misconceptions and mottos, as a poorly moderated, disorganized, and "cutesy" idea that never works with competitive candidates.

### AF 2020-02-25 0554
(01:56:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that attacks on [[Bernie Sanders]] for being a socialist or having expensive plans were ineffective because his supporters already accepted these aspects, and the attacks failed to understand that his alternative vision was why he had grassroots excitement.

### AF 2020-02-25 0554
(01:59:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the debate was entertaining but terribly executed, with bad questions and botched moderation, which he found "white-pilling" as it demonstrated the incompetence brought by demographic changes and affirmative action hires within the [[Democratic Party]] and media.

### AF 2020-02-25 0554
(02:07:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes mocked the debate's conclusion, stating that watching two women "fuck up a debate" was not encouraging for having a woman in charge of the [[country]], and that the personal question segment was a perfect, botched end to a poorly arranged event.(01:22:46) [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]] made a clever remark about the revolutions of the 1950s and 1960s during a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary debate.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(00:58:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that concentrated wealth in the hands of billionaires like [[Jeff Bezos]], [[Michael Bloomberg]], [[George Soros]], [[Sheldon Adelson]], and the [[Charles Koch|Koch brothers]] creates an unsustainable distribution of political power antithetical to the republic envisioned by the Founding Fathers.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:00:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that free-market ideology has become a moral imperative for many conservatives, [[who]] believe that allowing billionaires to amass and spend fortunes without restriction is the only moral way to conduct society, even if it produces negative outcomes.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:01:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[United States|American]] founding fathers created a system of checks and balances and federalism to distribute power, not a system where unlimited wealth could buy political influence.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:05:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified mass immigration as the number one threat to [[United States|American]] identity, stability, culture, and the economic well-being of the working and middle class.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:06:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the primary drivers for mass immigration are wealthy firm owners [[who]] benefit from a larger labor [[pool]] that suppresses wages and increases profits.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:07:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the economic surplus generated by immigration since 1965, estimated at $2.1 trillion, has accrued almost entirely to the owners of firms, while the costs have been borne by native workers competing for jobs.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:09:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that free trade policies, like immigration, benefit the owners of capital through "creative destruction" while displacing workers [[who]] are left without new employment opportunities.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:12:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[George Soros]] and the [[Charles Koch|Koch brothers]], despite being on opposite sides of the political spectrum, both advocate for open borders because their financial interests as investors and firm owners align.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:13:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that conservatives enable their political enemies by perpetuating a free-market system that allows billionaires to accumulate the wealth and influence needed to fund anti-conservative, globalist policies.

### AF 2020-02-17 0548
(01:16:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes called for a move beyond the capitalism-socialism dichotomy, advocating for an economic system that serves the nation, families, and the middle class rather than ideological dogma about free markets.

Fuentes announced the "[[AFPAC|America First Political Action Conference]]" ([[AFPAC]]) for February 28th, 2020, to be held during [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]], with [[Michelle Malkin]] as the headline keynote speaker and [[Scott Greer]] and [[Patrick Casey]] also appearing.

(00:35:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his show would continue on [[DLive]] and that he would announce a new, independent proprietary streaming home for *[[United States|America]] First* on Monday, February 17th, 2020.

### AF 2020-02-14 0547
(00:44:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that behind-the-scenes networks were forming in support of his movement, citing private support from figures like [[Michelle Malkin]] and [[Cassandra Fairbanks]] during the "Groyper Wars."

(01:00:07.540) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Barack Obama]] administration conducted deportations, maintaining a "bare minimum" semblance of immigration order compared to the proposed New Way Forward Act.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began a broadcast of his show "[[United States|America]] First" on a Wednesday evening.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:01:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the show's featured stories would be a status update on the coronavirus pandemic and an analysis of the results from the [[New Hampshire]] [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:02:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that Italian politician [[Matteo Salvini]] was going to trial in [[Italian Republic|Italy]] and that there was a development involving the [[United Nations]] and [[Israel]].

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:03:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes reflected that watching the 2016 [[Republican Party]] primary was exciting because it featured [[Donald Trump]], whereas the 2020 [[Democratic Party]] primary involved candidates like [[Bernie Sanders]], [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]], [[Amy Klobuchar]], [[Elizabeth Warren]], and [[Joe Biden]].

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:04:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he started his show after the 2017 presidential inauguration, a [[time]] he considered the worst for political interest following a major election.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:05:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced he would not be streaming on [[Google|YouTube]] for the next two weeks due to a second community guideline strike and demonetization, and was considering not returning to the platform.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:06:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes revealed he was working on a proprietary, long-term alternative streaming platform to ensure the show's future, with plans to launch it soon.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:08:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed concern that platforms like [[DLive]] could face pressure from media hit pieces by outlets like the [[Daily Beast]], [[Right Wing Watch]], or the [[The Huffington Post|Huffington Post]].

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:09:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a series of attacks on his movement, including a [[Daily Beast]] hit piece on [[Michelle Malkin]], a local paper hit piece on [[Jaden McNeil]] and [[America First]] students, a 68-[[page]] report by a human rights [[organization]], and criticism from [[Charlie Kirk]] and [[Ben Shapiro]].

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:09:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed individuals [[who]] had faced bans, including [[Patrick Howley]] on [[Twitter]] and [[Steve Franssen]].

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:10:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the [[America First]] movement was the most resilient and influential force in politics despite facing blacklisting, deplatforming, and a lack of resources, citing the impact of the "Groyper Wars."

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:12:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported the final results of the [[New Hampshire]] [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary: [[Bernie Sanders]] won with 25.7%, followed by [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]] with 24.4%, [[Amy Klobuchar]] with 19.8%, [[Elizabeth Warren]] with 9.2%, and [[Joe Biden]] with 8.4%.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:13:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that nearly 300,000 people voted in the [[New Hampshire]] [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary, a new record, though he noted that proportional turnout was lower than in 2008.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:14:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump]] received about 120,000 votes in the uncontested [[New Hampshire]] [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary, which he characterized as historic turnout for an incumbent president compared to [[Barack Obama]] in 2012, [[George W. Bush]] in 2004, and [[Bill Clinton]] in 1996.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:16:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Bernie Sanders]] was the front-runner for the [[Democratic Party]] nomination, despite media coverage focusing on [[Amy Klobuchar]]'s third-place finish and a narrative about a "moderate coalition" of [[Joe Biden]], [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]], and [[Amy Klobuchar]].

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:18:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Bernie Sanders]] represented an outsider threat to the [[Democratic Party]] establishment, similar to how [[Donald Trump]] was treated by the [[Republican Party]] establishment in 2016.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:19:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Bernie Sanders]] as a socialist [[who]] supported open borders, free healthcare for illegal immigrants, gun confiscation, and jailing white nationalists, and noted [[Bernie Sanders|Sanders]] had reversed his past populist stance that immigration hurt the working class.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:21:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]]'s strong performances in [[Iowa]] and [[New Hampshire]] were not indicative of national success because those states are whiter and more conservative relative to the broader [[Democratic Party]] electorate, and Buttigieg polled poorly in [[State of Nevada|Nevada]], [[State of South Carolina|South Carolina]], and [[Super Tuesday]] states.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:23:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Joe Biden]] and [[Elizabeth Warren]], [[who]] had serious national infrastructure, were fading, and if they dropped out, it would benefit [[Bernie Sanders]], as [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]]'s appeal was limited by his sexuality and lack of support among [[black]] and minority voters.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:23:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that betting markets had [[Bernie Sanders]] as the favorite for the nomination, followed by [[Michael Bloomberg]], then [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]].

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:24:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that a candidate needed 1,990 pledged delegates to win the [[Democratic Party]] nomination outright, and if no one reached that majority, the 771 superdelegates would vote at a contested convention, where a candidate would need 2,376 total delegates to win.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:27:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Michael Bloomberg]], despite not competing in [[Iowa]] or [[New Hampshire]], was polling well in [[Super Tuesday]] states like [[Kansas|Arkansas]] due to massive spending on advertisements.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:29:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes shifted the topic to the coronavirus pandemic, citing a [[CBS News|CBS]] report that [[China]]'s Hubei province reported 242 new deaths and 14,840 new cases, bringing the worldwide death toll to at least 1,357 and confirmed cases to over 60,000.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:31:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported the [[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention|CDC]] announced a new case in an [[United States|American]] evacuee from [[Wuhan]], bringing the total [[United States|U.S]]. cases to 14.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:31:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed skepticism about official coronavirus case numbers, citing faulty testing kits in the [[United States]] and the [[Chinese government]]'s control of data.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:33:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the apparent slowdown in new confirmed cases in [[China]] was due to a shortage of testing kits, not an actual decrease in the virus's spread.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:34:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the [[World Health Organization]] had renamed the virus COVID-19 to eliminate stigma, which he suggested could also obfuscate infection totals.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:36:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[Military Times]] report that the [[United States]] military, under an executive order approved by Defense Secretary [[Mark Esper]], was initiating pandemic plans in preparation for a potential global outbreak of COVID-19.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:37:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention|CDC]] report stating officials believed the coronavirus would likely take a foothold and spread within communities in the [[United States]].

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:39:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported a new finding that the incubation period for COVID-19 was at least 24 days, longer than the standard 14-day quarantine period, potentially allowing infected individuals to be released.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:41:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[World Health Organization]] report stating scientists had not found the animal source of COVID-19, noting the virus's genetic sequence matched bats but no bats were found at the [[Wuhan]] wet market originally cited as the origin.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:42:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that the [[Wuhan]] wet market was 20 miles from a sophisticated virus research laboratory authorized to handle high-level pathogens, implying a possible lab origin for the virus.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:45:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited an [[National Public Radio|NPR]] report quoting a [[World Health Organization]] chief scientist [[who]] expressed optimism that the spread of COVID-19 was slowing and could be contained, and that a vaccine could be available in 18 months.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:49:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Neil Ferguson]] of [[Imperial College London]], [[who]] estimated [[China]] was only detecting 5% of COVID-19 cases, suggesting the true number of infections could be around 1.2 million.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:50:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that the coronavirus pandemic could cause a major global economic recession by disrupting supply chains, citing a [[Michael Bloomberg|Bloomberg]] report that [[Germany]]'s economy might enter a recession due to the outbreak.

### AF 2020-02-12 0545
(00:52:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that reports of 24/7 operations at crematoriums in [[China]] could be used to estimate a higher true death toll from COVID-19 than official figures indicated.

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:00:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes began his show on a Thursday evening, announcing the main topic would be the coronavirus outbreak and a statement from the [[Elizabeth Warren]] campaign.

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:08:19) [[Elizabeth Warren]] stated that if elected president, she would punish online election disinformation with civil and criminal penalties, a policy Nicholas J. Fuentes argued would directly [[target]] the dissident right.

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:10:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Elizabeth Warren]]'s chances of winning the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] presidential nomination were fading as the Iowa caucus approached, with [[Joe Biden]], [[Bernie Sanders]], and [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]] leading the field.

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:23:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the control of technology and mass communications was the primary political issue, as it would determine the future ability of the dissident right to spread its message, contrasting the regulatory approaches of a potential second [[Donald Trump]] administration with a progressive one.

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:24:13) Section 230 of the [[New York|Communications Decency Act]] granted internet companies legal immunity as platforms, but Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that companies like [[Facebook]] and [[Twitter]] had become publishers by regulating content and should lose that immunity, a point also invoked by [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] like [[Nancy Pelosi]] and [[Elizabeth Warren]] for different regulatory ends.

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:32:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] administration would lead to the arrest and imprisonment of dissident right figures like [[Jared Taylor]], [[Peter Brimelow]], and himself, with little public outcry, effectively ending their movement.

(00:39:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes, in character as "Dr. Ari Silver," claimed to have been given an antidote for the coronavirus by the [[World Jewish Congress]].

### AF 2020-01-30 0536

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:51:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[World Health Organization]]'s statement, which claimed the emergency was due to cases outside [[China]] and advised against travel restrictions, was illogical since all international cases originated from [[China]] via travel.

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:56:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that pilots for [[American Airlines]] were suing to cancel flights with [[China]] due to fears of catching coronavirus, highlighting the risk of infection on long-haul flights.

### AF 2020-01-30 0536
(00:58:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes, in character as "Dr. Nick," joked about prescribing "a bullet to the head of everybody that is in on the take" [[who]] prioritized international trade over public health, before immediately disavowing the statement as hyperbolic and against his medical oath to do no harm.

### AF 2020-01-29 0535

(00:03:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the show would also discuss a resolution in the [[United States House of Representatives]] to repeal the 2002 Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF), which had been used to justify military actions in [[Iraq]], [[Syria]], [[Yemen]], and a strike in [[Iran]].

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
(00:04:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted a second resolution, specific to [[Iran]], would also be voted on, aimed at blocking funds for military action against [[Iran]] without congressional approval.

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
(00:19:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the [[United States House of Representatives]] was set to vote on repealing the 2002 AUMF and blocking funds for war with [[Iran]], efforts that gained momentum after the [[Donald Trump]] administration's strike killed [[Iran|Iranian]] General [[Qasem Soleimani]].

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
(00:20:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the [[White House]] issued a veto threat on the AUMF repeal but that [[Donald Trump]] later tweeted that [[Republican Party|Republicans]] should "vote their heart," creating confusion about the administration's position.

(00:21:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that [[White House National Security Advisor Jake Sullivan|White House National Security Advisor]] [[Robert O'Brien]] cited the 2002 AUMF as legal authority for the [[Qasem Soleimani|Soleimani]] strike, while [[Mark Esper|United States Secretary of Defense]] [[Mark Esper]] cited Article Two of the [[U.S. Constitution|United States Constitution]].

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
