(00:38:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes read a report from [[Washington Post|The Hill]] stating the [[Donald Trump]] administration was considering a [[China]] travel ban but had not reached a decision, with [[United Airlines]] suspending some flights while [[Delta]] and [[American Airlines]] had not.

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
(00:39:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[United States Secretary of Health and Human Services]] [[Alex Azar]] was speaking regularly with [[Donald Trump]] about the coronavirus and had not ruled out travel restrictions.

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
(00:40:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that other countries and airlines, including the [[United Kingdom]]'s [[British Airways]] and [[Hong Kong]]'s [[Cathay Pacific]], had already shut down flights from [[China]].

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
(00:41:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the lack of a coronavirus travel ban to the legal challenges faced by [[Donald Trump]]'s 2017 executive order restricting travel from seven [[Islam|Muslim]]-majority countries, including [[Syria]], [[Iran]], [[Somalia]], [[Iraq]], [[Afghanistan]], and [[Libya]].

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
(00:44:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Ebola]], [[H1N1]], [[SARS]], and [[MERS]] as the major epidemics of the past 30 years and stated the coronavirus was already on that level in terms of spread.

### AF 2020-01-29 0535
(00:48:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a prediction chart on [[Twitter]] showing coronavirus infections and deaths exceeding projections, with a potential end-of-February total of millions of infections and a million deaths.

### AF 2020-01-28 0534
(00:05:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes was banned from attending the [[Conservative Political Action Conference]] ([[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]]) in the previous year, along with [[Patrick Casey]] and other [[United States|America]] First conservatives [[who]] were disinvited or blacklisted.

### AF 2020-01-28 0534
(00:06:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the creation of the [[AFPAC|America First Political Action Conference]] ([[AFPAC]]), a counter-event to [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]] scheduled for February 28th in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]].

### AF 2020-01-28 0534
(00:07:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the successful execution of the Groyper Leadership Summit in December, which coincided with the [[Turning Point USA]] Student Action Summit, as evidence that his movement could organize secure and professional events.

### AF 2020-01-28 0534
(00:51:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the purpose of the one-sided peace plan was to be a non-starter for [[Palestinians]], thereby allowing [[Israel]] to claim it wanted peace while continuing to annex territory after [[Palestinian]] rejection.

### AF 2020-01-28 0534
(00:58:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the relationship between the [[United States]] and [[Israel]] as parasitic, arguing that [[United States|U.S]]. foreign policy had been hijacked by [[Jared Kushner]] and the [[Israel|Israeli]] government to benefit [[Israel]] at [[United States|America]]'s expense.

### AF 2020-01-28 0534
(01:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed several actions taken by the [[Donald Trump]] administration at [[Israel]]'s behest, including recognizing [[Israel|Israeli]] sovereignty over the [[Israel|Golan Heights]], designating [[Iran|Iran's]] [[Iran|IRGC]] as a terrorist group, solidifying aid, revoking [[Palestinian]] aid, and moving the [[United States Embassy in Jerusalem|U.S. embassy]] to [[Jerusalem]].

### AF 2020-01-28 0534
(01:03:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that figures like [[Sheldon Adelson]], [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi]], and [[Jared Kushner]] were "running the show" in [[United States|U.S]]. foreign policy regarding [[Israel]], which he considered a disgrace.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes opened his program "[[United States|America]] First" on a Monday evening in late January.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:01:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed a pattern of positive immigration developments for the [[Donald Trump]] administration, including the Migrant Protection Protocols, deals with Northern Triangle countries, and a "birth tourism" rule.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:02:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested [[Stephen Miller]] or [[Ken Cuccinelli]] might be the architect behind a series of recent immigration regulations from the [[Donald Trump]] administration.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:03:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the coincidence of [[International Holocaust Remembrance Day]] occurring on the same day as a [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] immigration victory.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:03:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes described [[International Holocaust Remembrance Day]] as a somber occasion marking 75 years since the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]].

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:04:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]] and the [[Southern Poverty Law Center|SPLC]] as organizations he characterized as "[[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] experts" [[who]] warn that jokes can spiral into systematic extermination.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:05:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he would treat [[International Holocaust Remembrance Day]] with utmost seriousness for commentators like [[Ben Shapiro]] and [[Jared Holt]].

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:05:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed a close personal connection with the [[Jews|Jewish people]].

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:06:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented on the death of [[Kobe Bryant]], [[who]] died in a helicopter crash on January 26th alongside his 13-year-old daughter and others.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:08:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized right-wing commentators [[who]] dismissed [[Kobe Bryant]]'s death by focusing on his celebrity status, past rape accusation, or by comparing it to the deaths of firefighters or troops.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:09:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that sports figures like [[Kobe Bryant]] are part of [[United States|America]]'s shared cultural experience and that being a master at one's craft deserves respect.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:13:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the inconsistency of some right-wing figures [[who]] defended [[Donald Trump]] and [[Brett Kavanaugh]] against sexual misconduct allegations but used a past accusation against [[Kobe Bryant]] to be glib about his death.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:18:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned a report that [[Kobe Bryant]] attended [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] mass on the morning of his death.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:20:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes heard that [[Kobe Bryant]] was [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] and was raised Italian.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:20:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes juxtaposed the death of [[Kobe Bryant]] with [[International Holocaust Remembrance Day]], stating "Kobe Bryant one day, six million [[Jews]] the next."

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:21:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated this was his third [[time]] observing [[International Holocaust Remembrance Day]] on his show.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:23:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled giving an interview to the [[Washington Post|The Washington Post]] during the "Groypers Wars" where he was asked to affirm details of the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] from a Wikipedia [[page]].

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:24:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned [[Elie Wiesel]] as a fellow [[Boston University]] alumnus.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:25:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[World War II]] was a terrible event that killed millions of [[Europe|Europeans]], including fighting-age men, which he argued contributed to [[Europe]]'s current state.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:25:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Germany|Germans]] were also put in concentration camps by the [[Allies]] after World War II, and that victims of Nazi camps included [[Russian]] civilians, [[Poles]], and Romani people.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:26:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a "cookie monster joke" he made the previous year on [[International Holocaust Remembrance Day]] that caused him significant trouble and led to him being labeled a [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] denier.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:26:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[International Holocaust Remembrance Day]] and the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] are used as a "political weapon" to silence dissent against globalization, demographic change, and policy regarding [[Israel]].

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:28:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that [[Donald Trump]] was criticized by [[Jews|Jewish]] groups like the [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]] and the [[Southern Poverty Law Center|SPLC]] for a [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] Remembrance Day statement that mentioned non-[[Jews|Jewish]] victims.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:29:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[The Jerusalem Post|Jerusalem Post]] article stating that [[Ilhan Omar]] and [[Jeremy Corbyn]] were "slammed by [[Jews]]" after posting statements in support of [[International Holocaust Remembrance Day]].

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:33:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he is not sympathetic to [[Ilhan Omar]], whom he called "anti-white" and "anti-[[United States|America]]," but argued the criticism of her remembrance statement exemplified a political "protection racket."

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:34:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the "Groypers Wars" as a grassroots movement of young "[[United States|America]] First nationalists" on college campuses [[who]] asked questions about immigration and foreign policy, which was attacked by media focusing on his [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] remarks.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:35:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Michelle Malkin]] was attacked as a friend of [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] deniers and anti-Semites for not disavowing him over one offhand remark.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:36:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that accusations of anti-Semitism or [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] denial are a "political weapon" used to destroy the careers of those not aligned with a "globalist agenda."

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:38:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes used [[Jack Posobiec]] as an example of someone whose past associations with figures like [[Richard Spencer]] and tweets using the number 1488 were overlooked because he is a "tireless defender of the [[Jews|Jewish people]]" and [[Israel]].

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:39:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Jack Posobiec]] receives retweets from [[Donald Trump]] and rubs shoulders with [[Donald Trump Jr.]].

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:40:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that during the "Groypers Wars," he was attacked for making an ironic remark about the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]], which was used to label the entire movement as anti-Semitic.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:42:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that both [[Ilhan Omar]] and himself, despite having little in common, are targeted because they threaten the status quo, with her on [[Israel]] and him on immigration and nationalism.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:44:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Stefan Molyneux]] unfollowed and disavowed him in 2019, saying he "will not stand by people making light of genocide."

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:53:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that immigrants [[who]] consume welfare create ethnic conflict in a multiracial democracy by fostering competition over public resources and a breakdown of national identity.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:55:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a statistic that 67% of immigrant households are on some form of welfare, and that 41% of [[black]] households have some form of public assistance.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(00:59:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that tax money taken from productive, generally white citizens is transferred via welfare to non-white groups and immigrants, calling it a wealth transfer scheme.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(01:04:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the "public charge" rule would limit low-skilled legal immigration because those working minimum wage jobs would need government supplementation.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(01:04:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] justices called for a review of the practice of nationwide injunctions by lower court judges, which they said causes chaos.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(01:05:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that nationwide injunctions from federal judges have slowed the [[Donald Trump]] administration's immigration agenda, including the travel ban, border wall funding, and DACA repeal.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(01:06:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if not for injunctions, the [[Donald Trump]] administration could have implemented the travel ban, shut down DACA, and funded the border wall much earlier in his term.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(01:07:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if the [[Supreme Court of the United States]] stopped frivolous nationwide injunctions, it would clear the way for the [[Donald Trump]] administration to fully implement its immigration agenda.

### AF 2020-01-27 0533
(01:08:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes reflected on whether his explanations of controversial topics like ethnic conflict and [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] remembrance would resonate with normal people or be seen as radical.

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(00:04:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the featured story for the episode would be the global spread of the coronavirus originating in [[China]].

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(00:53:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the coronavirus had spread to [[State of Japan|Japan]], [[Macau]], [[Hong Kong]], [[Thailand]], [[Vietnam]], [[Singapore]], the [[United States]], [[Saudi Arabia]], and [[France]].

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(01:41:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that race- and gender-based policies in both the public and private sectors of the [[United States]] and [[Europe]] were systematically designed to benefit non-whites and women at the expense of white men.

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(02:10:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that global initiatives on climate, refugee resettlement, and corporate diversity disproportionately targeted and imposed mandates on white-majority countries like those in [[Europe]] and the [[United States]], while exempting nations like [[China]], [[India]], and [[Saudi Arabia]].

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(02:24:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that if women were inherently superior business leaders as often portrayed in media, competitive companies would hire them voluntarily without needing mandates and penalties from institutions like [[Goldman Sachs]].

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(02:50:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that female competence at high levels of leadership, innovation, and power was a fictional archetype promoted in film and television with no basis in biological reality or historical precedent.

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(03:07:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the modern education system and workforce were being restructured to reward feminine traits like compliance, submission, and agreeableness over masculine virtues like competition and aggression, leading to the "empowerment of women" as a societal disaster.

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(03:24:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a report that a new 1,000-bed hospital built with prefabricated materials in [[Wuhan]] could be completed within six days using 35 diggers and 10 bulldozers to address a shortage of medical resources.

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(03:44:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed skepticism that the official [[China|Chinese]] figure of approximately 1,300 coronavirus cases warranted the extreme response of quarantining 40 million people, building emergency hospitals, and causing global panic, suggesting the true scale of the outbreak was being underreported.

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(04:05:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a documentary alleging a common practice in [[China]] where "sewer oil" was harvested from sewage, combined with animal fat, and sold to restaurants for use in cooking, estimating it was used in 10% of [[China|Chinese]] food.

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(04:16:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[China]]'s command economy and powerful state enabled it to undertake drastic measures like quarantining 40 million people and rapidly building a hospital, capabilities he considered inconceivable in the [[United States]].

### AF 2020-01-24 0532
(04:28:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised viewers to judge the severity of the coronavirus outbreak by the extreme containment actions taken by governments, such as quarantining 40 million people and halting outbound flights from major cities, rather than by official statements downplaying the risk.

### AF 2020-01-23 0531
(09:00:66) [[Cathy Zhu]], [[who]] was born in [[China]] and immigrated to the [[United States]] as a child, had previously offered to bring Nicholas J. Fuentes soup when he was sick.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the start of his show, focusing on the 2020 election and new polling data.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:00:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that a new poll from [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]] showed [[Bernie Sanders]] as the frontrunner in the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary race.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:01:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Joe Biden]] and [[Elizabeth Warren]] had been attacking [[Bernie Sanders]], and that [[Hillary Clinton]] had also criticized him from outside the race.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:01:43) [[Elizabeth Warren]] announced an exploratory committee for her presidential campaign in January 2019, which Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized as extremely early compared to the 2016 [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary timeline.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:03:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] candidates largely agreed on most issues except healthcare, citing climate, guns, foreign policy, and immigration as areas of consensus.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:03:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the Iowa caucus was scheduled for the following Monday, February 3rd, marking the true start of the primary season.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:04:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the theme of his show as [[Bernie Sanders]] becoming the frontrunner and the potential dangers of a [[Bernie Sanders|Sanders]] presidency or nomination.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:05:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the general election as a potential contest between [[Bernie Sanders]] and [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:10:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported reading news from [[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]], [[Fox]], [[The Daily Wire|Daily Wire]], and other sources to compile content for his show.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:10:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized a trend he observed in publications like [[FiveThirtyEight]] and a book, *The Bitcoin Manifesto*, of using female pronouns as generic impersonal pronouns, which he found obnoxious and indicative of a societal transformation.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:14:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated there were more candidates in the 2020 [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary before its first debate than in the entire 2016 [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary field.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:14:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that twenty candidates qualified for the first [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debates in June and July 2019.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:17:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the conflict between [[Elizabeth Warren]] and [[Bernie Sanders]] created a divide within the progressive wing of the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:18:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the major divide in the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary as between progressives/outsiders like [[Bernie Sanders]] and [[Elizabeth Warren]] and establishment moderates like [[Joe Biden]], [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]], and [[Amy Klobuchar]].

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:19:16) [[Elizabeth Warren]] received the endorsement of the [[The New York Times|New York Times]], which Nicholas J. Fuentes cited as evidence of her establishment credentials despite her progressive policies.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:22:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed a pattern where the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] frontrunner, whether [[Joe Biden]], [[Elizabeth Warren]], or now [[Bernie Sanders]], became the [[target]] of attacks from other candidates.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:23:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a new [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]] poll showing [[Bernie Sanders]] at 27% and [[Joe Biden]] at 24% nationally, marking the first [[time]] [[Joe Biden|Biden]] did not hold a solo lead in [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]]'s polling.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:26:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes drew a parallel to the 2016 [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary, where [[Donald Trump]]'s plurality support consolidated as the field narrowed, suggesting a similar dynamic could affect the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] race if candidates like [[Elizabeth Warren]] or [[Amy Klobuchar]] dropped out.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:28:54) [[Bernie Sanders]] suffered a heart attack during the campaign, and [[Joe Biden]] faced questions about his mental acuity, which Nicholas J. Fuentes cited as weaknesses for both leading candidates.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:29:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that 57% of [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] voters prioritized picking a nominee [[who]] could beat [[Donald Trump]], and [[Joe Biden]] led by a large margin on that electability question.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:31:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued it was a mistake to assume [[Bernie Sanders]] was not electable, suggesting [[Bernie Sanders|Sanders]] could present a compelling economic alternative to [[Donald Trump]] in a general election.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:32:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Bernie Sanders]] would surround himself with true believers in hardcore progressivism if elected, unlike [[Donald Trump]], and would implement radical policies including [[universal]] healthcare, [[universal]] education, and open borders.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:36:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes drew parallels between [[Donald Trump]]'s 2016 primary victory and [[Bernie Sanders]]'s 2020 campaign, suggesting [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] needed to believe [[Bernie Sanders|Sanders]] could be president to consolidate behind him.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:40:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that in a binary general election choice, a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] administration under someone like [[Bernie Sanders]] would use state power to [[target]] and dismantle right-wing movements far more aggressively than [[Donald Trump]] had.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:44:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that a [[Bernie Sanders]] administration would treat white identity politics like organized crime or terrorism, creating an office in the [[White House]] dedicated to fighting white supremacy and accelerating laws like red flag laws.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:45:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed the idea that electing a [[Democratic Party|Democrat]] to make things worse would ultimately benefit the right-wing movement, arguing persecution would simply intensify and cripple their ability to organize.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:46:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized elements of the alt-right, including [[Richard Spencer]], for promoting [[Bernie Sanders]] unironically based on a delusion that a left-wing working class could be won over, despite [[the left]]'s inseparable commitment to anti-white identity politics.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:47:04) [[Greg Conte]], an associate of [[Richard Spencer]], was shouted down and shoved by left-wing protesters at an anti-[[Iran]] war demonstration, an event Nicholas J. Fuentes cited as proof that left-wing populists are enemies of the right.

### AF 2020-01-22 0530
(00:49:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared the political left, including [[Bernie Sanders]], to be the enemy of his movement due to their anti-white ideology, regardless of their economic policies.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began streaming his show simultaneously on [[Google|YouTube]] and [[DLive]] for the second consecutive night.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:01:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the featured story for the night would be the outbreak of a novel coronavirus originating in [[China]] that had spread to the [[United States]] and other countries.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:02:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if conflict with [[Iran]], civil unrest in [[State of Virginia|Virginia]], or other anticipated crises did not materialize, a global pandemic from [[China]] could instead fuel content for his show.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:03:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the spread of diseases like tuberculosis, typhoid, and plague into the [[United States]] from [[Mexico]] was a direct argument for shutting down the border.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:04:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced an article from [[Axios]], a news publication he identified as a subsidiary of [[NBC News]], which analyzed the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration's performance on immigration.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:05:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a review cited by [[Ann Coulter]] which stated only two miles of new border barrier had been constructed since [[Donald Trump]] took office, with approximately 75 miles of replacement barrier built.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:07:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the impeachment trial of [[Donald Trump]] began that day but was receiving minimal public attention, with only about 30,000 concurrent viewers on [[C-SPAN]]'s live stream.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:09:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the impeachment trial's viewership to his own, stating he had drawn 10,000 live viewers on [[DLive]] for a reaction stream to a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debate the previous Tuesday.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:14:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that [[Jaden McNeil]], the former president of the disbanded [[Turning Point USA]] chapter at [[Kansas State University]], had founded the first "[[United States|America]] First Students" [[organization]] on that campus.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:15:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the [[Kansas State University]] [[Turning Point USA]] chapter disbanded due to disagreements over [[Charlie Kirk]]'s response to the Covington [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] incident, the "diaper protest," and the treatment of [[Michelle Malkin]] and himself.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:18:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued there was no existing campus infrastructure for nationalist students, unlike for libertarians, pro-life advocates, or Zionist groups, making [[America First Students]] a critical new [[organization]].

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:19:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the "Groyper Wars" proved that [[Charlie Kirk]] could encounter dozens of dissenting nationalist students on any campus in [[United States|America]], demonstrating a significant base for such views.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:20:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the failure to institutionalize the [[MAGA]] movement's changes within the [[Republican Party]] and government after [[Donald Trump]]'s 2016 election as a critical weakness that would allow figures like [[Nikki Haley]] and [[Mike Pence]] to revert to the status quo.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:22:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]]'s [[White House]] appointees were largely drawn from the campaigns of [[Marco Rubio]] and [[Ted Cruz]], the [[Republican Party|Republican National Committee]], and the [[George W. Bush]] administration, rather than from [[MAGA]] movement ranks.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:24:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that creating independent, institutionalized organizations like [[America First Students]] was the essential future for the nationalist movement, as infiltration of existing groups had failed and fringe organizations lacked effectiveness.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:27:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes began analyzing an [[Axios]] article detailing the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration's immigration enforcement actions, which he argued was biased toward creating complacency but nonetheless showed significant progress.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:29:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized the [[Axios]] article, which stated the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration had effectively built a "wall" through policy by returning asylum seekers to [[Mexico]] under the Migrant Protection Protocols and sending them to third countries like [[Guatemala]].

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:31:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited data from [[Syracuse University]]'s Transactional Records Access Clearinghouse showing only 117 people under the Migrant Protection Protocols had been granted asylum by an immigration judge since January of the previous year.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:32:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a [[Washington Post]] report that [[Donald Trump]] was preparing to divert an additional $7.2 billion in [[U.S. Department of Defense|Pentagon]] funding for border wall construction, five times what [[Congress]] had authorized.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:32:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes calculated that with approximately $17 billion total secured or planned for the border wall, the administration had essentially funded its estimated cost, though construction remained a failure.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:33:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Donald Trump]] for continuing to advocate bringing in more high-skilled workers from [[Asia]], despite the [[United States]] already admitting 1.1 million legal immigrants annually.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:34:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that all of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration's immigration enforcement policies were temporary and fragile, as a future president could overturn them, unlike a physical border wall which would be a permanent deterrent.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:35:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that even if border wall funding was secured and construction began in 2020, [[Donald Trump]]'s campaign rhetoric had shifted away from immigration to focus on the economy and [[black]] unemployment, which he viewed as an electoral mistake.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:38:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed concern that [[Donald Trump]] was losing support among white non-college-educated voters and that polling in battleground states like [[Arizona]] and [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] showed a close race with [[Joe Biden]].

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:40:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that reverting to a campaign message centered on GDP and minority unemployment, after having expanded the conservative conversation to include nationalism and identity, represented a catastrophic strategic error.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:41:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes conceded that despite challenges from courts, [[Congress]], bureaucrats, and the media, the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration had constructed a tenuous but working framework for border enforcement through deals with [[Mexico]], [[Guatemala]], [[Honduras]], and [[El Salvador]].

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:44:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes transitioned to discussing the coronavirus outbreak, citing a [[Fox News]] report that the virus had killed at least six people and sickened about 300 others since first reported in [[China]] at the end of 2019.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:45:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the first [[United States|U.S]]. case of the coronavirus was confirmed in [[Seattle]] after a man arrived from [[China]] before enhanced health screenings were announced at major [[United States|U.S]]. airports for passengers from [[Wuhan]].

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:46:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained the coronavirus was in the same family as [[SARS]] and [[MERS]], but that health officials did not yet know its mortality rate or precise transmission vectors.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:47:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed distrust for case numbers coming from the [[China|Chinese]] government, suggesting the true number of infected could be in the thousands rather than the hundreds reported.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:48:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that unsanitary cultural practices in countries like [[China]], [[African continent|Africa]], and [[Mexico]], combined with global travel, made a devastating pandemic inevitable and was a primary reason to oppose immigration.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:50:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that diseases like typhus, typhoid, and tuberculosis, which had been eradicated in the [[United States]], were re-emerging in cities like [[San Francisco Bay Area|San Francisco]] and [[Los Angeles]] due to unvaccinated immigrants living in dense conditions.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:51:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that the next [[United States|U.S]]. president should implement extreme travel restrictions, barring anyone with symptoms from entering the [[country]], to prevent a potential antibiotic-resistant pandemic.

(00:54:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted his concern over pandemics with the focus on climate change at the [[World Economic Forum]] in [[Switzerland]], stating a global biological threat was a more immediate danger.

### AF 2020-01-21 0529
(00:56:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes described himself as a germaphobe, detailing extensive hand-washing rituals, and argued that cultural practices in other countries, such as open defecation in [[India]], were major disease vectors threatening [[United States|America]].

(01:39:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented on a [[U.S. Constitution|Second Amendment]] rally in [[Richmond]], [[State of Virginia|Virginia]], noting that an estimated 25,000 armed protesters gathered peacefully without major incident, contrary to media predictions of violence.

(01:53:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Martin Luther King Jr.]] was a "disgusting pervert," "communist," and "rapist" [[who]] should not be celebrated with a federal holiday.

### AF 2019-12-10 0510
(01:06:20) In February 2018, producers [[Jason Wolfe]] and [[Pete Richie]] approached Nicholas J. Fuentes to profile him for a reboot of the [[Paramount Global|MTV]] series "True Life," pitching it as a show about young political activists in the age of [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2019-12-10 0510
(01:08:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed to participate in the [[Paramount Global|MTV]] project after producers promised to portray him in a positive light, offered creative control, and suggested he could review the final cut before it aired.

### AF 2019-12-10 0510
(01:10:25) Producers from [[Paramount Global|MTV]] filmed Nicholas J. Fuentes at his home for a week in February 2018, recording him doing his show, driving, attending an anti-gun protest in [[Chicago]], and meeting with a former [[Ku Klux Klan|KKK]] member named [[Shane Johnson]].

### AF 2019-12-10 0510
(01:11:47) After filming, [[Paramount Global|MTV]] postponed the "True Life" episode indefinitely and did not communicate with Nicholas J. Fuentes for approximately a year and a half before notifying him of a new air date for a repurposed documentary.

(00:48:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes assessed that while the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]]-controlled [[House]] would likely impeach [[Donald Trump]], the [[Republican Party|Republican]]-controlled [[Senate]] was highly unlikely to muster the two-thirds vote needed for conviction.

(01:09:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that demographic changes in states like [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]], [[State of Texas|Texas]], and [[Arizona]] would eventually give [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] [[who]] control media and academia total political control, allowing them to overturn any policy gains.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(00:11:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the 500th episode of his show, marking nearly three years on air, though he noted technical discrepancies in the numbering due to unnumbered early episodes and shows produced for [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]].

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(00:41:85) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his show began on his current channel in August 2017, immediately following the [[Charlottesville rally]].

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(00:47:89) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the first major wave of social media purges occurring in December 2017, following the [[Charlottesville rally]], which included significant terms of service changes on [[Twitter]].

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(01:03:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes reflected that the story of his show and his personal evolution serves as a cultural artifact of Generation Z and the dissident right-wing movement in [[United States|America]] in the aftermath of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] election.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(01:59:93) Nicholas J. Fuentes outlined five distinct eras in the history of his show: a proto-era from November 2015 to April 2016; a period on [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] from February to August 2017; a partnership phase from August 2017 to January 2018; an independent phase from January 2018 to February 2019; and a contemporary "rise" phase from February 2019 to the present.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(02:29:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the first era of his show, from November 2015 to April 2016, as "The Nicholas J. Fuentes Show," which he hosted while a 17-year-old student at his high school's television station.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(02:38:67) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his high school show experimented with various formats, including panel discussions, debates, and an interview with libertarian presidential candidate [[Austin Petersen]].

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(02:56:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized his early ideological stance during his high school show as libertarian, citing his endorsement of [[Ron Paul|Rand Paul]] and [[Ted Cruz]] and his initial skepticism of [[Donald Trump]] as a serious candidate.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:07:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the second era of his show, from February to August 2017, as the [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] period, where the program was first titled "[[United States|America]] First."

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:19:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted being recruited for [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] by [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]] after a public debate at [[Boston University]], where he was an outspoken [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporter.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:20:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]] first asked him if he would like to take a trip to [[Israel]] during an interview, an offer he declined.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:22:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]] connected him with [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]], a media group [[known]] for covering [[Donald Trump|Trump]] rallies, which was seeking original programming.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:22:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his show "[[United States|America]] First" debuted on [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] on February 5, 2017, after network heads [[Jacob Seals]] and [[Jacob Seals|Joe Seals]] suggested the name.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:23:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that his [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] show was initially canceled in May 2017 due to low viewership but was brought back by popular demand on May 29, 2017, running until August 2017.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:24:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was released from [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] following the [[Charlottesville rally]], though he was not present for the preceding "Tiki torch" march.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:25:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] period as a transition from a libertarian perspective to a more hardened nationalist worldview, influenced by debates with peers at [[Boston University]].

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:27:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the third era of his show, from August 2017 to January 2018, as a partnership with [[James Allsup]] under the company [[America First Media]], formed in the aftermath of [[Charlottesville]] to create a paleoconservative movement distinct from the alt-right.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:28:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that his partner [[James Allsup]]'s [[Google|YouTube]] channel, which had approximately 500,000 subscribers, was demonetized in the summer of 2019 and completely shut down in September 2019 despite having no strikes.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:31:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[America First Media]] partnership dissolved in January 2018 over irreconcilable differences, including conflicts dubbed the "Optics War" and "Thot Wars," which led him to define an unapologetically paleoconservative direction for the show.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:35:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the independent era from January 2018 to February 2019 as a period of development where he integrated lessons from previous phases into a comprehensive worldview and refined his presentation on taboo subjects.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(03:37:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the fifth and current era of his show, beginning in February 2019, as "the rise of [[United States|America]] First," marked by events like [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]], the Trainwrecks TV debate, a [[Miami]] meetup, college speaking engagements, and the Groyper Wars, leading to mainstream notoriety.

### AF 2019-11-15 0500
(04:08:93) Nicholas J. Fuentes thanked long-[[time]] viewers, including those from his [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] days and his earlier high school show, "The Nicholas J. Fuentes Show."

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began his broadcast by announcing a decisive victory in the "Groyper War" at the [[University of Florida]] earlier that evening.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:00:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his live stream on [[DLive]] covering [[Charlie Kirk]]'s [[Turning Point USA]] event at the [[University of Florida]] had approximately 19,000 concurrent viewers.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:00:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that sources on the ground at the [[University of Florida]] event indicated that supporters of his movement constituted easily more than half of the audience.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:02:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the ongoing "Groyper War" as an "[[United States|America]] First Revolt against the conservative establishment" that was escalating in its fourth week.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:02:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that the [[University of Florida]] event was the second-to-last stop on [[Charlie Kirk]]'s "Culture War" tour for [[Turning Point USA]].

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:05:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the final event of [[Charlie Kirk]]'s tour would be at [[North Carolina State University]] the following day, with [[Lara Trump]] in attendance.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:06:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised his supporters attending the [[North Carolina State University]] event to focus their questions on immigration and social conservatism, and to ask [[Charlie Kirk]] about his past tweets criticizing [[Donald Trump]] and his support for [[Nikki Haley]].

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:08:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes outlined a strategy to drive a wedge between [[Donald Trump]] and [[Charlie Kirk]] by exposing that [[Charlie Kirk]]'s positions were fundamentally opposed to those [[Donald Trump]] supports.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:09:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes instructed his supporters that if [[Charlie Kirk]] canceled the Q&A session at [[North Carolina State University]], they should make a scene to expose his hypocrisy on free speech, similar to the protest at [[University of California, Los Angeles|UCLA]].

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:10:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes revealed that the [[University of Florida]] event was not officially streamed by [[Turning Point USA]], and the only live stream was provided by an [[United States|America]] First supporter named Simon.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:11:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes described his movement as a completely grassroots, organic insurgency composed of college students and young people with no super PAC or 501(c)(3) backing.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:12:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited individuals like K Alexander, Patrick Bateman, [[Patrick Casey]], and Simon as being critical to the movement's success for asking pivotal questions and providing live streams of events.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:15:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Charlie Kirk]] and [[Turning Point USA]] could no longer dictate the conversation or ignore his movement, as they were now a force that had to be respected.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:16:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the event at the [[University of Florida]] featured [[Charlie Kirk]] and [[Graham Allen]].

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:18:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Charlie Kirk]] of dishonesty for claiming at the outset of the [[University of Florida]] event that there would be no official stream due to "AV issues," which Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted was a lie to avoid being recorded.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:20:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Turning Point USA]] canceled its own stream of the [[University of Florida]] event because they anticipated being booed and asked tough questions they could not answer.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:22:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Charlie Kirk]] wanted the tour to end as quickly and quietly as possible following a catastrophic month, so he could return to [[Washington D.C.|Washington]], D.C. and avoid facing questions from real students.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:24:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that sources on the ground confirmed at least a hundred of his supporters were in attendance at the [[University of Florida]], constituting more than 50% of the room, which he characterized as an authentic [[United States|America]] First insurgency.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:26:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Charlie Kirk]], during his monologue at the [[University of Florida]], admitted he was wrong to have previously supported the EB-5 visa program, a concession Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed was driven entirely by pressure from his movement.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:28:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the initial question about EB-5 visas that provoked [[Charlie Kirk]]'s original support came from a supporter at an event in [[State of Colorado|Colorado]] four weeks prior.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:28:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Charlie Kirk]]'s position on EB-5 visas was criticized by [[Michelle Malkin]] and [[Tucker Carlson]].

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:30:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Charlie Kirk]]'s concession on EB-5 visas tacitly admitted that the [[United States|America]] First movement was right on the issue, granting the movement legitimacy.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:31:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that [[Charlie Kirk]] still supported the RAISE Act and merit-based mass legal immigration, which Nicholas J. Fuentes opposed.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:33:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Charlie Kirk]] of continuing to smear his movement, citing an instance where [[Charlie Kirk]] said people [[who]] oppose affirmative action are using the issue to promote white identity.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:35:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the Q&A at the [[University of Florida]] was the first [[time]] [[Charlie Kirk]] had to field questions since the [[Ohio State University]] event two weeks prior.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:36:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed significant events that had occurred since the [[Ohio State University]] Q&A, including events with [[Dan Crenshaw]], a speech by [[Ben Shapiro]], and the [[University of California, Los Angeles|UCLA]] event.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:36:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Charlie Kirk]] announced a "two-line" system for the Q&A at the [[University of Florida]], with one line for leftists and another for the "far right or alt-right," a tactic that was booed by the audience.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:37:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that despite the two-line tactic, his supporters asked nearly every question at the [[University of Florida]] Q&A, with some wearing disguises like [[Bernie Sanders]] shirts.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:38:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the first question at the [[University of Florida]] Q&A, from a man claiming to be an engineer, [[who]] challenged [[Charlie Kirk]] on how mass immigration policies would displace [[United States|American]] STEM workers.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:40:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that [[Charlie Kirk]] responded to the engineer's question by asking if he would prefer merit-based or family-based immigration, and when the questioner said "merit-based," [[Charlie Kirk]] claimed victory, which was met with boos from the crowd.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:41:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes hailed the third question at the [[University of Florida]] Q&A as arguably the best of the entire "Groyper War," asked by a man wearing a yarmulke and a [[Bernie Sanders]] shirt.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:41:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the questioner in the yarmulke thanked [[Charlie Kirk]] for his support for [[Israel]] before accusing [[Turning Point USA]] of smearing [[Christianity|Christians]] and [[Catholic Church|Catholics]] as alt-right and asking why anyone should trust [[Charlie Kirk]] over Nicholas J. Fuentes.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:43:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the question mentioning his name triggered a sustained "[[United States|America]] First" chant from the audience at the [[University of Florida]].

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:45:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that a female questioner at the [[University of Florida]] accused event staff of moving her four spots ahead in the Q&A line because she was a woman and didn't fit the profile of an [[United States|America]] First supporter, a charge [[Charlie Kirk]] did not answer.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:46:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized that the Q&A at the [[University of Florida]] covered topics including the ideological consequences of mass immigration, LGBT issues, donor influence, affirmative action, and the firing of [[Ashley St Claire|Ashley St. Clair]] for taking a picture with him.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:47:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that [[Charlie Kirk]]'s primary tactic during the Q&A was to repeatedly ask questioners if they opposed [[Donald Trump]]'s immigration policy in an attempt to get them on record disagreeing with [[the president]].

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:50:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Charlie Kirk]] trying to shackle his globalist immigration agenda to [[Donald Trump]] only hurt [[Donald Trump]] by associating him with unpopular policies.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:51:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Charlie Kirk]] explicitly stated he did not believe "demography is [[destiny]]" and called out "white identitarianism" during the [[University of Florida]] Q&A.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:52:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Charlie Kirk]] for opposing white identity while supporting [[black]] and [[Jews|Jewish]] identity events, calling it a rich inconsistency.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:54:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that getting [[Charlie Kirk]] to state that demography is not [[destiny]] was a huge win, as it placed him on the losing side of a factual argument.

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:55:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared the [[University of Florida]] event a total and complete victory, marking the movement as undefeated in the "Groyper War."

### AF 2019-11-12 0497
(00:58:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that the next major phase of the movement would be at the [[Turning Point USA]] Student Action Summit (SAS) in [[State of Florida|Florida]] on December 19th.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(00:07:77) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosts the online show *[[United States|America]] First*.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(01:01:81) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the "Groyper War" began in late October 2019 when two [[America First]] viewers questioned [[Charlie Kirk]] about the USS Liberty incident and mass legal immigration at a campus event.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(01:11:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes planned for approximately 80 supporters to attend a [[Turning Point USA]] book talk at [[University of California, Los Angeles|UCLA]] featuring [[Donald Trump Jr.]] and [[Kimberly Guilfoyle]].

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(01:20:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that his followers' strategy at campus events was to attend respectfully, listen to presentations, and then ask policy questions during the Q&A sessions.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(01:35:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the media inaccurately portrayed the [[University of California, Los Angeles|UCLA]] event disruption as an attempt to shut down [[Donald Trump Jr.]], rather than a protest against figures like [[Charlie Kirk]].

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(02:14:99) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that commentators like [[Matt Walsh]] incorrectly accused his movement of helping the political left or embarrassing [[Donald Trump]]'s family.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(02:38:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the intent of the protests was to [[target]] "grifters and subversives" like [[Charlie Kirk]] whom he believed were harming the [[Donald Trump]] administration's [[United States|America]] First agenda.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(03:24:83) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the "Groyper War" as a campaign to expose hypocrisy on free speech and the nature of modern conservatism promoted by [[Charlie Kirk]].

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(05:31:77) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the upcoming 500th episode of his show, *[[United States|America]] First*, which began on [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] in February 2017 before moving to an independent channel in August 2017.

(07:31:09) On Veterans Day, Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed thanks to [[United States|American]] military veterans, including [[Dan Crenshaw]], despite political disagreements.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(08:45:67) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Charlie Kirk]] could have avoided the "Groyper War" controversy by simply answering questions from his supporters at campus events.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(09:56:61) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Turning Point USA]] used tactics like planting questioners and profiling attendees wearing MAGA hats or rosaries to prevent [[America First]] supporters from asking questions.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(11:58:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes read from a *Guardian* article that described the [[University of California, Los Angeles|UCLA]] event, which he stated gave a "[[fair]] summary" of the protest.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496
(13:51:61) Nicholas J. Fuentes disputed [[Turning Point USA]]'s claim that the [[University of California, Los Angeles|UCLA]] event was never scheduled to have a Q&A, noting the event ended 90 minutes early.

### AF 2019-11-11 0496

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:04:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that the show would also discuss the film *Joker*, which was on track to become the highest-grossing R-rated film of all [[time]] domestically.

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:16:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes attended a fifth viewing of *Joker* where he observed a group of disruptive female moviegoers using phones and playing videos during the film before leaving early.

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:19:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the media backlash against *Joker* stemmed from its premise of a victimized straight white male protagonist, which he believed challenged prevailing liberal narratives in [[Los Angeles|Hollywood]].

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:20:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that the commercial success of *Joker* and films like *Once Upon a [[Time]] in [[Los Angeles|Hollywood]]* disproved the notion that audiences demanded the liberal social messaging prevalent in other major studio releases.

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:23:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the overrepresentation of interracial couples in advertising and media was a prescriptive effort to manipulate social norms, not a reflection of demographic reality.

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:25:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the controversy and success of *Joker* exposed media fabrication and demonstrated a public appetite for films without overt political programming.

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:36:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the Kurdish forces in [[Syria]] as communists, terrorists, and affiliates of [[Antifa]] whose actions led to the slaughter of [[Christianity|Christians]].

(00:40:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States]] military presence in the [[Middle East]], including [[Syria]], [[Iraq]], [[Afghanistan]], [[Yemen]], [[Nigeria]], [[Burkina Faso]], [[Niger]], and [[Libya]], was perpetual and unaffected by electoral politics.

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:42:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Israel|Israeli]] media heavily criticized [[Donald Trump]] for the [[Syria]] withdrawal, with one member of the [[Israel|Knesset]] comparing the abandonment of the [[Kurdistan|Kurds]] to the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]].

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:43:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the primary reason for [[United States]] foreign policy in [[Syria]] and [[Iraq]] was the influence of the [[Israel lobby]], a fact [[known]] but unspoken in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] due to fear of reprisal.

### AF 2019-10-21 0481
(00:46:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s presidency had been co-opted by the establishment, resulting in only marginal changes to a foreign policy still directed by entrenched interests.

(00:47:47) [[Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan]] was reported to be seeking nuclear weapons, a development Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested could be used to justify renewed [[United States]] military intervention.

(00:54:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his public arguments about demographic change were making the topic mainstream.

(00:19:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States|U.S]]. tax system is designed to reward wealthy individuals through mechanisms like low capital gains taxes while punishing working people with higher payroll and sales taxes relative to their income.

(00:25:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[United States|U.S]]. socioeconomic conflict as a class war where the middle class pays the majority of taxes and receives deteriorating public services and consumer products, while both the wealthy and the poor pay little to nothing.

(00:31:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Turkey]]'s justification for its military operation in [[Syria]]—to prevent a terrorist corridor along its border—mirrored the justification used by [[Israel]] for its airstrikes in [[Syria]] and [[Iraq]].

(00:33:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the widespread condemnation of [[Turkey]]'s operation by the [[European Union]], the [[United States]], and [[Congress]] was primarily because it complicated [[United States|American]] leverage and the interests of the military-industrial complex and the [[Israel lobby]] in the [[region]].

### AF 2019-10-09 0474
(00:38:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Bashar al-Assad]] had consolidated control over most of [[Syria]] following the [[civil war]], with only a few rebel and [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] enclaves remaining.

(00:43:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that while [[Donald Trump]] announced pulling "50 soldiers" from the [[Syria|Syrian]] border, the [[United States|U.S]]. still maintained approximately 2,000 troops in northeastern [[Syria]], with additional large deployments in [[Afghanistan]], [[Iraq]], [[Saudi Arabia]], and [[Yemen]].

(00:45:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that [[Donald Trump]]'s timing for the troop withdrawal was precarious due to the ongoing impeachment proceedings, as upsetting the military-industrial complex could risk [[Republican Party|Republican]] support needed to avoid removal from office.

(00:48:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that [[Donald Trump]] might be using [[Turkey]]'s intervention in [[Syria]] to complicate the [[United States|U.S]]. occupation and force a de facto withdrawal, similar to how he used tariffs to pressure [[Mexico]] on immigration, bypassing Congressional and institutional resistance.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:03:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes saw the film "Joker" on its opening night with a friend, describing the experience as a significant cultural event for his community.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:08:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the film "Joker" provided representation and a sympathetic character study for alienated individuals, which he compared to the cultural impact of "[[Black]] Panther" for [[black]] audiences.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:22:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the government and media were attempting to sabotage the release of "Joker" to foster fear of the "lonely white male."

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:25:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the film "Joker" earned $13.3 million in Thursday previews, setting a new October record, and was projected to earn $92 million over its opening weekend.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:33:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that many critical reviews of "Joker" focused on its perceived political and social commentary rather than its artistic merits.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:36:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the [[NYPD]] deployed undercover officers to screenings of "Joker" due to fears of a mass shooting.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:38:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes connected the media's fear of "Joker" to online communities that meme the character, suggesting the establishment fears these alienated groups could be inspired to violence.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:45:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the intense fear surrounding "Joker" was a tacit admission by the media and government that social conditions for alienated white men are as bad as the film portrays.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(01:54:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that the hype around potential violence at "Joker" screenings could be an astroturfed narrative to create a pretext for future policy agendas like censorship or gun control.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(02:04:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended seeing "Joker" alone or with male friends, criticizing those [[who]] brought girlfriends or young children to the R-rated film.

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(02:52:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]], as the chief executive, was within his constitutional authority to investigate potential crimes by political figures like [[Joe Biden]] and [[Hunter Biden]].

### AF 2019-10-03 0472
(03:00:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the impeachment effort as a "coup attempt" and political theater meant to distract the public.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the return of his regular show, marking the first official episode back after a break and a previous show focused on e-drama.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:01:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the featured story for the episode was the two-year anniversary of the [[Las Vegas]] shooting, an event for which the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] closed its case in August 2018 without determining a motive.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:03:42) [[Donald Trump]] held his first summit with [[North Korea]] in [[Singapore]] in June 2018, initiating negotiations that had, according to Nicholas J. Fuentes, yielded no progress on denuclearization two years later.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:04:09) [[Bernie Sanders]] underwent a heart procedure for a blocked artery in [[Las Vegas]], forcing him to cancel all campaign events, a development Nicholas J. Fuentes argued confirmed the 78-year-old senator was unfit for the presidency.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:05:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the health of [[Donald Trump]], whom he described as generally high-energy, with that of [[Bernie Sanders]] and [[Joe Biden]], whom he characterized as confused and slurring his words during debates.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:20:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Joe Biden]], [[Bernie Sanders]], and [[Elizabeth Warren]] as the three leading candidates in the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary, all of whom were over the age of 70.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:24:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Elizabeth Warren]] stood to gain the most from [[Bernie Sanders]]'s health issues, as she would inherit his progressive voter base and consolidate support against the moderate frontrunner, [[Joe Biden]].

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:28:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the 2016 [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary as an example where [[Donald Trump]]'s character and fortitude, not just policy positions or fundraising, were decisive in his victory over candidates like [[Marco Rubio]] and [[Ted Cruz]].

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:29:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[North Korea]]'s missile test as a provocative act aimed at pressuring the [[United States]] to make concessions on sanctions relief ahead of scheduled nuclear negotiations.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:32:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the missile diplomacy between [[North Korea]] and the [[United States]] in 2019 was identical to the pattern of provocation and response seen in 2017.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:33:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that [[North Korea]]'s strategy was to advance its nuclear triad to create a credible deterrent, making [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. military]] intervention unacceptable, thereby forcing [[Washington D.C.|Washington]] to negotiate sanctions relief.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:36:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the Samson Option as [[Israel]]'s foreign policy doctrine of using its nuclear arsenal to destroy the world if the state faced imminent defeat by Arab forces.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:37:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[North Korea]] sought a reciprocal denuclearization process, where phased sanctions relief would be exchanged for phased disarmament, a framework he believed Pyongyang could exploit to secretly retain nuclear capabilities.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:39:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized the [[United States|U.S]]. negotiating position with [[North Korea]] as demanding "complete, verifiable, and irreversible denuclearization" before providing any sanctions relief, a stance that had seen no success after a year and a half of talks.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:40:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[Donald Trump|Trump administration]]'s [[North Korea]] policy as a middle path of maintaining maximum pressure through sanctions without accepting a nuclear-armed [[North Korea]] or pursuing forcible disarmament.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:42:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[North Korea]]'s provocative missile tests signaled desperation for sanctions relief rather than strength, indicating the [[United States|U.S]]. pressure campaign was working.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:48:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused the [[United States|U.S. government]], media, and [[big tech]] companies of colluding to suppress questions and investigation into events like the [[Las Vegas]] shooting and the [[Jeffrey Epstein]] case.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:49:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited Operation Northwoods, a declassified 1962 plan, as evidence that the [[United States|U.S. government]] had previously planned false flag terror attacks on [[United States|American]] citizens to create a pretext for war with [[Cuba]].

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:50:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the rapid attribution of motives in shootings like El Paso and New Zealand with the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]]'s failure to determine a motive for the [[Las Vegas]] shooting after a year-long investigation.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:52:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Mark Zuckerberg]], [[Google]], and [[Twitter|Jack Dorsey]] of cooperating with government and media interests to ban users [[who]] asked questions about events like the [[Las Vegas]] shooting, [[Sandy Hook]], and [[Jeffrey Epstein]].

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:54:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the real societal division was not between left and right but between a transnational globalist elite and the general populace, with the elite using media to create an illusion of [[democratic]] choice.

### AF 2019-10-02 0471
(00:59:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the ruling elite was hostile to the interests of the [[United States|American]] and [[Europe|European]] people, making the fundamental question of politics [[who]] constitutes that elite and whether they serve the people.

(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] primary was only competitive between [[Joe Biden]], [[Bernie Sanders]], and [[Elizabeth Warren]], with [[Kamala Harris]] and [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]] as potential but less serious contenders.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the previous [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debates had not significantly altered the polling landscape, citing [[Kamala Harris]]'s temporary gain after attacking [[Joe Biden]] in the first debate as an example of a fleeting impact.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debate stage reflected a party increasingly focused on racial politics, with candidates asked explicitly about combating white supremacy and systemic racism.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]] promoted a "Douglas Plan," a policy initiative framed as a Marshall Plan for [[black]] communities, which Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized as a form of reparations.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Democratic Party]]'s explicit racial framing of issues like white supremacy as a national threat demonstrated a shift toward tribal, race-based politics that the [[Republican Party]] had yet to match.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Andrew Yang]] had lost his outsider appeal by assimilating into standard [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] talking points on issues like public schools and gun control, squandering his initial innovative energy.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Joe Biden]]'s debate performance, marked by frequent gaffes, forgetfulness, and a generally unwell appearance, cast doubt on his physical and mental longevity for the remainder of the campaign.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes assessed [[Elizabeth Warren]] as a strong, steady, and likable performer whose consistent debate showings were slowly raising her poll numbers, positioning her as the only candidate likely to surpass [[Joe Biden]].

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Bernie Sanders]] appeared unlikable, bitter, and politically stale in the debates, with his support steadily declining as [[Elizabeth Warren]]'s rose, and his message sounding outdated to a "woke" [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] base.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that the debate would not change the polling standings, with long-term trends showing [[Elizabeth Warren]] rising, [[Bernie Sanders]] stagnating, and other candidates remaining non-competitive.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that modern political debates are won through memorable, attention-grabbing moments and soundbites, not substantive policy discussion, a lesson he credited [[Donald Trump]] with mastering in 2016.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes posited that a future candidate [[who]] combined [[Donald Trump]]'s understanding of the media attention economy with greater competence and fewer character flaws could achieve a political realignment and wield immense, potentially dictatorial power.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Despite demographic trends favoring [[Democratic Party|Democrats]], Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that [[Donald Trump]]'s unique charisma and campaign prowess made him likely to defeat any candidate on the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debate stage in 2020.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that watching the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debate made him feel better about supporting [[Donald Trump]], despite his administration's shortcomings, because the alternative was a party advocating for reparations and an office of white supremacy.

### AF 2019-09-12 0460
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned that [[Rudy Giuliani]] tweeted a pro-police, anti-[[Antifa]] animated video that depicted an officer preparing to violently confront protesters, which Nicholas J. Fuentes found surprisingly "based."

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes opened his [[September 11 attacks|September 11th]] episode by acknowledging the 18th anniversary of the attacks, framing it as both a somber occasion and a day with significant current events to discuss.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(00:39:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the featured story for the episode would be a proposed ban on flavored e-cigarettes by the [[Food and Drug Administration|FDA]], which he framed as a strategic move to avoid algorithmic punishment on [[Google|YouTube]].

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(00:46:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes outlined additional topics for the show, including [[Republican Party|Republican]] victories in two [[North Carolina]] special elections and a [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] decision on asylum policy.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(00:52:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] had submitted a strong suggestion to the [[Food and Drug Administration|FDA]] to ban all non-tobacco-flavored e-cigarettes, citing an epidemic of youth usage and a string of associated deaths.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(01:04:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his personal position was in favor of banning all e-cigarettes and similar substances, arguing they should be removed from society.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(01:11:63) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that some analysts believed the proposed e-cigarette ban was intended to benefit major companies like [[Juul]], which could bypass regulations with money and lobbyists.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(01:57:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Dan Bishop]], a [[Republican Party|Republican]] state senator, had won a fiercely contested special [[United States|U.S]]. [[House]] election in [[North Carolina]]'s 9th district, a race seen as a bellwether for [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s 2020 chances.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(02:04:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that another [[Republican Party|Republican]] candidate, [[Greg Murphy]], had decisively won a separate special election in [[North Carolina]]'s more solidly [[Republican Party|GOP]]-leaning 3rd district.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(02:08:99) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Cheri Bustos]], the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] Congressional Campaign Committee chairwoman, [[who]] acknowledged [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s last-minute rally contributed to [[Dan Bishop]]'s victory in the 9th district.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(02:30:69) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed that while the [[Republican Party|Republican]] wins in [[North Carolina]] were necessary, the narrow margin in the 9th district and a leftward shift in the suburbs revealed troubling demographic trends for the [[Republican Party]].

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(02:33:95) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the [[Republican Party]]'s national platform would soon become electorally insolvent due to changing demographics driven by immigration and generational trends.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(03:08:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] had allowed a sweeping new rule on asylum seekers to go into effect while it deliberated on the rule's constitutionality.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(03:13:79) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained the new asylum policy would prevent people [[who]] travel to the [[United States]] through [[Mexico]] from [[Israel|Central America]] from seeking asylum unless they first applied for protection in a [[country]] they passed through.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(03:15:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the policy would effectively shut down asylum cases from people coming through [[Mexico]] from the Northern Triangle countries of [[Honduras]], [[Guatemala]], and [[El Salvador]].

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(03:26:73) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a report that illegal border crossings through [[Mexico]] had been reduced by 56% following policy changes after a tariff threat from the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration in June.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(03:54:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that arrests of illegal immigrants at the border had fallen from 130,000 in May to 64,000 in August.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(03:59:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] had allowed [[Donald Trump|Trump]] to allocate money from the [[U.S. Department of Defense|Department of Defense]] for border wall construction following a declaration of a national emergency.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(04:00:95) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed cautious optimism on immigration for the first [[time]] in a year, citing the asylum ruling, reduced border numbers, and increased wall funding as positive trends.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459
(04:08:89) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump|Trump]], seated with [[Melania Trump]], [[Alex Azar]], and the acting [[Food and Drug Administration|FDA]] commissioner, announced the agency would require flavored e-cigarette companies to take their products off the market.

### AF 2019-09-11 0459

(04:44:87) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that banning flavored e-cigarettes was critical because flavors like bubble gum and mint were marketed towards children, whereas tobacco flavors were positioned as an alternative for adult smokers.
