### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:34:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused the media of using white nationalist violence as a "false flag" to generate visceral reactions and suppress critical thinking about gun grabs and [[Concept|First Amendment]] restrictions.

### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if personal safety were the objective, people should be more concerned about violence from [[black men]] and [[Hispanics]] than from white nationalists.

### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:36:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Kate Steinle]] and an Iowa girl as examples of people killed by immigrants, arguing such cases should prompt a shutdown of immigration.

### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:37:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the media for not showing mugshots or emphasizing the race of [[black]] and [[Hispanic]] suspects, while doing so for white suspects like the [[El Paso]] shooter.

### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:38:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[Dayton]] shooter's left-wing affiliations and retweets of [[Jared Holt]] and [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] candidates were not highlighted in major national news coverage.

### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:39:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the media's double standard in covering violence fits an "anti-white agenda" and that immigrant violence is ignored because it doesn't serve that agenda.

### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:40:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that opponents of mass immigration and multiculturalism, often branded as white supremacists, are actually the ones advocating policies that would reduce future violence.

### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:41:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel|Central America]]n countries like [[Nicaragua]], [[El Salvador]], [[Honduras]], and [[Guatemala]] have some of the highest murder rates on [[earth]].

### AF 2019-08-09 0437
(00:42:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that taking away guns would not stop violence, as seen in the [[United Kingdom]], and would leave people vulnerable to stabbings by immigrants.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:03:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump|President Trump]]'s administration had conducted [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]] raids in [[State of Mississippi|Mississippi]], detaining approximately 700 undocumented immigrants.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:04:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced an ongoing feud with [[The Daily Wire]], [[Ben Shapiro]], and other figures he described as "Conservative Inc." going after him.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:05:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a pattern of social media crackdowns following mass casualty events like the [[Charlottesville rally]], the Pittsburgh synagogue shooting by [[Robert Bowers]], the [[New Zealand mosque shooting]], and the [[El Paso shooting]].

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:12:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the responses of [[Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez]] and [[Tucker Carlson]] to the [[El Paso shooting]], noting [[Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez]] leaned into racial identity politics while [[Tucker Carlson]] downplayed white supremacy as an issue.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:13:49) [[Tucker Carlson]] characterized white supremacy and racial politics as tools used by the [[Democratic Party]] to divide people and distract from class conflict, an analysis Nicholas J. Fuentes noted was similar to [[Steve Bannon]]'s approach.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:15:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Tucker Carlson]] operated under limitations due to his platform on [[Fox News]] and the need to maintain advertisers.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:20:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the central issue of the 2016 and upcoming 2020 elections was national identity, specifically whether the [[United States]] is a white nation or a multiracial nation, rather than healthcare, taxes, or the economy.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:21:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a persistent media campaign promoting insect-based foods and meat alternatives like the [[Impossible Burger]], which he characterized as intentional, astroturfed propaganda.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436

(00:26:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the [[Impossible Burger]] was developed by a [[Jews|Jewish]] chemist.

### AF 2019-08-08 0436
(00:32:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that personnel within the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration, such as [[Kevin McAleenan]] and his subordinates, were thwarting [[the president's]] immigration agenda and needed to be replaced for any meaningful progress.

(00:34:02) An estimated 100,000 undocumented immigrants entered the [[United States]] in June 2019 alone, a number Nicholas J. Fuentes stated far exceeded the scale of even the most successful [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]] deportation operations.

### AF 2019-07-30 0430

### AF 2019-07-30 0430
(00:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed a division on the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] debate stage between progressives like [[Bernie Sanders]] and [[Elizabeth Warren]] and moderates like [[John Delaney]], [[John Hickenlooper]], and [[Tim Ryan]], [[who]] argued for a more moderate message to beat [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2019-07-30 0430
(00:05:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed the ten candidates on the debate stage as [[Bernie Sanders]], [[Elizabeth Warren]], [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]], [[Beto O'Rourke]], [[Amy Klobuchar]], [[John Hickenlooper]], [[John Delaney]], [[Tim Ryan]], [[Marianne Williamson]], and [[Steve Bullock]].

### AF 2019-07-30 0430
(00:06:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that candidates qualified for the debate by securing 65,000 individual donors or achieving 1% in three approved national polls, with 20 candidates qualifying in total.

### AF 2019-07-30 0430
(00:07:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the [[Democratic Party|Democratic National Committee]] used a new procedure to ensure an even distribution of top-tier, second-tier, and bottom-tier candidates across the two debate nights, unlike the lopsided June debate.

(00:08:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the main substantive division in the [[Democratic Party]] as being between supporters of [[Medicare]] for All, like [[Elizabeth Warren]] and [[Bernie Sanders]], [[who]] would make private insurance illegal, and supporters of a public option, like [[Peter Buttigieg|Pete Buttigieg]].

### AF 2019-07-30 0430
(00:09:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited polling showing [[Medicare]] for All polls poorly with independents and [[Republican Party|Republicans]], while a public option polls significantly better across all voter groups.

### AF 2019-07-30 0430
(00:12:00) Nicholas J. ### AF 2019-07-30 0430
(00:13:30) Nicholas J. ### AF 2019-07-30 0430
(00:16:00) Nicholas J. (00:19:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that during the foreign policy segment, [[John Hickenlooper]] argued for staying in [[Afghanistan]] to avoid creating a power vacuum, while [[Elizabeth Warren]] stated [[Donald Trump]] had not been hard enough on [[Iran]] regarding nuclear proliferation.

(00:09:47) [[Iran]]'s Ministry of Intelligence claimed to have detained 17 [[Iran|Iranian]] citizens accused of working for the [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]], with some facing execution for allegedly collecting intelligence in sensitive economic, nuclear, infrastructural, military, and cyber areas. (00:10:20) [[Donald Trump|Trump]] refuted [[Iran]]'s claim of capturing [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] spies in a tweet, calling the report "totally false" and part of a propaganda campaign by a "religious regime that is badly failing." (00:10:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel]] was trying to drive the [[United States]] into a war with [[Iran]], a conflict he argued was not in [[United States|American]] interests. (00:11:40) [[Boris Johnson]] was selected as the new Prime Minister of the [[United Kingdom]], succeeding [[Theresa May]], with many drawing comparisons between his populist, unconventional style and that of [[Donald Trump|Trump]]. (00:12:46) The [[United Kingdom]]'s [[Royal Navy]] was reportedly struggling to counter [[Iran]]'s naval power in the [[Persian Gulf]], highlighting the [[country]]'s diminished ability to project military force. (00:14:02) [[Twitter]] underwent its first website redesign in seven years, a change widely criticized by users including Nicholas J. Fuentes. (00:14:41) An article from [[Twitter]].com highlighted "three women" as the designers behind the platform's controversial new redesign. (00:17:14) [[Donald Trump|Trump]] pulled the [[United States]] out of the [[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action]] ([[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action|Iran nuclear deal]]) in May 2018, seeking a more comprehensive agreement covering [[Iran]]'s nuclear program, missile development, and support for militias across the [[Middle East]]. (00:18:26) The [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration imposed sanctions on [[Iran]]'s economy after withdrawing from the nuclear deal but allowed [[Iran]] to export oil under temporary waivers for approximately a year to keep global oil prices low. (00:18:57) The [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration withdrew waivers allowing [[Iran]] to export oil in May 2019, subjecting all [[Iran]]ian oil to sanctions and escalating tensions. (00:19:09) Two tankers, a Norwegian chemical tanker and a [[State of Japan|Japanese]] oil tanker, were attacked with torpedoes in the [[Gulf of Oman]] and the [[Strait of Hormuz]] in an incident the [[United States]] blamed on [[Iran]]. (00:19:26) [[Michael Pompeo|Mike Pompeo]] asserted that only [[Iran]] among [[United States|America]]'s enemies had the capability to carry out the tanker attacks, dismissing other potential actors. (00:20:03) [[Iran]] shot down an unmanned [[United States|American]] drone over the [[Persian Gulf]], which the [[United States]] had deployed in response to the tanker attacks. (00:20:20) [[Donald Trump|Trump]] authorized and then called off a major bombing raid targeting [[Iran]]ian nuclear infrastructure in retaliation for the downing of the [[United States|American]] drone, pulling back at the last minute. (00:21:11) [[Iran]]'s seizure of the [[United Kingdom|British]] tanker Stena Impero was a direct retaliation for the [[United Kingdom]]'s seizure of the [[Iran]]ian tanker Grace One two weeks prior. (00:21:18) [[Iran]]'s foreign minister stated that [[Iran]] did not want a confrontation with the [[United Kingdom]] and suggested the [[United States]] had ordered the seizure of the [[Iran]]ian tanker. (00:23:30) [[Donald Trump|Trump]] dismissed [[Iran]]'s claim of capturing [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] spies as "totally false" and part of a propaganda campaign by a failing regime, while also declaring [[Iran]]'s economy "dead." (00:36:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that wars in the [[Middle East]] were fought for [[Israel]] and benefited the military-industrial complex. (00:37:16) [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]] arrested only 35 people as part of a highly publicized operation targeting families with deportation orders, far short of the 2,000 migrants originally touted by [[Donald Trump|Trump]]. (00:37:52) [[Donald Trump|Trump]] had claimed the enforcement operation would take people out "by the thousands" and later called the raid, which netted 35 deportations, "very successful." (00:40:58) A new [[Gallup]] poll found that 27% of registered voters considered immigration their top concern, the highest such figure for any issue in the poll's history. (00:41:48) The [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration failed to install a single mile of new wall on previously unfenced sections of the [[United States]]-[[Mexico]] border in its first 30 months, according to a report from the [[Washington Examiner]]. (00:42:18) [[Customs and Border Protection|U.S. Customs and Border Protection]] confirmed that all fencing completed since [[Donald Trump|Trump]] took office replaced existing, dilapidated barriers, with no new wall built in areas that previously had none. (00:45:20) A friend of Nicholas J. Fuentes from [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] [[who]] worked on the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] campaign noted that unconventional, paradigm-shifting candidates like [[Donald Trump|Trump]] typically only change the political conversation and rarely win election, making [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s victory the most difficult hurdle. (00:46:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes campaigned for [[Donald Trump|Trump]] in [[New Hampshire]] the day after the "Access [[Los Angeles|Hollywood]]" tape leaked, facing heckling from voters [[who]] called [[Donald Trump|Trump]] a rapist. (00:47:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the only lasting achievements of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration would be a corporate tax cut and the elimination of the individual mandate from the [[Affordable Care Act (Obamacare)]], with no progress on the border wall or mass deportations.

### AF 2019-07-10 0422
(00:26:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[the United States]] is comprised of two groups: one that follows laws and social norms without constant policing, and another that requires constant surveillance and barriers to prevent theft, violence, and drug use.

(00:38:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the judiciary and unelected bureaucrats ultimately make the most important decisions in the [[United States]], citing the census case as an example where the executive branch was blocked from performing a basic administrative duty.

(00:40:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes described a bureaucratic process where low-level, unelected officials settle most legal interpretations and enforcement, with disputes only reaching high-level appointees or [[the president]] in rare cases of significant disagreement.

### AF 2019-07-02 0419
(00:42:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the [[United States]] government is fundamentally broken because it cannot perform basic functions like determining [[who]] is in the [[country]] legally, a failure he attributed to an unaccountable bureaucracy and judiciary.

### AF 2019-07-01 0418
(00:10:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the show's feature would focus on [[Antifa]] and an attack on journalist [[Andy Ngo]] in [[Portland]].

(00:52:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that media coverage of the [[Donald Trump]]-[[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]] summit was overwhelmingly negative, focusing on criticism instead of the diplomatic achievement.

### AF 2019-07-01 0418
(01:44:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified that [[Antifa]] is not a single, centralized [[organization]] but a decentralized movement or style of protest composed of independent cells.

### AF 2019-07-01 0418

### AF 2019-07-01 0418

(03:08:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized conservative commentators for highlighting that [[Andy Ngo]] was a "gay Asian man" when condemning [[Antifa]]'s attack, arguing it revealed an implicit hierarchy of victimhood that devalued white victims.

### AF 2019-07-01 0418
(03:31:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Antifa]] represents asymmetrical political warfare through intimidation and vandalism, making it more threatening than hierarchical groups like the [[Ku Klux Klan|KKK]] or [[National Socialist Movement]].

### AF 2019-07-01 0418
(03:56:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that [[Antifa]]'s ideology and tactics align with communism and Bolshevism, not fascism or Nazism, and criticized conservatives for using [[the left]]'s moral framework by calling them "the real [[Nazis]]."

(04:40:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]] sent [[John Bolton]] to [[Mongolia]] and brought [[Tucker Carlson]] as part of his official delegation to the [[DMZ]] summit with [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]], interpreting this as a signal of his non-interventionist stance.

### AF 2019-07-01 0418
(04:48:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]] uses personnel choices, like sidelining [[John Bolton]] and embracing [[Tucker Carlson]], to communicate diplomatic intentions to foreign leaders like [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]].

### AF 2019-07-01 0418
(04:56:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes described [[Donald Trump]]'s foreign policy as a "dual containment" strategy, where he leverages tensions in [[East Asia]] and the [[Middle East]] reciprocally, such as striking [[Iran]] when negotiations with [[North Korea]] stalled.

Fuentes listed several international terrorist attacks by [[Islam|Muslims]] that occurred in the weeks following the [[New York|Christchurch]] mosque shooting, including incidents in the [[Kingdom of the Netherlands|Netherlands]], [[Italian Republic|Italy]], and [[Indonesia]].

(06:07:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized mainstream media coverage from outlets like [[Fox News]] and [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]] as uniformly describing the drone incident as "[[Iran|Iranian]] aggression" and "[[Iran|Iranian]] provocation."

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:00:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the topics for his show, which included a discussion on [[black]] reparations hearings in the [[House Judiciary Committee]] and a legislative proposal by [[Josh Hawley]] to address tech censorship.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:09:39) Following a purge of figures like [[Alex Jones]] from [[Facebook]], Nicholas J. Fuentes started a [[Telegram]] channel and an official subreddit called "Nicker Nation" to provide alternative platforms for his audience.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:11:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes planned to review [[Donald Trump]]'s 2019 re-election rally in [[Orlando]], which he characterized as lacking the focus and insurgent energy of the 2016 campaign.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:12:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s 2019 rally message on issues like socialism was indistinguishable from traditional [[Republican Party]] figures like [[Marco Rubio]] and failed to emphasize core 2016 themes like immigration, trade, and foreign policy.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:14:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes described [[Donald Trump]]'s 2019 campaign persona as "Flanderized," becoming a caricature focused on personal grievances and past events like the 2016 emails without substantive policy focus.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:15:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that [[Donald Trump]]'s 2019 rally presentation made the 2020 election the [[Democratic Party]]'s race to lose, depicting [[the President]] as tired, stale, and operating on autopilot.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:17:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[C-SPAN|CNBC]] article detailing [[Josh Hawley]]'s bill, which would require large tech companies to earn immunity through external audits proving political neutrality, supervised by the [[Federal Trade Commission|FTC]].

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:20:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that without Section 230 protections, platforms like [[Google|YouTube]] and [[Facebook]] would face insurmountable legal liability for user content that would destroy their business models.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:21:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that major social media platforms exhibited a proven double standard by systematically targeting conservatives like [[Steven Crowder]] while taking minimal action against figures on [[the left]].

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:22:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered critics like [[Wilt Chamberlain|Will Chamberlain]] [[who]] argued the Section 230 approach was ineffective, asserting that [[Josh Hawley]]'s straightforward bill was a foolproof method to combat tech censorship.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:24:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Timothy Phillips]], the head of [[Americans for Prosperity]] and father of [[Cabot Phillips]] of [[Campus Reform]], as part of a network of organizations like [[Leadership Institute]] and [[Turning Point USA]] that he argued represented big business interests against the nationalist right.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:26:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared that organizations like [[Turning Point USA]], [[Campus Reform]], [[Americans for Prosperity]], and the [[Republican Party|RNC]] represented big business and were adversaries in what he termed the real war within the [[Republican Party]].

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:27:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed 2019 as a "crusade" to destroy the conservative establishment, rejecting calls for a united front and advocating for the infiltration and dismantling of controlled opposition groups.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:28:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to institutions like [[American Renaissance]], [[VDARE.com|VDARE]], and the [[American Identity Movement]] as examples of movements thriving on the margins of conservatism, capable of damaging establishment groups because they had "nothing to lose."

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:29:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented [[Josh Hawley]] as evidence that the [[United States|America]] First movement would outlive [[Donald Trump]], citing other new senators like [[Mike Braun]] and representatives like [[Matt Gaetz]] and [[Ron DeSantis]] as more competent and ideologically committed successors.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:30:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the departure of senators like [[Jeff Flake]], [[John McCain]], and [[Bob Corker]] with the arrival of a new class of [[Republican Party]] lawmakers, whom he described as more "based" and less aligned with neoconservative and pro-[[Israel]] priorities.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:31:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that defeating tech censorship was the top priority for the right, as a free internet was essential for red-pilling younger generations whose worldview was otherwise shaped by liberal-controlled media and education.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:33:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that even the threat of [[Josh Hawley]]'s bill could deter tech platforms from implementing abusive censorship policies, similar to how [[Google|YouTube]] altered its algorithms in response to earlier [[Senate]] scrutiny.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:35:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed a [[Fox News]] article on the [[House]] reparations hearing, noting that [[Republican Party]] witnesses like [[Coleman Hughes]] opposed reparations on practical grounds, while [[Democratic Party]] representatives like [[Jerry Nadler]] emphasized the enduring impact of slavery.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:35:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes mocked [[Republican Party]] objections to reparations as unconstitutional, arguing it was a convenient legalistic dodge from a more fundamental moral argument.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:37:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected reparations on the principle that no living white [[person]] owned slaves and no living [[black]] [[person]] was enslaved, dismissing arguments for collective national responsibility as selectively applied only to white wrongdoing.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:40:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes critiqued the asymmetry of identity politics, where [[black]] [[United States|Americans]] could openly advocate for group interests while white [[United States|Americans]] expressing racial self-interest were condemned as white nationalists.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:42:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the very [[concept]] of "white people" as a monolith responsible for slavery was a construct created by reparations advocates, [[who]] ignored the diverse immigration histories and experiences of discrimination faced by groups like the [[Ireland|Irish]] and [[Italian Republic|Italians]].

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:44:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented the choice facing a multiracial [[United States|America]] as either allowing all groups to engage in identity politics or forbidding it for everyone, predicting that the current double standard was unsustainable.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:45:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that the persistent racial disparity in outcomes between [[black]] and white [[United States|Americans]], absent acceptable biological explanations, would lead to an endless cycle of grievance politics and theories of systemic racism.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:48:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that in a free, multiracial society with inherent group differences, inequality of results is inevitable, creating permanent social friction and demands for explanatory theories and remedial policies.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:49:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed skepticism that any reparations program, no matter how large, would ever satisfy demands or narrow racial gaps, viewing it as a "bottomless pit" leading to further racial animosity.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:52:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s strategy of appealing to [[black]] and [[Hispanic]] voters with modest criminal justice reform was futile when the [[Democratic Party]] was promising direct monetary reparations.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:53:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that implementing reparations, where one racial group works to pay another, would be the worst possible development for racial relations in the [[United States]], exacerbating existing tribal tensions.

### AF 2019-06-19 0410
(00:55:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes sarcastically reiterated his belief in "total equality" as his guiding credo, framing his opposition to reparations and racial favoritism within that egalitarian principle.

(01:17:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes reacted to [[Donald Trump]]'s re-election rally, calling it disappointing, boring, a redux of 2016, and lacking the energy and connection that won the 2016 election.

### AF 2019-06-18 0409
(01:18:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the rally failed to pivot away from the perceived failures of [[Donald Trump]]'s first term, such as on immigration and foreign wars, and instead relied on stale talking points.

### AF 2019-06-18 0409
(01:18:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested [[Donald Trump]] should have used the rally to declare war on establishment [[Republican Party|Republicans]] like [[Mitch McConnell]] and energize his base with a more fanatical, scorched-[[earth]] message.

### AF 2019-06-18 0409
(01:19:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Donald Trump]] for promising an immigration system that works during his campaign speech, noting that [[Donald Trump]] had a [[Republican Party|Republican]] [[Congress]] for two years and did not accomplish his core immigration goals.

### AF 2019-06-18 0409
(01:19:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the main accomplishments of [[Donald Trump]]'s first two years with a [[Republican Party|Republican]] [[Congress]] were a corporate tax cut and eliminating the individual mandate, policies he argued would have also occurred under Presidents [[Marco Rubio]] or [[Ted Cruz]].

### AF 2019-06-10 0403
(00:48:49) [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] published an article titled "Say farewell to the Klepto Queens of the [[United Kingdom|British]] far right" on his website Dangerous.com, alleging that two producers, [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]], engaged in financial fraud and sabotage within the right-wing movement. (00:52:51) The article accused [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]] of stealing tens of thousands of dollars from [[Tommy Robinson]], defrauding [[Alex Jones]] with inflated expense claims, and endangering the lives of [[Gavin McInnes]] and [[Tommy Robinson]] by leaking their security information to [[the left]]-wing [[organization]] [[Hope Not Hate]]. (00:56:33) The article further alleged that [[Lauren Southern]] (real name [[Lauren Boebert|Lauren Simonson]]) was aware of and encouraged the activities of her producers, [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]]. (01:01:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the alleged actions of [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]] demonstrated a pattern of problematic behavior when individuals not living conservative lifestyles, particularly women and homosexuals, are brought into conservative political movements. (01:06:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Milo Yiannopoulos]], [[Lauren Southern]], [[Kaelin Robertson]], and [[George Lulyn John]] as grifters motivated by money rather than genuine political conviction. (01:09:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that personal character is the most important factor for success in an insurgent nationalist political movement, citing the failures of [[Richard Spencer]] and [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] as examples of ideological and financial approaches undermined by character deficits. (01:25:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] received $15 million from the [[Mercer family]] after being fired from [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]] and disinvited from [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]], but subsequently fell into debt and irrelevance due to personal character failings. (01:33:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Mike Cernovich]] as an archetypal example of a grifter, noting his rapid shift from authoring "The [[Donald Trump|MAGA]] Mindset" to disavowing [[Donald Trump]], conservatism, and even [[Catholic Church|Catholicism]]. (04:44:49) [[Donald Trump]] threatened to impose a 5% tariff on all goods imported from [[Mexico]] on June 10th, 2019, unless [[Mexico]] changed its policies to reduce the flow of migrants across the [[United States]] border. (05:03:29) The [[Donald Trump|Trump administration]] announced a deal with [[Mexico]] on Friday, June 7th, 2019, and suspended the threatened tariffs, claiming [[Mexico]] had agreed to deploy [[U.S. Department of Defense|National Guard]] troops to its southern border and to implement measures related to asylum seekers. (05:40:93) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that the details of the [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] deal were disputed, with the [[Mexican government]] stating there was no secret written agreement and only a commitment to review migrant flows in the future, while the [[Donald Trump|Trump administration]] insisted a deal had been signed. (05:57:79) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s retreat from the tariff threat followed a pattern of making threats on issues like immigration and then backing down, which he characterized as a strategy to convince voters of his seriousness ahead of the 2020 election rather than a genuine attempt to solve the problem. (07:03:29) The [[Vatican]] issued a document during Pride Month affirming traditional gender roles, which Nicholas J. Fuentes praised as evidence that the [[Catholic Church]] is fighting globalism. (07:49:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes released a premium show analyzing the newly announced [[National Conservatism|National Conservatism Conference]], organized by [[Youram Hazony|Yoram Hazony]] and [[David Brog]], questioning its funding and associations. (08:50:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed a [[The New York Times|New York Times]] article about former alt-right figure [[Caleb Cain]], criticizing it for contradictions, such as comparing the engagement of [[ContraPoints]] to [[Stefan Molyneux]]. (09:13:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Caleb Cain]]'s story of being radicalized and then de-radicalized by online content mirrored the alleged final actions of [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]], [[who]] reportedly reached out to journalists claiming to have been de-radicalized after their activities were challenged. (13:36:39) [[Lauren Southern]] began her career as a content creator on the Canadian outlet [[Rebel Media]], alongside figures like [[Gavin McInnes]] and [[Faith Goldy]]. (14:34:25) [[Lauren Southern]] hired two homosexual producers, [[Kaelin Robertson]] and [[George Lulyn John]], [[who]] had previously worked for [[Rebel Media]] and [[Alex Jones|InfoWars]], to film her documentaries. (25:13:89) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[George Lulyn John]] was reportedly a former transgender female [[who]] later identified as a gay man. (42:04:65) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that [[Lauren Southern]] had contacted him two years prior during the "Thought Wars" debate about the role of women in the right-wing movement, where he argued they were a net negative. (43:04:71) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the allegation that [[Kaelin Robertson]], brought into the movement by [[Lauren Southern]], leaked [[Gavin McInnes]]'s itinerary information to [[Antifa]], endangering his life. (46:01:61) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the proposed [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] deal included a "third safe [[country]]" agreement, which would allow the [[United States]] to reject asylum claims from migrants [[who]] passed through [[Mexico]] without first seeking asylum there, a provision he considered a potential game-changer. (48:54:31) [[Donald Trump]] tweeted that the [[United States|U.S]]. had "fully signed and documented" an immigration deal with [[Mexico]], but that it required a vote by [[Mexico]]'s legislative body and that tariffs would be reinstated if approval was not forthcoming. (49:40:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] immigration deal to other unfulfilled "agreements in principle" by the [[Donald Trump|Trump administration]], including denuclearization with [[North Korea]], a trade deal with [[China]], and the withdrawal of troops from [[Syria]]. (50:55:61) [[Marcelo Ebrard]], [[Mexico]]'s foreign minister, stated at a press conference that there was no secret [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] agreement, only an understanding to evaluate the flow of migrants in the coming months and consider further actions if numbers did not fall. (52:57:33) According to reports cited by Nicholas J. Fuentes, part of the [[United States|U.S]].-[[Mexico]] deal involved [[Mexico]] deploying its [[U.S. Department of Defense|National Guard]] to its southern border with [[Guatemala]] and expanding the Migrant Protection Protocols (Remain in Mexico) policy. (53:44:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified that the Migrant Protection Protocols, which allow asylum seekers to wait in [[Mexico]] while their claims are processed, were not the same as the more consequential "third safe [[country]]" agreement he had hoped for. (56:59:95) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that [[Mitch McConnell]] and congressional [[Republican Party|Republicans]] might have thwarted [[Donald Trump]]'s tariff threat, but argued [[Donald Trump|Trump]] should have let them override it to expose their opposition to his immigration agenda. (01:00:00.23) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if [[Donald Trump]] were serious about immigration, he would have imposed the tariffs on [[Mexico]], dared [[Mitch McConnell]] to override them, and allowed [[Republican Party|Republican]] voters to revolt against the party for blocking the measure.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(00:12:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that his show would cover immigration, a new bill by [[Josh Hawley]], and the 75th anniversary of D-Day.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(00:38:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was required by [[Ezra Levant]] and his producers to express excitement about hosting his show.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(01:01:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes described D-Day on June 6, 1944, as the largest amphibious assault in world history, an Allied invasion of [[France]] at the beaches of Normandy that turned the tide of World War II against [[Germany]].

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(01:08:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that public figures like [[Ben Shapiro]] frequently invoke the imagery of storming the beaches of Normandy in political rhetoric, which has watered down the significance of D-Day.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(01:23:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized World War II as a contest for geopolitical power between [[Germany]] and the [[United Kingdom]], rather than a grand struggle between good and evil.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(01:59:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the lesson of D-Day is that subsequent generations, particularly baby boomers, failed in their stewardship of the civilization that the Greatest Generation fought to preserve.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(02:05:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes introduced a new bill by Senator [[Josh Hawley]] of [[State of Missouri|Missouri]] aimed at forcing video-sharing websites like [[Google|YouTube]] to disable recommendation systems for videos featuring children, following a report that the algorithm helped spread such content among predators.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(03:31:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]] report stating that illegal immigration at the [[United States|U.S]].-Mexico border in May reached a two-decade high, with over 132,000 apprehensions, a level not seen since the presidency of [[Bill Clinton]].

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(03:45:82) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited projections from researcher Stephen Kopitz that over 1 million border apprehensions and 500,000 undetected illegal crossings were expected at the [[United States|U.S]].-Mexico border in 2019.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(03:52:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]] report that [[United States|U.S]]. [[Customs and Border Protection|Border Patrol]] agents in the Del Rio Sector apprehended more than 500 migrants from the continent of [[African continent|Africa]] in one week, the first large group solely from Central and [[South Africa]] recorded in [[border patrol]] history.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(04:40:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump]] had threatened to impose tariffs on [[Mexico]], starting at 5% on June 10th and potentially rising to 25% by October 1st, if Mexico did not address immigration issues.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(04:58:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Jen Psaki|White House Press Secretary]] [[Sarah Sanders]] and Vice President [[Mike Pence]] stated the [[United States|U.S]]. was still moving forward with tariffs on [[Mexico]], with negotiations ongoing but Mexican proposals deemed insufficient.

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(05:15:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes outlined that proposed provisions in a deal with [[Mexico]] included deploying 6,000 Mexican soldiers to its southern border and implementing a "Third Safe [[Israel|Country]]" agreement to require asylum seekers to apply in the first safe [[country]] they enter, rather than the [[United States|U.S]].

### AF 2019-06-06 0402
(06:13:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he had privated all videos on his [[Google|YouTube]] channel as a precautionary measure against aggressive enforcement of new policies, but that all shows were mirrored on the alternative video hosting website [[BitChute]].

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:00:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes began the 400th episode of his show, "[[United States|America]] First," on a Wednesday evening, acknowledging a significant new development in the news.

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:01:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his intention to detail the conflict between [[Carlos Maza]] and [[Steven Crowder]], including the subsequent policy changes by [[Google|YouTube]] and the list of affected creators.

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:08:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the ongoing events as a "second adpocalypse" instigated by [[Vox]], representing a broader systematic shift from a relatively unregulated "Internet 2.0" era to a more controlled and authoritarian online environment.

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:28:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed creators demonetized as a result of the new policy, including [[News2Share]], [[The Golden One]], [[James Allsup]], [[Republican Party|Red Elephants]], [[Jesse Lee Peterson]], [[Martin Sellner]], [[Steven Crowder]], [[Red Ice TV]], and [[the left]]-wing podcast [[Drunken Peasants]].

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:29:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that accounts like [[Thulean Perspective]] (Varg Vikernes) and [[Zurious Music]] had been removed, while [[Gavin McInnes]], [[American Renaissance]], and [[Owen Benjamin]] had specific videos deleted.

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:30:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that commentators across the political spectrum, including [[Ben Shapiro]], [[Dan Bongino]], and [[Tim Pool]], were publicly criticizing [[Google|YouTube]]'s actions.

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:33:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the demonetization strategy was a more pernicious form of censorship than outright bans, as it silently crippled creators' livelihoods without generating equivalent public outcry.

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:34:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes connected the [[Google|YouTube]] controversy to broader antitrust probes by the [[Federal Trade Commission]] and the [[U.S. Department of Justice]] into major tech companies like [[Apple Inc.|Apple]], [[Amazon Inc.|Amazon]], [[Facebook]], and [[Google]].

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:40:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] congresspersons had threatened to review [[Google|YouTube]] and [[Facebook]]'s Section 230 legal protections if the companies did not more aggressively remove "hateful white nationalist content."

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:43:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the multi-year campaign of deplatforming figures like [[Alex Jones]], [[Laura Loomer]], and [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] as a systematic effort by major tech companies, motivated by post-2016 election vows, to prevent another political upset like [[Donald Trump]]'s victory.

### AF 2019-06-05 0401
(00:44:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the ultimate goal of [[Google|YouTube]], [[Google]], and the [[Democratic Party]] was absolute control over political discourse, targeting even small creators to ensure all influential voices were part of the established media conglomerate system.

### AF 2019-06-03 0399
(00:03:23) [[Jared Taylor]] and [[American Renaissance]] pursued a case against [[Twitter]], which was discussed on Nicholas J. Fuentes's show almost a year prior.

### AF 2019-06-03 0399
(00:08:29) [[Hunter Avallone]] canceled a scheduled debate with Nicholas J. Fuentes on April 20th, later agreeing to debate a communist YouTuber instead.

### AF 2019-06-03 0399
(00:50:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if [[Donald Trump|Trump]] successfully broke up [[Google]] and solved tech censorship, his presidency would be considered a success regardless of other unfulfilled promises.

Fuentes.

(20:37:840) Nicholas J. (00:01:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] "heartbeat bill," which requires an ultrasound and prohibits abortion if a fetal heartbeat is detected, constitutes a de facto abortion ban.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:02:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that an [[Alabama]] law imposes a blanket ban on abortion, making it a felony for doctors to perform the procedure.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:03:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that a law in [[India|Indiana]] requiring fetal remains to be buried or cremated was being challenged by abortion rights activists [[who]] argued it imposed an undue burden.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:04:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited the election of [[Donald Trump]] and his nomination of [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] justices with creating a political environment favorable to overturning *Roe v. Wade*.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:07:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]] article reporting that a public library in Delaware County, [[State of Ohio|Ohio]], was offering a drag queen class for teenagers.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:12:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that a man self-immolated in front of the [[White House]] shortly after he had tweeted about a slow news day.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:14:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized a [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]] report stating that Special Counsel [[Robert Mueller]]'s first public statement on the [[Russia]] probe did not exonerate [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:17:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the purpose of the [[Russia]] investigation was to delegitimize the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration and undermine the credibility of his electoral victory.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:20:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi]] received 27 standing ovations during a joint session of [[Congress]], contrasting this with concerns over [[Russian]] election interference.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:24:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained the political strategy behind recent [[state]] abortion laws, arguing they are designed to create legal challenges that will force the newly conservative-majority [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] to reconsider *Roe v. Wade*.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:27:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Netflix]], through its Chief Content Officer [[Ted Sarandos]], threatened to divest from [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] and sue the [[state]] if its heartbeat abortion bill went into effect, vowing to work with the [[ACLU]].

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:29:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited previous economic boycotts in [[India|Indiana]] over a religious liberty bill and in [[State of South Carolina|South Carolina]] over the Confederate flag as examples of corporate power overriding [[state]] political will.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:31:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced [[Viktor Orbán]]'s argument that economic power is a foundational requirement for a nationalist movement to resist globalist pressures.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:33:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Netflix]]'s intervention in the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] abortion debate demonstrated an explicit political agenda, not a profit-driven response to consumer tastes.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:38:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[State of Louisiana|Louisiana]]'s [[state]] legislature passed a heartbeat abortion bill and that [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] Governor [[John Bel Edwards]] said he would sign it into law.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:39:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[State of Missouri|Missouri]]'s only abortion clinic in [[St. Louis]] was fighting the [[state]]'s refusal to renew its license, which could make [[State of Missouri|Missouri]] the first [[state]] in over 45 years without abortion services.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:40:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that a federal judge temporarily blocked [[State of Mississippi|Mississippi]]'s heartbeat abortion law after a challenge from the [[Center for Reproductive Rights]].

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:41:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[The Satanic Temple]] was challenging the [[India|Indiana]] law requiring burial or cremation of fetal remains, citing religious freedom.

### AF 2019-05-29 0396
(00:48:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a study finding that 74% of women cited interference with education, work, or care for dependents as their reason for having an abortion, while less than 1% cited rape or incest.

(01:00:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed the film *Hidden Figures* and its thesis that [[black]] women mathematicians were critical to [[National Aeronautics and Space Administration|NASA]]'s moon mission, attributing [[United States|American]] rocket success instead to [[Germany|German]] scientists brought over under [[Project Paperclip]].

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosts the [[United States|America]] First show, announcing coverage of the [[European Union]] Parliament election results and an article from [[Michael Bloomberg|Bloomberg]] about immigration in [[State of Japan|Japan]].

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:02:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that nationalist parties in [[France]], [[Italian Republic|Italy]], [[Hungary]], and the [[United Kingdom]] performed well in the [[European Union]] Parliament elections, but the results were more nuanced than initial appearances suggested.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:04:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced he would analyze a [[Michael Bloomberg|Bloomberg]] article detailing a new immigration program in [[State of Japan|Japan]] initiated under former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]].

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:07:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Jeff Bezos]]'s ex-wife, [[MacKenzie Scott]], for receiving a $37 billion divorce settlement and then being celebrated for pledging to give half of it to charity.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:11:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes gave an unironic salute to [[United States|American]] troops and veterans in observance of Memorial Day.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:12:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began analyzing a [[Michael Bloomberg|Bloomberg]] article which reported that [[Tokyo]] is becoming more ethnically diverse due to increased immigration and tourism, a trend the article framed as a positive development.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:16:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[Michael Bloomberg|Bloomberg]] article's celebratory tone towards [[Tokyo]]'s increasing diversity as a "perverse and sick mentality" held by rootless cosmopolitans [[who]] want every [[city]] to resemble [[New York|New York City]] or [[London]].

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:18:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[State of Japan|Japan]]'s unique culture and racial homogeneity are inseparable and valuable, and that the push for immigration there sacrifices this cultural integrity for economic growth and propping up the welfare [[state]].

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:23:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that both the political left and right, including figures like [[Bernie Sanders]] and [[Charlie Kirk]], prioritize economics and workers over preserving national culture, in contrast to nationalist movements.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:25:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the immigration program in [[State of Japan|Japan]] is justified by elites as necessary to support the [[country]]'s economy and welfare [[state]], sacrificing thousands of years of cultural consistency for financial gain.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:29:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed sadness that [[State of Japan|Japan]], which he viewed as a last vestige of a homogeneous culture, is also succumbing to mass immigration, though he held out hope that the [[State of Japan|Japanese]] would resist more effectively than [[Europe|Europeans]].

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:30:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began his analysis of the [[European Union]] Parliament elections, noting he would provide a detailed breakdown on the premium show.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:31:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that the [[European Union]] has a "[[democratic]] deficit" because the [[European Commission]], not the elected Parliament, drafts all legislation, rendering the elections largely symbolic.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that in [[France]], [[Marine Le Pen]]'s [[National Rally]] party narrowly beat President [[Emmanuel Macron]]'s coalition by 23% to 22%, a victory he considered modest given [[Emmanuel Macron|Macron]]'s deep unpopularity.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:38:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that in [[Italian Republic|Italy]], [[Matteo Salvini]]'s [[League]] party won a sweeping victory with 34% of the vote, making it one of the largest single parties in the [[European Union]] and prompting Salvini to call for new national elections.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:40:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that as Interior Minister, [[Matteo Salvini]] reduced illegal immigration into [[Italian Republic|Italy]] by 92% since 2015.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:41:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that in [[Hungary]], Prime Minister [[Viktor Orbán]]'s [[Fidesz]] party won an outright majority with over 52% of the vote.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:41:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated there was potential for [[Marine Le Pen]], [[Matteo Salvini]], and [[Viktor Orbán]] to form a Eurosceptic, nationalist coalition within the [[European Union]] Parliament.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:42:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that while nationalist gains in the [[European Union]] elections were modest, the significant takeaway was the collapse of the centrist establishment parties, which lost 70 seats and saw their combined share drop from 54% to 43%.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:43:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States|America]] First movement benefits from political disorder and the destabilization of the neoliberal establishment, whether the challenge comes from the nationalist right or the radical left.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:46:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the main opponent of the [[United States|America]] First movement as the establishment right, exemplified by figures like [[Ben Shapiro]] and the [[Republican Party|GOP]], not the progressive left.

### AF 2019-05-27 0395
(00:49:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the nationalist policies gaining ground in [[Europe]] with the actions of [[Donald Trump]], whom he criticized for focusing on criminal justice reform, securing AIDS medication, and maintaining high immigration levels.

### AF 2019-04-05 0361

### AF 2019-04-05 0361
(01:07:10) During the debate, Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his support for [[Donald Trump]]'s transgender military ban, belief in only two genders, opposition to censorship, and a personal opposition to interracial relationships.

### AF 2019-04-05 0361
(01:08:52) Following the debate, opponents of Nicholas J. Fuentes raided his [[Twitch Inc.|Twitch]] chat with offensive messages and reported the channel, leading to its termination by the platform.

### AF 2019-04-05 0361
(01:15:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused his debate opponent [[Destiny]] of being a pedophile [[who]] supports ethical child pornography, beats his wife, and abandoned his children.

(01:47:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Kanye West]], [[Nas]], [[A Tribe Called Quest]], [[MF Doom]], and [[Kendrick Lamar]] as some of his favorite musical artists.

### AF 2019-04-05 0361
(01:58:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the economic policies of [[Donald Trump]], such as corporate tax cuts and deregulation, were not the primary reasons for his election, which was instead driven by promises on immigration, trade, and foreign policy.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:00:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes opened his show by apologizing for a schedule delay caused by a lengthy debate he had just participated in on another platform.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:01:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes participated in a four-hour political debate on the "TrainwrecksTV" [[Twitch Inc.|Twitch]] podcast after reaching out to the host to be included.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:03:29) The debate featured Nicholas J. Fuentes and [[Carl Benjamin|Sargon of Akkad]] arguing against [[Destiny]] and [[Hasan Piker]].

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:03:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes had previously debated [[Destiny]] twice on his own show but found the recent encounter frustrating due to interruptions and personal attacks.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:04:39) During the debate, [[Destiny]] repeatedly called Nicholas J. Fuentes a "Nazi" and accused him of wanting to kill all [[Jews]].

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:13:46) During an intermission in the debate, [[Destiny]] asked Nicholas J. Fuentes if he believed in the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]], which Nicholas J. Fuentes interpreted as an attempt to get him deplatformed.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:14:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the political left jealously guards its monopoly on moral framing and institutional power to crush dissent.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:28:00) During the debate, [[Hasan Piker]] accused Nicholas J. Fuentes of inconsistency for arguing that the cost of the [[U.S. Department of Justice|Mueller Report]] proved a lack of evidence while also stating he didn't care about government expenditures.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:30:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the fundamental issue revealed by the [[U.S. Department of Justice|Mueller Report]] was a media conspiracy to deliver a partisan narrative, with 95% of media controlled by six corporations.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:32:56) [[Destiny]] argued that the media was not liberal, citing [[Fox News]] as the number one [[cable news network]], a point Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed as ignoring broader media control.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:33:48) [[Destiny]] further claimed that excluding the largest left-wing outlets, the top content creators on [[Google|YouTube]] were right-wing, which Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized as a dishonest, arbitrary constraint.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:35:14) The debate's final topic was the [[New York|Christchurch]] mosque shooting in [[New Zealand]], which Nicholas J. Fuentes acknowledged was a white nationalist terrorist attack.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:36:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the root causes of violence like the [[New York|Christchurch]] attack were isolation, censorship, and political disenfranchisement, not merely the ideas themselves.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:38:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that mainstream left-wing figures frequently advocate violence against their political enemies, such as punching [[Nazis]] or throwing grenades through [[Richard Spencer]]'s window.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:41:27) To illustrate the impossibility of "good faith" dissent, Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the case of [[James Watson]], the Nobel laureate co-discoverer of DNA, whose career was destroyed after he made a passing remark about group differences in IQ.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:43:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that his opponents' real issue was not with violence but with political dissent, as they could not tolerate him stating things they believed to be untrue, even within a liberal paradigm that ostensibly values free speech.

### AF 2019-04-04 0360
(00:45:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that people like him are "taking all the arrows" for being willing to say things others are not, acting as martyrs to demonstrate the totalitarian nature of contemporary society.

(00:46:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that his friend [[Faith Goldy]] had been banned from platforms like [[Airbnb]], [[Google|YouTube]], [[Twitter]], and [[Facebook]] for her views.

(00:47:26) [[Elizabeth Warren]] advocated for making certain political speech a crime, which Nicholas J. Fuentes equated to creating political prisoners.

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes delivered his first campus speech at [[Iowa State University]] on Wednesday, March 6th, 2019, an event organized by the [[Iowa State]] [[Republican Party|College Republicans]].

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:05:10) The [[Iowa State]] [[Republican Party|College Republicans]] issued a public statement on March 7th, 2019, disavowing any association with Nicholas J. Fuentes and claiming they were misled to believe he was a [[Turning Point USA]]-approved speaker.

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:05:40) The [[Iowa State]] [[Republican Party|College Republicans]]' statement asserted that the views of Nicholas J. Fuentes were in direct contradiction to those of the [[Republican Party]] and their own club.

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:07:20) During the Nicholas J. Fuentes event at [[Iowa State University]], [[Alexis Holmes]] of the [[Black]] Student Alliance and [[Anthony LaBruna]] of the [[Republican Party|College Republicans]] held a public conversation on free speech in the lecture hall.

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:08:50) [[Anthony LaBruna]] publicly denounced Nicholas J. Fuentes after his speech, stating that everything Fuentes said represented views not held by the [[Republican Party]].

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:09:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Republican Party]]'s failure and potential extinction were linked to its disavowal of the views he expressed at [[Iowa State University]].

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:10:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Iowa State]] [[Republican Party|College Republicans]], specifically their head, were directly involved in organizing his event, reserving the room, and discussing strategies to evade the press.

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:11:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[Iowa State]] [[Republican Party|College Republicans]]' post-event disavowal as an act of cowardice and treachery to avoid campus controversy.

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:12:10) A police officer from [[Iowa State University]] informed Nicholas J. Fuentes just before his speech that the [[Republican Party|College Republicans]] were claiming they had not reserved the room, momentarily threatening the event.

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:16:30) [[The left]]-wing publication [[Unicorn Riot]] published a large cache of messages from the public [[Discord]] server associated with Nicholas J. Fuentes, which he described as not a leak but a collection of publicly accessible material.

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:16:50) [[Unicorn Riot]] also published a dump of messages from the [[American Identity Movement|Identity Evropa]] Discord server, a group whose member [[Patrick Casey]] Nicholas J. Fuentes spoke with at [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]].

### AF 2019-03-08 0343
(00:19:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his public Discord server explicitly notes that he is not liable for user posts and that he does not see the majority of the quarter-million messages sent there.

(00:27:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited being kicked off [[Streamlabs]], the [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]] situation, and the [[Iowa State University]] event as sources of significant tension and stress in his life during that period.

(00:29:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he would be traveling to see [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]] the following morning and would be absent from the show until the following Monday.

### AF 2019-02-26 0339

### AF 2019-02-26 0339
(01:28:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the [[Indian Air Force]] conducted airstrikes on [[Pakistan]] in retaliation for the [[Kashmir]] suicide bombing, marking the first such incursion into [[Pakistan|Pakistani]] territory in roughly 50 years.

### AF 2019-02-26 0339
(01:30:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the [[India|Indian]] airstrikes, which [[Pakistan]] claimed caused no casualties, to a 2017 missile strike ordered by [[Donald Trump]] on [[Syria]] that he said also hit evacuated targets.

(01:38:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Pakistan]]'s Prime Minister [[Imran Khan]] promised a response to the [[India|Indian]] airstrikes and convened the [[National Command Authority]], the body overseeing [[Pakistan]]'s nuclear arsenal.

(01:50:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes connected the [[India]]-[[Pakistan]] conflict to ongoing [[United States|U.S]]. peace talks with the [[Afghanistan|Taliban]] in [[Afghanistan]], suggesting [[Pakistan]] had threatened to disrupt those talks if [[India]]'s retaliation was too severe.

### AF 2019-02-26 0339
(01:54:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the measured nature of [[India]]'s airstrike was a positive sign that [[Pakistan]] would not interfere with, and might even facilitate, the [[United States|U.S]]. negotiations with the [[Afghanistan|Taliban]] for a withdrawal from [[Afghanistan]].

### AF 2019-02-26 0339
(01:59:00) Nicholas J. (02:07:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated his ban from [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]] was likely due to a [[The Huffington Post|HuffPost]] article published after the previous year's event that listed him among "white nationalists" attending the conference.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:22:08) [[Mitch McConnell]] passed the funding bill in the [[Senate]] with 84 votes, a margin Nicholas J. Fuentes argued was not necessarily veto-proof given [[Donald Trump]]'s high support among [[Republican Party|Republican]] voters.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:23:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued [[Donald Trump]] should have vetoed the funding bill and dared [[Mitch McConnell]] to override it, forcing [[Republican Party|Republicans]] to explain to voters in 2020 why they worked with [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] to pass amnesty.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:25:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the funding bill undermined [[Donald Trump]]'s national emergency declaration because the bill gave local authorities veto power over wall construction, while the emergency required seizing [[state]] and local land.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:26:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Mitch McConnell]] would only back the national emergency declaration if [[Donald Trump]] signed the spending bill.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:28:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the current funding bill process mirrored the one from the previous year: a government shutdown in January, a continuing resolution, a narrowly averted second shutdown, and an omnibus spending bill with similar funding and restrictions for border security.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:31:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that discussions of amnesty, like those in the funding bill which granted immunity to sponsors of unaccompanied migrant children, historically triggered massive influxes of immigrants, citing caravans in February and October of the previous year.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:35:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the funding bill as a substantial betrayal of [[Donald Trump]]'s base that would hurt his reelection prospects, leaving him with few accomplishments like tax cuts or moving the [[U.S. embassy to Israel]] to rally voters in key states.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:37:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported hearing from people in the [[White House]] that [[Mitch McConnell]] was "down on [[Donald Trump|Trump]]" and intended to "ride him out," not wanting him to be the nominee again.

### AF 2019-02-15 0333
(00:38:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that [[Donald Trump]]'s strategy was to create the appearance of fighting for the wall so that in 2020 he could claim he tried but was blocked, finessing the definition of a wall to include repairing existing fencing.

(00:42:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes, citing [[Scott Greer]], argued that [[Donald Trump]] should be viewed as a chaos agent [[who]] delegitimized the media and the [[Democratic Party]], and drove factions apart, rather than a change agent [[who]] would fix policy.

(00:43:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to stalled military engagements in [[Syria]], [[Afghanistan]], and [[Iraq]] under [[Donald Trump]] as evidence of a disruption to liberal internationalism, even if not a full withdrawal.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(00:52:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes began his Friday evening broadcast, describing it as a casual, low-key episode without a call-in segment.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(00:52:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the topics for the show would be a potential immigration deal, recent comments from [[Candace Owens]], and political developments in [[State of Virginia|Virginia]].

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:03:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported on a potential immigration deal framework being negotiated in [[Congress]] ahead of a February 15th deadline, with details reported in the [[Washington Examiner]] and other papers.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:05:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s 2016 campaign promise was for a 2,000-mile, 30-foot concrete border wall paid for by [[Mexico]], which had since been negotiated down to strategically placed steel slats.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:06:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the proposed immigration deal would allocate between $1.3 billion and $2 billion for border security, covering far fewer miles and less robust barriers than the steel slat wall [[Donald Trump|Trump]] favored, and would include money for technology, port security, and additional personnel.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:08:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the proposed $1.6 billion for border security with the $716 billion allocated for military spending in the 2018 Omnibus spending bill, which [[Donald Trump|Trump]] had signed while promising never to sign a similar bill again without border security funding.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:09:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed policy achievements of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration that benefited the [[Republican Party|GOP]] establishment, including tax cuts, deregulation, approval of the Keystone pipeline, drilling in [[Alaska]], and moving the [[United States|U.S.]] [[United States Embassy in Jerusalem|embassy]] in [[Israel]] to [[Jerusalem]].

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:11:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Republican Party|Republican]] leaders, including [[Mitch McConnell]], were preparing to present [[Donald Trump|Trump]] with an immigration deal similar to the previous year's, offering $1.6 billion for border security, and threatening to override a presidential veto if he rejected it.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:13:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that attempting to secure border wall funding through [[Congress]] was a "fool's errand" because major donors to both the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] and [[Republican Party|Republican]] parties benefit from mass migration for cheap labor and future votes.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:14:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the 2018 murder of [[Molly Tibbetts]] in [[Iowa]] by an illegal immigrant [[who]] worked at a dairy farm owned by the brother of a powerful [[Iowa]] [[Republican Party|Republican]] as an example of how business interests within the [[Republican Party]] rely on illegal labor.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:16:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced [[Stacey Abrams]]'s 2018 statement in [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] that demographic change would allow [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] to win without having to persuade voters, illustrating the political incentive for mass immigration.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the only way a border wall would be built was if [[Donald Trump|Trump]] declared a national emergency, a process that would face legal challenges and take years.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:19:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed that [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s difficulty in building the wall stemmed from a lack of legislative experience, hostile personnel, and opposition from all sides, comparing it to the challenges faced by former legislator [[Barack Obama]].

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:20:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that for the "[[United States|America]] First" movement to govern effectively, it must mature beyond an outsider, anti-intellectual stance and develop a professional network of educated people with legislative expertise and infrastructure.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:23:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented on a resurfaced video from December showing [[Candace Owens]], communications director for [[Turning Point USA]], making remarks about [[Adolf Hitler]] and nationalism at an event in [[London]].

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:23:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Candace Owens]] as saying, "If [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]] just wanted to make [[Germany]] great and have things run well, okay, fine," and that his error was wanting to "globalize" and make "everybody to be [[Germany|German]]."

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:25:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes disputed [[Candace Owens]]'s characterization, arguing that [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]]'s expansionist projects like the Anschluss with [[Austria]] and taking the Sudetenland were aimed at uniting historically [[Germany|German]] peoples, not global conquest.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:28:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes sarcastically criticized [[Candace Owens]] for omitting [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]]'s killing of [[Jews]] during the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] as a problem, joking that the number was "closer probably to at least a billion."

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:29:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed with [[Candace Owens]]'s point that people associate nationalism with [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]], clarifying that this association is specifically with *ethnic nationalism*, the idea that a nation is defined by its people rather than geographic or civic boundaries.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:30:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced statements by [[Bill Clinton]] in 2018 and [[Francis Fukuyama]] in 2017 distinguishing between undesirable "ethnic" or "tribal" nationalism and desirable "inclusive" or "civic" nationalism.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:32:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that "inclusive nationalism" is a contradiction because defining a group inherently excludes others, and that ethnic nationalism is demonized because it threatens elites [[who]] profit from mass migration and transnational capital.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:33:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the cultural treatment of [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]] with that of other historical figures like [[Joseph Stalin]], [[Nikita Khrushchev]], [[Fidel Castro]], [[Che Guevara]], and [[Mao Zedong]], suggesting [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]] is uniquely mythologized as an irredeemable super-villain to permanently discredit ethnic nationalism.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:36:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he is not a [[Germany|Nazi]] or [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]] apologist but a conservative traditionalist [[Catholic Church|Catholic]], and that introducing nuance into the discussion of [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]] is key to understanding current political paradigms.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:37:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Candace Owens]] as a "diversity hire" and suggested [[Turning Point USA]] should follow its own advice on meritocracy, implying her position was due to identity politics.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:38:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported on the political scandal in [[State of Virginia|Virginia]], where a second rape allegation had been made against Lieutenant Governor [[Justin Fairfax]] by [[Meredith Watson]], concerning an incident at [[Duke University]] in 2000.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:39:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[State of Virginia|Virginia]] Governor [[Ralph Northam]]'s advisers, according to [[BuzzFeed]], had instructed him to read [[Alex Haley]]'s *Roots* and [[Ta-Nehisi Coates]]'s *The Case for Reparations* as part of a strategy to recover from his blackface scandal.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:40:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes highlighted reports that [[Northam]]'s staff was exploring policy proposals to address [[State of Virginia|Virginia]]'s history of racism, targeting affordable housing, education, and public transportation for [[black]] Virginians.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:41:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[State of Virginia|Virginia]] scandal demonstrated a political lesson: offending a demographic group ([[black]] people) led to that group receiving proposed government benefits, whereas the white voting base that elected [[Donald Trump|Trump]] was overlooked in his [[State of Illinois|State]] of the Union address.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:42:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the opioid epidemic, which disproportionately kills white people, as an example of a "white issue" that does not receive commensurate political attention, suggesting white people would gain benefits if they organized and made demands like other groups.

### AF 2019-02-08 0328
(01:44:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the parents of [[Molly Tibbetts]] forgiving her illegal immigrant killer as an example of a "sickness" or lack of racial self-assertion among white people.

### AF 2019-02-07 0327
(01:48:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes made his first appearance on the "Weekly Sweat" podcast from his hotel room on the night of the [[Charlottesville]] rally, joining hosts [[Daryl Cooper|Beardson]] and [[Paul Miller|Paul Town]].

(01:53:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes encountered [[Ezra Levant]] of [[Rebel Media]] at [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]] in 2018, where Levant recognized him and dismissed his request for a photo.

(01:54:07) [[Ben Shapiro]] and figures in the "alt-light" media space attempted to sideline or destroy Nicholas J. Fuentes's career.

(01:56:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that mass migration promotes unsustainable population growth and energy consumption, a point he claimed the mainstream right avoids making.

### AF 2019-02-06 0326

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(02:02:76) Nicholas J. Fuentes previously maintained a stance that [[Donald Trump]] could do no wrong and had a strategic plan, a position he now re-evaluates.

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(04:44:94) Nicholas J. Fuentes previously argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s rhetoric on [[Syria]] and [[Iran]] did not constitute an official policy change, a framework he applies to immigration rhetoric.

(06:36:84) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he does not personally care about politicians wearing blackface, calling it "fine."

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(08:19:34) The *[[United States|America]] First* program with Nicholas J. Fuentes began broadcasting on February 5, 2017, on the [[Right Side Broadcasting Network]].

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(10:04:76) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]] with helping him get his show on the [[Right Side Broadcasting Network]], calling her his "friend and mentor."

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(12:56:66) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the problem with [[Donald Trump]]'s [[State of Illinois|State]] of the Union was not its bipartisan tone but its insufficient focus on the urgent need for a border wall, especially following a government shutdown.

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(14:28:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes positioned the [[State of Illinois|State]] of the Union address between the government reopening and a February 15th funding deadline as a missed opportunity for a renewed push on border security.

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(24:23:60) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]] listens to criticism from [[Ann Coulter]] because they are friends and she is a major voice in the conservative movement.

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(29:04:54) [[Donald Trump]] has scheduled a "[[Donald Trump|MAGA]] rally" in [[El Paso]], [[State of Texas|Texas]], for February 11th, which Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested could be an opportunity for a stronger stance on immigration.

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(54:30:84) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the primary driver of immigration from [[Mexico]] to the [[United States]] is the vast disparity in wealth, not [[United States|U.S]]. foreign policy.

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(01:01:19:76) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that older generations ("boomers") cannot conceive of a non-white majority [[United States]] because they grew up during a period of uncontested white cultural hegemony.

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(01:04:07:12) Commentator [[Wilt Chamberlain|Will Chamberlain]] accused Nicholas J. Fuentes of being an anti-Semite on [[Twitter]].

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(01:05:42:62) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed to have defeated [[Wilt Chamberlain|Will Chamberlain]] in two debates, one online and one in [[person]].

### AF 2019-02-06 0326
(01:07:18:60) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that predominantly white, [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] states like [[Vermont]] and [[New Hampshire]] do not experience the same social problems as majority-minority [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] cities, implying race is a key variable.

### AF 2019-02-05 0325
(00:22:40) [[Donald Trump]] delivered his second [[State of Illinois|State]] of the Union address, a 90-minute speech that Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized as a major disappointment and a strategic failure. (00:59:82) The address, which Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed live, was delayed from its original January 29th date after [[Donald Trump]] conceded to [[Nancy Pelosi]]'s demand to hold it in the [[House of Representatives]]. (01:06:34) [[Donald Trump]]'s speech spent only approximately three minutes on immigration, a subject Nicholas J. Fuentes identified as [[the president's]] most urgent priority, instead focusing on topics like the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]], AIDS, and criminal justice reform. (02:13:86) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s most independent presidential power is the "bully pulpit," his control over his own rhetoric and messaging to the [[United States|American]] people.
