(00:33:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized a tweet by [[Mike Cernovich]] that attacked [[Christianity]] and suggested a moderated form of [[Islam]] was the [[Kanye West|West]]'s only hope, calling it an example of a double standard where insulting [[Christianity]] is acceptable but criticizing other religions is not.

### AF 2019-01-28 0319
(00:35:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the 35-day federal government shutdown ended on a Friday, a decision that followed a pattern where [[Donald Trump]] takes an action that causes initial panic, which Nicholas J. Fuentes then attempts to explain.

### AF 2019-01-28 0319
(00:36:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Sarah Huckabee Sanders]] stated if [[Congress]] did not reach a border funding deal by February 15th, [[Donald Trump]] would declare a national emergency to build the wall.

### AF 2019-01-28 0319
(00:37:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[FiveThirtyEight]] analysis by [[Nate Silver]] which tracked polling during the shutdown and found that public blame on [[Donald Trump]] for the shutdown increased from roughly 48-49% at the start to over 50% by its end.

### AF 2019-01-28 0319
(00:38:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the government shutdown strategy failed because public discontent, which was supposed to pressure [[Democratic Party|Democrats]], backfired and turned against [[Donald Trump]], eliminating his leverage.

### AF 2019-01-28 0319
(00:39:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[The McClatchy Company|McClatchy]] and [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]] published stories alleging [[Jared Kushner]] revived a rejected proposal to offer permanent protection to 1.8 million "Dreamers" in exchange for $25 billion in border security, including some wall funding.

(00:40:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Lindsey Graham]], a co-sponsor of the Gang of Eight amnesty bill, was reportedly pushing the same immigration deal as [[Jared Kushner]] behind closed doors while publicly advocating for a [[state]] of emergency to fund the wall.

### AF 2019-01-28 0319
(00:43:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that pressure from commentators like [[Ann Coulter]] and others [[who]] have the ear of [[Donald Trump]] is useful and effective, contrasting it with calls to abandon electoral politics.

### AF 2019-01-28 0319
(00:44:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that while achievements like tax cuts and moving the [[United States Embassy in Jerusalem|U.S. Embassy]] to [[Jerusalem]] could have been done by a generic [[Republican Party|Republican]] president like [[Ted Cruz]] or [[Marco Rubio]], the core "[[United States|America]] First" promises on issues like immigration remained unfulfilled.

### AF 2019-01-25 0318
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced a monologue focused on analyzing the end of a 35-day government shutdown and the compromise bill that reopened the government for three weeks.

### AF 2019-01-25 0318

### AF 2019-01-25 0318
(00:32:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a previous government shutdown in early 2018 that was reopened with a three-week continuing resolution, after which immigration was not revisited as promised, leading to a second brief shutdown.

(00:08:16) During the call with [[Daryl Cooper|Beardson]], Nicholas J. Fuentes learned that [[Richard Spencer]] had declared he would disassociate from anyone [[who]] appeared on "irony bro" shows.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:15:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes brought on a caller with the username "Jamaican Intellectual," [[who]] revealed he was actually Australian.

(00:19:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed his admiration for [[Jordan Peterson]], praising him for being straightforward and frank, and for making leftist guests on his show feel like they are "in crazy town."

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:20:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes brought on a caller named "Vic Maxim," [[who]] identified himself as a pagan.

(00:22:34) Vic Maxim stated that in his pagan worldview, "the people are our god," a [[concept]] Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed as "a bunch of Gnostic nonsense."

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:23:23) When asked by the pagan caller about his vision for a perfect society, Nicholas J. Fuentes responded that he does not believe in a perfect society on [[Earth]], only in heaven after this life.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:25:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified himself as a "[[Catholic Church|Catholic]] integralist" in response to the pagan caller's questioning.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:26:25) The pagan caller Vic Maxim suggested that [[Christianity|Christians]] have historically been subverted by "a special group of people," a claim Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected as "patently false."

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:27:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the pagan idea that [[Christianity]] creates [[Jews|Jewish]] subversion, while paganism is the answer, was incorrect, citing the long history of enmity between Christendom and [[Jews]].

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:27:26) The pagan caller pointed to [[Pope Francis]] as an example of a leftist leader, which Nicholas J. Fuentes conceded, but argued for historical perspective.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:28:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused the pagan caller of being part of the "alt-right," which the caller denied, leading Fuentes to label him a "pagan atheist race realist."

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:30:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes defined "[[Catholic Church|Catholic]] integralism" for the pagan caller, explaining it means [[state]] officials are informed by [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] morals and are subordinate to the Church, though the institutions are separate.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:32:18) The pagan caller advocated for a revolutionary overthrow of the secular government to implement a religious [[state]], a position Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected, stating he is not a revolutionary.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:33:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes asked the pagan caller why he watched the show, noting that when he insults people on air, he is insulting people like him.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:34:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the election of [[Donald Trump]] as evidence that political change can happen within the existing system, countering the pagan caller's revolutionary arguments.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:34:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended the pagan caller read "Revolution from the Middle" by [[Samuel Francis]] before ending the call.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:35:43) After the call, Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed confusion that a pagan atheist revolutionary would be a fan of his show, which is [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] and advocates for change from within the system.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:36:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the pagan caller's description of [[Friedrich Nietzsche|Nietzsche]] as a "perspectivist" as a profoundly dumb misreading used to dilute the philosopher's ideas.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:37:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes brought on a caller [[who]] identified himself as a "Zoomer" from the early 2000s.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:39:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed sympathy for young people [[who]] become transgender, attributing it largely to bad parenting and viewing them as victims more than as inherently bad.

(00:40:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the transgender activist "[[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]]" as an example of a "broken sad [[person]]" from a broken home [[who]] gives him a lot of heat, but for whom he has [[Christianity|Christian]] mercy.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:43:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned trying out a new boom arm microphone to fix audio issues caused by him banging on his desk, but considered returning it due to poor audio quality and distraction.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:45:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes brought on a caller named "Real Question," [[who]] asked for his thoughts on [[National Aeronautics and Space Administration|NASA]] and space exploration.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:48:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed his internal conflict between traditionalist critiques of modernity and a belief that certain elements of futurism and technological advancement are inevitable and potentially good.

(00:49:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that while IQ may not be the ultimate measure, the disparities it highlights resonate with observable reality and the experiences of people like [[Charles Murray]], [[Jason Richwine]], [[Jared Taylor]], and [[Jordan Peterson]].

(00:52:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered the caller's argument about historical civilizations by pointing to the ancient [[Greece|Greeks]] and Plato, suggesting a long trajectory of advancement ahead of other groups.

(00:55:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the point of IQ arguments in politics is to legitimize the [[concept]] of tribe and highlight biological distinctions, which supports arguments against immigration and for the nation-[[state]].

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(00:56:32) The caller "Real Question]] questioned whether Nicholas J. Fuentes believed the ancient Egyptians were white, leading to a brief debate where Fuentes argued they were not [[black]] or Arab, but likely lighter-skinned and related to [[Greece|Greeks]] and [[Rome|Romans]].

### AF 2019-01-04 0306

(01:00:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[universal]] healthcare leads to rationing, lower quality, or higher costs due to fixed resources, and is more feasible in smaller, homogenous, high-trust countries like [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]] or [[Scandinavian]] nations.

### AF 2019-01-04 0306
(01:01:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested a mixed healthcare system with a government-guaranteed baseline of catastrophic coverage and private supplemental options, citing ideas from [[Anne Coulter]], [[Ben Carson]], and [[Ron Paul|Rand Paul]].

(01:05:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared a focus for 2019 on opposing "fake free market worshiping, [[Israel]] loving, zio-cons," specifically naming [[Ben Shapiro]].

### AF 2019-01-01 0304
(01:30:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the government shutdown, which began under a [[Republican Party|Republican]]-majority [[House]], had no bill that could pass both chambers of [[Congress]].

### AF 2019-01-01 0304
(01:50:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]] needed to secure border wall funding and improve his personnel, having already removed [[James Mattis]] and [[James Mattis|John Kelly]].

(02:10:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that [[Chuck Schumer]] and [[Nancy Pelosi]] opposed funding a border wall both ideologically, due to support for open borders, and politically, to deny [[Donald Trump]] his central campaign promise.

(02:33:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared [[Donald Trump]]'s situation to [[George W. Bush|George H. W. Bush]]'s broken "read my lips" tax pledge, which contributed to his failed reelection.

(02:52:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Donald Trump]]'s path to reelection in 2020 depended on securing funding for the border wall.

(03:16:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[Republican Party|Republican]]-majority [[House]], before adjourning, passed a spending bill with $5.6 billion for border wall funding, which was then before the [[Senate]].

(03:38:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Mike Pence]] had proposed a compromise deal with $2.5 billion for the wall during back-channel negotiations, which [[Donald Trump]] rejected.

### AF 2019-01-01 0304
(03:52:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]] said a broader immigration policy deal, addressing issues like chain migration, would be negotiated after the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] decided the fate of DACA.

### AF 2019-01-01 0304
(04:22:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Donald Trump]] for persistently claiming that miles of border wall had already been built, arguing that zero miles of a concrete wall existed and only fencing had been constructed or repaired.

### AF 2019-01-01 0304
(04:55:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]] had never negotiated a government shutdown with this level of resolve before, as a previous attempt was undermined when courts intervened on DACA.

### AF 2019-01-01 0304
(05:10:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Donald Trump]]'s communications strategy, arguing he should deliver a focused, nationally televised address on the need for a border wall instead of relying on scattered social media posts and press conferences.

(05:21:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Donald Trump]]'s press conference digression into topics like [[Mitt Romney]]'s op-ed and Obamacare when asked about the government shutdown as an example of unfocused messaging.

(07:28:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes proposed that [[Donald Trump]] might be using the threat of withdrawing from wars in [[Syria]] and [[Afghanistan]] as leverage in negotiations with the establishment to secure funding for the border wall.

(15:48:72) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Donald Trump]] cited his administration's move of the [[United States]] [[United States Embassy in Jerusalem|embassy]] to [[Jerusalem]] as evidence of his support for [[Israel]].

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(16:21:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Barack Obama]] signed a law providing [[Israel]] with $3.8 billion per year over ten years, making [[Donald Trump]]'s cited figure of $4.5 billion significant.

(17:12:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]] was the primary reason for [[United States]] military involvement in [[Iraq]], [[Afghanistan]], [[Syria]], [[Yemen]], and [[West Africa]].

(18:10:70) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[United States]] foreign aid to [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]] and [[Jim Jordan|Jordan]] increased substantially following the 1979 [[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action|Camp David Accords]] and the 1996 "Clean Break" memo, linking it to [[Israel]]'s strategic interests.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(19:34:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump]] retweeted commentator Brad Blakeman, [[who]] said "the [[United States|American]] people understand that we have been played by foreign actors [[who]] would rather have [[us]] fight their battles for them."

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(20:45:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]]'s [[U.S. National Security Council|National Security Advisor]] [[John Bolton]] and Defense Secretary [[Mattis]] had stepped down, facilitating the withdrawal from [[Syria]].

(21:17:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Turkey]], under [[Recep Tayyip Erdogan|Erdogan]], hates [[Israel]] and would likely expand its operations against Kurdish forces in [[Syria]], harming [[Israel]]'s regional position.

(22:26:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the [[United States Senate]] voted to end support for the war in [[Yemen]] and that liberal opposition to [[Saudi Arabia]] reduced the chance of war with [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(23:40:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that acting [[White House]] Chief of Staff [[Mick Mulvaney]] might be a positive influence on [[Donald Trump]]'s foreign policy.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(24:05:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted his view that [[Donald Trump]] was hindered by bad personnel with critics like [[Mike Cernovich]] [[who]] argued [[Donald Trump]] never intended to fulfill his promises.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(24:37:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that the appointment of [[John Bolton]] as [[U.S. National Security Council|National Security Advisor]] was widely predicted to lead to wars in [[Iran]] and [[Syria]], which did not happen.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(26:01:70) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that a government shutdown over border wall funding had begun the previous Friday with no end in sight, as [[Donald Trump]], [[Chuck Schumer]], and [[Nancy Pelosi]] refused to compromise.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(27:59:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted a [[Donald Trump]] tweet stating the border wall would be built using shutdown negotiation money plus funds already available to the [[U.S. Department of Defense|Department of Defense]] and [[U.S. Department of Homeland Security|Department of Homeland Security]].

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(30:21:86) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the appointments of [[Mick Mulvaney]] as Chief of Staff and [[Bill Barr|Barr]] as Attorney General were good picks, and that the departure of [[Mattis]] was positive.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(31:35:82) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Barack Obama]]'s presidential legacy was limited, citing the partial implementation of the Affordable Care Act and the failure to withdraw troops from [[Afghanistan]] and [[Iraq]].

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(32:30:70) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited [[Donald Trump]] with cutting [[Islam|Muslim]] refugee admissions by 90%, [[Hispanic]] refugee admissions by 40%, and rejuvenating the economy.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(35:50:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]] ended the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA) program created by [[Barack Obama]], leading [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] to demand its legalization during a January government shutdown.

(37:51:72) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that a federal judge ruled [[Donald Trump]]'s termination of DACA was unconstitutional, and the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] declined to fast-track an appeal, removing [[Donald Trump]]'s leverage in negotiations.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(39:01:92) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that then-Speaker [[Paul Ryan]] scuttled a potential immigration deal that would have traded DACA protections for cuts to legal immigration and border wall funding.

### AF 2018-12-27 0302
(40:04:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that federal courts repeatedly obstructed [[Donald Trump]]'s policies, such as the travel ban, preventing faster progress on his agenda.

(38:33:77) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that National Socialism was defined as a heresy by the [[Papacy]] and was a largely secular ideology.

(41:00:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that widespread adherence to [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] sexual morality would immediately reconstruct the [[United States|American]] family and address demographic decline, a more effective solution than race realism propaganda.

(42:04:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the "alt-right's fatal conceit" that presenting racial facts alone could solve systemic issues, arguing the [[Kanye West|West]] suffers from a deeper spiritual malaise.

(45:31:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes sarcastically suggested [[Patrick Little]] might have a shot at the presidency in 2036 if he cleaned up his personal conduct.

(45:56:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that figures like [[Richard Spencer]] and [[Patrick Little]] were delusional and detached from reality for believing they could be elected president.

### AF 2018-12-21 0301
(48:34:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised a high school student to focus on reading foundational texts and enjoying youth, but to consider volunteering for political campaigns for optics and networking.

### AF 2018-12-21 0301
(51:25:61) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended that young activists get involved with organizations like [[Turning Point USA]] and [[Young Americans for Liberty]], attend county [[Republican Party|GOP]] meetings, and build a network.

(52:32:85) Nicholas J. Fuentes emphasized the need for a young [[person]]'s movement, contrasting it with older demographics in movements like QAnon, and promoted the "Zoomer" identity.

(55:08:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Jews]] are the top group in the progressive hierarchy of oppression, followed by a floating middle of other groups, with white people at the bottom and [[Asians]] closer to whites.

### AF 2018-12-21 0301
(56:47:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes added that disabled people are also near the top of the oppression hierarchy, with the term "retarded" becoming almost as taboo as racial slurs.

### AF 2018-12-21 0301
(59:53:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that cleaning [[house]] in the [[Catholic Church]] to install a traditionalist leadership would provide a more lasting backbone for a right-wing revival than draining the political swamp, which would have temporary effects.

(01:00:58:88) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that draining the political swamp would be less impactful long-term, as [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] would likely hold power indefinitely within a decade.

### AF 2018-12-21 0301
(01:05:42:68) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he believes the climate changes but is skeptical that man-made pollution is a major cause, criticizing the mono-causal focus on CO2 emissions by climate activists.

(01:08:48:86) Nicholas J. Fuentes described [[Enoch Powell]] as "based," "Redpilled," and "way ahead of his [[time]]."

(01:18:05:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes said he would likely vote for [[Donald Trump|Trump]] over [[Richard Spencer]] in the 2020 presidential election.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:00:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted the 300th episode of his program, marking a milestone in his broadcasting career.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:03:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes met with a fan of his show for lunch and subsequently visited a party store to inquire about balloons for the 300th episode celebration.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:04:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the arrival of new "[[United States|America]] First" merchandise, including apparel made available with the help of special friends.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:05:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes revealed a new sweatshirt design featuring a Pepe-style cartoon of himself with a weapon, which he clarified was intended as a nonviolent meme for self-defense.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:07:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes also unveiled a new "Mark IV [[United States|America]] First" mug, available for purchase alongside the sweatshirt.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:09:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Millennial Matt]] had recommended the merchandise platform GearBubble, reassuring him of its quality.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:09:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a debate he had the previous night with "little Ronnie Cameron," which he characterized as painful and unproductive.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:10:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that he never identified with the "alt-right" label, citing a tweet from January 2018 where he called the alt-right "goofy."

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:12:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes humorously announced his "inauguration" into the alt-right on the 300th episode, presenting a whiteboard with new guidelines for the movement.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:13:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed a new rule for the alt-right: "no homophobes allowed," arguing that homosexuals and pagans constitute a significant portion of the movement and are needed.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:14:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes established a second rule requesting that people stop using a racial slur against his girlfriend, [[who]] he described as being "half [[black]]."

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:15:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared a third rule that the movement must defer to its leader, whom he described as a "[[king]] of all trades" with money and connections, such as having taken pictures with [[Laura Bush]].

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:16:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes issued a fourth rule: "please don't try to keep me away from brunch," warning that it would not end well for anyone.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:17:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes transitioned to discussing serious news, beginning with the issue of border security and a potential government shutdown.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:17:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that earlier in the week, the [[Washington Post]] and other papers had run headlines stating [[Donald Trump]] was backing off his demand for $5 billion in border wall funding.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:18:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that the [[United States Senate]] had passed a continuing resolution to temporarily fund the government, which [[Donald Trump]] subsequently said he would not sign without border wall funding.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:19:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[Democratic Party]] would take control of the [[United States House of Representatives]] on January 3rd, weakening the [[Republican Party|Republican]] negotiating position for border wall funding.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:21:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted a tweet from [[Donald Trump]] criticizing the lack of border security funding while the [[United States|U.S]]. fights for the security of other countries like [[Israel]].

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:22:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the [[United States House of Representatives]] had passed a temporary spending bill along party lines that included over $5 billion for a border wall.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:23:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Paul Ryan]] and [[Senate Republicans]] had tried to give [[Donald Trump]] only $1.6 billion for border security in the continuing resolution, with strict language preventing its use for a wall.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:25:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that a procedural rule in the [[United States Senate]] requiring 60 votes to end debate was preventing a simple majority vote on border wall funding, though he noted a special rule for appropriations bills might allow it.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:26:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned that [[Ann Coulter]], [[Tucker Carlson]], and [[Michelle Malkin]] had been publicly critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s handling of border wall funding.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:27:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump]] had given an order to withdraw all 2,000 [[United States|U.S]]. combat troops from [[Syria]] very quickly.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:27:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes connected the resignation of Defense Secretary [[James Mattis]] to [[Donald Trump]]'s decision to pull troops from [[Syria]] and [[Afghanistan]], citing [[James Mattis]]'s resignation letter which stated he wanted a defense secretary whose views aligned more closely with [[the president's]].

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:28:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]] had announced the withdrawal of 7,000 [[United States|American]] troops from [[Afghanistan]], approximately half of the [[United States|U.S]]. force there, which the military described as phase one of ending the war.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:29:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed his personal dislike for [[James Mattis]], criticizing him for supporting transgender individuals and homosexuals in the military, being a [[Democratic Party|Democrat]], and implementing cultural sensitivity training.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:30:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that new [[White House]] Chief of Staff [[Mick Mulvaney]] might be making a positive impact, alongside potential pressure from [[Ron Paul|Rand Paul]], leading to movement in foreign policy.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:30:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes opined that war with [[Iran]] was "out of the question" given the current [[state]] of relations with [[Saudi Arabia]].

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:31:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that Acting Attorney General [[Matthew Whitaker]] had said he would not recuse himself from the [[Robert Mueller]] investigation, despite recommendations from an ethics committee.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:35:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted a tweet from [[Donald Trump]] justifying the withdrawal from [[Syria]], stating that the [[United States|U.S]]. had been campaigning on it for years and that it was [[time]] for others like [[Russia]], [[Iran]], and [[Syria]] to fight [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]].

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:36:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed several commentators—[[Bill Kristol]], [[Bret Stephens|Brett Stephens]], the late [[Charles Krauthammer]], and [[Max Boot]]—[[who]] he claimed would advocate for the [[United States|U.S]]. to remain in the [[Middle East]] forever.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:41:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the political establishment, including neocons, [[the left]], and figures like [[Paul Ryan]], laughs at online critics because they continue to hold power and "pull the levers."

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:44:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended that supporters call [[the White House]] to politely pressure [[Donald Trump]] to focus on border security and ending wars in the [[Middle East]], crediting [[Jason Miller|Jay McFeels]] for promoting this tactic.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:44:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Ann Coulter]], [[Michelle Malkin]], and [[Tucker Carlson]] had publicly criticized [[Donald Trump]] over border wall funding, with [[Ann Coulter]] sacrificing her [[Twitter]] follow from [[the president]].

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:48:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes, in response to a viewer question, stated that as a [[Christianity|Christian]] he is called to love everybody, including [[black]] people, but clarified that [[Christianity|Christian]] love is different from materialist love and that he has a preference for his own people.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:50:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes read a message purportedly from his mother congratulating him on the 300th episode.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:52:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented on [[Paul Joseph Watson]], stating he finds it hard to dislike him despite finding some of his jokes lacking, and acknowledged Watson had given him a shout-out.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(00:57:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised a viewer on whether to grow a beard, stating it depends on the individual's physiognomy and coverage, using [[Gavin McInnes|Gavin McInnis]] as an example of someone [[who]] needs a beard due to a lack of chin.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(01:00:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes, in response to a question, said he is a big fan of [[Ron Paul|Rand Paul]], met him at a book signing, and would have voted for him in the [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary had he not dropped out.

### AF 2018-12-20 0300
(01:02:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed the idea of joining the [[Eastern Orthodox Church]], arguing it lacks the authority of [[Rome]] or [[Jerusalem]].

(00:06:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes scheduled a debate for later that evening with [[Ronnie Cameron]] on [[The Kill Stream]], hosted by [[Ethan Ralph]].

(00:06:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Ronnie Cameron]] as an opponent with little influence and predicted the debate would be a "blood bath."

(00:08:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced he would not host his show on Christmas [[Adam Kinzinger|Eve]] or Christmas Day.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began his monologue by announcing his return to the show and stating that [[Donald Trump]] had recently taken an action unpopular with his political base, which would be a central topic of discussion.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:00:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the upcoming discussion as another episode of a recurring segment where he faces online criticism for bad headlines, which he described as a fun game.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:00:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the main topics for the evening: the ongoing budget battle in the [[United States]] [[Congress]] over border wall funding, with a government shutdown deadline of December 21st.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:01:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he would discuss a vindicating article he found on Vice.com, which he claimed was further evidence of a societal trend he had previously predicted.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:01:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted his past prediction that media would promote insect consumption as a meat substitute, which he attributed to the agenda of "global homo."

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:02:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes revealed the title of the Vice article as "Why are so many women searching for ultra-violent porn," citing data on searches for tags like "extreme brutal gang bang" and "rape."

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:03:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes humorously framed the discussion of the Vice article as an alliance between "full cell nation" and "incel nation."

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:04:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if [[time]] permitted, he would also discuss [[Tucker Carlson]] being in hot water again at [[Fox News]], promising new insights beyond the usual narrative of left-wing advertiser boycotts.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:05:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes sought to counter the perception that he was an eternal "[[Donald Trump|Trump]] cheerleader" by stating his take had always been nuanced, acknowledging [[Donald Trump]]'s correct instincts but the overwhelming opposition he faced.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:07:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes began establishing his credibility on the border wall funding issue by referencing his past tweets, stating the current impasse stemmed from [[Donald Trump]] not forcing the issue during the last spending bill.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:08:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted the sequence of the budget battle, starting over a year prior when [[Donald Trump]] offered [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] protections in exchange for wall funding, which [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] rejected, leading to a government shutdown.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:08:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Paul Ryan]] shut [[Donald Trump]] out of negotiations and passed an omnibus spending bill in March that gave $715 billion to the military but no funding for the wall, which Nicholas J. Fuentes called an absolute disaster.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:09:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted his own March tweet criticizing the omnibus bill but expressing hope [[Donald Trump]] would use it to pressure [[Paul Ryan]] and the [[Republican Party|GOP]], though he ultimately blamed congressional leadership more than [[the president]].

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:10:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the next deadline on September 30th, quoting his September 16th tweet criticizing [[Republican Party|GOP]] leadership for delaying the wall funding fight until after the midterm elections, which he called a stall tactic.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:11:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted his post-midterm tweet stating [[Donald Trump]] needed to replace [[Nielsen]] and [[Kelly]] and secure wall funding by December or 2020 would be difficult.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:11:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes synthesized that the mess was created by [[Republican Party|GOP]] congressional leadership, specifically [[Paul Ryan]] and [[Mitch McConnell]], [[who]] set the agenda and failed to deliver promised votes, not solely by [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:12:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes read a live tweet from [[Donald Trump]] stating the wall would be made of "artistically designed steel slats" rather than concrete, which Nicholas J. Fuentes said added nothing new to the conversation.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:13:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes addressed the day's bad headlines, citing a [[Washington Post]] report stating [[Donald Trump]] was backing off his demand for $5 billion in wall funding, which he called deceptive media framing.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:13:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Sarah Huckabee Sanders]] from a press conference, where she said the [[White House]] would explore other ways to get the $5 billion and could support a $1.6 billion [[Senate]] [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] proposal if coupled with other funding.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:14:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes interpreted [[Sarah Huckabee Sanders]]'s statement as a commitment to get $5 billion "one way or the other," either through the spending bill or by reallocating funds from other agencies like the [[U.S. Department of Defense|DOD]].

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:16:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed bewilderment that people [[who]] purport to understand the media lies would believe the deceptive headline about [[Donald Trump]] backing down without reading the article.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:17:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited further details from the [[Washington Post]] article, stating [[Mitch McConnell]] later offered [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] the previously agreed $1.6 billion plus an additional $1 billion for other immigration priorities, which [[Nancy Pelosi]] and [[Chuck Schumer]] rejected.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:18:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed the situation, suggesting [[Sarah Huckabee Sanders]]'s offer to accept $1.6 billion might be a tactic to call the [[Democratic Party|Democrats]]' bluff and frame them as responsible for a shutdown when they inevitably reject it.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:20:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the current negotiations to those in January, where [[Donald Trump]] initially offered [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] amnesty but ultimately secured $1.6 billion for the wall without giving anything up.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:21:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the [[administration]]'s sloppy framing for shifting from taking "full responsibility" for a shutdown to wanting to "avoid a shutdown," but concluded [[Donald Trump]] was still negotiating.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:22:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was not optimistic about the process, as forcing [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] capitulation would require [[Donald Trump]] to be content with a prolonged government shutdown and a scorched-[[earth]] executive campaign, which he had never done.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:23:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded his assessment by stating he still believed [[Donald Trump]] was committed to building the wall and ending illegal immigration, but was hampered by subversive personnel, uncooperative [[Republican Party|GOP]] leadership, and a hostile media.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:24:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]] was the only [[person]] in electoral politics still actively fighting on immigration, and that critics seeking to turn people away from electoral politics likely had bad ulterior motives, implying they were federal agents.

(00:26:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes mocked the idea of running "[[Richard Spencer|Spencer]]" for president as an example of the destructive alternative to supporting [[Donald Trump]].

(00:27:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed his qualifications for optimism about [[Donald Trump]]'s next two years: a "based" [[U.S. Department of Homeland Security|DHS]] secretary and chief of staff, neither of which had happened, and wall funding that looked to be less than a third of the predicted cost.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:28:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the solution was not to blame [[Donald Trump]] but to work towards filling legislatures and important positions with "based red-pill type people" over a 30-year span, building upon existing efforts rather than abandoning them.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:29:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes read from the Vice article, which cited data from a former [[Google]] data scientist showing a quarter of straight porn searches by women were for videos featuring violence against women, with search rates for extreme content at least twice as common among women than men.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:31:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a 2012 study from the [[University of North Texas]] and the [[University of Notre Dame]] where 52% of women surveyed reported fantasies about forced sex, and 62% reported at least one fantasy around a forced sex act.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:32:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes emphasized the data, stating that meeting any woman meant a coin flip chance she had a rape fantasy.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:33:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the importance of the data was to highlight the dichotomy between what women say they want (consent culture) and what their search behavior and fantasies reveal, which he had been pointing out for over a year.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:33:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed the trend to a repressed female desire for male aggression, arguing that complementary male and female natures are hardwired, and societal messages against male domination cause these desires to manifest perversely.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:35:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that healthy, natural sexuality ordained by God exists within the procreative union of marriage, which provides stability for rearing children, and that being unmoored from these principles leads to perverse manifestations of intrinsic desires.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:37:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected the libertarian framing of a choice between oppressive theocracy and total freedom, arguing the real dichotomy is between sacred marital unions and complete, unfiltered hedonistic degeneracy.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:39:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that human nature is flawed and limited, and that attempts to surpass these limitations through doctrines of equality and having it all lead to societal failure and perversion, as evidenced by the porn search data.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:42:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes applied a conservative philosophy to the issue, stating that recognizing and optimizing society around human limitations, rather than trying to exceed them, leads to a better society.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:43:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes addressed women directly, stating that just as men have obligations like going to war or providing, women must also accept their obligations and "do your [[fair]] share," which he defined as being mothers rather than congresswomen or businesspersons.

### AF 2018-12-18 0298
(00:44:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared, "We need mommy back in the [[house]]," citing below-replacement fertility, skyrocketing divorce, male suicide from loneliness, and anxious children as consequences of women abandoning domestic roles.

(00:46:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes sarcastically congratulated society on "women's liberation," which he characterized as liberating women from babies and domesticity, resulting in the described pornographic trends.

(03:59:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended the Classical Theist [[Google|YouTube]] channel, which led a viewer to the work of [[David Bentley Hart]] and a conversion from atheism to theism.

### AF 2018-11-09 0280
(04:03:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes declined to give a "Heil Hohenzollern" in honor of the 100th anniversary of Kaiser [[Wilhelm II]]'s abdication, citing it as "a little edgy" and noting he was not [[Germany|German]].

### AF 2018-11-09 0280
(04:08:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes wished a happy birthday to a viewer named David and mocked [[Will Nardi]] for being "friend-zoned" by a woman for two years.

### AF 2018-11-09 0280
(04:20:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes reflected that [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] played an important role as a gateway during the 2015 political awakening, despite later becoming a "Flanderized" character consumed by his own personality.

### AF 2018-11-09 0280
(04:33:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized a Protestant viewer questioning [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] confession, stating Protestantism was a 500-year-old rebellion against the apostolic tradition of [[Saint Peter]]'s Church.

### AF 2018-11-09 0280
(04:42:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted he rarely receives reciprocal invitations to appear on other shows despite frequently hosting guests, comparing himself to [[Donald Trump|Trump]] getting "killed on trade."

### AF 2018-11-08 0279
(01:00:11.600) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed opposition to the potential [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] nomination of [[Amy Coney Barrett]], citing a general opposition to women serving on [[the court]].

### AF 2018-11-08 0279
(01:02:03.580) Nicholas J. Fuentes assessed that a hypothetical 2020 presidential matchup between [[Donald Trump]] and [[Robert O'Rourke|Beto O'Rourke]] in [[State of Texas|Texas]] would be competitive but likely favor [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2018-11-08 0279
(01:03:00.800) [[Tucker Carlson]] aired a segment arguing that conservatism should be centered on the family rather than the individual, a view Nicholas J. Fuentes endorsed.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a monologue analyzing the results of the 2018 [[United States]] midterm elections.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:01:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes had predicted the [[Republican Party]] would gain between three and five seats in the [[United States Senate]] and face a difficult fight for control of the [[United States House of Representatives]].

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:05:59) The [[Republican Party]] lost [[United States Senate]] races in [[State of West Virginia|West Virginia]] and [[State of New Jersey|New Jersey]], with Nicholas J. Fuentes attributing the [[State of West Virginia|West Virginia]] loss to a Libertarian candidate splitting the vote.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:06:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Republican Party|Republican]] [[Senate]] candidates in [[State of Ohio|Ohio]] and [[State of Michigan|Michigan]] [[who]] did not embrace [[Donald Trump]] lost their elections.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:09:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the 2018 midterm results, where [[Donald Trump]]'s party lost 27 [[House]] seats, with the 2010 midterms where [[Barack Obama]]'s party lost 63 [[House]] seats.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:10:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that historically, a president's party typically loses seats in the first midterm election, making the [[Republican Party]]'s [[Senate]] gains and modest [[House]] losses an atypical outcome.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:14:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed the [[Republican Party]]'s failure to repeal the Affordable Care Act in 2017 to congressional leadership, citing [[John McCain]]'s decisive vote against the legislation.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:14:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act for primarily benefiting corporations rather than the middle class, arguing it did not help [[Republican Party|Republican]] turnout in the 2018 midterms.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:16:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes blamed [[Paul Ryan]] and [[Mitch McConnell]] for passing a large omnibus spending bill in early 2018 that did not fund [[Donald Trump]]'s border wall.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:16:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited [[Donald Trump]] with nominating [[Brett Kavanaugh]] to the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] and highlighting the migrant caravan as issues that galvanized the [[Republican Party|Republican]] base before the 2018 midterms.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:18:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that congressional leadership, not [[Donald Trump]], was responsible for legislative failures on healthcare, tax cuts, and immigration during [[Donald Trump|Trump's]] first two years in office.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:19:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Donald Trump]]'s executive accomplishments as including diplomacy with [[North Korea]], avoiding war with [[Syria]] and [[Iran]], renegotiating [[North American Free Trade Agreement|NAFTA]], and enforcing border laws.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:20:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited exit polls showing [[Donald Trump]] had a 90% approval rating among [[Republican Party]] voters following the 2018 midterms.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:20:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Paul Ryan]] retired as [[Mike Johnson|Speaker of the House]] and [[Mitch McConnell]] would need to follow the party's direction, while a new pro-[[Donald Trump|Trump]] class of senators like [[Mike Braun]] was elected.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:22:22) According to 2018 exit polls cited by Nicholas J. Fuentes, 51% of men voted for the [[Republican Party]] while 59% of women voted for the [[Democratic Party]].

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:23:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Jair Bolsonaro]] won the female vote in [[Brazil]] by projecting strength and a capacity to protect families, not by pandering to women.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:24:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited exit polls showing 90% of [[black]] voters supported the [[Democratic Party]] in the 2018 midterms, dismissing efforts by [[Candace Owens]] and [[Kanye West]] to attract [[black]] voters to the [[Republican Party]].

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:25:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[black]] voters supported [[Barack Obama]] at 97%, [[Hillary Clinton]] at 91%, and [[Doug Jones]] in Alabama at 97%, showing consistent [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] alignment.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:27:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited exit polls showing 70% of [[Hispanic]] voters and 77% of Asian voters supported the [[Democratic Party]] in the 2018 midterms.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:28:14) [[Bill Kristol]] tweeted "demography is [[destiny]]" following the 2018 midterm elections, according to Nicholas J. Fuentes.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:29:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a speech by [[Samuel Francis]] at an [[American Renaissance]] conference about white resistance to identity politics.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:30:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that non-white demographic growth would make the [[United States]] a majority-minority nation by 2045, leading to permanent [[Democratic Party]] majorities opposed to gun rights and free speech.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:32:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited exit polls showing [[Jews|Jewish]] voters supported the [[Democratic Party]] by 80% in the 2018 midterms, a higher margin than atheists or other religious groups.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:33:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the 80% [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] support among [[Jews|Jewish]] voters with the 56% [[Republican Party|Republican]] support among [[Christianity|Christian]] voters in the 2018 midterms.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:34:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that healthcare was the most important issue for 41% of 2018 midterm voters, followed by immigration at 23% and the economy at 22%, blaming the [[Republican Party]] for failing to pass healthcare legislation.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:35:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned that [[Paul Nehlen]], [[who]] campaigned on historical issues like the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]], lost the [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary in [[Wisconsin]]'s 1st congressional district.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:36:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes blamed [[Paul Ryan]] and [[John McCain]] for the [[Republican Party]]'s failure on healthcare, which exit polls showed was the most important issue for voters.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:36:56) According to exit polls cited by Nicholas J. Fuentes, 87% of [[Democratic Party]] voters believed white people were favored in [[United States|America]], while 85% of [[Republican Party]] voters believed minorities were favored.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:39:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Nancy Pelosi]] would become [[Mike Johnson|Speaker of the House]] and her first priority would be to demand [[Donald Trump]]'s tax returns.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:40:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Steven King|Steve King]] won re-election in Iowa's 4th congressional district despite a national [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] campaign labeling him a white nationalist.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:40:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Robert O'Rourke|Beto O'Rourke]] lost the [[State of Texas|Texas]] [[United States Senate]] race to [[Ted Cruz]] but by only 2.6%, demonstrating [[State of Texas|Texas]] was becoming a battleground [[state]] due to [[Hispanic]] support for O'Rourke.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:41:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Bob Corker]], [[John McCain]], [[Jeff Flake]], and [[Paul Ryan]] as [[Republican Party|Republican]] critics of [[Donald Trump]] [[who]] retired or died before the 2019 [[Congress]].

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:43:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned rumors that [[Kris Kobach|Chris Kobach]] might be appointed [[U.S. Department of Justice|United States Attorney General]] and that [[Rod Rosenstein]] would resign within 48 hours, losing control of the [[Robert Mueller]] probe.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:43:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended [[Jefferson Beauregard Sessions III|Jeff Sessions]] as a strong attorney general on immigration, citing support from [[Ann Coulter]] and [[Tucker Carlson]], despite his recusal from the [[Robert Mueller]] investigation.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:44:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed gubernatorial results: [[J.B. Pritzker]] won in [[State of Illinois|Illinois]], [[Scott Walker]] lost in [[Wisconsin]], [[Mike DeWine]] won in [[State of Ohio|Ohio]], [[Ron DeSantis]] won in [[State of Florida|Florida]], and [[Brian Kemp]] won in [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]].

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:45:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Democratic Party]] won governorships in [[State of Nevada|Nevada]], [[State of New Mexico|New Mexico]], [[Kansas]], [[Wisconsin]], [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[State of Illinois|Illinois]], and [[State of Maine|Maine]] in the 2018 elections.

### AF 2018-11-07 0278
(00:45:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Kris Kobach|Chris Kobach]]'s loss in the [[Kansas]] gubernatorial race as a significant defeat, suggesting Kobach should be given a role in the [[Donald Trump|Trump administration]] as a potential successor.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a live election night broadcast for the 2018 midterms with guests [[Bryden Proctor]] and [[Vince]].

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(00:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes jokingly blamed [[Ben Shapiro]] for the persistent audio issues plaguing the live stream.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(01:04:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]]-controlled [[House]] led by [[Nancy Pelosi]] could lead to an economic recession, which [[Republican Party|Republicans]] could use to their advantage in the 2020 presidential election.

(01:47:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that election fraud occurred, citing [[Project Veritas Action|Project Veritas]] footage of poll workers guiding voters of a certain skin color.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(01:52:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes had predicted that [[State of Maine|Maine]]'s 2nd congressional district and [[New York]]'s 19th congressional district would be won by the [[Republican Party]].

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(01:53:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes voted for [[Kevin de León]] in the [[State of California|California]] [[Senate]] jungle primary in an attempt to disrupt [[Dianne Feinstein]]'s campaign.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(01:54:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes was interested in how close [[John Cox]] would come to [[Gavin Newsom]] in the [[State of California|California]] gubernatorial race.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(01:54:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes was interested in how close [[Kevin de León]] would come to [[Dianne Feinstein]] in the [[State of California|California]] [[Senate]] race.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(01:56:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[The Associated Press]], [[NBC News|NBC]], and [[CBS News|CBS]] still considered the [[House of Representatives]] competitive for the [[Republican Party]], contrary to [[Fox News]]'s call.

(02:02:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes believed a [[Democratic Party]] victory in the [[House of Representatives]] would not be as large as [[The New York Times]] was projecting.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(02:17:08) [[Robert O'Rourke|Beto O'Rourke]] was performing closer than expected to [[Ted Cruz]] in the [[State of Texas|Texas]] [[Senate]] race, which Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed partly to O'Rourke's name sounding [[Hispanic]].

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(02:23:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes blamed low [[Republican Party]] turnout in the 2018 midterm elections on white suburban women and argued that [[black]] voters had turned out strongly for the [[Democratic Party]].

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(02:24:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Democratic Party]]'s margin in the national popular vote for the [[House of Representatives]], projected at 8.6%, was enough for them to win control.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(02:42:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that [[Robert Mueller]] would release key findings of his investigation into [[Donald Trump]]'s campaign shortly after the 2018 midterm election.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(03:17:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes made a $5 bet that [[Nancy Pelosi]] would not become Speaker of the [[House of Representatives]] following the [[Democratic Party]]'s victory in the 2018 midterms.

### AF 2018-11-06 0277
(03:19:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the [[Republican Party]] lost approximately 27 seats in the [[House of Representatives]] but gained 3 seats in the [[Senate]] during the 2018 midterm elections.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began the broadcast of his show *[[United States|America]] First* on the evening before the 2018 midterm elections.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:00:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes titled the episode "the most important election of our lifetimes," a phrase he described as hyperbolic but reflective of widespread political rhetoric.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:01:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that betting markets gave [[Donald Trump|Trump]] the same odds of winning the 2016 election as they gave for the outcome of the 2018 midterms, approximately 33-67.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:02:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that an unidentified, cigar-shaped object had entered the solar system, with some speculating it could be an alien probe or light sail due to its unusual trajectory.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:04:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[United States]] had imposed new sanctions on [[Iran]], a story he argued was quickly overshadowed by midterm election coverage.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:04:47) [[Hassan Rouhani]] made an aggressive statement in response to [[United States]] sanctions, which Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized as a significant development.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:05:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a poll showing the [[Democratic Party]]'s generic congressional ballot lead had shrunk to 3%, within the margin of error.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:08:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed the [[Senate]] map, identifying [[Minnesota]] and [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] as the only "likely [[Democratic Party|Democrat]]" seats among 45 safe or likely [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] holds.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:08:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[State of West Virginia|West Virginia]]'s [[Senate]] race, featuring incumbent [[Joe Manchin]], had moved from "Leans [[Democratic Party|Democrat]]" to "Toss-up" territory.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:10:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that [[Mike Pence]] would act as a tie-breaking vote in the [[United States Senate]] if it were split 50-50, functionally giving [[Republican Party|Republicans]] a majority.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:10:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes considered [[State of Texas|Texas]] and [[Tennessee]] [[Senate]] seats safe for the [[Republican Party]], which would bring the party to 50 seats before counting competitive races.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:11:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Arizona]], [[India|Indiana]], [[State of Missouri|Missouri]], [[Montana]], [[State of Nevada|Nevada]], and [[State of West Virginia|West Virginia]] as the seven toss-up [[Senate]] races.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:11:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted [[Republican Party|Republicans]] would ultimately hold between 53 and 55 [[Senate]] seats, with a possibility of up to 60 if voter turnout mirrored surprising 2016 patterns.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:13:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that betting markets and [[FiveThirtyEight]] gave [[Republican Party|Republicans]] approximately an 85% chance of retaining control of the [[United States Senate]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:14:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that in 2016, [[Wisconsin]], [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], and [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] were not considered in play for [[Donald Trump|Trump]] until he won them.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:16:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that the [[United States Senate]] holds the power to confirm judicial nominees, a process [[Donald Trump|Trump]] had used to appoint many judges.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:16:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that a procedural change made by former Senator [[Harry Reid]] allowed judicial nominations to bypass the cloture rule, easing confirmations.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:17:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s travel ban executive order was initially blocked by federal judges but was ultimately upheld by the [[Supreme Court of the United States]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:18:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] had promised a comprehensive executive order on immigration, potentially to be issued around the [[time]] of the midterms.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:19:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that appointing conservative judges would shape [[United States]] law on issues like gun rights, free speech, and unions for the next 30 years.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:20:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s campaign strategy and policy focus for keeping [[Republican Party|Republicans]] competitive in [[House]] races during a historically difficult midterm cycle.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:21:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s renegotiation of [[North American Free Trade Agreement|NAFTA]] and focus on immigration during the [[Brett Kavanaugh]] confirmation hearings as key policy pushes.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:21:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the RealClearPolitics average showed [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] with a 7.5% advantage in the generic congressional ballot.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:22:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a report from the [[Brennan Center for Justice]] which concluded [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] would need an 11% minimum advantage in the popular vote to overcome [[Republican Party|Republican]] gerrymandering and win the [[House]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:23:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that the [[Republican Party]] gained a structural advantage in [[House]] districts after the 2010 census, when they won control of many [[state]] legislatures and governorships.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:25:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified a primary cleavage within the [[Democratic Party]] between the progressive left and the moderate establishment, exemplified by figures like [[Robert O'Rourke|Beto O'Rourke]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:27:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to [[Heidi Heitkamp]]'s [[Senate]] race in [[North Dakota]] moving from "Leans [[Democratic Party|Democrat]]" to "Likely [[Republican Party|Republican]]" as evidence of [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] losing support among the white working class.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:28:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested [[Joe Manchin]] could lose his [[State of West Virginia|West Virginia]] [[Senate]] race due to a Libertarian candidate spoiling the vote.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:29:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled the 2017 [[Democratic Party|Democratic National Committee]] leadership election between [[Tom Perez]] and [[Keith Ellison]] as a previous instance of internal party conflict.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:32:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] won the [[House]], [[Nancy Pelosi]] would become Speaker despite some [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] candidates distancing themselves from her.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:33:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]]-controlled [[House]] would launch multiple investigations into [[Republican Party|Republicans]], focusing on [[Russia]], the [[Brett Kavanaugh]] confirmation, and [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s tax returns.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:35:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] [[House]] would grind the administration's work on immigration enforcement and deregulation to a halt through investigative burdens.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:35:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s offer to bargain on [[DACA]] with [[Republican Party|Republicans]]' refusal to compromise, stating [[Barack Obama]] never made similar concessions to [[Republican Party|Republicans]] on [[Obamacare]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:36:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Barack Obama]] and [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] passed the [[Affordable Care Act]] without a single [[Republican Party|Republican]] vote.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:37:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes posited that if [[Republican Party|Republicans]] retained the [[Senate]], they could pack the judiciary, allowing [[Donald Trump|Trump]] to govern through executive orders that would likely be upheld by the courts.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:38:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] [[House]] victory could cause a market crash or recession, which [[Republican Party|Republicans]] could then use as a campaign issue for the 2020 election.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:40:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced plans for live election night coverage featuring himself, Vince from [[Republican Party|Red Elephants]], and [[Bryden]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:41:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he would vote for [[Bruce Rauner]] for Governor of [[State of Illinois|Illinois]] despite disliking him, because he preferred Rauner over the [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] candidate [[JB Pritzker]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:42:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes said he would vote for [[Democratic Party|Democrat]] [[Dan Lipinski]] in his congressional district but would not vote for [[Arthur Jones]], the [[Republican Party|Republican]] nominee.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:43:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes revealed he bet $300 on [[Donald Trump|Trump]] through a prediction market during the 2016 election when the odds were approximately 33%.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:44:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reflected on his personal faith, stating it was built on logic and a rejection of materialism rather than a direct spiritual experience.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:48:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that his family acquired a dog he is allergic to, which has caused him year-round allergy problems.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:51:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes responded to a question about [[Rishon LeZion|Tel Aviv]] falafel by joking he would have to ask his "[[Jews|Jewish]] producers."

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:51:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Ronald Reagan]] was not a true conservative, citing [[Ronald Reagan|Reagan]]'s support for free trade, immigration amnesty, gun control in [[State of California|California]], and policies that led to [[North American Free Trade Agreement|NAFTA]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:52:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes defined the 1980s [[Republican Party]] coalition as comprising foreign policy hawks, free-market libertarians, and the religious right, with only the last group representing true paleoconservatism.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:53:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed neoconservatives originated from left-wing [[Jews]] [[who]] became anti-Soviet hawks after the 1967 war.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:54:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[George W. Bush]]'s immigration policies, wars, and financial irresponsibility as examples of non-conservative governance.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:55:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes blamed [[Ronald Reagan]] for granting amnesty to three million immigrants, which he argued "ruined [[State of California|California]]."

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(00:56:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the [[Republican Party]] should be the "party of [[Donald Trump|Trump]]," not the "party of [[Ronald Reagan|Reagan]]."

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(01:00:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes responded to criticism of his new show logo by banning the commenter and stating he would not tolerate negativity.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(01:02:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted a plan to read [[Jared Holt]]'s old, embarrassing [[Facebook]] posts outside his [[house]] with a megaphone but decided against it for fear of legal repercussions.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(01:03:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes denounced [[Arthur Jones]], the [[Republican Party|Republican]] nominee in his district, as a "clown" and a "buffoon" for his [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] denial statements.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(01:04:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented on a clip of [[Tucker Carlson]] debating [[Ben Shapiro]], saying [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]] was "triggered by facts that didn't care about his feelings."

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(01:04:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged [[Ben Shapiro]] to a debate, claiming [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]] only debates "kids" and that such a debate would "be over" for [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]].

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(01:11:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes evaded a question about why he turned his camera off during a stream with [[Christopher Cantwell]], implying he did not want to be associated with him.

### AF 2018-11-05 0276
(01:13:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes addressed journalist [[Jared Holt]], stating that by writing articles aimed at ruining people's livelihoods, Holt was a "participant" in the conflict, not merely an "observer."

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the show's topic for the evening would be the 80th birthday of [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]] and an analysis of paleoconservatism versus neoconservatism.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:05:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the conservative movement had been dominated for 30 years by neoconservatives, whom he contrasted with paleoconservatives like [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]].

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:06:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Bill Kristol]] and the [[Weekly Standard]] as key figures in the rise of neoconservatism, which he said sidelined paleoconservatives after the 1990s.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:07:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed [[William F. Buckley|William F. Buckley Jr.]] helped purge figures like [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]] from the conservative movement.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:10:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes read from a column [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]] wrote after the [[Charlottesville rally]], in which [[Patrick Buchanan|Buchanan]] questioned the ideological foundation of the statement "all men are created equal."

### AF 2018-11-02 0275

(00:15:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that [[Ben Shapiro]] once tweeted at him, invoking [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]] in a criticism of Fuentes's views on free trade or [[Israel]].

(00:19:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a [[Prager University|PragerU]] video in which [[Dennis Prager]] argued that the [[United States]] was founded on values, not race or ethnicity.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:22:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented a whiteboard comparison characterizing neoconservatives as ethnically [[Jews|Jewish]], [[Israel]]-first, pro-free trade, pro-war, profit-driven, and agents of the devil, while characterizing paleoconservatives as [[Christianity|Christian]], white, [[United States|America]]-first, protectionist, non-interventionist, demography-focused, and aligned with God.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:23:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Bret Stephens]], [[Max Boot]], [[Ben Shapiro]], [[Bill Kristol]], [[Charles Krauthammer]], and [[David Horowitz]] as examples of [[Jews|Jewish]] neoconservative intellectuals.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:23:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that figures like [[Paul Wolfowitz]], [[Douglas Feith]], and [[Richard Perle]] had worked for [[Israel]] in the 1990s.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:24:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]] and the writers at [[American Conservative Magazine|The American Conservative]] magazine as predominantly [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] paleoconservatives.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:25:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Ben Shapiro]] supports [[Israel]] as a nation for [[Jews]] but opposes [[United States|America]] being a nation for white people, which [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]] justifies by calling [[Israel]] an ideological nation.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:26:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]]'s campaign slogan was "[[United States|America]] First," a phrase [[Ben Shapiro]] and other [[Jews|Jewish]] figures have called anti-Semitic.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:27:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the [[AIPAC]] and the neoconservative push for wars in [[Iraq]], [[Libya]], [[Afghanistan]], [[Yemen]], and proposed wars in [[Iran]] and [[Syria]] were done to benefit [[Israel]].

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:28:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that neoconservatives like [[Ben Shapiro]], [[George W. Bush]], and [[Mitt Romney]] favor free trade and oppose tariffs, in contrast to paleoconservative protectionism.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:29:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced an article [[Ben Shapiro]] wrote on March 12, 2003, criticizing [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]] and [[American Conservative Magazine|The American Conservative]] magazine for opposing the [[Iraq War]] and discussing the [[Israel lobby]].

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:39:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced he would cover the upcoming midterm election results live on his next show with commentators [[Bryden]], [[Jazz Hands]], [[McFeels]], and an invited [[Republican Party|Red Elephants]].

(00:40:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed the censorship of YouTuber [[Ralph Retort]], whose channel was demonetized and banned by [[Google|YouTube]] after a [[The Wall Street Journal|Wall Street Journal]] reporter contacted him for a potential hit piece.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:42:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Google|YouTube]] refunded $27,000 in charitable donations [[Ralph Retort]] had raised for [[St. Jude Children's Research Hospital]] during a charity stream.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:43:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the post-[[Charlottesville]] censorship campaign began with the removal of the [[Daily Stormer]], [[Milo Yiannopoulos]], [[Chuck Johnson]], and [[Andrew Anglin]], and later included [[Gavin McInnes]].

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:46:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Patrick Buchanan|Patrick J. Buchanan]] could only publish his books and appear on programs like [[Laura Ingraham]]'s and [[Sean Hannity]]'s because he was established; a new commentator with his views would be censored today.

### AF 2018-11-02 0275
(00:47:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that stopping political censorship by tech oligarchs would require [[state]] action, such as repealing Section 230 protections or pursuing antitrust legislation against companies like [[Google]].

(00:43:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that whether the [[Republican Party]] accepted or disavowed [[Steven King|Steve King]] would be a defining moment signaling control of the party's direction.

(01:25:00) Nicholas J. (01:37:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that meaningful biological differences between men and women necessitate different social roles, and that radical egalitarianism and the destruction of male-only spaces have contributed to social dysfunction and high suicide rates among men.

(01:40:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the explanatory power of the "[[Jews|Jewish]] Question" is overstated compared to more fundamental issues like the roles of men and women and religious versus secular governance.

(01:40:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes told women in the movement that if they were truly traditional, they should focus on having children to create more men for the nationalist cause instead of tweeting or podcasting.

### AF 2018-10-31 0273
(01:43:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes, in response to a question about whether truth inevitably prevails, stated that while God will ultimately prevail, in the short term, achieving good outcomes requires strategic planning and fighting, not just relying on righteousness.

### AF 2018-10-31 0273
(01:47:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that political rhetoric often involves tactical presentation, such as framing abortion as "pro-choice" or gun control as "common sense reform," and that the morality of such tactics depends on the scope of the evil one is fighting.

(01:50:03) A caller named Ian criticized [[Britney Venti]], calling her a "five out of ten" [[who]] benefited from a niche market of losers, prompting Nicholas J. Fuentes to defend her by saying he would never attack her looks despite her criticism of him.

(01:52:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he would never forgive a viewer named "Brainsake [[Blaze Media|Blaze]]" for griefing his [[Minecraft]] server by repeatedly destroying builds like "Europa," because the individual had not apologized for the [[time]]-wasting vandalism.

### AF 2018-10-31 0273
(01:58:20) A caller asked Nicholas J. Fuentes why he doesn't confront figures like [[Ben Shapiro]] at public Q&A sessions, to which Fuentes replied that it would be degrading, create an unlevel playing field, and likely be ineffective as [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]] could dismiss challenging questions with prepared answers.

(01:00:27) Nicholas J. (01:01:06) Nicholas J. ### AF 2018-10-31 0273
(01:02:08) Nicholas J. (01:14:04) A caller from [[Alabama]] asked Nicholas J. ### AF 2018-10-31 0273

(01:33:58) Nicholas J. (01:32:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he is against pure democracy and [[universal]] suffrage, believing it does not work, though he allowed for [[democratic]] elements at the [[state]] or municipal level within an overarching non-[[democratic]], theocratic framework.

(01:21:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a disproportionate [[Jews|Jewish]] presence in influential media institutions, combined with a demonstrated ethnic bias in favor of mass immigration, presents a critical problem for the [[country]]'s future.

(01:21:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that many [[Jews|Jewish people]], as articulated in the articles, see themselves as part of the [[Jews|Jewish]] nation and strangers in a strange land, which he argued is incompatible with being [[United States|America]] First nationalists.

(01:27:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized figures [[who]] cynically elevated [[Kanye West]] as a political game-changer to attract [[black]] voters to the [[Republican Party]], only for him to abandon politics after a perceived slight.

(01:28:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Kanye West]] fundamentally does not trust white people, a sentiment he believes is shared by many [[black]] people and explains low [[black]] support for the [[Republican Party]].

(01:29:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the civic nationalist fantasy is unworkable because tribal identity, history, and [[heritage]] matter more than abstract [[United States|American]] ideals, citing the failure to integrate [[Kanye West]] and the message of [[Michelle Goldberg]]'s article.

(01:31:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that [[black]] people vote 97% for the [[Democratic Party]], a level of uniformity he found statistically curious and indicative of a systemic racial alignment rather than incidental political preference.

(01:36:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned whether figures like [[Kanye West]] and [[Michelle Goldberg]], [[who]] advocate for love and demographic replacement respectively, genuinely love or care about the white people they are displacing.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266

(08:06:90) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Ezra Levant]] of The Rebel, [[Mark Levin]] of [[Blaze Media|CRTV]], and [[Ben Shapiro]] of [[The Daily Wire]] as examples of [[Jews|Jewish]] figures in conservative media.
