### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(09:20:70) During his debate with [[The Young Turks|Cenk Uygur]], [[Tucker Carlson]] adopted a conciliatory and reasonable tone, which Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized as a failed strategy that involved retreating on key issues like the migrant caravan.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(14:42:66) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s confrontational strategy differed from and was more effective than [[Tucker Carlson]]'s fence-sitting approach, noting that [[Tucker Carlson]] had bashed [[Donald Trump]] in the introduction to his book.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266

(18:26:96) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to the 2017 [[Charlottesville rally]], with its imagery of people with tiki torches chanting "[[Jews]] will not replace [[us]]," as the event that solidified the public definition of the "alt-right."

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(22:17:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that if [[Donald Trump]] successfully turned back the migrant caravan using the military, it would be a major political victory on immigration ahead of the midterm elections.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(24:35:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that overly aggressive tactics against the caravan, such as firing on migrants, could mobilize [[Hispanic]] voters and potentially help [[Democratic Party|Democrat]] [[Robert O'Rourke|Beto O'Rourke]] win a [[Senate]] seat in [[State of Texas|Texas]].

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(25:29:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that migrants passing through [[Mexico]] without applying for asylum there could jeopardize their legal asylum claims in the [[United States]], as economic hardship is not grounds for political asylum.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(26:43:88) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified a weakness in [[Tucker Carlson]]'s debate argument, pointing out that current [[United States|U.S]]. law requires the government to accept and process asylum seekers, often releasing them into the [[country]] due to a lack of detention capacity.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(30:13:64) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that after a second tax cut, the [[Heritage Foundation]], [[American Enterprise Institute]], and donors like the [[Charles Koch|Koch brothers]] would have achieved all their major legislative goals for [[Donald Trump]]'s first term.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(31:20:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed confidence that [[Republican Party|Republicans]] would perform well in the [[Senate]] during the midterms and that the [[House]] results would be a surprise, potentially even resulting in [[Republican Party|Republicans]] retaining control.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(32:12:74) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that if [[Democratic Party|Democrats]], led by [[Nancy Pelosi]], took control of the [[House]], it could ultimately benefit [[Donald Trump]]'s 2020 re-election campaign by changing the national mood.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(35:47:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Russia]]'s development of new missiles and provocative actions were a direct response to [[North Atlantic Treaty Organization|NATO]] expansion towards its borders, creating an inescapable security dilemma.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266
(38:22:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that the narrative of [[Russia]] collusion with the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] campaign, which dominated news for over two years, faded from public discourse following the [[Brett Kavanaugh]] hearings.

### AF 2018-10-22 0266

(00:16:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the dissident right should not join the [[Freemasons]], which he characterized as a subversive, devil-worshiping part of Illuminism.

(00:23:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[John Bolton]]'s opposition to the [[United Nations]] stemmed primarily from the [[United Nations|UN]]'s routine condemnation of [[Israel]], not a principled rejection of international norms.

(00:36:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that supporting abortion rights makes one complicit in murder, stating that doctors [[who]] perform abortions and mothers [[who]] seek them are executing the will of those [[who]] support the legality of the procedure.

(00:40:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Planned Parenthood]] and the [[Democratic Party]] as the biggest proponents of abortion, whom he described as agents of the devil.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(00:45:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a statistic that one and a half billion human beings have been aborted since 1980, referring to them as lost souls.

(00:48:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged the [[democratic]] peace theory, arguing that the correlation between democracies and peace is confounded by other variables like historical alliances and free trade agreements, not [[democratic]] structures.

(00:50:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that people are not generally against war and can be very pro-war, citing near-unanimous congressional support for entering [[World War II]] after the attack on [[Pearl Harbor]] as an example.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(00:51:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that liberal democracy lends itself to rule by financial elites and oligopolistic interests, questioning what moral authority a society would have if it achieved political ends through genocide.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(00:54:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that liberal democracy cannot thrive in societies with an average IQ below 95, citing failed [[democratic]] experiments in [[African continent|Africa]] as evidence.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(00:55:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to [[Turkey]] and [[Brazil]] as examples of failing states with systemic violence and corruption, arguing they are not functioning democracies and that [[Brazil]] is on the verge of authoritarianism.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(01:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that liberalism developed organically in [[United Kingdom|Britain]] over 900 years and cannot be successfully imposed on other nations through "soft power" like funding universities or subverting culture.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(01:01:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Egyptian]] military, which kept [[Hosni Mubarak]] in power, was a result of [[United States|American]] intervention and funding.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(01:02:42) Following the [[Arab Spring]], [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]] held elections and elected the [[Muslim Brotherhood]], which Nicholas J. Fuentes said was subsequently deposed by [[United States|American]] action.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(01:03:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that attempts to spread democracy in the [[Middle East]], like the [[Arab Spring]], fail because populations want to return to their traditional roots, as evidenced by the election of the [[Muslim Brotherhood]] in [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]].

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(01:04:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that over 50% of the population in the [[Middle East]] and [[North Africa]] is under 25 and consumes Western media, but argued this has not led to liberalism, citing the rise of [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] as a counter-example.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(01:10:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the rise of [[Viktor Orbán]], [[Matteo Salvini]], [[Vladimir Putin]], [[Xi Jinping]], [[Recep Tayyip Erdogan|Recep Tayyip Erdoğan]], [[Jair Bolsonaro]], and [[Donald Trump]] as evidence that liberalism is in retreat globally.

### AF 2018-10-19 0265
(01:11:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the proclaimed triumph of liberal democracy after the [[Cold War]] was a misnomer, representing instead the consolidation of power by a very small elite.

(01:16:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that a member of the [[British Royal Family]] was once removed for converting to [[Catholic Church|Catholicism]], while the marriage of a half-[[black]] [[United States|American]] actress was celebrated, which he saw as a demoralizing show of power.

Discovery|Time Warner]].

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:00:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted [[Ian Fletcher]] for an interview on his show.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:00:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his initial political views were shaped by [[Milton Friedman]] and [[Thomas Sowell]].

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:02:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that [[Patrick Buchanan|Pat Buchanan]] also wrote about the historical success of tariffs during the Industrial Revolution.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:02:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the [[American Enterprise Institute]], the [[Cato Institute]], and the [[Heritage Foundation]] as organizations pushing free trade dogma.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:06:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[China]]'s exponential wealth growth over 25 years coincided with a lack of [[United States|U.S]]. trade barriers and aggressive [[China|Chinese]] protections.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:08:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] frequently highlighted the disparity in auto tariffs between the [[United States]] and [[China]].

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:10:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that [[Paul Krugman]] argued insufficient stimulus prolonged the recession, a logic he compared to free trade defenses.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:12:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized [[Ian Fletcher]]'s argument that a trade deficit means the [[United States]] cannot get something for nothing and must pay via assets, currency, or debt.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:15:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Ben Shapiro]] and other free trade proponents often argue a trade deficit with [[China]] is beneficial, analogous to buying groceries.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:18:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced [[Ian Fletcher]]'s debunking of the myth that multinational corporations' location is irrelevant, noting most business is domestic.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:20:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that [[Barack Obama]] argued manufacturing jobs were not coming back and that [[United States|America]] had moved past that stage of the economy.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:23:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized a theme from [[Ian Fletcher]]'s book that free trade optimizes for present consumption but mortgages long-term wealth and strategic capacity.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:26:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared free trade to communism, suggesting it sounds good in theory but fails in practical reality, especially regarding strategic challenges.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:33:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes asked [[Ian Fletcher]] to explain [[China]]'s currency manipulation and its damaging effects on the [[United States|U.S]]. economy.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:37:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] had levied tariffs on approximately $250 billion of [[China|Chinese]] goods and discussed adding more.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:39:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that [[United States|U.S]]. allies engage in protectionist practices as bad as or worse than [[China]]'s.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:46:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that international climate agreements like the Paris Accords often squeeze the [[United States]] financially while giving [[China]] a pass.

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:46:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes asked [[Ian Fletcher]] to evaluate the strategic argument for the [[Trans-Pacific Partnership]] to strengthen ties with Pacific countries and counter [[China]].

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:48:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed, noting the [[United States]] foots the bill for military presence in countries like [[State of Japan|Japan]] and [[South Korea]].

### AF 2018-10-17 0263
(00:53:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded the interview with [[Ian Fletcher]], thanking him for his insights.

### AF 2018-10-12 0260

(00:09:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized fascism as an industrial-age, modern-era expression of monarchy that developed in southern [[Europe|European]], [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] countries like [[Italian Republic|Italy]] and [[Spain]].

### AF 2018-10-12 0260
(00:10:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that fascism failed to gain traction in [[United States|America]] due to its Anglo-Protestant culture of individualism and limited government, which is intrinsic to the [[country]]'s political tradition.

### AF 2018-10-12 0260
(00:14:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected political labels and doctrines, identifying as a paleoconservative [[who]] believes in returning to an older [[United States|American]] constitutional order oriented around Anglo-Protestant culture.

(00:16:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated there is a legitimate dialogue to be had between his element and self-described fascists or the classical alt-right, as long as the approach is reasonable.

(00:25:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified as a "med supremacist," ranking [[Italian Republic|Italians]] first, and cited [[Samuel J. Hyde|Sam Hyde]]'s joke that Slavs are only good at making vodka, AK-47s, and spacecraft.

(00:33:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that if [[Republican Party|Republicans]] retained control of the [[House]], [[the left]]'s reaction would be more radical than in 2016, as mainstream media has told them the government is illegitimate, citing calls for incivility from [[Hillary Clinton]] and [[Eric Holder]].

### AF 2018-10-12 0260
(00:34:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a [[Republican Party|Republican]] midterm victory would provide a pretext to "clean [[house]]" and appease the base rather than the [[Charles Koch|Koch brothers]], setting up a huge victory for 2020.

(00:48:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed the decline in [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] religiosity and liberal views to Vatican II, which he said oriented the church away from tradition and toward appeasing the modern world, losing true believers without gaining new ones.

(00:52:09) C. Fuentes's thoughts on Vatican II being misinterpreted.

(00:57:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that increasingly nothing holds [[United States|America]] together, as [[the left]] no longer believes in the [[United States|American]] creed, and with the loss of shared culture, race, and ethnicity, the [[country]] is being carved up between coastal progressive cities and the traditional heartland.

Fuentes expressed pessimism, citing the destruction in [[Chicago]].

(00:22:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that a major flaw in free trade theory is the national security risk of outsourcing manufacturing to potential adversaries like [[China]], a point international relations scholars see as an inevitable conflict.

(00:17:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that after apartheid ended under [[Nelson Mandela]], the [[South Africa|South African]] government began buying land from white farmers to redistribute to [[black]] citizens, increasing [[black]] land ownership from roughly 13% in 1994 to about 27% by 2015.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:32:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was leaving "a little window" for himself to potentially marry a 19-year-old when he is 30, hedging his stance on age differences.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:33:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that turning 20 meant people could no longer dismiss him as a "teenager," though it also removed an excuse to avoid physical confrontations with critics like [[Patrick Little]].

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:36:54) A caller asked Nicholas J. Fuentes when "Yusuf" (likely [[Yusuf]] or [[Joseph]]) would return to the show, praising his performance in a debate against [[Steven Crowder]].

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:37:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that the caller's guest had easily defeated [[Steven Crowder]] in a debate "behind enemy lines," remaining calm and collected, and promised to email him to return to the show.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:39:05) A caller named "Joe the Boomer" confirmed he had sent Nicholas J. Fuentes a St. Michael lapel pin as a birthday gift, which Nicholas J. Fuentes had opened early despite instructions not to.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:41:14) A caller named Ian Weber asked Nicholas J. Fuentes which [[SpongeBob SquarePants]] character he would be, leading to a discussion about which characters various political figures resemble.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:42:27) The caller Ian Weber suggested [[Ben Shapiro]] would be the character [[Plankton]], and Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed, citing [[Plankton]]'s short stature and evil nature, while also noting [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]]'s greed made him a hybrid of [[Plankton]] and [[Mr. Krabs]].

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:45:54) A caller named "Ascend Beyond" stated he discovered Nicholas J. Fuentes through his speech at [[American Renaissance]] and praised the show for being more entertaining and bold than mainstream conservative pundits like [[Joe Walsh]].

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:46:57) The caller "Ascend Beyond" noted that [[Ann Coulter]] was one of the few mainstream figures willing to engage with Nicholas J. Fuentes's ideas, and Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed she was "getting woke."

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:50:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that intellectuals like [[Jordan Peterson]] and [[Douglas Murray]] provide the components of racial IQ disparities but avoid the logical conclusion that mass non-white immigration undermines advanced [[democratic]] systems.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:51:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that hearing [[Stefan Molyneux]] explain the need for a 95 average IQ for democracy was a pivotal moment in his thinking, making sense of global disparities.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:52:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the decline in average IQ in [[United States|America]] and [[Europe]] is directly linked to the importation of tens of millions of people with lower average IQs, a point he said intellectuals dance around.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:53:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that while people accept biological differences in physical traits between races, they reject differences in mental biology, despite stronger heritability data for IQ than for homosexuality.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:54:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that racial realists like [[Jared Taylor]], [[who]] advocate for a separation of [[black]] and white [[United States|America]], are more merciful as they seek to prevent future racial conflict, not motivated by hatred.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(00:55:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that bringing low-IQ populations into a high-IQ society like the [[United States]] condemns them to a permanent underclass, whereas in their own societies they can rise and fall based on merit.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:00:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that racial egalitarianism is a naive and fatalistic ideology akin to believing humans can fly, and that ignoring fundamental biological differences will lead to disaster.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:01:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized postmodernists for inconsistently arguing that race is not real, inconsequential, or unclassifiable, while simultaneously basing identity politics on race.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:02:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that race, rather than ideology, will be the defining dividing line of the 21st century.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:02:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced commentator "Lucian" (likely [[Lucian Wintrich]]) [[who]] argued that low-IQ immigrants are needed to do certain jobs, a view Nicholas J. Fuentes called more sinister than his own as it creates a permanent underclass.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:05:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes used a chess analogy, comparing competing in a low-IQ society to playing an equally skilled opponent, while competing in a high-IQ society is like a novice playing grandmaster [[Garry Kasparov]], guaranteeing permanent loss.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:07:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended the act of ranking races by average IQ as a simple statement of quantitative, objective fact, comparing it to ranking countries by GDP, and contrasted this with the certainty claimed by climate change proponents.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:08:30) A caller named "Bobach" called in solely to wish Nicholas J. Fuentes a happy birthday and express his love for the show.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:10:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes congratulated Simon on his engagement, calling it the "first [[United States|America]] First marriage."

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:12:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes acknowledged the caller's question about whether Pope [[Francis]]'s potential complicity in abuse scandals invalidates his role as Vicar of [[Jesus Christ|Christ]], calling it "deeply profoundly disturbing" and admitting he was not a theological expert on the threshold for such invalidation.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:17:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that the stripping away of tradition under [[Vatican II]] was troubling, as [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] doctrine should be unchanging, and expressed uncertainty between Sedevacantist arguments and the need for concrete papal authority.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:19:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested having a future discussion on the show between a classical theist and a Sedevacantist to delve deeper into the questions of papal legitimacy raised by the caller.

(01:20:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded the call by praising the engaged couple, Simon and Alyssa, as a "white pill" example of young trad [[Catholic Church|Catholics]] marrying against the odds.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:22:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes ended the call-in portion of the show on an uplifting note, citing the engagements of callers Simon and "Brain6" as proof that "everybody will get a GF" and "we're all going to make it."

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed [[Richard Spencer]]'s article about a shortened timeline for white demographic decline, arguing that having educated people in positions of influence and resources is beneficial in any scenario, and that the movement could always revert to being "unemployable rallygoers" if necessary.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:39:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that [[Jared Holt]] likely had a troubled relationship with his father or experienced parental divorce, driving his behavior, and expressed a desire to "crack that egg."

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:40:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he does not hate [[Jared Holt]] or even liberals and Satanists, claiming he has no hatred in his heart and approaches conflicts as a [[Christianity|Christian]], but acknowledged that such messaging is often dismissed or attacked.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:41:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the establishment, by dismissing white concerns and ruining lives, is the primary institution "creating [[Nazis]]," radicalizing young men [[who]] turn to the internet for solutions after being called "racist" and "fragile."

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:50:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed his birthday cake preferences, stating he is a "chocolate guy" [[who]] dislikes fruity desserts, and admitted to having a growing sweet tooth as he gets older.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(01:57:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Europe|European]] immigrants were more assimilable than [[Hispanic]] immigrants because they shared a [[Christianity|Christian]] civilizational background, similar economic culture, and closer biological and cultural ties, unlike more distant racial and civilizational groups.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(02:00:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[United States]] was never a "100% white [[country]]" and that immigration is not inherently bad, but distinguished between controlled immigration and the current "invasion" of millions [[who]] do not assimilate.

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(02:09:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes outlined his short-term plans to focus on the show, save money, complete an associate's degree, and invest in real estate to avoid a traditional career, stating, "I don't wanna go to college, get a career, where I'm under the gun."

### AF 2018-08-17 0221
(02:11:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed a misplaced nostalgia for 1970s [[United States|America]], imagining a [[time]] of road trips, folk music, motels, and local culture without constant internet connectivity, which he viewed as more innocent and quaint than today's "soulless" commercial culture.

(24:16:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Stefan Molyneux]] received two strikes against his [[Google|YouTube]] videos, which were later reversed.

### AF 2018-08-16 0220

(30:11:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that corporate powers were tightening censorship screws on [[Facebook]] and [[Google|YouTube]] to eliminate alternative media and re-establish a monopoly on information.

(48:07:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized Protestant critics of the [[Catholic Church]], arguing that Protestantism's lack of a central authority like the Pope leads to doctrinal splintering and cannot claim moral high ground.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:00:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes returned to hosting his show after a trip to [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]], which coincided with the Unite the Right 2.0 rally but was planned independently weeks prior.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:02:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes met with influential contacts within media, the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration, and other areas in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]], gaining insights into the internal workings of the government.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:03:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a major problem for commentators covering the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration is the reliance on speculation about dynamics within the [[White House]] and [[White House|Oval Office]].

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:05:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]]'s administration was waging an unprecedented global trade war, exerting influence against countries including [[Turkey]], [[Iran]], [[Russia]], [[China]], [[North Korea]], [[Mexico]], and [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]].

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:06:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Lucian Wintrich]] was no longer working for [[The Gateway Pundit]] after appearing on his show, with figures like [[Jared Holt]] claiming the appearance led to his firing.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:12:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that people [[who]] share his ideological views hold positions within the [[White House]], media, elite universities, and think tanks, including some [[who]] previously worked in the [[Obama]] administration.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:13:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that social media censorship was the most urgent issue facing his movement, confirming this view was shared by his contacts in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]].

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:13:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the banning of [[Gavin McInnes]] and the [[Proud Boys]] from [[Twitter]] as evidence that platform censorship was accelerating beyond the earlier actions against [[Alex Jones|InfoWars]].

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:16:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that major social media companies like [[Twitter]], [[Facebook]], and [[Google]] are legally protected from prosecution for content on their platforms because courts deemed them conducive to free political speech.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:19:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that if social media platforms censor political speech, the legal rationale for their protections vanishes, opening them to lawsuits that could render companies like [[Google]] and [[Twitter]] insolvent.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:20:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the [[Republican Party]] and figures like [[Reince Priebus]] and [[Mike Pence]] as responsible for filling the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration with personnel [[who]] believe in free trade, open borders, and often dislike [[Donald Trump]] himself.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:22:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that a major long-term concern was the lack of a viable successor to [[Donald Trump]] and an infrastructure to institutionalize his reforms, warning that figures like [[Nikki Haley]] could reverse all progress.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:27:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes condemned what he called the "former movement" or "lunatic section" of the dissident right for advocating counterproductive actions like displaying antisemitic signs, which he argued harms the cause and misleads youth.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:30:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes condemned the Unite the Right 2.0 rally and its leaders, stating he would never attend, but acknowledged the event rhetorically benefited his side by highlighting [[Antifa]] violence.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:31:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that figures like [[Mike Cernovich]] and [[Gavin McInnes]] are lumped by the media into the same "alt-right" category as Unite the Right 2.0 attendees, necessitating public disavowals to avoid being targeted for censorship.

### AF 2018-08-14 0219

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:36:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that media outlets like the [[Washington Post]] and [[Vox]] acknowledged [[Antifa]] as the violent party at the Unite the Right 2.0 rally.

(00:38:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Jason Kessler]] approached him months earlier about Unite the Right 2.0, but he told him to "get away from me."

(00:41:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed [[Jared Holt]] filed the complaint that led to [[Alex Jones]]'s removal from [[Spotify]] and allegedly contributed to [[Lucian Wintrich]] losing his job at [[The Gateway Pundit]].

### AF 2018-08-14 0219
(00:43:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes described [[Jared Holt]] as a beneficial foil, creating a "hero and villain" dynamic that helps define his own public identity, similar to how the [[United States]] defined itself against the [[Soviet Union]].

(00:50:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted debating a "Satanists Against Fascism" protester in front of the [[White House]], arguing that if Satan is evil and they are against fascism, it implies fascism is good.

(00:14:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes juxtaposed the systemic anti-white hatred he documented with the simultaneous, coordinated deplatforming of [[Alex Jones|InfoWars]] from services like [[Spotify]], [[Facebook]], [[Google|YouTube]], [[LinkedIn]], [[Pinterest]], [[Stitcher]], and [[MailChimp]].

(00:17:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the political left's stated opposition to racism was a lie, as evidenced by its tolerance and encouragement of anti-white hatred, which he claimed revealed a true goal of targeting white men.

(00:20:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that within [[the left]]'s framework, only white men were capable of racism, citing the term "reverse racism" as proof that prejudice was only recognized when directed from white people to others.

(00:22:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented demographic projections indicating that by 2065, white people in [[United States|America]] would decline from 67% to 47% of the population, and by 2100 in [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]], white people would be only 24% of the population.

(00:24:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited global demographic projections that by 2100, the white population worldwide would be less than 1 billion, while Africans and Asians would comprise 83% of the global population.

### AF 2018-08-08 0217
(00:28:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the murder rate for white farmers in [[South Africa]] was 156 per 100,000, nearly double the rate in [[Honduras]], which he identified as the murder capital of the world.

(00:35:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized conservatives like [[Ben Shapiro]] and [[Steven Crowder]] for focusing on combating communism and socialism instead of what he viewed as the more immediate threats of demographic change and anti-white hatred.

### AF 2018-07-25 0207
(00:57:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the white picket fence nuclear family of the 1950s is preferable to the current [[state]] of people getting blackout drunk, killing themselves, using drugs, and facing a border invasion, criticizing those [[who]] view that past ideal cynically.

(00:59:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the bleak reality for many modern women as being alone, drinking wine with friends, $100,000 in student loan debt, jobless, and receiving calls from their mom, a stark contrast to the fun, kitschy life sold in commercials and shows like *13 Reasons Why*.

### AF 2018-07-25 0207
(01:00:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the narcissistic premise of *13 Reasons Why*, where a teenage girl kills herself to get the final word and blames everyone via pre-recorded messages, as symptomatic of the "me generation" and its pathetic cries for attention on platforms like [[Twitter]].

### AF 2018-07-25 0207
(01:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes, a [[Catholic Church|Catholic]], expressed disappointment in how the [[Catholic Church]] handled its pedophilia issue over 50 years, attributing it to the Church's attempts to accommodate the modern world since [[Vatican II]] and the failure of its human leadership.

### AF 2018-07-25 0207
(01:04:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended the [[Catholic Church]] by noting the rate of pedophilia is the same or lower than in other religious institutions, but identified the big problem as letting homosexuals into the clergy, linking it to the appeasement of modernism.

### AF 2018-07-25 0207
(01:06:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes understood the Protestant Reformation's grievances, such as the Church absolving sins for profit, but argued that faith requires believing [[Jesus Christ|Christ]] would protect his Church from doctrinal error despite human corruption.

Discovery|HBO]] series *The Young Pope*, noting that while the protagonist was prideful and not [[Jesus Christ|Christ]]-like, his traditionalist approach of closing the Church's doors to make it mysterious again was necessary to counter the post-[[Vatican II]] appeasement of a satanic world order.

### AF 2018-07-25 0207

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:00:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that the episode would feature two leftist guests, [[Vic Berger]] and [[David Sherratt]], marking the first [[time]] his show had hosted individuals from that political perspective.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:00:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes confirmed that [[David Sherratt]] would be joining the show via [[Google]] Hangouts, while [[Vic Berger]] was present in the studio.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:02:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified that a physical representation of [[Vic Berger]], not the actual [[person]], would be present during the show.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:03:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes initiated a conversation with guest [[David Sherratt]], [[who]] described himself as a leftist shitposter [[who]] primarily engages with right-wing figures on [[Twitter]].

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:05:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes raised the issue of payment processors like [[Peter Thiel|PayPal]] suspending accounts of right-wing figures such as [[Faith Goldy]], [[Red Ice]], and [[Millennial Matt]] for alleged hate speech.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:07:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned whether payment processors constituting an oligopoly have a public interest obligation not to discriminate based on political viewpoint.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:09:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered that not all on the right support unfettered free markets and argued the [[state]] has an interest in upholding the public good against viewpoint discrimination by essential financial services.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:10:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Twitter]]'s legal affirmation that it reserves the right to ban users for any reason, including based on race or other immutable characteristics, to illustrate the potential dangers of unchecked corporate discrimination.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:13:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged the distinction between social media and financial access, citing the [[United States|American]] legal precedent that money constitutes speech.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:14:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the combination of social media censorship, loss of employment, and denial of financial services creates an effective economic embargo against expressing dissident views, raising a question of public interest.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:15:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to the popularity of figures like [[Tucker Carlson]] and [[Donald Trump]] as evidence that nationalist views are popular and only face suppression from institutions like [[Peter Thiel|PayPal]].

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:17:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that all political factions, including [[the left]], use rhetorical tactics and framing, such as "pro-choice" instead of "pro-abortion," and that this is a matter of political strategy, not unique deception.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:18:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes used the example of [[Barack Obama]]'s 2008 campaign, which used moderate rhetoric to win states like [[India|Indiana]], to illustrate that all politicians use optics and rhetoric suited to their audience.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:19:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged [[David Sherratt]] to provide an example of [[Tucker Carlson]] misleadingly presenting himself, arguing that [[Tucker Carlson|Carlson]]'s advocacy for borders and national identity is straightforward.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:21:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that left-wing movements advocating "no borders, no nation, no deportation" and to "abolish [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]]" demonstrate a clear disagreement with the foundational views of figures like [[Tucker Carlson]].

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:22:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the popularity of figures like [[Faith Goldy]], evidenced by people's desire to fund them, contradicts the claim that their views are unpopular and only face suppression because they are widely disliked.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:23:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged the "loud minority" claim by comparing the reach of figures like [[Richard Spencer]] to the mainstream, uncensored platform of [[Bernie Sanders]] and his supporters.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:24:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned this equivalence, asking for examples of major verified left-wing figures like [[Keith Ellison]] being banned comparable to right-wing figures.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:25:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged this, asking for evidence of [[American Renaissance]]'s connection to violence, to which [[David Sherratt]] vaguely referenced eugenicist groups.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:25:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the ban of [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] for harassment related to [[Leslie Jones]] as an example of a non-violence-related ban, disputing [[David Sherratt]]'s claim that all bans were violence-related.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:28:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the de-verification and ban of [[James Allsup]] for an off-color remark as evidence of arbitrary enforcement, countering the claim of an equal playing field.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:29:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that left-wing figures like the [[Chapo Trap House]] podcast face no comparable barriers to funding on platforms like [[Patreon]], unlike banned right-wing figures.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:29:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended the substance of the "14 Words" slogan as innocuous and equivalent to statements like "[[Black Lives Matter]]," simply asserting the importance of securing a future for white children.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:30:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the substantive concern behind the "14 Words"—the demographic decline of white populations—is a real issue, citing a [[Dominion of Canada|Canadian]] government video celebrating a "beige" future.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:32:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes posed a hypothetical about similar demographic decline happening to [[black]] or [[Jews|Jewish]] populations, suggesting there would be a public outcry not present for white decline.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:34:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes shifted the hypothetical to the [[black]] race facing extinction through mass immigration and race-mixing, questioning if the response would be as cavalier.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:35:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized [[David Sherratt]]'s position as being "completely okay with the extinction of the [[Black]] race" through voluntary means, which [[David Sherratt]] did not dispute.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:35:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[David Sherratt]] and many progressives as fundamentally not caring if white people, along with their associated ancestry, culture, and tradition, disappear.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:36:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned [[who]] determines what is "good" or "bad" and cited the subtraction of religion over the past 200 years as an example of a change that has not necessarily led to improvement.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:36:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that contemporary society, despite material and technological advances, is in a worse place than 100 years ago, citing high suicide and drug abuse rates as indicators of social decline.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:37:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the current period of relative peace is an anomalous result of [[United States|American]] unipolar power and will change with the rise of [[China]] and other powers, leading to future breakdowns.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:37:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes conceded that material conditions like health and wealth have improved but argued that skyrocketing suicide and drug abuse rates reveal a deeper societal misery and lack of fulfillment.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:39:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed disbelief that anyone could view modern society as an improvement over life 100 years ago, aside from specific technological and medical advances.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:40:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged this by highlighting the homogeneous demographics of those nations, arguing their social systems only function effectively in culturally and ancestrally cohesive societies.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:41:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the progressive project of selectively discarding traditions has been a folly, leading to a disjointed society constantly creating new problems with each attempted solution.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:45:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested reinstating elements of arranged marriage to introduce more prudence and parental involvement into marital decisions.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:46:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the civilizational problem as a "buffet style" approach that discards foundational structures like marriage and religion, leading to a cascade of social dysfunctions.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:48:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the societies currently thriving and re-embracing tradition, like those in [[Europe|Eastern Europe]] and Asia, contrast with collapsing, anarchic Western [[Europe|European]] nations struggling with mass immigration.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:49:21) [[David Sherratt]], [[who]] is from [[United Kingdom|England]], challenged Nicholas J. Fuentes's criticism of [[Europe]] as dangerous, noting Nicholas J. Fuentes had never been to [[London]].

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:49:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended his characterization by referencing mass sexual assaults in [[Cologne]] and terrorist attacks at concerts as evidence of danger in [[Europe]].

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:50:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed [[United States|America]]'s higher murder rate to its larger population and higher number of [[Democratic Party|Democrats]].

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:50:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes asked [[David Sherratt]] if he believed the [[United Kingdom]] was a better society now than in the 1890s, to which [[David Sherratt]] definitively agreed.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:51:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that past societies lacked modern social dysfunctions like rampant degeneracy, family collapse, mass suicides, and drug epidemics.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:51:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes corrected him, stating the largest increase in drug abuse is from pharmaceuticals, not illicit drugs.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:52:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[David Sherratt]] of constantly moving the goalposts to avoid acknowledging a broad decline in social well-being over the past century.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:53:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that despite material progress, modern society fails to provide people with satisfaction, fulfillment, and a sense that their lives matter, reducing them to "soulless consumption units."

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:54:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded the discussion with [[David Sherratt]], summarizing their fundamental disagreement on values and progress, and promised a more focused debate in the future.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:55:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes reflected that debates with leftists often reach an impasse due to irreconcilable differences in fundamental values, particularly their materialist conception of improvement.

### AF 2018-07-20 0204
(00:56:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he usually avoids debates with leftists for this reason but found value in the public disagreement for the audience.

(00:19:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed he was unaware at the [[time]] of [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]]'s later conversion to what he termed the "Talmudic faith," though she still identified as [[Christianity|Christian]].

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:19:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]] was initially a strong supporter ("knicker") [[who]] turned against him to align with [[Ben Shapiro]] for attention.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:19:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed with [[Milo Yiannopoulos]]'s characterization of [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]] as a "jihadi war bride," extending the critique to all women in politics.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:20:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]] was moving to [[Los Angeles]] to work with [[The Daily Wire]], despite what he perceived as [[Ben Shapiro]]'s reluctance to give her a show due to her poor performance.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:21:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] invited him for an interview in [[Miami]] but canceled it after Nicholas J. Fuentes had already landed, which he interpreted as a sign that he was "too controversial for Milo."

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:22:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was now friends with many of [[Milo Yiannopoulos]]'s former associates, such as [[Timothy Gionet|Baked Alaska]], while others worked at [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]].

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:22:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes displayed images of the [[France|French]] World Cup team, questioning whether the players looked "entirely [[France|French]]" and suggesting the victory was effectively "[[African continent|Africa]]'s win."

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:23:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes connected criticism of [[Donald Trump]]'s [[Russia]] summit to a broader, flawed conception of nationalism based solely on citizenship rather than shared ancestry or culture.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:26:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed the November 2015 [[Paris]] attacks committed by the [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|Islamic State]] as a "con" of mass migration into [[France]].

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:28:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed the July 2016 [[Nice]] truck attack, which killed scores including children during Bastille Day celebrations, as another "con" of migration.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:31:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the [[Manchester Arena]] bombing at an [[Ariana Grande]] concert as an example of migrant terrorism, though it occurred in the [[United Kingdom]].

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:33:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged the pro-migration argument that migrants are needed to shore up [[Europe|European]] economies with low birth rates, stating they instead take welfare and increase crime.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:33:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Germany|German]] government statistics claiming 75% of refugees face long-term unemployment.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:35:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Germany|German]] government statistics alleging that 92% of the rise in violent crime was attributable to the migrant population.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:35:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the [[Cologne]] New Year's [[Adam Kinzinger|Eve]] 2015 attacks, where approximately 2,000 crimes including 600 sexual assaults were reported.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:36:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited sociologist [[Robert Putnam]]'s work to argue that diversity decreases social trust even within homogeneous communities.

### AF 2018-07-17 0202
(00:37:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded his analysis by stating the "cons" of migration—terrorism, crime, poverty, and decreased social trust—far outweighed the "pro" of a World Cup trophy.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:00:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes began the episode of his show, [[America First]], by announcing a focus on the recently concluded [[Donald Trump|Trump]]-[[Russia]] summit in [[Helsinki]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:00:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Donald Trump|Trump]]-[[Vladimir Putin|Putin]] summit represented a positive trajectory for [[United States|U.S.]]-[[Russia]] relations, which he considered vital given both nations' status as major nuclear and conventional military powers.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:01:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he had received numerous messages from people apologizing for their criticism of him during the [[Syria]] strike controversy and asking to be unblocked on social media.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:03:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]] represented a future for nationalist and right-wing politics within the system, providing hope that was absent with previous pro-amnesty and free trade candidates.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:05:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that premature declarations of [[Donald Trump]] as a sellout after setbacks communicated a pernicious message to right-wing voters to disengage from the political process.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:06:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared criticisms of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]]-[[Vladimir Putin|Putin]] summit in [[Helsinki]] to those of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]]-[[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]] summit in [[Singapore]], noting both were accused of legitimizing dictators but were preferable to military conflict.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:07:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[John McCain]], [[Lindsey Graham]], and [[Ben Sasse]] as "chicken hawks" [[who]] advocated for aggressive posturing against [[Russia]] but would not personally fight in a resulting war.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:08:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned conducting a Periscope stream critiquing [[Christian Picciolini]], [[who]] runs the [[organization]] [[Life After Hate]] and claims to be a former neo-Nazi turned liberal humanist.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:09:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes described [[Christian Picciolini]] as an obvious federal asset or agent, citing his [[organization]]'s mission to reach out to alt-right youth and his appearances on [[MSNBC]] and [[Technology Entertainment Design|TED]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:10:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes invited [[Christian Picciolini]] to appear on his show for a conversation, an invitation he claimed Picciolini saw but did not accept.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:13:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that [[Vladimir Putin|Putin]] employed power plays during the summit, including arriving an hour late and delivering a longer opening statement than [[Donald Trump|Trump]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:16:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the vast majority of media and political reaction to the summit focused solely on the issue of election meddling, ignoring discussions on resolving the [[Syria]]n [[Civil War]], preventing [[Middle East]] conflict, and denuclearizing [[South Korea|Korea]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:17:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the political establishment's fixation on [[Russian]] election meddling was a deliberate strategy to delegitimize [[Donald Trump]] and, by extension, the populist demands for closed borders, protectionism, and a scaled-back [[United States|American]] empire he represented.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:21:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Ben Sasse]], [[John McCain]], [[Lindsey Graham]], [[Bill Kristol]], [[Paul Ryan]], and [[Neil Cavuto]] as figures [[who]] publicly criticized [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s performance at the [[Helsinki]] press conference.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:22:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered [[John McCain]]'s criticism by referencing a photograph of [[McCain]] with members of the [[Syria|Syrian]] opposition [[who]] later became part of [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:24:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the confrontational diplomacy advocated by [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s critics was a product of a 25-year period of [[United States|American]] unipolar power and was not how diplomacy with adversaries had historically been conducted.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:25:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that accusations of "whataboutism" regarding [[United States|U.S]]. election interference were a [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] term from the [[Cold War]] used to dismiss valid comparisons, and that all nations pursue their own self-interest in foreign affairs.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:26:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a 2016 quote from [[Barack Obama]] where [[Obama]] argued that public shaming was ineffective against [[Russia]] because [[Vladimir Putin|Putin]] would simply deny meddling allegations.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:27:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the hashtag "[[Donald Trump|Trump]] treason" trended on [[Twitter]] following the summit, with figures like former [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] director [[John Brennan]] accusing [[Donald Trump|Trump]] of treason.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:29:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that skepticism of [[United States|U.S]]. intelligence agencies was warranted, citing their role in promoting false claims about [[Saddam Hussein]]'s [[weapons of mass destruction]] that led to the [[Iraq]] War.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:30:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the intelligence promoting the [[Iraq]] War was created by people working with [[AIPAC]] and [[Israel]], whose stated strategic interest was to remove [[Saddam Hussein]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:31:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited former National Intelligence Director [[James Clapper Jr.|James Clapper]], [[who]] admitted to giving the "least truthful" answer possible to [[Congress]] in 2013 when he denied the [[NSA]] collected data on millions of [[United States|Americans]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:32:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited former [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] director [[John Brennan]], [[who]] was found by the [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] Inspector General to have lied about the [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] spying on [[United States|U.S]]. senators investigating the agency's torture programs.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:33:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[James Comey]], [[Andrew McCabe]], and [[Peter Strzok]] as further examples of intelligence officials [[who]] had lied or demonstrated bias, bringing the credibility of the intelligence community's election meddling assessment into question.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:36:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he did not care if [[Russia]] meddled in the 2016 election, arguing that foreign collusion was commonplace, citing the influence of [[Mexico]] through mass immigration and [[Israel]] through lobbies like [[AIPAC]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:38:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes read a 2014 description of the [[AIPAC]] conference, which noted it drew over 15,000 attendees, two-thirds of [[Congress]], and featured speakers including then-Vice President [[Joe Biden]] and presidential candidates [[Donald Trump]], [[Hillary Clinton]], [[Ted Cruz]], and [[John Kasich]].

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:40:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he would condemn [[Russian]] election meddling only when two-thirds of [[Congress]] condemned [[Israel]]'s interference in the [[United States|U.S]]. election system and when both parties condemned [[Mexico]]'s interference.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:41:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the narrative of [[Russian]] collusion was solely about delegitimizing [[Donald Trump]] to maintain a status quo that benefits the political and media establishment.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:41:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a new transnational elite class, which does not share the religion, culture, or interests of the majority of [[United States|Americans]], has diverged from the public on issues like nationalism, mass immigration, and war over the past 25 years.

### AF 2018-07-16 0201
(00:43:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]] disrupts the elite class's status quo, which is why they seek to take him out through narratives of treason and illegitimacy.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:10:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to [[American Identity Movement|Identity Evropa]]'s activism using [[United States|American]] flags and the slogan "Make [[United States|America]] Beautiful Again" as an example of his "optics" strategy being adopted by others in the movement.

(02:11:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that groups like the [[League of the South]] and [[Heimfront]] had marginalized themselves or shut down, which he had either predicted or called for.

(02:14:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he did not know much about the [[Assyrian]] or [[Coptic]] churches or the claim that the [[Ark of the Covenant]] is in [[Ethiopia]], but affirmed the [[Eastern Orthodox Church]] as the closest sister church to [[Roman Catholicism]], despite its non-recognition of the [[Pope Francis|Pope]].

(02:16:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes reflected on his increased social media following, which allowed him to easily counter and embarrass critics from his high school days.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:17:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed with a viewer that the [[United States]] had more in common with nations like [[Hungary]], [[Israel|Poland]], [[Italian Republic|Italy]], [[Czech Republic|Czechia]], [[China]], and [[State of Japan|Japan]] than with Western [[Europe]], and that shifting alliances would be a viable way to curb cosmopolitan influence.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:18:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed a desire to challenge [[Steven Crowder]] at a "Change My Mind" event and stated he liked [[Stefan Molyneux]] but doubted [[Stefan Molyneux|Molyneux]] would have him on his show due to past online disputes.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:20:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the dissident right grew initially because it was fun and funny, and that people taking themselves too seriously with a joyless attitude was counterproductive to building a relatable and effective movement.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:21:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that contractual issues needed to be resolved before he could consider reconciling with former partner [[James Allsup]], but expressed openness to making amends in the future.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:22:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[China]]'s reported GDP growth was inflated by fudged numbers and debt-financed spending, making it a "paper tiger" and comparing its perceived rise to that of [[State of Japan|Japan]] in the 1980s.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:24:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes acknowledged [[China]]'s large, educated population and built-up capital gave it value, but noted its below-replacement fertility rate would be a future problem.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:24:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he liked commentator [[Jesse Lee Peterson]], finding him fun and smart.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:24:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that while there is nothing inherently immoral about race-mixing, it is impractical and selfish because it decreases marital compatibility and coherence for children, making it inadvisable.

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:27:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes used a hypothetical scenario of being married to a [[black]] woman and discussing a topic like the N-word to illustrate the tribal passions and lack of relatability he believes make interracial relationships problematic.

(02:28:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes refused to unblock people on [[Twitter]] [[who]] had been nasty to him, stating that if he blocked them, it was for a good reason and they did not deserve access to his content.

(02:30:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified understanding [[SpongeBob SquarePants]] references as a cultural marker for [[Generation Z]].

### AF 2018-07-12 0199

### AF 2018-07-12 0199
(02:33:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes described himself as loosely "neo-reactionary," agreeing with the movement's critique of excessive [[Enlightenment]] liberalism and its emphasis on hierarchy, authority, and power.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:04:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that figures like [[Ann Coulter]] and [[Patrick Buchanan|Pat Buchanan]] had successfully mainstreamed hard-hitting messages through platforms like [[Fox News]].

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:05:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that institutionalizing the reforms initiated by [[Donald Trump]] would require decades and reasonable, non-alienating advocates.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:06:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted a conversation with [[Stefan Molyneux]] about the necessity of providing spiritual answers, like [[Christianity]], to counter despair among young people.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:07:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the emergence of a movement of young, sane, reasonable, nationalist, conservative, and religious individuals, which he believed represented the future.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:10:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained his opposition to multiracialism and multiculturalism was based on the common-sense prediction of conflict between different groups competing for resources.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:12:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Donald Trump]] was scheduled to announce his nominee to replace [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] Justice [[Anthony Kennedy]] at 9:00 PM Eastern [[Time]].

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:14:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that rumors indicated the choice had been narrowed to [[Brett Kavanaugh]] and [[Amy Coney Barrett]].

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:14:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the nomination as a choice between prioritizing immigration, favored by the nationalist coalition, or abortion, favored by the [[Christianity|Christian]] conservative base.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:16:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized abortion as a horrible blot on [[United States|American]] history, worse than slavery in many cases, and akin to a genocide.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:19:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed with [[Jake Lloyd]]'s framework, stating that immigration ensures the [[country]] will not remain the same, which was what initially attracted him to [[Donald Trump]] over [[Ted Cruz]].

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:20:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled a past debate with [[Michael Gilliger]] where he defended [[Donald Trump]], noting it was a "blast from the past."

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:21:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if unchecked, immigration would lead to the [[country]] being "completely overrun by foreigners" within a couple of decades, making debates like abortion irrelevant.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:21:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested attacks on [[Brett Kavanaugh]]'s abortion record were opportunistic by Tea Party elements, and he believed [[Brett Kavanaugh|Kavanaugh]] was a very pro-life [[person]].

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:21:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that underlying all policy issues was the [[country]]'s demographic situation, and a homogeneous, harmonious population was necessary for advanced, good policy.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:22:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that betting markets indicated [[Brett Kavanaugh]] was the likely [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] pick.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:24:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[United States|America]] was the only first-world [[country]] to border a third-world [[country]], creating a massive wealth gap and a natural "diffusion" of people seeking resources.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:25:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] with a restrictionist stance on immigration was crucial for upholding future presidential actions on border security and processing.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:26:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the refugee ban cases as examples where district and circuit court judges acted as a "one-man veto" against [[the president's]] immigration authority.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:29:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes mocked the [[state]] of right-wing discourse being represented by debates between [[Ben Shapiro]] and "some blonde airhead."

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:30:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted seeing a television commercial for anti-AIDS medication featuring men kissing and an "obvious tranny," illustrating how social acceptance had rapidly expanded.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:30:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that in high school he held libertarian views on social issues but now believed a virtuous society was foundational, citing examples of drag queens in schools and pornography shown to children.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:31:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed hope that the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] could begin to rein in cultural excesses through legalism, though he acknowledged the culture was "already so far" gone.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:35:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes used [[Donald Trump]]'s immigration rhetoric as an example of successful messaging, starting by attacking "violent criminal illegals" and [[MS-13]] rather than leading with abstract policy.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:35:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized conservatives for conceding ground by reacting defensively to left-wing accusations on issues like immigration and social issues instead of launching their own forceful arguments.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:36:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the libertarian message of GDP growth and social permissiveness was insufficient, and there was a "tremendous appetite" among young people for substantive, wholesome social traditionalism.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:37:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested a "fourth religious awakening" of social conservatism had propelled [[George W. Bush]] and [[Donald Trump]] into office.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:38:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes shifted the discussion to the tactics of the upcoming [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] confirmation battle and its impact on the midterm elections.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:40:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes confirmed the [[Senate]] math was 50-49 with McCain out, giving [[Republican Party|Republicans]] a 51-vote majority with Pence's tiebreaker, requiring every [[Republican Party|Republican]] senator's support.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:41:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed [[Amy Coney Barrett]]'s confirmation prospects, noting she had received [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] votes nine months prior but might lose [[Republican Party|Republican]] votes [[Susan Collins]] and [[Lisa Murkowski]] over abortion concerns.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:42:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes described [[Amy Coney Barrett]]'s confirmation calculus as a "double-edged sword," potentially gaining some [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] votes but losing two [[Republican Party|Republican]] ones.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:42:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned vulnerable [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] senators like [[Joe Donnelly]], [[Joe Manchin]], [[Heidi Heitkamp]], and [[Claire McCaskill]] [[who]] might face pressure in [[Donald Trump|Trump]]-won states.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:43:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes raised the "nuclear option" of confirming with a simple majority, a precedent set by [[Harry Reid]], and asked [[Jake Lloyd]] about [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] threats of future court-packing in retaliation.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:46:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed court-packing was mostly a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] bluff, citing commentators from [[Vox]] and [[Vice]], and mocked [[Michael Moore]]'s talk of [[civil war]] despite liberals being disarmed.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:47:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted [[Brett Kavanaugh]] would be the nominee, get confirmed via the nuclear option, and provide a major boost for [[Republican Party|Republicans]] in the midterms by showcasing a consequential victory.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:48:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued [[Neil Gorsuch]]'s confirmation was an "unimpeachable" win for conservatives that even [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] acknowledged, and a second confirmation just before the election would be a "grand slam."

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:49:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the enthusiasm gap, where [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] were motivated by anger at [[Donald Trump]], could be countered by a big, unequivocal victory like a [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] confirmation to energize [[Republican Party|Republicans]].

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:52:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes joked about receiving nasty messages calling him a "cuck" whenever negative [[Donald Trump|Trump]] headlines appeared, only for his optimistic predictions to be vindicated months later.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:54:25) [[Jake Lloyd]] and Nicholas J. Fuentes commiserated about the "delayed gratification" of being vindicated after taking criticism for defending [[Donald Trump]]'s actions in the moment.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:57:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded the interview, encouraged by the movement of people like himself, [[Jake Lloyd]], [[Faith Goldy]], and [[Republican Party|Red Elephants]], and promoted [[Jake Lloyd]]'s [[Google|YouTube]] channel and [[Twitter]].

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(00:59:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Brett Kavanaugh]]'s background: a 53-year-old D.C. Circuit Court judge, [[Catholic Church|Catholic]], [[who]] led the investigation into [[Vince Foster]]'s suicide and helped draft the Starr Report urging [[Bill Clinton]]'s impeachment.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:00:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes highlighted [[Brett Kavanaugh]]'s work with [[George W. Bush]] on validating the 2000 election and his chairmanship of the [[Federalist Society]]'s religious liberty practice group.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:01:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes read from [[Brett Kavanaugh]]'s 2008 ruling in *Agri-Processor v. NLRB*, which stated combating the employment of illegal aliens was "central to the policy of immigration law."

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:02:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes read from [[Brett Kavanaugh]]'s 2014 ruling in *Fogo de Chao v. [[U.S. Department of Homeland Security|DHS]]*, which stated "mere economic expediency does not authorize an employer to displace [[United States|American]] workers for foreign workers."

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:03:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes read from [[Brett Kavanaugh]]'s 2014 ruling in *[[United States|American]] Meat Institute v. Department of Agriculture*, which upheld [[country]]-of-origin labeling to support [[United States|American]] manufacturers, farmers, and ranchers.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:04:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared [[Brett Kavanaugh]] "rock solid on immigration law," which was crucial for upholding future [[Donald Trump]] immigration actions that lower courts might obstruct.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:04:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted [[Brett Kavanaugh]] was 53 and could serve on [[Supreme Court of the United States|the Court]] for 35 years, calling his nomination "revolutionary" and "huge" for creating a "decidedly restrictionist, nativistic" court.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:05:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed the confirmation math, noting [[Republican Party|Republicans]] had 50 votes with [[Mike Pence]]'s tiebreaker, and the key would be securing votes from [[Republican Party|Republicans]] [[Susan Collins]] and [[Lisa Murkowski]].

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:05:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that propaganda painting [[Brett Kavanaugh]] as weak on abortion might actually help secure [[Susan Collins]] and [[Lisa Murkowski]]'s votes, unlike if [[Amy Coney Barrett]] were the nominee.

### AF 2018-07-09 0196
(01:06:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted the "nuclear option" would be invoked for a simple majority confirmation, likely after Labor Day, providing a "massive boon" for [[Republican Party|Republicans]] in the midterms.

(01:27:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]] was a major sponsor of the 1965 immigration bill and the "nation of immigrants" [[concept]].

(01:28:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[George W. Bush]], the [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]], the [[Southern Poverty Law Center|SPLC]], federal bureaucrats, and evangelical voters as groups with responsibility for mass immigration trends.

(01:29:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] support mass immigration for political gain, as immigrants overwhelmingly vote for their party.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(01:30:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that special interests and corporations support mass immigration as a source of cheap labor.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(01:31:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a "sizable [[Jewish lobby]]" has pushed for mass immigration because, as a cosmopolitan people, [[Jews]] find it easier to thrive in countries without a strong ethnic or racial identity.

(01:32:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that early proponents of anti-racism in the 1960s and 70s were [[Jews|Jewish]], believing it would help reduce anti-Semitism.

(01:34:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized mass immigration as the result of a confluence of interests among corporations, banks, and elites, rather than a one-dimensional conspiracy.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(01:57:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that rural life fosters self-reliance, physical strength, discipline, and traditionalism, which align with right-wing values, while [[city]] life necessitates compromise, tolerance, and public cooperation, lending itself to liberal values.

(02:00:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the historical conflict in the [[Rome|Roman Empire]] between the traditionalist countryside, represented by figures like [[Cato the Elder]], and the progressive urban centers.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(02:01:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[United States]] was an agrarian republic for a long [[time]], which contributed to its historically right-wing character, but that demographic shift to cities has changed its political orientation.

(02:53:18) [[Leo Pirate]] asked Nicholas J. Fuentes if he would support a theocratic [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] [[state]] in the [[United States]].

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(02:54:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that a global [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] theocracy would be preferable to the current degenerate [[state]] of the [[country]], but acknowledged it was impractical.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(02:59:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[state]] cannot exist in neutrality and that subtracting [[Christianity]] from public life creates a vacuum filled by other religions like humanism, leading to societal degeneracy.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:00:50) [[Leo Pirate]] asked how far Nicholas J. Fuentes would go to enforce catechism-based morality, contrasting the character of [[Jesus Christ]] with the historical actions of the [[Catholic Church]].

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:02:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that liberty of conscience is a fiction and that the [[state]] must enforce a positive morality, with [[Christianity]] being the preferable foundation to prevent societal decay.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:05:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended the papacy, citing [[Jesus Christ|Christ]]'s declaration to [[Peter]] that "upon this rock I will build my church" and [[Peter]]'s prominence in the Gospels and Acts as biblical justification for apostolic succession.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:06:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that Protestantism's lack of a central temporal authority leads to endless doctrinal disagreement and thousands of denominations, unlike the unified [[Catholic Church]].

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:08:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed this interpretation as a "wild extrapolation," emphasizing the pun on [[Peter]]'s name meaning "rock."

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:09:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced debater [[Jay Dyer]] and the early church fathers as supporting the primacy of the Bishop of [[Rome]].

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:11:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that [[Leo Pirate]]'s argument about commonalities actually proved the need for a single authoritative church to define doctrine.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:16:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Joseph de Maistre]], arguing that a [[state]] must be founded on "irrational" or religious authority to be stable, as authority based solely on reason can always be challenged.

(03:29:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that modern [[Judaism]], based on the [[Talmud]], rejected [[Jesus Christ]], and pointed to anti-[[Christianity|Christian]] sentiment in media as evidence against the idea that [[Christianity]] is a [[Jews|Jewish]] plot.

### AF 2018-07-06 0195
(03:30:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if [[Jews]] or other groups were using [[Christianity]] as "slave morality" to undermine the [[Kanye West|West]], they would be promoting it instead of attacking it in media and schools.

(03:37:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated this trend vindicated his view that people naturally turn to the church in response to nihilism and modernism.

(00:27:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced cases from the [[Federation for American Immigration Reform]] detailing crimes by illegal immigrants, including a drunk driver deported 15 times [[who]] severely injured a six-year-old, and an illegal alien [[who]] shot and killed [[Ronald De Silva]].
