(01:56:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a video by [[Carl Benjamin|Sargon of Akkad]] that contested coverage by [[The Young Turks]] comparing [[United States|U.S]]. border policy to [[Nazis]], to which he had also responded.

### AF 2018-06-22 0186
(02:22:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized a [[Cato Institute]] tweet and an article on HumanProgress.org that defined human progress through material gains like cheaper televisions, arguing this flawed perspective ignored rising suicide rates, drug abuse, and societal misery.

### AF 2018-06-22 0186
(03:31:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes, a [[Catholic Church|Catholic]], questioned whether the disruptive, profit-maximizing nature of [[capitalism]] was compatible with preserving tradition, community, and religion, citing the social decay correlated with economic globalization.

### AF 2018-06-22 0186
(04:37:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted [[Europe|Western Europe]] and the [[United States]], which experienced relative laissez-faire economics and subsequent social fragmentation, with post-communist [[Europe|Eastern Europe]] countries like [[Israel|Poland]] and [[Hungary]], which preserved stronger traditional and social structures despite material disadvantages.

### AF 2018-06-22 0186
(05:45:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected the libertarian premise that individuals are the best judges of their own wants, arguing that in the absence of objective moral truth, a neutral society naturally becomes disordered and evil triumphs.

### AF 2018-06-22 0186
(07:01:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes described a potential compromise between [[Hoppe]]an libertarianism and the traditionalism of [[de Maistre]], advocating for a society with free-market incentives and consequences but overseen by a [[vanguard]] enforcing just, objective rules to prevent moral decay.

### AF 2018-06-22 0186
(08:42:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded the interview by thanking [[J.F. Gariepy]] for a discussion on the philosophical differences underlying views of progress and tradition, noting the importance of these ideas for the future of the [[Western world]].

(00:23:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained the "scam" of child smuggling, where children brought by coyotes enter foster care, become eligible for [[DACA]], and can later bring their families through chain migration.

(00:24:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the [[Republican Party]] leadership wanted open borders as much as the [[Democratic Party]], citing their quick opposition to family separations.

(00:29:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Matteo Salvini]] in [[Italian Republic|Italy]] was talking about "cleansing migrants neighborhood to neighborhood" and that [[Angela Merkel]] was on the verge of a government shutdown.

(00:34:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[time]] could be on the side of nationalists as immigration progresses, making its effects more visible and felt in daily life, such as through gang violence or cultural displacement.

(00:38:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed that white [[United States|Americans]] and Canadians lack a strong tribal identity compared to minority groups, creating a void that figures like [[Donald Trump]] can fill by invoking national mythology.

(00:59:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that people seeking identity will not find it solely in race or nation but in the [[Catholic Church]], which answers fundamental questions about existence through its theology, rituals, and direct lineage to [[Jesus Christ|Christ]].

Fuentes proposed that [[Kanye West]]'s albums "Yeezus," "Life of Pablo," and "[[Kanye West|Ye]]" formed a new triumvirate similar to his first three albums. (00:39:48) [[Kanye West]]'s album "[[Kanye West|Ye]]" consisted of seven songs totaling 23 minutes, a concise format with [[little]] filler compared to "Life of Pablo." (00:40:44) [[Kanye West]]'s album "Life of Pablo" had a sloppy, delayed release with changing details, while "[[Kanye West|Ye]]" was seen as crisp and professionally mastered. (00:42:19) [[Kanye West]]'s personal life, including having children with [[Kim Kardashian]], contributed to a matured and happier sound on "[[Kanye West|Ye]]." (00:44:11) [[Kanye West]]'s support for [[Donald Trump|Trump]] did not prevent his album "[[Kanye West|Ye]]" from receiving largely positive critical reviews. (00:45:51) [[Pusha T]] had rumored that [[Kanye West]]'s album would be super political, but "[[Kanye West|Ye]]" contained minimal overt political content beyond a mention of [[Donald Trump|MAGA]] hats. (00:46:59) [[Kanye West]]'s album "[[Kanye West|Ye]]" had a low-key release without a major [[ad]] campaign, which Nicholas J. Fuentes saw as evidence of his genuineness. (00:48:25) The song "I Thought About Killing You" on "[[Kanye West|Ye]]" explores dark thoughts, with [[Kanye West]] stating "the brightest thoughts are often next to the darkest thoughts." (00:50:51) [[Kim Kardashian]] consistently defended [[Kanye West]] against critics like [[Taylor Swift]] and in media appearances, demonstrating loyalty. (00:52:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that artists like [[Kanye West]] have an obligation to explore ideas beyond spousal approval, similar to politicians. (00:54:51) Guest Jason "[[Party Goy]]]] ranked [[Kanye West]]'s top three albums as "College Dropout," "Late Registration," and "Graduation." (00:55:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes ranked [[Kanye West]]'s top three albums as "Graduation," "My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy," and "Yeezus." (00:57:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes considered the songs "Paranoid" and "Streetlights" on [[Kanye West]]'s "808s & Heartbreak" to be severely underrated. ### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced upcoming topics for his show, including the [[Samantha Bee]] controversy, new steel and aluminum tariffs on allies, and the pardon of [[Dinesh D'Souza]] by [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:02:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes attempted to find a free-market advocate to debate tariffs on his show but was unsuccessful, mentioning potential guests like [[Donald Trump]], [[Tom Woods]], and [[Thomas Sowell]].

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:04:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes directed viewers to sign up for his mailing list at NicholasJFuentes.com for an upcoming announcement about premium content and merchandise.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:05:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes promoted the official [[Twitter]] account for his show, [[United States|America]] First NJF, for polls, guest suggestions, and show-related content.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:06:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that free trade has been a dominant policy in the [[United States]] for about 30 years, supported by administrations from [[Ronald Reagan]] to [[Barack Obama]], including the passage of [[North American Free Trade Agreement|NAFTA]] under [[Bill Clinton]].

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:07:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that multinational corporations benefit from free trade by outsourcing labor and manufacturing to countries like [[Mexico]] and [[India]] while retaining access to [[United States|U.S.]] markets.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:07:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that major think tanks like the [[Heritage Foundation]], the [[Cato Institute]], and the [[American Enterprise Institute]], funded by billionaires like the [[Charles Koch|Koch brothers]], produce studies and policies that support free trade interests.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:11:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[United States]]'s largest steel and aluminum imports come from [[Germany]] and [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]], not [[Asia]].

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:13:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[United States]] trade deficits of $18 billion with [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]], $71 billion with [[Mexico]], and $92 billion with the [[European Union]] as evidence these allies drain the [[United States|American]] economy.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:13:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the [[United States]]'s trade relationships with allies to its relationship with [[China]], which has a $600 billion trade deficit and allegedly steals $500 billion in intellectual property annually.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:14:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized figures like [[Bill Kristol]] for arguing that [[Donald Trump]] was abandoning longtime allies like [[Europe|European]] partners [[who]] take economic and military advantage of the [[United States]].

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:15:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that countries like [[Germany]], [[France]], [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]], and [[Mexico]] protect their own industries with trade barriers while demanding unfettered access to [[United States|U.S.]] markets.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:16:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes supported [[Donald Trump]]'s rhetoric about "[[fair]] trade, not free trade," emphasizing the need for reciprocity and protecting [[United States|American]] manufacturing and raw materials for national security.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:17:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the free trade argument of "comparative advantage," stating it wrongly assumes [[United States|American]] workers can transition from manufacturing to high-tech jobs.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:18:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the classical economic [[concept]] of comparative advantage, as described by [[David Ricardo]] and [[Adam Smith]], is invalid in the 21st century due to the high mobility of capital and technology.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:19:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that free trade between states like [[State of Illinois|Illinois]] and [[India|Indiana]] is logical, but free trade with [[China]] creates unfair competition due to vastly different labor costs and regulations.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:22:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that modern international trade, unlike the 18th-century "goods for goods" model, involves complex financial instruments, with trade deficits reconciled by selling assets like real estate, stocks, and bonds.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:24:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that the [[United States]]'s $600 billion annual trade deficit with [[China]] is offset by selling [[China]] [[United States|American]] assets, including real estate, corporate equities, and debt, which [[China]] then uses to manipulate currency exchange rates.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:26:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited that foreign investors owned 20% of [[United States|U.S.]] corporate equities in 2018, up from 12% in 2007, and purchased $100 billion in [[United States|American]] real estate in 2015 alone.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:27:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[China]]'s exports to the [[United States]] as low-quality "cheap consumer goods" and agricultural products, in exchange for which the [[United States]] gives up valuable assets and incurs debt.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:29:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that foreigners owned over 44% of [[United States|U.S.]] public debt, totaling $6.3 trillion, and 29% of corporate bonds, requiring the [[United States]] to pay trillions in interest annually.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:30:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Ben Shapiro]] as one of the most vocal defenders of free trade ideology, with whom he had a feud originating from a debate about free trade on [[Twitter]].

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:31:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that tariffs are an avoidable tax and that buying [[United States|American]] products is the solution to avoiding tariffs on foreign goods like [[Germany|German]] cars.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:33:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his feud with [[Ben Shapiro]] began over a debate on free trade on [[Twitter]], where [[Ben Shapiro]] criticized his views as ignorant.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:34:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that tariffs provide diplomatic leverage, citing how [[Donald Trump]] used trade pressure on [[China]] to gain cooperation on [[North Korea]] and could use it on the [[European Union]] regarding [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:35:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]] uses tariffs comprehensively, linking trade concessions to diplomatic goals, such as easing tariffs if the [[European Union]] cooperates on [[Iran]] sanctions.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:37:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the [[United States]] subsidizes the [[European Union]]'s defense and economies and should use its power to call in favors and pursue [[United States|American]] interests.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:39:51) [[Samantha Bee]], on her show *Full Frontal*, called [[Ivanka Trump Kushner|Ivanka Trump]] a "feckless cunt," which Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted with the swift cancellation of [[Roseanne Barr]]'s show for a racist tweet days earlier.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:42:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Samantha Bee]]'s comment was scripted and approved by multiple layers of production, unlike [[Roseanne Barr]]'s impulsive tweet, yet only resulted in an apology and two advertisers, [[AutoTrader]] and [[State Farm]], pulling out.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:44:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized conservatives [[who]] defended [[ABC News|ABC]]'s cancellation of [[Roseanne Barr]]'s show, arguing that [[the left]] did not reciprocate this "good faith" when [[Samantha Bee]] faced controversy.

(00:46:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that the political left, which controls major media, [[Los Angeles|Hollywood]], and multinational corporations, had engaged in a [[Twitter]] campaign to normalize pedophilia that week, demonstrating a lack of moral equivalency.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:47:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes described figures like [[Ben Shapiro]] as "controlled opposition" [[who]] exist to prevent the right from organizing against real evil and to promote compromise on social issues.

(00:49:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed hope that [[Kanye West]]'s new album would be released as announced on June 1st, stating that his previous album *Life of Pablo* "changed my life."

(00:52:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes responded to a viewer's question about repetitive content in right-wing media by stating his show aims to provide fresh takes on current events through extensive reading and context.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:54:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes, in response to a question about privatizing the [[Federal Reserve]], expressed skepticism about denationalizing currency but criticized the [[Federal Reserve]]'s arbitrary control, suggesting a predictable formula tied to economic production as an alternative.

(00:57:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he does not encourage violence against women under any circumstances, including self-defense, emphasizing that "we're gentlemen."

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(00:59:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes, in response to a question about nationalism without free markets, argued that the [[United States]] was never a free trade [[country]] until the 1990s and was historically funded by tariffs until the 16th Amendment introduced the income tax in 1913.

(01:00:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Alexander Hamilton]]'s financial arrangements post-Revolutionary War as a genius feat that made [[United States|America]] a creditor nation, contrasting it with current policies he views as economically detrimental.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(01:01:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes supported government-funded infrastructure projects like railroads and the [[Hoover Dam]] as historically useful areas of government action that can foster nationalism.

### AF 2018-05-31 0175
(01:01:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for killing regulations and cutting taxes to promote internal economic freedom and competition, while maintaining tariffs to protect against unfair international competition from countries like [[China]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a debate episode on regime change in [[Iran]] with guest [[Jacob Wohl]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:00:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] withdrew from the [[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action|Iran nuclear deal]], coinciding with military strikes between [[Israel]] and [[Iran]] around [[Syria]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:00:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes described a situation where [[Saudi Arabia]], [[Israel]], and the [[United States]] were being pushed toward direct military conflict with [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:01:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Jacob Wohl]] had previously appeared on his show to debate aid for [[Israel]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:06:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a war in [[Iran]] would be almost unwinnable for the [[United States]], citing its difficult terrain, population of over 80 million, and large military.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:11:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered that past regime change efforts in [[Iraq]] and [[Libya]] based on similar assumptions about popular support failed and led to instability and the rise of groups like [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:12:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a sanctions regime against [[Iran]] would be far less costly to the [[United States]] than a military engagement, citing the prolonged occupation and insurgency that followed the [[Iraq]] war.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:13:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged the characterization of [[Iran]] as an apocalyptic regime, citing its pragmatic decision to end the [[Iran]]-[[Iraq]] War and its moderation after the Khobar Towers bombings when faced with [[United States|U.S.]] military reprisal.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:19:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a [[United States|U.S.]] attack on [[Iran]] would likely rally the population against [[United States|America]] and vindicate hardline Islamic views, leading to a worse outcome, as seen in [[Libya]] and [[Syria]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:21:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the 1953 [[United States|U.S.]]-backed coup in [[Iran]], noting that the installed [[Shah]]'s regime lasted only 25 years before collapsing.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:26:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that removing the [[Iran]]ian regime was not a major [[United States|U.S.]] strategic interest, but rather served [[Israel]]'s interest, and that a nuclear-armed [[Iran]] would not fundamentally change the regional balance of power.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:28:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel]] possesses an arsenal of 80 to 200 nuclear warheads, according to sources like the Federation of [[United States|American]] Scientists and former officials like [[Colin Powell]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:32:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered that in the history of nuclear proliferation, no nuclear power has ever given fissile material to non-[[state]] actors, making the urgency of war overblown.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:33:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes traced regional destabilization and the [[Iran]]-[[Saudi Arabia]] proxy conflict to the power vacuum created by the [[United States|U.S.]] removal of [[Saddam Hussein]] in [[Iraq]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:34:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes attributed the push for wars in [[Iraq]], [[Syria]], and potentially [[Iran]] to neoconservatives in the [[George W. Bush|Bush]] administration, whom he identified as acting to secure [[Israel]]'s northern border.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:37:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the 1996 "Clean Break" memo, written by figures like [[Douglas Feith]] and [[David Wurmser]] for [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Netanyahu]], as a document outlining a strategy for [[Israel]] to secure its [[region]] by removing [[Iraq]], [[Syria]], and [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:40:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the Office of Special Plans, established by figures from the "Clean Break" memo, fabricated evidence linking [[Saddam Hussein]] to [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|Al-Qaeda]] and passed classified [[United States|U.S.]] intelligence to [[AIPAC]] and the [[Israel]]i government.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:41:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that when advocates say war with [[Iran]] is in [[United States|America]]'s interest, one must be highly skeptical because it is demonstrably in [[Israel]]'s interest.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:48:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Iran]]'s regional proxies like [[Hezbollah]] and [[Hamas]] have not conducted terror attacks on [[United States|U.S.]] soil, and their interests are confined to the [[Middle East]].

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:49:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that allies like [[Saudi Arabia]], the [[United Arab Emirates|UAE]], and [[Qatar]] have given tacit support to [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]], and [[Israel]] conducts aggressive espionage on [[United States|U.S.]] soil, yet face no calls for regime change.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:56:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for a return to diplomacy and a deal like the 2015 [[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action]] ([[Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action|JCPOA]]), which he said had been verified by the [[International Atomic Energy Agency|IAEA]] and had made it impossible for [[Iran]] to develop a weapon while in compliance.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(00:58:58) [[Jacob Wohl]] summarized the central disagreement as whether a nuclear [[Iran]] is an existential threat to the [[United States]], a premise he accepted and Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected.

### AF 2018-05-09 0161
(01:01:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded the debate, stating that while he disagreed with [[Jacob Wohl]]'s position, he was a reasonable and civil spokesperson for it.

(00:24:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that pro-[[Israel]] lobbies like [[AIPAC]] should be registered as foreign agents and some shut down for espionage, while acknowledging massive and disproportionate [[Israel|Israeli]] influence on [[United States|U.S]]. policy.

(00:24:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the foreign policy debate over the [[Iran deal]] as a conflict between two sets of hostile influences: one from [[Saudi Arabia]] and [[Israel]] on the [[State of Illinois|State]] and Defense Departments, and another from [[Obama]], [[John Kerry]], the [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]], the [[United Nations|UN]], and [[Europe|European]] countries.

### AF 2018-05-08 0160
(00:27:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed concern that [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s rhetoric contrasting the "[[Iran|Iranian]] people" with their government mirrored pre-invasion propaganda used against [[Iraq]], signaling a potential justification for occupation or regime change.

### AF 2018-05-08 0160
(00:28:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes interpreted [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s statement as an overture for a new deal with [[Iran]], outlining conditions and offering a path to prosperity similar to the approach taken with [[North Korea]].

### AF 2018-05-08 0160
(00:30:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes outlined [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s deal-making strategy with [[North Korea]], which began with provocations, escalated with threats and military drills, and ultimately brought [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]] to the negotiating table.

### AF 2018-05-08 0160
(00:31:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[North Korea]]'s isolation, with [[China]] as its sole major lifeline, made it susceptible to pressure, unlike [[Iran]], which has economic ties to [[Europe]], [[China]], and [[Russia]].

### AF 2018-05-08 0160
(00:32:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that adversaries of [[Iran]], namely [[Israel]] under the [[Likud]] party and [[Saudi Arabia]] under [[Mohammed bin Salman]], would actively work to sabotage any diplomatic process or deal.

### AF 2018-05-08 0160
(00:34:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[AIPAC]], neoconservatives in the [[U.S. Department of State|State Department]], commentators like [[Bret Stephens]] and [[Charles Krauthammer]], and media figures like [[Ben Shapiro]] as actors pushing for war with [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-05-08 0160
(00:35:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Iran]]'s domestic situation, including economic struggles, pollution, healthcare issues, and regular anti-government protests, made its regime precarious and potentially susceptible to external pressure.

(00:01:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted [[Alex Jones|Alex]] on his previous show to discuss practical activism strategies for young people and high school students.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:02:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes appeared on [[JF Garaipe]]'s stream with [[Richard Spencer|Spencer]], which became the number 10 stream worldwide on [[Google|YouTube]].

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:04:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes encountered technical difficulties preventing him from streaming on [[Facebook]], a platform that charges $15 monthly for the service.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:05:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes highlighted an article about himself published by [[Right Wing Watch]], written by research associate [[Jared Holt]].

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:09:11) [[Right Wing Watch]] published an article titled "At [[American Renaissance|AmRen]] 2018, White Nationalists Boast About Grooming the Next Generation of Racists," featuring Nicholas J. Fuentes prominently.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:10:08) The [[Right Wing Watch]] article used a screen grab of Nicholas J. Fuentes from a video he made for [[Boston University]]'s *BU Today* paper during the 2016 election.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:11:20) The [[Right Wing Watch]] article described Nicholas J. Fuentes as a "white nationalist podcaster" [[who]] spoke after [[Jared Taylor]] at the [[American Renaissance]] event.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:12:29) The [[Right Wing Watch]] article accused Nicholas J. Fuentes of making anti-Semitic and racist remarks and supporting anti-Semitic candidates like [[Wisconsin]]'s [[Paul Nealon]].

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:13:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes was seen mingling with members of the New Right at [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]] earlier in the year, according to the [[Right Wing Watch]] article.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:14:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes observed at the [[American Renaissance]] conference that white advocacy displays a generational pattern, with older attendees remembering a lost white [[United States|America]] and younger ones nostalgic for a homeland they never experienced.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:14:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes was warned by friends in the established conservative movement that attending the [[American Renaissance]] conference would be bad for his career, but he considered the cause more important.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:16:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that hit pieces from organizations like [[Right Wing Watch]], funded by left-wing billionaires, work their way from outlets like [[Media Matters]] and [[BuzzFeed]] to mainstream press like [[NBC News|NBC]].

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:17:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected the label of racist, arguing his discussions on racial differences, such as average IQ disparities between groups, are about empirical data and the impact of immigration, not racial superiority.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:18:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that racial differences are evident in various fields, such as the overrepresentation of [[black]] athletes in the [[National Basketball Association|NBA]] or the superior senses of [[Aboriginal]] peoples, but do not imply a hierarchy of races.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:19:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes distinguished his arguments from media portrayals for the benefit of sensible, normal people [[who]] can watch his show and judge its factual, empirical content for themselves.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:20:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Jared Holt]]'s profession of monitoring and writing about figures like himself as a "boring, lame" lifestyle, contrasting it with generative jobs like plumbing or electrical work.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:22:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes shifted the show's focus to cultural news, including developments regarding [[Kanye West]] and the television show *Last Man Standing*.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157

(00:26:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the December 2017 [[Alabama]] special [[Senate]] election, where [[black]] turnout comprised 29% of voters—a higher proportion than in the 2012 [[Obama]] election—as evidence of [[Democratic Party|Democrats]]' reliance on strong minority turnout.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:27:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that even a slight shift in [[black]] voting patterns, potentially influenced by figures like [[Kanye West]], could jeopardize [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] strategies in swing states like [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]], [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]], and [[State of Ohio|Ohio]].

(00:29:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted [[Kanye West]]'s effective engagement of [[black]] audiences with the failed outreach of traditional [[Republican Party|Republican]] figures like [[Dinesh D'Souza]] through movies and [[Fox News]] appearances.

(00:31:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[black]] voters have no political leverage when they vote 95% for [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] every election, creating a moral hazard for [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] politicians.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:38:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the return of *Last Man Standing* as evidence of incremental cultural progress, arguing that victories in the culture war build upon each other, similar to how [[the left]] built its influence.

### AF 2018-05-03 0157
(00:39:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the political right must embrace incrementalism and practical compromises to achieve measurable victories, rather than insisting on ideological purity that leads to failure.

### AF 2018-04-30 0154

(00:05:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes described [[American Renaissance]] as the gold standard of dissident right conferences, praising its smooth operation and contrasting it with chaotic events like those involving [[Richard Spencer]] at [[Michigan State University]]. (00:06:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the termination of his [[America First]] show's relationship with [[Maker Support]] due to the platform withholding funds and failing to communicate after its payment processor, [[Stripe]], placed an indefinite hold on all payouts. (00:08:30) The host of the [[Google|YouTube]] channel [[The Quartering]], [[who]] knew the [[Maker Support]] CEO personally, attempted to intervene in the payment dispute but received no answer. (00:10:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes had become the top creator on [[Maker Support]] with over 250 subscribers before the payment issues began. (00:12:23) A user named Dirt Kevin in Nicholas J. Fuentes's [[Discord]] server made a controversial joke, leading a female college friend of Fuentes to complain and subsequently contact [[Richard Spencer]] and the [[National Policy Institute]] with accusations that Fuentes was condoning sexual assault. (00:15:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed several women, including [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]], [[Emily Faulkner]], and [[Tara McCarthy]], as sources of career-damaging "subterfuge" against him and others in his movement. (00:16:02) [[Israel]] conducted a massive airstrike with bunker-busting bombs on military sites in [[Syria]]'s [[Hama]] and [[Aleppo]] provinces, killing 11 [[Iran|Iranians]] and 15 [[Syria|Syrians]] and destroying approximately 200 missiles, which registered as a 2.6 magnitude earthquake. (00:17:48) [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi]] presented a dossier in English titled "[[Iran]] Lied," claiming [[Israel Defense Forces|Mossad]] operatives stole over 100,000 files from a warehouse in [[Tehran]] in 2017, which he said provided conclusive evidence that [[Iran]] was pursuing a clandestine nuclear weapons program in violation of the 2015 deal. (00:19:19) [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi]] had for months been delivering English-language addresses from his desk aimed at a Western audience, calling for the liberation of the [[Iran]]ian people from their regime. (00:22:48) The 1996 "Clean Break Memo," authored by [[Richard Perle]], [[Douglas Feith]], and [[David Wurmser]] for then-Prime Minister [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi]], outlined a new [[Israel|Israeli]] strategy to "transcend" its regional problems by removing [[Iraq]]'s [[Ba'athist]] regime as a prelude to rolling back [[Syria]] and [[Iran]] to secure [[Israel]]'s northern border from [[Hezbollah]]. (00:27:33) [[Richard Perle]] and [[Douglas Feith]], authors of the Clean Break Memo, later served in the [[United States]] [[U.S. Department of Defense|Defense Department]] under the [[George W. Bush]] administration and helped establish the [[Office of Special Plans]]. (00:28:28) The [[Office of Special Plans]], according to *The Guardian*, served as an unfiltered conduit for intelligence from the office of [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Israeli Prime Minister]] [[Ariel Sharon]], bypassing [[Israel Defense Forces|Mossad]] to provide the [[George W. Bush|Bush administration]] with alarmist reports on [[Saddam Hussein]]'s [[Iraq]]. (00:30:47) [[Douglas Feith]] authorized [[Israel|Israeli]] visitors to the [[Office of Special Plans]] without requiring them to fill out standard security forms. (00:34:55) [[Israel]] possesses an estimated 200 nuclear warheads, developed through an undeclared program that allegedly used uranium stolen from the [[United States]] and [[Europe]], and has never signed the [[Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty]]. (00:35:44) The [[Stuxnet]] cyberweapon, reportedly developed by [[Israel]], successfully targeted [[Iran]]'s nuclear infrastructure because [[Israel]] had stolen the same [[Germany|German]] blueprints for the equipment that [[Iran]] acquired via [[Pakistan|Pakistani]] scientist [[A.Q. Khan]]. (00:37:19) [[Donald Trump]] faces a May 12 deadline to decide whether to reimpose sanctions on [[Iran]], a move urged by [[Israel]] but opposed by [[Europe|European]] leaders like [[Emmanuel Macron]] and [[Angela Merkel]] [[who]] recently visited to preserve the nuclear deal. (00:40:08) A full-scale invasion and occupation of [[Iran]] to dismantle its nuclear program would require an estimated 250,000 [[United States]] soldiers and result in a longer, bloodier conflict than the [[Iraq War]] due to [[Iran]]'s larger population, robust military, and mountainous terrain. (00:42:25) [[Iran]]'s political and economic goals of reintegration into the global economy make the pursuit of a nuclear weapon counterproductive, unlike [[North Korea]]'s ideology of *Juche*, which prioritizes national self-sufficiency. (00:43:50) Figures like [[Mike Cernovich]] and [[Lauren Southern]], [[who]] were vocal critics of war in [[Syria]], faced a test of whether they would similarly oppose a war with [[Iran]] promoted by [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi]]. (00:45:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented that the potential reunification of [[North Korea]] and [[South Korea]] would be a powerful testament to the unifying force of shared ethnicity and ethnic nationalism, which he contrasted with demographic trends in the [[United States]]. (00:53:12) At a rally in [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[Donald Trump]] voiced support for bringing in farm workers on H-2B visas, a position Nicholas J. Fuentes opposed as a departure from [[Donald Trump|Trump's]] campaign promises. (00:56:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel]] has killed more [[United States|Americans]] and planned more terrorist attacks on [[United States|U.S.]] soil than [[Iran]], citing incidents like the 1967 attack on the [[USS Liberty]] and the 1950s "Lavon Affair" bombings in [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]]. (00:58:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized figures like [[Richard Spencer]] and [[Mike Cernovich|Mike Enoch]] for their persistently negative "[[black]]-pilled" commentary on [[Donald Trump]], arguing it ignored legal and political constraints while serving their desire to be the [[vanguard]] of the movement. (01:02:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended researching the "1980s Plan for [[Israel]]" by [[Oded Yinon]] as a more extreme document than the Clean Break Memo regarding [[Israel]]'s regional ambitions. (01:03:14) For news analysis, Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended cross-referencing headlines from a wide range of sources including [[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]], [[Fox News]], [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]], [[Washington Post|The Washington Post]], [[The New York Times]], and [[Antiwar.com]].

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(00:30:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes planned his "[[United States|America]] First Election HQ" podcast schedule to cover all 89 toss-up or leaning congressional elections, a dozen [[Senate]] races, and all 36 gubernatorial races leading up to the November midterms.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(00:33:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes created a comprehensive election analysis resource for the 2018 midterms after finding existing resources from [[Real Clear Politics]], [[FiveThirtyEight]], and [[Larry Sabato]] insufficient.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:12:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that [[George Takei]]'s birthday was on April 20th, describing him as a [[patriot]] and hero [[who]] did nothing wrong.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:13:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Shinzo Abe]] visited [[Donald Trump]] at [[Mar-a-Lago]] and that [[North Korea]] was in direct contact with the [[United States]] at the highest levels.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:14:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that then-[[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] director and [[U.S. Department of State|Secretary of State]] nominee [[Michael Pompeo|Mike Pompeo]] had traveled to [[North Korea]] to meet face-to-face with [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]] on April 1st to set up a summit.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:14:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[South Korea]] stated [[North Korea]] was 100% committed to total denuclearization and that any deal would not require the [[United States]] to remove its 30,000 troops from [[South Korea]].

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:14:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[North Korea]] announced it would cease ICBM and nuclear tests and was dismantling a nuclear test site, declaring its nuclear program "mission accomplished."

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:15:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s strategy of bellicose rhetoric and military demonstrations in the Pacific, which critics called neoconservative, had led to unprecedented diplomatic advances with [[North Korea]].

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:16:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted [[Donald Trump]]'s aggressive approach to [[North Korea]] with [[Barack Obama]]'s policy of "strategic patience," which he characterized as a hands-off strategy that failed.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:17:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that the [[United States]] missile strike on [[Syria]] the previous week was intended to give [[Donald Trump]] an upper hand in negotiations with [[North Korea]], a view he stated was echoed by analysts from [[C-SPAN|CNBC]], [[State of Japan|Japanese]], and [[China|Chinese]] press.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:18:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized rhetoric that discourages voting and political engagement, arguing it could lead to [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] victories in 2018 and 2020, resulting in policies like mass illegal immigrant legalization, loss of the [[U.S. Constitution|Second Amendment]], and activist judicial appointments.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:23:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States]] is in a transitional period where the [[Democratic Party]], having lost white and working-class voters, relies on importing new voters, creating a narrow window for political action before demographic changes become irreversible.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:26:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes welcomed rapper [[Beardly Beardson]] as a guest on his show, describing him as a friend from the 2016 election cycle.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:28:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned appearing on the "Bloodsports" stream the previous night with [[JFNA|JF]], [[Andy Warski]], and [[Ethan Ralph]], noting tension between [[Andy Warski]] and [[JFNA|JF]].

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:28:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he chose not to exploit the conflict between [[Andy Warski]] and [[JFNA|JF]], despite being friends with both and [[Timothy Gionet|Baked Alaska]], emphasizing he prefers unity over division.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:33:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes and guest [[Beardly Beardson]] discussed "GamerGate HD," a movement advocating for the removal of women from video games, including playing, developing, and writing about them.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:42:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes described segregating women into a single "Girl Chat" text channel on the [[America First]] Discord server, which he characterized as a ghettoization strategy that eventually led them to leave.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:44:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes and [[Beardly Beardson]] blamed women for societal problems, with [[Beardly Beardson]] suggesting they were at the core of nearly all issues, including the [[Iraq War]] and the [[Syria]] missile strike.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:45:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that the rise of feminism occurred because labor-saving devices left women bored, leading them to enter the workforce and pursue destructive hobbies.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:48:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]]'s body mass index (BMI) placed him just under obese, which he described as the "gold standard" for a tasteful level of being unhealthy.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:54:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized commentator [[Will Nardi]] for a tweet about being bullied by liberals and for writing for [[Vice.com]], calling him a "rat" and mocking his low-view livestreams.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(01:57:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that his success was due to talent and hard work, not just controversy, and criticized figures like [[Will Nardi]] for lacking originality and regurgitating libertarian ideas.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(02:08:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes praised [[Andy Warski]] and [[JFNA|JF]] as a necessary combination for the "Bloodsports" stream, with [[JFNA|JF]] providing academic substance and [[Andy Warski]] adding personality and accessibility.

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(02:11:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Democratic Party|Democratic National Committee]] was suing the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] campaign and [[Russia]] for stealing the election, which [[Donald Trump]] responded to by saying he could counter-sue to access [[Democratic Party|DNC]] servers and information on the [[Awan brothers]].

### AF 2018-04-20 0149
(02:13:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that morality cannot exist without God, stating that atheists [[who]] critique [[Christianity|Christian]] morality have no objective standard and are left with subjective preference or nihilistic possibilities like living in a simulation.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began his broadcast by welcoming viewers to [[United States|America]] First and announcing a planned interview with guest [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] to discuss biology and human sociobiology.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:01:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes experienced technical difficulties with his camera and audio feed while attempting to connect with his guest [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes successfully connected with [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] and shifted the discussion to a new development involving a potential co-host named Aaron for the show "Worski Live."

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:04:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that Aaron, a potential co-host for "Worski Live," withdrew from a planned intellectual vetting process conducted by [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]], and would not be appearing on the show again.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:05:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes revealed his intended strategy for the vetting was to praise Aaron and Andy's niche of free speech and real exchange of ideas, rather than to bully her.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:06:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed his view that the ongoing "[[Timothy Gionet|Baked Alaska]]" controversy had become exploitative and shifted the conversation to the topic of a recent missile strike on [[Syria]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:06:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a tweet where he satirically stated "every operation overseas is neoconservatism" in response to the [[Syria]] strike, prompting [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] to argue this was an oversimplification.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:10:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes acknowledged the criticisms of the [[Syria]] strike, including parallels to the [[Iraq]] and [[Afghanistan]] wars, the unverified nature of the chemical attack claims, and the principle of national sovereignty.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:11:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated it made no strategic sense for the [[Bashar al-Assad|Assad]] government to use chemical weapons at a [[time]] when the [[United States]] had announced a withdrawal from [[Syria]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:15:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that the strike coincided with [[Israel|Israeli]] interests and fully recognized that [[Israel]] wanted to see [[Bashar al-Assad]] removed from power.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:15:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a plan by [[Oded Yinon]] from the 1980s for [[Israel]] to see the nations around it disintegrate into a "mosaic of tribal fiefdoms" to eliminate threats.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:16:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that neocons in the [[Donald Trump]] administration like [[John Bolton]] and [[Michael Pompeo|Mike Pompeo]] absolutely wanted the [[Syria]] strike for [[Israel]]'s national security interests.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:16:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited [[Tucker Carlson]] as one of the last true journalists alive for asking a senator why the [[Syria]] strike was in [[United States|American]] interests and receiving the answer "if you care about [[Israel]]."

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:16:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed his belief that [[Donald Trump]] was separate from the people he surrounded himself with and was committed to keeping the [[United States]] out of the [[Middle East]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:17:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the symbolic strike theory suggested the action was meant to deter [[weapons of mass destruction]] use by [[North Korea]] and achieve denuclearization in [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:18:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes asked [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]], as a scientist, if advances in technology would make nuclear weapons readily available to non-[[state]] actors like [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] within 50 to 100 years.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:20:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that unapologetic support for [[Israel]] was a primary reason the [[United States]] could not get along with Arab allies or countries like [[Syria]] and [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:20:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented the nightmare scenario of nuclear proliferation where [[Iran]] getting a weapon prompts [[Saudi Arabia]], [[Qatar]], [[Turkey]], and [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]] to follow suit, creating a volatile [[region]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:24:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel]]'s long-term viability depended on the success of its current plan to exploit structural weaknesses and disintegrate neighboring nation-states like [[Iraq]] and [[Syria]] into tribal fiefdoms.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:25:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if nation-states like [[Iraq]] and [[Syria]] remained divided and the [[United States]] continued to be used as an instrument for this disintegration, [[Israel]] would remain viable; otherwise, it would require a comprehensive peace with [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:27:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced [[Samuel Huntington|Sam Huntington]]'s "Clash of Civilizations" theory, suggesting an alliance between the Western and Orthodox civilizations ([[United States]], [[Europe]], [[Russia]]) to stop WMD proliferation in [[Islam|Muslim]] countries was doable.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:28:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended it was in the interest of all advanced civilizations to prevent WMD proliferation in [[Islam|Muslim]] countries and that now was the [[time]] to set the standard before a multipolar world emerged.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:31:00) [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] stated that Nicholas J. Fuentes admitting the chemical attacks might not have been caused by [[Bashar al-Assad|Assad]] while still advocating for action violated the presumption of innocence, a principle he argued must also apply to countries.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:32:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Russia]] had blocked a [[United Nations]] resolution to send independent investigators into [[Syria]], complicating the establishment of evidence.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:33:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the [[Syria]] strike as a constrained option to demonstrate a willingness to use military force, which he argued was necessary to make diplomacy work for denuclearizing the [[Korean Peninsula]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:34:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if achieving denuclearization on the [[Korean Peninsula]] resulted from the missile strike, it outweighed the negative consequences of creating bad blood in the [[Middle East]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed that his primary concern with [[North Korea]] was not an immediate strike, but the instability of a nuclear arsenal in the hands of a fragile regime over the next 50 to 100 years.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:36:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[North Korea]] already had a nuclear arsenal, whereas [[Iran]] only had a capability, and that one positive externality of the [[Iraq War]] was that [[Syria]], [[Libya]], and [[Iran]] temporarily suspended their nuclear programs.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:38:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes conceded that the fundamental disagreement with [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] was between the practicality of nonproliferation versus the inevitability of proliferation and the futility of trying to rebuild trust with the [[Islam|Muslim]] world.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:41:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Syria]]'s air defense, reportedly supplied by [[Russia]], had shot down 71 missiles and successfully intercepted subsequent [[Israel|Israeli]] strikes, and stated he was "rooting for" [[Bashar al-Assad|Assad]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:41:00) [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] pointed out the contradiction in Nicholas J. Fuentes rooting for [[Bashar al-Assad|Assad]] while also supporting the strike against him.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:42:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he did not see the strike as intended for regime change or impeding [[Bashar al-Assad|Assad]]'s war efforts, but as a necessary action for the [[United States]]' diplomacy with [[North Korea]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:43:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States]] was already entrenched in the [[Middle East]], having done immense damage, and that meddling was now a tool at its disposal to address the greater concern of [[North Korea]]'s nuclear arsenal.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:44:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes used the example of the [[Ronald Reagan|Reagan]] administration supporting [[Osama bin Laden]] and the Mujahideen in [[Afghanistan]] to counter the [[Soviet Union]] to illustrate how foreign policy prioritizes immediate, greater threats over long-term consequences.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:45:00) [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] asserted that Nicholas J. Fuentes was taking the neocon position by trying to "social engineer a part of humanity" with missiles, describing a mentality within an escalation of violence that leaves no room for peace and guarantees intergenerational hatred.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:46:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he simply did not believe in world peace, arguing that security is achieved through deterrence and a degree of fear of reprisal, which he characterized as realism, not neoconservatism.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:47:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes defined neoconservatism as wanting to rewrite maps, change ideologies, and impose values, whereas realism was about pursuing national security interests in a dangerous, Hobbesian world.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:48:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the [[Syria]] strike had no casualties, as the targeted facilities were empty, and was deemed a failure by [[Israel]], [[Syria]], and [[Russia]], but he would take such a strike over the possibility of war with [[North Korea]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:49:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Syria]] already had bad blood with the [[United States]] due to unwavering support for [[Israel]], and that disengaging from [[Israel]] was necessary for any future peace.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:51:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered that [[Barack Obama]]'s strategy of showing goodwill—withdrawing from wars, offering the [[Iran]] deal, and having a frosty relationship with [[Israel]]—did not work, as rival powers like [[Russia]], [[Iran]], and [[North Korea]] did not reciprocate with respect.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:55:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that in the real world, he did not care if [[Iran]] had a "right" to nuclear weapons; he believed the [[United States]] should pursue its security interests even when they contradict the interests or rights of other nations.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:56:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if [[Iran]] or [[China]] were the world hegemon, they would not respect other countries' rights, illustrating that international competition is not about justice but about countries pursuing their own security and stepping on others.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:57:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[China]]'s Belt and Road Initiative as an example of a rising power taking advantage of and abusing neighboring nations through debt-trap diplomacy, not acting on principles of justice.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:58:00) [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] characterized Nicholas J. Fuentes's view as a neocon and interventionist approach where the [[United States]] feels entitled to things it would not grant others, a system that only holds as long as [[United States|America]] remains the global hegemon.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(00:59:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes distinguished his "realist" view from neoconservatism, stating neocons care about imposing democracy, while he only cares about containing threats to [[United States|America]], which is what "[[United States|America]] First" means.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(01:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that all great powers in history, like [[United Kingdom|Great Britain]] and [[Germany]], put their interests before others, which is realism, not a unique ideology.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(01:02:00) [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] predicted that within a hundred years, [[United States|America]] would not be the economic power it is now, and that [[China|Chinese]] or [[Islam|Muslim]] people would think exactly like Nicholas J. Fuentes but from a position of power, making his current strategy unsustainable.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(01:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed a multipolar world order would emerge within the century, but argued it would be silly not to pursue [[United States|American]] interests to the fullest now in hopes of goodwill later, as history shows countries with power use it to disarm competitors.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(01:04:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced centuries of conflict with the [[Islam|Muslim]] world since the first caliphates to argue that declawing [[United States|America]] and recognizing the rights of others in hopes they won't think the same way is a "fool's errand."

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(01:05:00) [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] concluded that the sentences Nicholas J. Fuentes was saying publicly would be used against [[United States|America]] in a hundred years when it is no longer technologically dominant, and advised thinking about air defense and surviving a potential nuclear [[holocaust]].

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(01:06:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized the core disagreement as being about proliferation versus nonproliferation and realpolitik versus a longer-term view, thanking [[John F. Kennedy|J.F.]] for adding complexity to the discussion.

### AF 2018-04-18 0148
(01:09:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes acknowledged there would be negative outcomes and unintended consequences from the strike, but believed the positives of pressuring [[North Korea]] outweighed the negatives.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:05:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the publication of a premium podcast episode analyzing the [[Syria|Syrian]] [[Civil War]] from an [[Israel|Israeli]] perspective, referencing the 1996 "Clean Break Memo" written by [[Richard Perle]] for [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:05:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the premium podcast would examine historical figures including [[David Ben-Gurion]] and Oded Yinon, as well as neoconservative architects of the [[Iraq]] War like [[Richard Perle]] and [[Douglas Feith]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:06:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] met with [[Shinzo Abe]] of [[State of Japan|Japan]] at the Mar-a-Lago resort to discuss [[North Korea]] and trade.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:07:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the foreign policy focus of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration had shifted from the [[Middle East]] to the Pacific, specifically [[North Korea]] and [[China]], contrasting it with the [[Middle East]]-centric approaches of [[George W. Bush|Bush]] and [[Obama]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:11:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that the strategic importance of [[Middle East|Middle Eastern]] energy for the [[United States]] was declining due to increased domestic production, reducing the prominence of countries like [[Saudi Arabia]] and [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:14:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s tariffs on steel and aluminum, his renegotiation of a trade deal with [[South Korea]], and the [[Syria]] strikes were all tactics directed at influencing [[North Korea]] and, by extension, [[China]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:15:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] made the revised trade deal with [[South Korea]] contingent on progress toward peace with [[North Korea]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:18:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] and [[Shinzo Abe]] held a meeting where [[Donald Trump|Trump]] confirmed high-level discussions were occurring directly between the [[United States]] and [[North Korea]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:19:50) [[Donald Trump|Trump]] took credit for the success of the [[South Korea|South Korean]] Olympics and gave his blessing for [[North Korea]] and [[South Korea]] to end the Korean War, according to Nicholas J. Fuentes.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:20:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that an unnamed [[South Korea|South Korean]] official stated both Koreas were preparing an official statement to bring an end to the Korean War, which has continued under an armistice since 1953.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:21:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that a summit between [[North Korea]] and [[South Korea]] was scheduled for April 28th, and a summit between [[Donald Trump|Trump]] and [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]] was planned for May, with five potential locations under consideration.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:24:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that past agreements with rogue regimes like [[Iran]], [[Syria]], and [[Libya]] had failed because they involved incremental, synchronized reforms that the regimes later reneged on.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:25:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the problem with the [[Iran]] nuclear deal negotiated by the [[Obama]] administration was that it lifted sanctions and provided [[Iran]] with access to $150 billion in frozen assets without enforceable guarantees of compliance.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:30:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] would pursue a renegotiation of the [[Iran]] nuclear deal using a similar strategy of pressure and diplomacy as he was employing with [[North Korea]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:32:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that, according to [[Vox]], [[John Bolton]] had proposed extensive air raids to destroy the [[Syria|Syrian]] government's war-making capability in response to a chemical weapons attack, but [[Donald Trump|Trump]] rejected this in favor of a limited strike on three empty facilities.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:34:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that, according to the *[[The Wall Street Journal|Wall Street Journal]]*, [[Donald Trump|Trump]] wanted an Arab coalition from [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]], the [[United Arab Emirates]], [[Qatar]], and [[Saudi Arabia]] to replace the [[United States]] military presence in [[Syria]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:38:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] still desired to meet with [[Vladimir Putin|Putin]] and was reportedly furious when the [[United States]] expelled [[Russian]] diplomats in response to the assassination of a former [[Russian]] spy in the [[United Kingdom]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:43:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] Justice [[Neil Gorsuch]] sided with liberal justices in striking down a federal statute mandating deportation for immigrants [[who]] commit a "crime of violence," ruling the language was too ambiguous.

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(00:58:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]] was a "preeminent rogue [[state]]" in the [[Middle East]] with significant influence in the [[United States]] government, but that [[Iran]] also pursued its interests aggressively in [[Iraq]], [[Syria]], [[Yemen]], and [[Bahrain]].

### AF 2018-04-17 0147
(01:00:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized figures like [[Richard Spencer]] and [[Alex Jones]] for their pro-[[Russia]] views, noting [[Richard Spencer]]'s friendship with [[Government of Russia|Kremlin]]-linked philosopher [[Alexander Dugin]] and favorable coverage of [[Alex Jones]] on [[state]]-funded outlet [[Russia Today]].

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:23:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Russia]]'s failure to intercept coalition missiles during the April 2018 [[Syria]] strike, despite prior threats to do so, demonstrated its military weakness relative to the [[United States]].

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:35:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted in February 2018 that a chemical weapons attack would occur in [[Syria]] before summer and that [[Donald Trump]] would respond with a limited, one-off military strike.

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:36:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the primary purpose of the April 2018 [[Syria]] strike was to demonstrate the [[United States]]' willingness to use military force to deter the proliferation of [[weapons of mass destruction]], thereby creating leverage for upcoming negotiations with [[North Korea]] and [[Iran]].

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:43:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel]] sought the overthrow of [[Bashar al-Assad|Assad]] in [[Syria]] to counter [[Iran]]'s military presence, which [[Israel]] viewed as a forward operating base for supplying weapons to [[Hezbollah]].

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:44:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the relationship between the [[United States]] and [[Israel]] as a de facto defense alliance that risked dragging the [[United States|U.S]]. into conflicts in the [[Middle East]].

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:46:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended his friend [[Timothy Gionet|Baked Alaska]] against online criticism, stating he believed [[Timothy Gionet|Baked Alaska]] was a "good guy" with a solid character, though he expressed concern about the "parasitic" and drug-using company [[Timothy Gionet|Baked Alaska]] kept.

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:50:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed admiration for commentator [[Lauren Southern]], calling her "mommy of the movement" and appreciating her defense of him during online controversies.

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:51:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes described his debate with [[James Allsup]] on the "Bloodsports" program as a "vicious, ruthless, savage takedown," comparing his victory to Anakin Skywalker's defeat of Obi-Wan Kenobi in *Star Wars*.

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(00:59:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes sarcastically credited "[[Black]] Pill" online critics with preventing [[Donald Trump]] from launching a full-scale war in [[Syria]], suggesting their tweets and complaints were the "thin [[black]] line" that stopped neoconservative domination.

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(01:00:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized elements within the nationalist movement [[who]] held anti-[[United States|American]] or pro-[[Russian]] views, stating his allegiance was solely to [[United States|America]] and not to any foreign power.

### AF 2018-04-16 0146
(01:10:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes articulated a foundational belief that [[Donald Trump]] was a patriotic "good man" trying to do the right thing for the [[country]] against powerful opposition, rather than a compromised figure maliciously betraying his supporters.

### AF 2018-04-13 0145
(00:09:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes introduced a guest named [[Youssef]] [[who]] had debated [[Steven Crowder]] on a segment of [[Steven Crowder|Crowder]]'s "Change My Mind" series.

### AF 2018-04-13 0145
(17:10:29) [[Youssef]] noted that critiques of the war in [[Syria]] and pro-[[Israel]] foreign policy, common on shows like Nicholas J. Fuentes's, align with the views of many left-wing voters.

### AF 2018-04-13 0145
(20:55:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that he was a strong advocate for free-market economics in high school but later shifted his focus toward the public good and supporting families, even at the cost of economic efficiency.

### AF 2018-04-09 0141
(00:40:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States]] government is controlled by a permanent, transnational "deep [[state]]" within the [[U.S. Department of Defense|Pentagon]], [[U.S. Department of State|State Department]], and intelligence community, rather than by its elected civilian leadership.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:03:28) A debater named Yusef engaged [[Steven Crowder]] in a debate about socialism at the [[University of Illinois]] and, according to Nicholas J. Fuentes, effectively defeated him.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:05:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the modern conservative tradition is distorted and wrongly equated with capitalism and libertarianism, rather than traditional right-wing values of order and tradition.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:06:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the government founded in the [[United States]] in 1789 was liberal, specifically classical liberalism, which he distinguishes from modern progressivism.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:07:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized figures like [[Ben Shapiro]] and [[Fox News]] for promoting a warped conception that old liberalism is actually conservatism.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:09:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified young people involved with [[Young Americans for Liberty]] and [[college Republicans]] as being indoctrinated into believing the right wing is solely about low taxes and GDP growth.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:10:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he does not consider himself alt-right, identifying instead as a traditionalist, perennialist, and reactionary [[who]] believes in order, religion, and tradition.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:10:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes traced the term "alt-right" to its use by [[Richard Spencer]] in 2008, later broadened to describe alternatives to [[George W. Bush|Bush]], [[Ronald Reagan|Reagan]], and [[William F. Buckley]] conservatism.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:11:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes called for a reassessment of what it means to be on the right wing in 2018, questioning whether the movement is paleoconservative, alt-right, or reactionary.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:11:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes defined the core of right-wing belief as order, tradition, continuity, and an acknowledgment of the limitations and unchanging nature of human beings.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:12:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that modern conservatives want the fruits of a white, [[Christianity|Christian]], patriarchal society—like safety and high-paying jobs—but reject the demographic and cultural roots that produced those outcomes.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:13:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes used a factory analogy to criticize conservatives [[who]] believe they can replace a population with people from the third world and still get the same societal results.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:15:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified egalitarianism—the belief that all people are equal and interchangeable—as the philosophical premise underlying the conservative assumption that population replacement does not matter.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:16:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the allure of the alt-right is that it is the only group that rejects egalitarianism, which he claimed has no basis in science, history, or empirical fact.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:17:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the ruling class cannot allow any challenge to egalitarianism because if people realize groups are different, the entire system of mass immigration and multiculturalism would collapse.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:18:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes, reflecting on his [[time]] in college, claimed that groups like [[college Republicans]] exhibit no diversity of thought and all fall within the same narrow political window.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:19:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that conservatism is dominated by liberal and globalist intellectuals, and recommended reading authors like [[Russell Kirk]] and [[Edmund Burke]] instead of [[Milton Friedman]], [[Friedrich Hayek]], or [[Ayn Rand]].

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:20:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for pushing a traditionalist, right-wing ideology, arguing that taboo positions can be integrated into traditionalism, which recognizes human nature and distinctions between groups.

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:21:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the "new conservatives" of the 1940s-60s have been erased because [[William F. Buckley]] and libertarians won the battle to define conservatism, leading to figures like [[Ronald Reagan]].

### AF 2018-04-06 0140
(00:23:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a surprise guest, Sean (Prince Hubris), to discuss the "Ricky Vaughn" situation and [[Paul Nealon]].

Bush]], and a nationalist, [[America First]] route advocated by [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:30:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]] was a major factor behind [[United States]] military involvement in [[Iraq]], [[Syria]], [[Libya]], [[Yemen]], and [[Afghanistan]], as part of a 25-year policy of sending [[United States|American]] soldiers to serve foreign interests.

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:34:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed the three publicly stated objectives for the 2003 [[United States]] invasion of [[Iraq]]: destroying [[weapons of mass destruction]], punishing [[Saddam Hussein]] for an alleged role in the [[September 11 attacks|9/11 attacks]], and building a democracy.

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:34:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[United States]] found no stockpiles of nuclear or biological weapons in [[Iraq]] after the 2003 invasion, and that [[Saddam Hussein]]'s involvement in [[September 11 attacks|9/11]] was an outright lie manufactured by the [[George W. Bush]] administration.

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:38:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the real objective of the [[Iraq]] War was to build a democracy in the [[Middle East]], an ideological crusade advanced by 25 to 30 neoconservative intellectuals in the [[George W. Bush]] administration, a majority of whom were [[Jews|Jewish]] according to the [[Israel|Israeli]] newspaper [[The Jerusalem Post|Haaretz]].

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:41:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the core motivation for these neoconservative intellectuals was a vested national security interest in [[Israel]], aiming to systematically undermine the military capability of Arab countries to make war with [[Israel]].

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:42:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes categorized every major [[country]] in the [[Middle East]] into three groups regarding [[Israel]]: those at peace and receiving [[United States]] aid (e.g., [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]], [[Jim Jordan|Jordan]], [[Saudi Arabia]]), those destroyed by [[United States]] wars (e.g., [[Libya]], [[Somalia]], [[Iraq]]), and those in the crosshairs for future wars (e.g., [[Syria]], [[Iran]]).

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:43:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the cost of the [[Iraq]] War over 15 years was 4,500 [[United States|American]] dead, 35,000 [[United States|American]] injured, an estimated 250,000 total violent deaths in [[Iraq]], and a projected total cost of up to six trillion dollars.

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:44:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the only objective achieved by the [[Iraq]] War was making [[Israel]] safer, and that the same forces were advocating for [[United States]] military action in [[Iran]] and deeper involvement in [[Syria]].

### AF 2018-03-20 0128
(00:54:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that an agent [[who]] had approached him for potential representation ceased contact because Nicholas J. Fuentes kept "talking about this [[Israel]] stuff."

### AF 2018-03-20 0128

### AF 2018-03-16 0126
(00:31:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Republican Party]]'s future viability depends on adopting a working-class, economic nationalist message to win states in the [[Midwest]], [[Rust Belt]], and rural areas, citing the narrow victories of [[Donald Trump]] in [[State of Michigan|Michigan]] and [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]].

### AF 2018-03-16 0126
(00:58:99) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Republican Party]] requires 270 electoral votes to win a presidential election and 51-52 senators for a majority, a threshold unattainable with the traditional [[George W. Bush]] and [[Ronald Reagan]] platform alone.

(01:04:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Steve Bannon]] as a proponent of the economic nationalism strategy he endorsed as the path forward for the [[Republican Party]].

### AF 2018-03-16 0126
(01:18:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that nearly every institutional force in the [[United States]], including media and education, actively pushes women into the workforce while stigmatizing the choice to be a homemaker.

(02:16:62) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized left-wing political protests, like national school walkouts, as government-subsidized activities supported by taxpayer-funded public schools and teachers, while comparable right-wing demonstrations are inconceivable.

(02:54:80) Nicholas J. (04:08:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that wealth and prosperity, particularly "accelerated late-stage capitalism," have a corrupting tendency and exacerbate societal degeneration, though he does not believe this decline is inevitable.

Fuentes advised a caller that concerns about physical appearance and "optics" primarily mattered for individuals serving as the public face of the movement at rallies or demonstrations, citing [[Richard Spencer]]'s speech in [[Gainesville]] with [[Mike Cernovich|Mike Enoch]] as a negative example.

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:06:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the most needed activism involved joining political campaigns or local party organizations like the [[Republican Party]], where specialized skills and influence were more valuable than public presentation.

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:09:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended the right to own semi-automatic rifles like the AR-15, framing it as a God-given right integral to defending life, property, and liberty against tyranny.

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:12:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the deterrent effect of an armed populace, referencing a historical quote that a potential invasion of the [[United States]] would fail because there would be "a gun behind every blade of grass."

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:13:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that left-leaning individuals concerned about [[Donald Trump]] as a tyrant should logically oppose disarming the populace.

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:15:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes engaged with a caller [[who]] argued that semi-automatic rifles were critical for self-defense during natural disasters, citing a recent incident where a firearms instructor used an AR-15 to stop a knife attack.

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:17:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed openness to banning bump stocks only as a tactical concession to secure significant pro-gun legislation like national concealed carry reciprocity, but opposed any bill with vague language that could enable a broader ban on semi-automatic firearms.

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:20:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[National Rifle Association|NRA]] had met with [[Donald Trump]] and opposed new gun control measures, making a legislative vote unlikely.

(00:21:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized "red flag" firearm confiscation laws, like those in [[State of California|California]], warning they could be abused by activist judges to disarm law-abiding citizens.

(00:23:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[SHARE Act]], which would have legalized suppressors and established national concealed carry reciprocity, was shelved following the [[Las Vegas]] shooting and the [[Parkland]] shooting.

(00:24:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended that viewers join [[Gun Owners of America]] for its effective advocacy and action alerts.

(00:25:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted how [[Gun Owners of America]] members flooded the [[Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives|ATF]] with communications to defeat a proposed ban on privately owned body armor pushed by [[Dianne Feinstein]].

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:28:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States]] needed to abandon the "free trade dogma" that manufacturing was unnecessary and instead rebuild its industrial base to secure the working-class vote.

### AF 2018-03-02 0117
(00:30:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the [[Republican Party]] must institutionally reorient itself to be more labor-oriented and working-class to capitalize on its 2016 gains.

(00:00:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes will debate [[Costin Alamariu|RC Maxwell]], also [[known]] as [[Black]] Hannity, on the topic of ethnic nationalism versus civic nationalism.

(00:08:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the significant activist response to the [[Parkland]] shooting with the relative lack of political push following the [[Las Vegas]] shooting, which was the deadliest mass shooting in [[United States]] history.

(00:17:56) Nicholas J. (00:20:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes described engaging with protesters at a [[Moms Demand Action]] rally in [[Chicago]], where he proposed compromises like enhanced background checks and arming teachers, but found most attendees unwilling to reason.

(00:31:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes posited that the fictional nation of Wakanda in *[[Black]] Panther* represents an idealized, traditional, isolated, and xenophobic ethno-nationalist [[state]], a [[concept]] he argued could be used to make similar real-world political positions more relatable.

(00:34:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that cultural myths, like those presented in *[[Black]] Panther*, could shift public perception away from the "melting pot" ideal and toward support for coherent, traditional societies based on ethnic sameness.
