### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:08:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes invoked [[Jung]]'s [[concept]] of the collective unconscious to argue for a kinship and connection by blood that binds people with their ancestors and community.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:11:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected the [[concept]] of a white "ethno-[[state]]" involving a tactical withdrawal, arguing that the whole of the [[United States]] belongs to his movement and that they must think big and reclaim the entire [[country]], referencing the founders' mindset of manifest [[destiny]].

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:13:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed with [[Nehlen]]'s position that social media platforms like [[Twitter]] and [[Facebook]] should be regulated to apply [[Concept|First Amendment]] protections online, citing their monopolistic control over communication and barriers to market entry.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:15:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the 14th Amendment as precedents for regulating private companies like social media platforms to ensure equal protections, comparing it to ending discrimination at lunch counters and on buses.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:16:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted receiving criticism from [[Nehlen]]'s campaign spokesperson, whom he identified as "[[John]] Smith," a [[person]] he described as "crypto [[Jews|Jewish]]" and "crypto homosexual," for not discussing censorship on his show.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:20:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed disappointment in the direction of [[Nehlen]], whom he had campaigned for in 2016, citing Nehlen's association with [[David Duke]], getting banned from [[Twitter]], tweeting about [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]] on [[Gab]], and being removed from the [[Republican Party]] in [[Wisconsin]].

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:22:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Nehlen]] had spent his political capital too soon by being unstrategic, and that with more competent guidance he could have remained relevant and pushed the envelope longer as a political psyop.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:23:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to the political rise of [[Donald Trump]] as evidence that change within the system is possible, citing the rapid shift from mainstream media credibility and policies under [[Barack Obama]] to discussions about ending chain migration and building a border wall.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:27:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if rumors were true that leaders like [[Mike Cernovich|Mike Enoch]] knew about [[Eli Mosley]]'s lies and concealed the [[Jews|Jewish]] and homosexual identity of [[Nehlen]]'s spokesperson, it would be a reckless betrayal that took advantage of young, impressionable men in the movement.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:31:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the presence of an [[Israel|Israeli]] politician's son and [[The Atlantic|Atlantic]] journalist with [[Richard Spencer]] as "fishy" and "not good," arguing that movement leaders must avoid being liabilities and maintain transparency if they claim to lead.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:34:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that removing statues of figures like [[Thomas Jefferson]] and [[Robert E. Lee]] while erecting statues of Marxists represents a fundamental change in the [[country]]'s soul, as statues memorialize virtues and ideals for society.

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:39:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that lasting political change must be a long, drawn-out process built brick by brick, contrasting it with [[the left]]'s century-long project from the [[Frankfurt School]] to [[Barack Obama]].

### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:41:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Steve Bannon]] told [[Sean Hannity]] the political battle would be won "every day for the next 20 years, every election year for the next 20 years."

(00:43:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[the left]] has a built-in call to action (e.g., "go out and vote") for every problem, while the right faces an uphill battle to walk people through rethinking foundational beliefs, such as on race and IQ.

(00:45:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested using nostalgia for early 20th century [[United States|America]]—citing the beauty, style, and architectural values of places like 1920s [[New York|New York City]]—as a motivator to contrast with today's "hollow atomized... cyberpunk dystopia."

(00:48:23) Nicholas J. ### AF 2018-02-16 0110
(00:50:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he began asking movement leaders for a concrete plan to get "from point A to point B" in October and received no answers, leading him to conclude the movement was directionless and demanded better leadership.

(00:50:55) Nicholas J. (00:16:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a primary motivator for politicians in [[Europe]] and the [[United States]] to bring in immigrants is the need for young, able-bodied workers to support a burgeoning welfare [[state]] and offset an aging native population.

(00:25:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the premise of *[[Black]] Panther*, which features a technologically advanced African nation called Wakanda untouched by colonialism, as a pathetic and invented narrative necessary due to the absence of a comparable real-world history.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(00:28:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the fundamental explanation for racial disparities, which he termed "[[Black]] failure," is inherent biological and cultural differences between races, a fact he claims mainstream society refuses to acknowledge.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(00:29:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[United States]] immigration system and [[United States|American]] mythology of the past 50 years are based on the false premise that all racial and ethnic groups are fundamentally the same and will assimilate identically.

(00:33:50) [[Nancy Pelosi]] held the floor of the [[United States House of Representatives]] for eight hours in a speech advocating for [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] recipients, which Nicholas J. Fuentes described as a publicity stunt.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(00:36:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Nancy Pelosi]]'s pride in her grandson's rejection of his white identity reveals a deliberate agenda by "rootless transnationals" to destroy racial, ethnic, national, and religious identities to create a homogenized, rootless global population.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(00:39:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his opposition is not to other races existing in their own nations, but to racial mixture and integration, paraphrasing [[Oswald Mosley]] by saying "we can live in peace and friendship in separate nations... but what we cannot have is the mix."

(00:43:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited data showing that children from mixed-race marriages have a much higher propensity for drug abuse and mental illness, which he presented as evidence against the idea that "the beauty is in the mix."

(00:48:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his arguments in the debate with [[Halsey English]] were based on empirical facts from sources like [[Peter Schaefer]] of [[Princeton University]], the General Social Survey, and publicly available data on [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]] employees, not extremist literature.

(00:55:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes declined an invitation to a debate between [[Jared Taylor]] and [[Tariq Nasheed]], criticizing [[Tariq Nasheed]] as insufferably arrogant and stating that low expectations allow such behavior from [[black]] nationalists without criticism.

(00:56:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the reaction to [[Nancy Pelosi]]'s story with the hypothetical outrage if a figure like [[Mitch McConnell]] or [[Ben Carson]] expressed pride in a white child's wish to be [[black]], highlighting a perceived double standard.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(00:57:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Christianity]], through scriptures like the New Testament's mention of enduring nations and the Old Testament's focus on tribes and kingdoms, affirms the legitimacy of separate nations and opposes global homogenization.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(01:03:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that in the current era, 95% of media is controlled by six corporations that act as gatekeepers to prevent fringe, dissident political opinions from entering the mainstream.

(01:04:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Mark Zuckerberg]]'s [[Facebook]] and [[Twitter|Jack Dorsey]]'s [[Twitter]] as monopolistic social media platforms whose arbitrary censorship poses a major challenge to free speech in the [[Kanye West|West]].

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(01:05:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes endorsed "shall not censor" legislation proposed by [[Paul Nealon]] as a solution, arguing that platforms like [[Twitter]], [[Google|YouTube]], and [[Facebook]] are monopolistic trusts that must either be broken up or submit to government regulation to protect free speech.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(01:07:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed opposition to the term "ethnostate" as impractical jargon and ideologically rooted in a top-down, design-based political theory akin to communism, which he believes fails because societies evolve organically rather than being constructed.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(01:08:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes aligned his political tradition with conservative pragmatists like [[Otto von Bismarck]], [[Edmund Burke]], [[Cato the Elder]], [[Klemens von Metternich]], and [[Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston]], [[who]] favored evolutionary societal change over radical design.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(01:10:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for an evolutionary approach to demographic change, focusing on policies to increase the white fertility rate and strengthen young men and families, rather than designing a specific racial quota or system.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(01:11:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes praised [[Otto von Bismarck]] as a pragmatic nationalist and anti-war statesman whose brilliant statecraft, including instigating the Franco-Prussian War through a leaked telegram, led to the unification of [[Germany]] in 1871.

### AF 2018-02-08 0104
(01:13:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected the argument that the Pilgrims at [[Plymouth Rock]] created an ethnostate as a valid precedent, stating that transplanting an existing civilization to virgin soil is different from radically redesigning a centuries-old, advanced civilization like the modern [[United States]].

(00:34:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that to reach a bigger audience, the movement must appeal more broadly, contrasting the current "alt-right" strategy with the earlier "big tent" approach that included figures like [[Mike Cernovich]], [[Breitbart News|Breitbart]], [[Steve Bannon]], and [[Donald Trump]].

(00:17:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes described a "cold [[civil war]]" between a faction comprising [[Democratic Party|Democrats]], the deep [[state]], elements of the [[Republican Party]], high finance, the media, and [[Los Angeles|Hollywood]], and another faction led by [[Donald Trump]] and his allies.

(00:19:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a commentator named James [[who]] advocated for mass roundups and deportations of illegal immigrants.

(00:35:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States|American]] people lack control of their [[country]] and are not represented by their government, preventing necessary reforms.

(00:43:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[black]] [[United States|Americans]] have a legitimate grievance when they say [[United States|America]] has a legacy of racism, from their perspective.

(00:43:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that reconciliation between [[blacks]] and whites may be impossible, ultimately leading to separation.

(00:52:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the [[alt-right]]'s rhetorical style to his past libertarian ideology, where all issues were reduced to free-market solutions.

(00:54:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that white people should not be held guilty for historical injustices like slavery or the [[Native American]] genocide, as no living white [[person]] perpetrated them and no living [[black]] [[person]] was directly victimized by them.

(00:55:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was tired of white people being held guilty for historical sins while not receiving credit for achievements like landing on the moon, inventing Western medicine, and the Industrial Revolution.

(03:00:76) Nicholas J. (03:36:70) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported the [[Office of the Inspector General]] extended its deadline and would release a full report in late March or early April due to the extensive scope of its investigation into [[Hillary Clinton]] and [[Barack Obama]].

(04:29:74) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed murmurs from [[Congress]] suggested the contents of the memo were "worse than Watergate" and would force a major restructuring of the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] and intelligence community.

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(04:53:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained the memo was drafted by [[Republican Party|Republican]] lawmakers led by [[Devin Nunes]], compiling evidence from the [[Office of the Inspector General]] and oversight of the special counsel investigation into 2016 [[Russian]] hacking.

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(05:21:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated [[Republican Party|Republicans]] unilaterally voted to release the memo using a 40-year-old rule that bypassed the minority party, and a courier had delivered it to the [[White House]] for [[Donald Trump|President Trump]]'s approval.

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(06:08:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted the memo's contents revolved around [[Fusion GPS]], the [[Donald Trump|Trump]]-[[Russia]] dossier, and [[Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act|FISA]] warrants, alleging [[Fusion GPS]] was contracted by [[Hillary Clinton]] and the [[Democratic Party|Democratic National Committee]] to collect opposition research on [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(06:37:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes alleged [[Fusion GPS]] was given access to the national security database and [[Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act|FISA]] surveillance apparatus by the [[Barack Obama administration|Obama administration]] to illegally [[target]] [[Donald Trump]] as a political opponent.

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(07:48:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the dossier was a 35-[[page]] document compiled by former [[United Kingdom|British]] spy [[Christopher Steele]], which alleged [[Donald Trump]] was compromised by [[Vladimir Putin]] during a trip to [[Moscow]].

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(08:14:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes said the dossier was published by [[BuzzFeed]] around a year prior and was used as the basis for appointing Special Counsel [[Robert Mueller]] after the election.

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(09:33:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported the [[House Oversight Committee]] had been subpoenaing the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] and [[U.S. Department of Justice|Department of Justice]] for missing documents and witnesses to substantiate the dossier's claims, which [[Devin Nunes]] called "total BS."

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(10:15:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed if the memo's allegations of a high-level conspiracy against [[Donald Trump]] were true, it would result in "serious heads rolling" and major upheaval, surpassing the Watergate scandal.

(11:06:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes connected the memo's release to the previous week's news that the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] announced it was missing 50,000 text messages between agents [[Peter Strzok]] and [[Lisa Page]] from December 2016 to May 2017, allegedly related to a "secret society" conspiring against [[the president]].

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(12:02:88) Nicholas J. ### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(13:23:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed [[Hillary Clinton]] installed a private, unsecured email server in her home to conceal these dealings, which subsequently transmitted classified information, endangering national security.

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(17:17:98) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the ongoing special counsel investigation as part of a broader play for a "coup" to contest the legitimacy of [[Donald Trump]]'s presidency.

(18:00:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes named [[Hillary Clinton]], [[Barack Obama]], [[John McCain]], and [[Lindsey Graham]] as part of "the swamp" making a play to contest [[Donald Trump]]'s legitimacy through the special counsel.

(18:46:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the conflict as a war between the democratically elected [[Donald Trump]] and a "deep [[state]]" that had controlled [[United States|U.S]]. policy for 50 years, which was attempting to establish itself as the rightful ruler through public opinion.

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(28:04:92) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed the political strategy, arguing that even if both parties were blamed for a shutdown, [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] had more vulnerable [[Senate]] seats to lose in the 2018 midterms, giving [[Donald Trump]] an asymmetrical advantage.

(30:29:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[the left]]-wing paradigm dominated media, [[Los Angeles|Hollywood]], and culture, setting the default narrative that conservatives had to operate within and defend against.

### AF 2018-01-29 0096
(33:44:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[Donald Trump|President Trump]] tweet from Friday confirming his [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] proposition had two purposes: to seek a long-term fix and to illustrate [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] insincerity by their rejection of a "liberal plan."

(00:14:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that for political dissidents, social media platforms like [[Twitter]], [[Google|YouTube]], and [[Facebook]] are the only outlets to spread their message, as traditional media like newspapers, television, and radio have significant barriers to entry.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:15:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that major newspapers, television stations, and radio are controlled by very small corporate hands, creating an oligopoly.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:17:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that television broadcasting is tightly controlled, requiring government licenses from the [[Federal Communications Commission|FCC]] and significant investment capital.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:18:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a [[democratic]] system hinges on people being informed, and in the 21st century, the only alternative for dissident communication is social media.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:19:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that censorship on platforms like [[Twitter]] and [[Google|YouTube]] represents the suppression of the only decentralized medium for mass communication available to dissidents.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:20:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Paul Nealon]] was suspended from [[Twitter]] for posting a simple chart showing [[Jews|Jewish]] representation in media.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:20:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that it is important to know [[who]] runs the media, as it has a strong sway over the [[country]]'s politics and public opinion.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:21:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Paul Nealon]] was simply observing patterns of media representation, which could be attributed to various factors like IQ differences or group evolutionary strategy.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:22:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that if censorship continues, the public will only be able to get information through tightly controlled, censored outlets.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:22:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned [[who]] decides what constitutes hateful or "[[Germany|Nazi]]" speech on social media, arguing that the line is arbitrarily drawn.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:24:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited examples from the [[United Kingdom]] and [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]] where police investigated individuals for hate speech for discussing [[Islam|Muslim]] over-representation in crime.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:24:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that politicians are at the mercy of information gatekeepers like [[Twitter]] and [[Google|YouTube]], which can influence election outcomes.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093

(00:26:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced he would be campaigning for [[Paul Nealon]] over the summer and encouraged his viewers to do the same.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:26:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that figures like [[Ben Shapiro]] and [[Dave Rubin]] claim to be for free speech but do not know to what end, leading them to not fight for it correctly.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:27:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned why [[Paul Nealon]] was banned for his infographic while commentators like [[Jake Tapper]] and publications like [[The Atlantic]] can criticize the racial composition of [[Donald Trump]]'s cabinet.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:28:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that political correctness functions to allow criticism of white people and white men specifically, while prohibiting criticism of over-representation of other groups in negative contexts.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:29:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that it is acceptable in mainstream discourse to point out that the 1%, media, and government are controlled by white men, but not to discuss over-representation of other groups in crime, terrorism, or media.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:30:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the movement is forced to ask tough questions about group representation because they are hunted down with speech codes for simply wanting to put [[America first]].

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:31:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed the scandal involving [[Logan Paul]], [[who]] filmed a video in a [[State of Japan|Japanese]] suicide forest, made fun of a corpse, and later posted a suicide prevention video.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:32:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the "Logan [[Ron Paul|Paul]] episode" illustrates that the masses get what they deserve and that popular opinion or the mob is not a righteous or just governing force.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:33:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the liberal idea of expanding franchise and democratization, using the popularity of [[Logan Paul]] as an example of the mob's poor judgment.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:34:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Logan Paul]] is not a responsible [[person]], yet his audience expected responsibility from him and were outraged when he did not provide it.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:35:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the people [[who]] were outraged at [[Logan Paul]] are hypocrites, as they watch violent movies and support politicians like [[Barack Obama]] and [[Hillary Clinton]], [[who]] he claimed committed real war crimes.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:36:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that the same people outraged at [[Logan Paul]] will tell people like him or [[James Allsup|James Alsup]] to kill themselves on [[Twitter]].

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:36:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the "Logan [[Ron Paul|Paul]] episode" demonstrates that the mob is a faction with systemic flaws and malice, and should not be absolutist or govern the [[country]].

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:37:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced an article arguing for expanding voting rights to felons, questioning the driving impulse to see disenfranchisement as necessarily bad.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:37:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned why felons, illegals, first-generation immigrants, or people [[who]] do not own property should have "skin in the game" to vote.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:38:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States|American]] system of expanding franchise needs to be questioned and that society should be restructured on a hierarchy and balance of power, as the founders intended.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:39:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes, citing [[Patrick Buchanan]], argued that the foundation of the current [[democratic]] system is built on a pile of sand.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:42:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was still a 33% shareholder in [[America First Media]] and was waiting for a buyout to fund a new set for his show.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:45:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the [[organization]] of a rally by Richard Spencer in [[Gainesville]], where Spencer was on a big empty stage with a microphone, while [[Eli Mosley]] and [[Mike Cernovich|Mike Enoch]] stood behind him slouching in ill-fitting clothes.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:45:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if a movement claims to be competent, it should show up with professional optics, including flags, a podium, and efficient [[organization]], unlike the rally he described.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:46:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the [[Leadership Institute]], which he said is paid millions to teach simple event planning, like booking a venue with a capacity one-third the size of expected attendance to ensure a packed crowd.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:47:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended his criticisms of movement optics, stating he has the courage of his convictions and wants nothing but the survival of his people, but there is a right and a wrong way to achieve it.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:48:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that he blocks people on [[Twitter]] when they are rude, use profanity, or are not followers, but does not block followers offering constructive criticism.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:49:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes defined "Americanism" as [[United States|American]] nationalism or patriotism, the belief in [[United States|American]] culture being put first.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:49:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended the book *[[Who]] Are We?* by [[Samuel Huntington|Sam Huntington]] to understand [[United States|American]] identity.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:50:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes, summarizing [[Samuel Huntington|Sam Huntington]], stated that from 1600 to 1775, [[United States|American]] identity was ethnic, racial, and cultural; from 1775 to 1945, it was ethnic, racial, cultural, and political; from 1945 to 1965, it was racial, cultural, and political; from 1965 to 1990, it was merely cultural and political; and from 1990 to today, there is no cultural identity.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:52:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that while [[United States|American]] identity has eroded, it was not always the case, and the question is whether to rebuild a racial, cultural, and political identity, ideally akin to [[United States|America]] in 1945.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:54:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he had not seen the show *The Man in the High Castle* on [[Hulu]], but heard it was okay.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:55:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified the biggest systemic problem with Protestantism as *sola scriptura* (Bible alone) and the absence of an apostolic clergy with final authority on doctrine.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:56:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that without a central authority like the [[Catholic Church]], any group, like the Latin [[United States|American]] "Saint of Death" cult, can claim to be [[Christianity|Christian]], leading to doctrinal chaos.

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:56:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Catholic Church]], through the Bishop of [[Rome]] and Petrine Supremacy, is the only church with the legitimacy and infallibility to decide matters of faith, as established by [[Jesus Christ|Christ]].

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:57:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes encouraged readers to explore the works of [[G.K. Chesterton]] and [[C.S. Lewis]] on the subject of conversion and the [[Catholic Church]].

### AF 2018-01-24 0093
(00:59:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded the show by promoting subscriptions to "[[America First]] Premium" on [[Maker Support]] and asking for interest in the proposed "Premium Plus" tier and board game streams.

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a debate on [[United States|American]] policy towards [[Israel]] with guest [[Jacob Wohl]].

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:01:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States]] should treat [[Israel]] like any other [[country]] and cut all foreign aid, citing [[Israel]]'s illegal nuclear program, espionage activities, and history of selling [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. military]] technology to foreign countries.

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:07:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered that [[Israel]], the largest recipient of [[United States|U.S]]. foreign aid since 1978, conducts aggressive espionage against the [[United States]], steals technology, and expands settlements contrary to [[United States|U.S]]. policy, demonstrating a lack of [[United States|American]] influence.

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:18:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his opposition was not to [[Israel]]'s actions but to funding them with [[United States|U.S]]. aid, arguing that [[Israel]] deserves the most scrutiny as the number one recipient of foreign aid since 1978.

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:22:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced foreign policy scholars [[John Mearsheimer]] and [[Stephen Walt]], [[who]] argued in their book *The [[Israel Lobby]]* that the [[Mediterranean Sea|Eastern Mediterranean]] [[region]] where [[Israel]] is located is not strategically important for the [[United States]].

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:25:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted a former [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] official [[who]] characterized intelligence received from [[Israel]] as "laughably bad" and "gossip stuff," and stated it was never essential to [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. military]] power.

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:27:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the influence of the [[AIPAC]], which two-thirds of [[Congress]] attends annually, explains why the [[United States]] continues to fund [[Israel]] despite receiving poor intelligence.

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:29:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes drew a parallel between [[Pakistan]], a nuclear [[state]] that supports terrorist groups, and [[Israel]], a nuclear [[state]] whose defense minister expressed a preference for [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] control in [[Syria]] over [[Iran|Iranian]] proxies.

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:30:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited statements by [[Yoav Gallant|Israeli Defense Minister]] [[Moshe Ya'alon]] and a military intelligence chief indicating [[Israel]] did not want to see [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] defeated in [[Syria]], a position he argued was opposite to [[United States|U.S]]. policy under the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration.

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:35:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]]'s policy in [[Syria]] aimed to prevent a stable government in [[Damascus]] and retain control of the [[Israel|Golan Heights]], which was different from the [[United States|U.S]]. goal of stabilizing the [[region]] to eradicate [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]].

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:37:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Israel]] possesses an illegal nuclear program, citing the 1965-1980 "Apollo Affair" investigation where [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] Deputy Director [[Carl Duckett]] believed missing highly enriched uranium had been diverted to [[Israel]].

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:42:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited multiple sources, including a 2013 National Intelligence Estimate and the case of [[Larry Franklin]], to argue that [[Israel]] runs the third most aggressive espionage operation in the [[United States]] after [[Russia]] and [[China]].

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:45:02) [[Jacob Wohl]] asked Nicholas J. Fuentes if, as a [[Jews|Jewish]] [[person]], he was prejudiced in favor of [[Israel]], to which [[Jacob Wohl]] replied, "Absolutely."

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:45:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that individuals with prejudices in favor of other countries, rather than the [[United States]], have no place in [[United States|American]] politics, referencing the national motto "E pluribus unum."

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:46:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that more than half of the 25 neoconservative intellectuals [[who]] advocated for the war in [[Iraq]] were [[Jews|Jewish]], implying a connection to their pro-[[Israel]] prejudice.

(00:52:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented evidence that the [[Iraq]] war was promoted for [[Israel]]'s benefit, quoting [[Philip Zelikow]] of [[the President's]] Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board and General [[Wesley Clark]], [[who]] said the real, unspoken threat was to [[Israel]].

### AF 2018-01-15 0086
(00:55:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited post-war reviews which found that [[Israel]] provided false intelligence on [[Iraq]]'s WMD program and that [[Israel|Israeli]] officials publicly advocated for the war, demonstrating a shared interest in regional destabilization.

(01:01:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes read quotes from [[Israel|Israeli]] Prime Ministers [[David Ben-Gurion]] and [[Yitzhak Shamir]] to argue that [[Israel]] has a history of brutality and endorsement of terrorism, countering the claim that it is a "shining light" of morality.

### AF 2018-01-12 0085
(00:10:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] faced a political dilemma: either give [[Donald Trump]] concessions on the wall, chain migration, and the diversity visa lottery, or be held liable for a government shutdown and suffer in the midterm elections.

### AF 2018-01-12 0085
(00:12:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that if no [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] deal was made by March 5th, all [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] recipients would lose legal protection, and every day without a deal, a thousand [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] recipients lost protection and faced deportation risk.

### AF 2018-01-12 0085
(00:27:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes alleged that [[Hillary Clinton]] used her position as [[Secretary of State]] to funnel money into the [[Clinton Foundation]] and the [[Democratic Party]], used a private email server to cover it up, and that the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] covered up these actions.

### AF 2018-01-12 0085
(00:41:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was a big fan of [[Richard Nixon]], whom he considered among the last great [[United States|American]] statesmen and a pragmatist similar to [[Donald Trump]], praising his foreign policy of détente and the opening to [[China]].

### AF 2018-01-12 0085
(00:45:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Mike Pence]] was the only prominent member of the [[Donald Trump]] cabinet, besides [[Jefferson Beauregard Sessions III|Jeff Sessions]] and [[James Mattis]], to have lasted, and that [[Donald Trump]] had always been respectful of him.

### AF 2018-01-12 0085
(00:58:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Lauren Southern]] was a civic nationalist, not in his movement, and needed to stop straddling the line between the "alt-light" and his faction, and to stop giving play to "alt-light" people while ignoring his side.

### AF 2018-01-12 0085
(01:02:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes said he challenged Richard Spencer on his podcast, asking what made their position winnable in a hundred years when they would be a minority with no free speech or guns, and that Richard Spencer had no answer, believing instead in an impossible revolution.

(01:04:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes said he would have Richard Spencer on his show again, calling him a smart and interesting character, and that their previous argumentative conversation was a productive dialectic.

(00:11:18) Nicholas J. ### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:14:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the division between urban and rural life, and between cosmopolitan and traditional values, has been a feature of human civilization since ancient times, citing examples from the [[Rome|Roman Empire]], [[Babylon]], [[Israel]], and [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]].

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:14:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[city]] life, while offering economic and cultural benefits, tends towards moral degeneration due to higher population density, increased temptation, corruption, lower social trust, and higher crime.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:16:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed indifference towards net neutrality, describing it as a debate about bandwidth and companies like [[Twitter]] and [[Google|YouTube]] wanting [[Comcast]] to foot the bill for high-speed video streaming.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:16:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned he and [[James Allsup]] have discussed potentially streaming video games like *Civilization*, *Rust*, or *Minecraft* on [[Twitch Inc.|Twitch]].

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:18:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the 2017 [[Alabama]] special [[Senate]] election, where [[Doug Jones]] defeated [[Roy Moore]], was not a legitimate referendum due to dubious turnout numbers, a child sex scandal, and a poorly run campaign by [[Roy Moore]], and therefore holds no lessons for the 2018 midterms.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:19:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that in the [[Alabama]] special election, [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] turnout was 95% of their 2016 presidential election turnout while [[Republican Party|Republican]] turnout was less than 50%, and that [[black]] turnout exceeded levels from when [[Barack Obama]] was on the ticket in 2012.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:20:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that more informative examples for the 2018 political climate were the special elections in [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] and [[Montana]], not the [[Alabama]] race.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:22:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the 2017 [[State of Virginia|Virginia]] gubernatorial election, where [[Ed Gillespie]] lost, was not a referendum on [[Donald Trump]] because [[Ed Gillespie]] was not a strong immigration candidate and focused on issues like MS-13 and Confederate statues instead of a Trumpist platform.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:23:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that if [[Corey Stewart]] had been the [[State of Virginia|Virginia]] [[Republican Party|Republican]] candidate, the election might have been a referendum on [[Donald Trump]]'s platform, but [[Ed Gillespie]]'s candidacy was not.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:24:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes reiterated that the results of the [[Alabama]] [[Senate]] election were dubious, citing anomalous turnout numbers compared to 2016 and 2012.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:25:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the idea candidates must appeal to the majority of the electorate was disproven by [[Donald Trump]]'s 2016 primary and general election victories over candidates like [[Marco Rubio]] and [[Ted Cruz]].

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:25:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to [[Paul Nehlen]]'s failed primary challenge in [[Wisconsin]]'s 1st congressional district, a seat long held by [[Paul Ryan]], as an example where an establishment candidate with a large war chest defeated a challenger, not evidence against running on a Trumpist platform.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:26:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the [[Republican Party]] delivered [[Donald Trump]]'s agenda in [[Congress]] in spite of [[Paul Ryan]] and [[Mitch McConnell]], not because of them, and blamed [[John McCain]]'s lone vote for the failure to repeal Obamacare.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:26:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Paul Ryan]] and [[Mitch McConnell]] had no plan to repeal Obamacare ready when [[Donald Trump]] was inaugurated on January 20, 2017, despite their long tenures in leadership.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:29:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Australia]] faces a strategic dilemma, relying on [[China]] for economic prosperity while depending on the [[United States]] for security, walking a tightrope between the two powers.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:30:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed [[Australia]]'s future role as a choice between aligning with Asian countries due to geography or with Western countries due to [[heritage]] and history, a decision that will define its role in the Pacific in coming decades.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:30:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes proposed an "Anglo alliance" between former countries of the [[British Empire]]—[[Dominion of Canada|Canada]], the [[United States]], [[Australia]], [[India]], the [[United Kingdom]], [[Ireland]], and [[South Africa]]—as the best future option, aligning nations along ethnic, cultural, and civilizational lines as predicted by scholars like [[Samuel Huntington|Samuel P. Huntington]] and [[Robert D. Kaplan]].

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:31:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Samuel Huntington|Samuel P. Huntington]]'s *Clash of Civilizations* thesis and [[Robert D. Kaplan]]'s writings on ethnic and racial fault lines as frameworks for understanding how nations will realign in the post-Cold War world.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:32:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United Kingdom]] should turn away from the [[European Union]] and towards its former colonial holdings to form a solid Anglo alliance for the 21st century.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:32:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes defended the existence of a distinct [[United States|American]] culture, rooted in English, Protestant, and liberal traditions, against critics like [[Destiny]] [[who]] claim [[United States|Americans]] have no culture.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:34:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the organized infrastructure of mainstream conservatism, exemplified by the [[Turning Point USA]] conference with speakers like [[Sebastian Gorka]], [[Ben Shapiro]], and [[Brian Kilmeade]], with the disorganization of the [[America First]] or alt-right movement, which lacks consistent media output, publications, and cooperation.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:35:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified [[Ben Shapiro]] as the number one conservative commentator in [[United States|America]] due to his machinery, including [[The Daily Wire]] publication and [[Fox News]] platform.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:36:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized alt-right leaders for being unable to consistently produce podcasts or maintain websites, arguing that the movement has better ideas and people but lacks the [[organization]] and consistency to win.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:36:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Generation Identity]] and similar Identitarian groups are suited for [[Europe]] due to its history with that political style, but that such an insurgency would not work in the [[United States]] because of its two-party system and electoral structure.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:37:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that proportional representation systems in countries like the [[United Kingdom]] and [[Germany]] allow for smaller fringe parties like [[UK Independence Party|UKIP]] or the [[Green Party]] to have a voice, whereas the [[United States|US]] system forces a choice between [[Republican Party|Republican]] and [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] machines.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:38:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the only viable path in the [[United States]] is taking over the [[Republican Party]], not forming a separate [[Europe|European]]-style nationalist party.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:38:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed with [[Lauren Southern]]'s position that ethno-nationalism is appropriate for [[Europe]] but stated that in [[North America]], maintaining a white supermajority is optimal, though there is more historical wiggle room regarding minorities.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:39:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the [[United States]] was 20% [[black]] at its founding and that between 1900 and 1965, limited immigration from [[China]] and [[State of Japan|Japan]] occurred, arguing that a [[country]] can be 90% one racial group and still have limited, non-mass immigration from other places.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:41:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes endorsed the tactic of encouraging conservative-minded people to move to swing states in the [[Rust Belt]] and [[Midwest]] like [[Minnesota]], [[Wisconsin]], [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]], and [[New Hampshire]] to buy [[time]], as traditional [[Republican Party|Republican]] strongholds like [[State of Texas|Texas]], [[Arizona]], [[State of Florida|Florida]], [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]], [[State of Colorado|Colorado]], [[State of Virginia|Virginia]], and [[North Carolina]] are predicted to turn blue due to demographic changes.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:42:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted the [[Southwest]] and [[Southeast]] could break away from the [[Republican Party]] in the coming two decades due to demographic shifts.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:43:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that rethinking the [[Republican Party]] platform to be more labor-friendly and union-friendly could permanently flip states like [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]] by winning the white union vote, buying more [[time]] without requiring a mass population movement.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:45:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes distinguished his response to criticism from that of [[Tara McCarthy]], stating he never threatened to quit the movement if criticized or demanded defense from criticism, but takes issue with unfair, redundant, or unserious criticisms of his promotion of [[Catholicism]].

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:46:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his criticisms of others in the movement are intended to improve optics, planning, and [[organization]], unlike what he characterized as counterproductive criticisms from shitposters or potential astroturfers.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:48:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed his goals for the next five years as seeing the border wall built, [[Donald Trump]] re-elected, the [[Republican Party]] consolidated around the [[America First]] platform, [[Steve Bannon]] leading the political movement, and like-minded candidates entering congressional leadership.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:49:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the consolidation of [[Ronald Reagan]]'s conservatism within the [[Republican Party]] failed, leading to the [[Bush family]] and 25 years of bad government, and that consolidating the Trumpist gains within the party is the most important objective for success.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:49:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted [[Donald Trump]] is setting himself up for a strong position in the 2020 election, with [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] in a weak position, a dynamic similar to 2016, making re-election "most definitely within the realm of possibility."

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:50:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that many in the alt-right movement are making a mistake by shutting themselves off from the mainstream right and engaging in purity spiraling, which prevents growth and outreach.

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:51:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] as one of the best things to happen to the alt-right for creating a funnel from mainstream conservative commentators like [[Ben Shapiro]] and [[Steven Crowder]] into the nationalist movement, a pathway also facilitated by figures like [[Gavin McInnes]], [[Alex Jones]], and [[Paul Joseph Watson]].

### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:51:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed there is a concerted effort within the movement's leadership to cut off the flow of people from the alt-light, boarding the movement off from the rest of the world, which will lead to a plateau or decline unless the movement adopts a more pragmatic, [[America First]] nationalist brand.

(00:52:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that attendees of the [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]] [[who]] are part of the dissident right should go to the event in legitimate suits with legitimate purpose to network and discuss, rather than LARPing, as a test of the movement's seriousness.

(00:53:27) Nicholas J. ### AF 2017-12-22 0075
(00:54:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes emphasized the importance of a soft message and gradual red-pilling, recounting his own path from civil nationalism and support for [[Ron Paul]] to his current views, and criticizing those [[who]] attack newcomers as "cucks" as counterproductive.

(00:57:00) Nicholas J. (01:00:58) Nicholas J. (01:07:50) Nicholas J. (01:00:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that *Star Wars: The Last Jedi* presented an anti-white narrative by depicting the heroic Resistance as composed almost entirely of women, [[black]] people, and Asians, while the villainous First Order consisted solely of angry white men.

(02:12:41) Nicholas J. (01:00:36.780) Nicholas J. Fuentes broadcasts *[[America First]]* Monday through Friday at 7 PM Central [[Time]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:01:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the start of an episode of his show, [[America First]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:17:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the escalating nuclear crisis with [[North Korea]] had become the dominant international affairs issue of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] presidency.

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:19:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration's approach to [[North Korea]] with the "strategic patience" policy he attributed to the [[Obama]] administration.

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:23:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that a war with [[North Korea]] would be justifiable due to the fundamental conflict between its goal of securing nuclear weapons and the [[United States]]'s strategic objective of preventing WMD proliferation.

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:28:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the [[United States]] had pursued military action against [[Iraq]] and [[Afghanistan]] while not addressing nuclear programs in [[North Korea]], [[Iran]], [[Pakistan]], and [[Israel]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:31:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused the [[United States]] of supporting and bankrolling a humanitarian disaster in [[Yemen]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:33:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes placed culpability for the [[North Korea|North Korean]] crisis on the past inaction of former presidents [[Bill Clinton]] and [[George W. Bush]], calling it criminal negligence.

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:40:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-il]] was less reckless and accelerationist than his successor, [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:44:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the long-term significance of the [[North Korea]] crisis was as a litmus test for future [[United States]] relations with a rising [[China]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:48:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[China]] as a more formidable potential adversary than the former [[Soviet Union]], with a GDP nearly equal to that of the [[United States]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:51:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes credited [[Donald Trump]] with setting a cooperative yet assertive tone in the [[United States]]'s relationship with [[China]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(00:51:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended the books *The Tragedy of Great Power Politics* and *The [[Israel Lobby]]* by [[John Mearsheimer|Mearsheimer]], and *World Order* and *On [[China]]* by [[Henry Kissinger]].

### AF 2017-11-28 0059
(01:04:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Jordan Peterson]] for attending a rescheduled event at [[Ryerson University]] after fellow panelist [[Faith Goldy]] was disinvited due to her appearance on the *Daily Stormer* podcast.

(01:25:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed the view that the post-Vatican II papacy was illegitimate and had been infiltrated, aligning himself with traditionalist [[Catholic]] sects like those following [[Bishop Williamson]].

(00:03:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the availability of [[America First]] mugs for sale on AmFirstMedia.com, priced at $10.00 and scheduled to begin shipping in December.

### AF 2017-11-27 0058
(00:44:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a 2010 [[Pew Research Center|Pew Research]] study finding that 15% of all new marriages in the [[United States]] were interracial, a significant increase from 3.2% in 1980.

### AF 2017-11-27 0058
(00:48:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a study from the [[United States National Library of Medicine|National Library of Medicine]] which found that mixed marriages involving [[blacks]] and whites were the least stable, followed by [[Hispanic]]-white couples, while Asian-white marriages were more stable than same-race white marriages.

### AF 2017-11-27 0058
(00:51:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited another [[United States National Library of Medicine|National Library of Medicine]] study which found that interracial couples demonstrated higher levels of mutual intimate partner violence than monoracial white couples.

### AF 2017-11-27 0058
(00:52:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a study from the [[University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill|University of North Carolina]] which concluded that partners in inter-ethnic unions generally reported lower levels of relationship quality than those in same-ethnic unions.

### AF 2017-11-27 0058
(00:59:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a [[United States National Library of Medicine|National Library of Medicine]] study which found that mixed-race adolescents had increased risks for health problems, substance abuse, and behavioral problems compared to non-mixed adolescents.

### AF 2017-11-27 0058
(01:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a [[National Survey on Drug Use and Health|National Household Survey on Drug Abuse]] finding that drug use was highest among those reporting to be of mixed-race.

### AF 2017-11-27 0058
(01:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a 2008 study which found that biracial Asian [[United States|Americans]] were twice as likely as monoracial Asian [[United States|Americans]] to be diagnosed with a psychological disorder.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:00:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced a Thanksgiving call-in special for his show, inviting viewers to contact him via Skype.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:01:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted receiving an unsolicited email from a woman attempting to sell him a beauty product kit and requesting he purchase it and create an unboxing video for his channel.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:03:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes lamented the lack of high-quality audio equipment for his show, attributing it to not having "[[Israel]] money" for headsets and other production gear.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:11:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes, referencing a conversation on Nationalist Review, argued that [[Steve Bannon]]'s strategy to win working-class [[Hispanic]] and [[black]] voters was a pragmatic necessity for the [[Republican Party]]'s electoral survival, not an act of capitulation.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:15:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised that the optimal system of government for the [[United States]] should be a modified version of its original constitutional federalism, potentially incorporating elements of "Caesarism" to address future challenges.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:19:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes, influenced by Oswald Spengler, argued that societies naturally degenerate due to entropy, making utopian political ideals like those of [[George Lincoln Rockwell]] unrealistic.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:21:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes, referencing Oswald Spengler, suggested that a "second religiosity" and strong, Caesarist government could potentially reverse Western cultural decay and reinstitute traditional family values.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:28:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Ben Shapiro]] and similar commentators for offering only a negative critique of liberalism without providing a positive, actionable vision based on responsibility, tradition, and nationalism.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:34:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified a lack of clear, actionable objectives as a fundamental weakness of the alt-right and the broader right-wing movement, contrasting it with [[the left]]'s defined legislative goals.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:39:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes, from a [[Christianity|Christian]] perspective, discussed the tension between human responsibility to act and the need to surrender to God's omnipotent will, particularly in the context of eschatological certainty.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:44:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the primary culpability for [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] terrorist threats in the [[Kanye West|West]] lies with Western governments that import the fighters, not the fighters themselves [[who]] are products of a war-torn environment.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:45:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the border between the [[United States]] and [[Mexico]] represents the largest wealth disparity and longest contiguous border between a First World and a Third World [[country]].

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:46:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Faith Goldy]]'s prediction that [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]]'s white population would decline to 20% within a hundred years, a demographic change he criticized.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:52:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued against pure free trade, contending that [[China]]'s massive trade surplus allows it to acquire [[United States|U.S.]] assets, debt, and currency, which it then uses to manipulate exchange rates and perpetuate the imbalance, threatening [[United States|American]] economic sovereignty.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(00:56:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes diverged from the alt-right's primary focus on creating a white ethnostate, arguing that demographic issues are a symptom of a deeper cultural and spiritual collapse within white society that must be addressed.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:00:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed rising tensions between [[Saudi Arabia]] and [[Iran]], tracing the conflict to a [[Saudi Arabia|Saudi]]-led boycott of [[Qatar]] in July, the forced resignation of [[Lebanon]]'s Prime Minister [[Saad Hariri]], and a missile strike by [[Houthis|Houthi]] rebels, viewing it as a regional cold war orchestrated by [[Saudi Arabia|Saudi Crown Prince]] [[Mohammed bin Salman]].

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:09:18) A viewer named Alyssa Cordelia thanked Nicholas J. Fuentes for recommending the documentaries *Marching to Zion* and *Neon Genesis Evangelion* and asked for documentary recommendations on Vatican II.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:13:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised that red-pilling apathetic friends with right-wing values requires patience, persistence, and an approach focused on asking questions and using humor, rather than aggressive debate.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:18:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified that he did not definitively label [[David Duke]] a federal agent but expressed suspicion due to Duke's conspicuous past teaching English in [[Laos]] and his consistently damaging public optics for the movement.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:19:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that while [[David Duke]] made early contributions on issues like affirmative action, association with him is politically detrimental to achieving electoral goals and passing reform, necessitating a pragmatic separation.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:24:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized Richard Spencer for endorsing [[The Daily Stormer]], arguing that such associations represent a reckless, goal-less offensiveness that has resulted in significant tactical losses for the movement, such as being deplatformed.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:29:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed *The Turner Diaries* as "larpy, ridiculous nonsense" and criticized its revolutionary ethnostate narrative as an impractical alternative to the hard work of winning elections and building a political movement.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:30:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that while [[Islam]] has a rich cultural history, it is fundamentally incompatible with Western civilization and belongs in its native regions, not in the [[Kanye West|West]], though he opposed the anti-[[Islam|Muslim]] double standard of pro-[[Israel]] commentators.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:42:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes responded to a question about Slavs being white by stating that from a utilitarian perspective, they must be "grandfathered in" due to demographic necessity, though he distinguished Northern [[Italian Republic|Italians]] and Neapolitans as white while excluding Sicilians.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:45:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the core problem facing the [[Kanye West|West]] is not primarily racial but a loss of cultural identity and values, which allowed for subversion and demographic change, a point he believes the ethnostate-focused alt-right ignores.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:52:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized modern [[Protestantism]] as nearly heretical in its practices, arguing that its "Bible alone" theology systematically leads to theological degeneration and a lack of authority, unlike the incidental corruptions possible in [[Catholicism]].

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(01:55:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes distinguished his ideology as communitarianism rather than collectivism, asserting that the family and community are the fundamental building blocks of society, and that individualism fails to provide existential meaning.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(02:01:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the revolutionary, anti-electoral visions of some on the far right are impractical, and that winning elections through a patriotic, [[United States|American]] nationalist message is the only viable path to political power and reform.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(02:08:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes, a former libertarian, rejected libertarianism's core principle, arguing that the goal of society should be virtue and what is good for people, not maximal liberty, which can lead to harm.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(02:18:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes endorsed waging political battles at the family dinner table during the holidays, advising a strategy of planting seeds with simple, earnest arguments about the [[country]]'s direction rather than attempting to win debates aggressively.

(02:20:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced plans for a [[Kanye West|West]] Coast college tour in 2018 with [[James Allsup]], with a sign-up sheet posted on Allsup's [[Twitter]].

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(02:23:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that commentators like [[Ann Coulter]], [[Rush Limbaugh]], and [[Sean Hannity]] remain relevant despite the shift to new media, due to their millions of followers, though their traditional mediums are fading.

### AF 2017-11-22 0056
(02:32:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes, when asked to choose between commentators [[Lauren Southern]] and [[Lana Lokteff]], selected Southern, describing her as beautiful and smart while making a joking critique of her Canadian accent.

(41:38:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes responded to a comment from [[James Paul]] about his age and content.

(45:03:86) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Robert E. Lee]] was a good man [[who]] served his [[country]].

(45:13:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[David Duke]] was a federal agent, citing his [[time]] teaching English in [[Cambodia]] in the 1970s during or after the Vietnam War, and considered him a "[[black]] dot" on the political movement due to his past as a Klansman.

### AF 2017-11-21 0055
(46:27:84) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump's]] success in advocating policies like a [[Islam|Muslim]] ban and criticizing Mexican immigrants was due to his disarming, humorous persona as a reality TV star.

(47:33:68) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized members of the alt-right for marching with torches and talking about physically removing people, contrasting their aggressive optics with [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s more persuasive approach.

(49:17:64) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced criticism of himself on "Nationalist Review" where commenters called him a "cuck."

(49:43:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he and [[David Duke]] were mutuals on [[Twitter]] and considered him a meme, similar to how [[Samuel J. Hyde|Sam Hyde]] used him, but asserted Duke was not viable for their political movement.

(50:37:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] decision legalizing same-sex marriage in all 50 states as the culmination of a decades-long cultural campaign, not an inflection point.

(51:21:86) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed ambivalence about civil unions for homosexuals but stated he was against homosexual marriage in the church and considered homosexuality a deviant, degenerate way of life.

(54:50:62) Nicholas J. Fuentes responded to a Thanksgiving greeting from a viewer named CoolApple.

(55:02:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised a viewer named Saxon Runes that women seeking a traditional ("trad") man should go to church and avoid comic book readers, feminists, Reddit users, Rick and Morty watchers, and "vapors."

(55:27:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was not a fan of [[Michael Savage]]'s radio show and had never listened to it, though he had listened to [[Mark Levin]], [[Sean Hannity]], and [[Alex Jones]].

### AF 2017-11-21 0055
(55:48:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected the [[concept]] of an "ethnostate" as a misguided and politically unviable term, arguing that policies like deportations and ending the entitlement system could achieve demographic objectives without using the term.

### AF 2017-11-21 0055
(57:00:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes reiterated to a viewer named Maximilian Pickleman that he did not want an ethnostate, as [[United States|America]] was never one.

(57:11:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was not a big fiction reader, preferring to learn about topics not taught in the education system, but named Fyodor Dostoevsky as his favorite fiction author.

(57:30:80) Nicholas J. Fuentes thanked a viewer named Gene E for calling his show "worth more than two shekels."

(57:36:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes responded to a viewer named Thomas Jefferson about his MBTI type, stating he had received results of INTP, ENFJ, INTJ, and most recently INFJ or INTP.

(58:08:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes labeled the [[organization]] [[Young Americans for Freedom]] as "cucks" for their stance on mass immigration.

(58:20:70) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that people in the alt-right had poisoned minds by making the [[concept]] of an ethnostate seem feasible, creating a problematic element within the movement.

### AF 2017-11-21 0055
(58:54:70) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded his show *[[America First]]*, directing viewers to his content on [[Gab]] under the handle Nick J. Fuentes and noting he might not be on [[Twitter]] for long.

(59:19:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his show *[[America First]]* airs Monday through Friday at 7 PM Central, 8 PM Eastern, followed by *Overdrive with [[James Allsup]]* at 8 PM Central, 9 PM Eastern.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(01:06:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes considered [[Samuel J. Hyde|Sam Hyde]] his favorite account on [[Twitter]] and checked it daily for content he found humorous.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(02:53:74) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Samuel J. Hyde|Sam Hyde]] was the last comedian worthy of the title in the modern day, surpassing mainstream comedians like [[Bill Burr]], [[Louis C.K.]], and others.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(04:46:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Samuel J. Hyde|Sam Hyde]] as a modern-day Diogenes, willing to live an ascetic life and forgo mainstream success by not compromising his principles.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(06:56:94) Nicholas J. Fuentes watched the trailer for *Incredibles 2* and was excited due to an emotional connection to the [[Pixar]] franchise from his childhood.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(09:03:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes interpreted the plot of *Incredibles 2* as propaganda promoting stay-at-home fathers and working mothers, contrasting it with what he saw as the relatively nonpartisan original film.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(10:25:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that by 2017, television, [[Los Angeles|Hollywood]], and social media platforms like [[Twitter]], [[Facebook]], and [[Google|YouTube]] were ubiquitously saturated with a political and social agenda, leaving no cultural reprieve.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(11:59:90) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the rebooted *Star Wars* sequels and *Rogue One* as examples of films that featured female protagonists and diverse casts as part of a deliberate ideological messaging campaign by [[Disney]].

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(12:39:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to the film *Spider-Man: Homecoming*, which featured a character mentioning the [[Washington Monument]] was built by slaves and had a working-class white union construction worker as the villain, as another example of pervasive messaging.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(13:11:92) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the film adaptation of *A Wrinkle in [[Time]]* as an example where classic source material was altered to feature [[black]] women as ambassadors of the universe, which he saw as part of a broader pattern.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(15:44:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the cultural divide in the [[United States]] was irreconcilable because it was based on fundamental disagreements over truth, knowledge, and metaphysics, such as materialism versus belief in a soul and creator.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(16:56:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that one side of the cultural conflict, which he associated with postmodernism and relativism, sought to dominate all cultural institutions rather than coexist in separate spheres.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(18:05:66) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that teachers with master's degrees, educated by professors [[who]] were themselves taught by Marxists over decades, were indoctrinating young people at a fundamental level within the education system.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(20:23:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes admitted to having seen *Star Wars: The Force Awakens* and *Rogue One*, despite his criticism of their "female protagonist" focus.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(22:51:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes could only identify a handful of historical women, such as [[Joan of Arc]] and possibly [[Rosa Luxemburg]], [[who]] matched the "spunky, rule-rewriting" archetype he argued was artificially prevalent in modern media.

### AF 2017-11-20 0054
(24:43:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the promotion of career-focused, independent female archetypes in media leads women to end up "35 alone, no kids, ovaries dried up," which he claimed tears apart the fabric of society.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced a debate on his show with [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] ([[Destiny]]) regarding immigration policy, framing it as a rematch of a previous two-hour discussion.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:01:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his previous debate with [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] on immigration was relatively civil and lasted two hours.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:02:15) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] outlined his debate method, which involved restating Nicholas J. Fuentes's arguments for verification before challenging them.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:02:51) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] summarized Nicholas J. Fuentes's argument that the 1965 Hart-Celler Act did not represent the will of the [[United States|American]] people, [[who]] would have opposed its transformative demographic effects had they been fully informed, a summary Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed with.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:04:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that poll questions about judging immigrants on non-national origin criteria were not equivalent to supporting the Hart-Celler Act's specific provisions to eliminate national origin quotas and numerical limits entirely.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:06:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that [[Congress]] may have had a better understanding of the Hart-Celler Act's transformative demographic effects than the general public, citing historical intellectual antecedents like the Kalergi Plan and Hooton Plan in [[Europe]].

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:06:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that mass immigration, as enabled by the 1965 Act, was never put to a national referendum or made a central issue in a major presidential or midterm election.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:13:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that intellectuals had previously theorized using demographic transformation to inflict harm on [[Europe]], providing an intellectual antecedent for speculating about deceptive motives behind the Hart-Celler Act.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:14:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a 1965 [[Cornell University|Cornell]]/Roper Center poll indicating [[United States|Americans]] least wanted immigrants from [[Asia]] (15%), the [[Middle East]], [[Mexico]] (11%), and [[Latin America]], and most wanted immigrants from [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]] and [[United Kingdom|England]].

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:17:57) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] argued that Nicholas J. Fuentes's poll about immigrant preferences did not demonstrate that [[United States|Americans]] wanted to restrict immigration based on national origin, which was the central question of the Hart-Celler Act.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:20:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the passage of the Hart-Celler Act to the [[Iraq War]], arguing the public was misled about its consequences, expecting immigrants from [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]] and [[Region of Scandinavia|Scandinavia]] but receiving them from [[Mexico]] and [[Asia]].

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:21:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a 1944 Roper poll where 41% of [[United States|Americans]] said the [[United States]] should "stop" [[Mexican]] immigration after World War II, arguing it showed a consistent public opinion against such immigration from 1944 to 1965.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:24:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that congressional consensus does not reflect national consensus, using the example of 99 [[United States|U.S]]. Senators voting for aid to [[Israel]] as a demonstration of lobbying power rather than public will.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:29:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] of misleadingly representing the 1965 [[Kamala Harris|Harris]] poll data as showing a supermajority (70%) for change, when only 36% explicitly supported changing from national origin to skills.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:39:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the 70% approval in the December 1965 poll was based on public misinformation, citing statements by Senator [[Edward Kennedy|Ted Kennedy]] that the act would not change the nation's demographic composition or cause immigrant floods.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:41:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed the Immigration Act of 1790, 1795, 1798, the 1802 law, the 1882 [[China|Chinese]] Exclusion Act, the 1908 end to [[State of Japan|Japanese]] immigration, the 1917 Asiatic Barred Zone Act, and the 1921/1924 national origin quotas as a 200-year precedent of immigration restriction overturned by the Hart-Celler Act.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:45:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that overturning 200-year precedent requires a majority, not a plurality, and that undecided or ambivalent voters in a fundamental transformation would side with maintaining the status quo.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:48:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the social contract in the [[United States]] was formed by a charter society in 1790, and that the will of the descendant population in 1965 should have precedence over recent immigrants in determining the nation's civilizational [[destiny]].

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(00:50:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that political blowback against the Hart-Celler Act manifested decades later, culminating in the election of [[Donald Trump]] in 2016, and cited earlier resistance like bilingual school rejections and official English laws.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:03:30) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] summarized Nicholas J. Fuentes's "original intent" argument, stating the [[U.S. Constitution]]'s preamble phrase "ourselves and our posterity," combined with [[John Jay]]'s Federalist No. 2 description of "a people descended from the same ancestors," showed the document was designed to protect [[Europe|European]] [[Christianity|Christian]] people.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:04:23) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] challenged Nicholas J. Fuentes's original intent argument by noting [[John Jay]] also wanted to keep [[Catholics]] out of [[New York]], questioning if that intention should also be respected.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:04:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented a framework from [[Samuel Huntington]]'s *[[Who]] Are We?* charting the erosion of [[United States|American]] identity components: ethnic (1607-1965), racial (1775-1990), cultural (1607-1990), and political (1775-present), arguing for preserving what remains.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:07:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the "e pluribus unum" motto referred to creating one [[United States|American]] identity from many *[[Europe|European]]* identities, noting the 1790 census showed the population was 80% [[United Kingdom|British]] and 98% Protestant.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:08:50) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] argued that [[John Quincy Adams]]'s and [[George Washington]]'s quotes did not specify only [[Europe|European]] immigrants, and that Nicholas J. Fuentes was projecting a modern multicultural definition onto historical texts.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:09:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that interpreting [[John Quincy Adams]]'s quote as supporting non-[[Europe|European]] immigration was anachronistic, ignoring the historical context of a slave-holding society where founders like [[Thomas Jefferson]] believed [[blacks]] and whites could not live under the same government.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:10:20) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] stated the [[U.S. Constitution]] does not favor [[Europe|European]] immigrants, placing the burden of proof on Nicholas J. Fuentes to demonstrate that the founders' [[concept]] of "white" aligned with his modern definition.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:10:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Benjamin Franklin]]'s 1751 essay expressing a preference to increase "the lovely White" population and excluding "Swarthy" [[Germany|Germans]], except [[Saxony]], whom he called "the most stupid of her own nation."

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:11:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that founder quotes about casting off [[Europe|European]] skin referred to creating a new [[United States|American]] identity from existing [[Europe|European]] stocks, not an invitation for global immigration.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:12:04) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] argued the Federalist Papers were written to unite disparate colonies into one federal government, not as a commentary on immigration, and that Nicholas J. Fuentes extrapolated his entire argument from the single word "posterity."

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:13:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Alexander Hamilton]] warning that an "influx of foreigners" produces a "heterogeneous compound" that corrupts the national spirit, and [[Edmund Randolph]] stating slaves are not constituent members of society.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:14:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Thomas Jefferson]]'s belief that whites and [[blacks]] could not live in the same government, and that [[Thomas Jefferson|Jefferson]], [[James Madison]], [[Henry Clay]], [[John Randolph]], and [[Abraham Lincoln]] supported colonizing Africans back to [[African continent|Africa]].

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:15:58) [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] argued [[Alexander Hamilton]]'s quote opposed *mass* immigration generally, not non-[[Europe|European]] immigration specifically, and thus did not support Nicholas J. Fuentes's original intent argument.
