(01:17:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified his immigration policy position as being against mass immigration generally and preferring a hierarchy with preference for Anglo-Saxon/Northern [[Europe|European]] immigrants, then Eastern/Southern [[Europe|Europeans]], then skilled non-whites.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:19:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[George Washington]] signing the 1790 and 1795 Immigration Acts, which limited naturalization to "free white persons," demonstrated the founders' intent for a white nation.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:21:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the founders did not codify a white nation in the [[U.S. Constitution]] because, in the 18th-century colonial world, the [[concept]] of sovereign non-white countries mass-immigrating was inconceivable, not because they intended otherwise.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:26:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the term "posterity" in the [[U.S. Constitution]]'s preamble, defined by [[John Jay]] as a people of the same ancestors, meant the document was dedicated to the offspring of the founding [[stock]], not to immigrants from [[Mexico]] or [[African continent|Africa]].

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:28:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes and [[Destiny|Stephen Bonnell]] debated whether the founders' intent is defined solely by the [[U.S. Constitution]]'s text or also by their contemporaneous laws and writings, with Nicholas J. Fuentes maintaining the latter demonstrated an intent for a white nation.(01:44:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that immigrants from countries with severe problems are likely to bring those problems to the [[United States]], and that a sufficient number of such immigrants could cause the [[country]] to resemble their nations of origin.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:45:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged the assertion that immigrants commit less crime than the native-born population, arguing that any crime committed by immigrants constitutes a net negative that would not have occurred without their presence.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:45:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the economic benefits of immigration are a separate argument from the social costs, which include crime, disease, and cultural disruption.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:47:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that immigrant communities, such as Chinatown, physically and culturally resemble the countries the immigrants left, citing this as evidence that people's problems cannot be divorced from their homelands.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:48:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the high murder rate among [[black]] [[United States|Americans]] is comparable to rates in [[Kanye West|West]] African and Sub-Saharan African countries, which he presented as evidence that ancestral problems persist across generations.

(01:49:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that immigrants from [[Mexico]] bring drug cartels and other crime to the [[United States]], and that they form enclaves where they do not learn English.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:49:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that second-generation immigrants commit more crime than the first generation, which he attributed to them integrating toward native-born crime norms.

(01:50:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Hispanic|Hispanics]] and [[blacks]] commit much more crime proportionally than white people, a point he later attempted to support with [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] arrest data.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(01:52:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes reiterated his position that the social problems observed in [[black]] [[United States|American]] communities demonstrate an inability to divorce people from the systemic problems of their ancestral homelands.

(01:56:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the societal systems created by [[black]] populations in [[African continent|Africa]] are demonstrative of the effect [[black]] people will have in [[United States|American]] society.

(01:57:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced the so-called "Warrior Gene" when questioned about a genetic predisposition for crime, though he stated he was not a doctor and could not speculate.

(01:58:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the moral cost of citizens being murdered for the sake of cheaper consumer goods is too high to justify immigration.

(01:59:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the cultural disruption and multiple negatives brought by immigrants, including crime, are not outweighed by any positives.

(02:01:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] data showing that white people, [[who]] are 67% of the population, account for 44% of murders, which he presented as evidence of disproportionate crime by other groups.

(02:01:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes was challenged on his crime statistics with the argument that they are not adjusted for the younger median age of the [[Hispanic]] population compared to the white population.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:03:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the higher [[Hispanic]] fertility rate means they will remain a younger demographic for a long [[time]], sustaining higher crime rates compared to whites.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:04:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he would prefer white people to have more children, even if it meant more crime from young white people, because it is "our [[country]]" and he wants it to remain culturally the same.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:05:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that crime from immigrants is problematic because it is "from somewhere else" and is controllable, whereas crime from the native population is an inherent part of society.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:05:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if his sole goal was less crime, he would advocate for becoming an "Asian [[country]]," but his primary concern is preserving the existing cultural character of the nation.

(02:08:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized studies on immigrant crime rates, arguing that [[unknown]] numbers of illegal immigrants make accurate incarceration or crime rate calculations impossible.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:10:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that diseases such as tuberculosis, polio, and the bubonic plague are being spread in the [[United States]] due to immigration from countries where these diseases are common.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:13:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a report from the [[Minnesota]] Department of Health stating that in 2014, 26% of foreign-born tuberculosis cases in the [[state]] were from people born in [[Somalia]].

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:15:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that refugees and immigrants are the same type of people and pose similar risks, claiming that people being resettled from [[Somalia]] are similar to those immigrating from [[Mexico]].

(02:18:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a 2015 report from the Southern Medical Association which cautioned that unscreened illegal entries may expose [[United States|Americans]] to diseases that have been virtually eradicated.

(02:19:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the scale, persistence, and regional concentration of [[Mexican]] immigration differ from past [[Europe|European]] immigration, hindering assimilation.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:29:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed six reasons from scholar [[Samuel Huntington]] why [[Mexican]] immigration is different and more resistant to assimilation: contiguity to the [[United States]], unprecedented scale, high rates of illegality, regional concentration in the Southwest, persistent waves over [[time]], and a historical claim to the [[region]] dating to before the [[United States|U.S]]. conquest.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:33:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited data that two-thirds of [[Mexicans]] in the [[United States]] lived in the [[Kanye West|West]] in 2000, with nearly half in [[State of California|California]], demonstrating extreme regional concentration.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:34:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized large-scale [[Mexican]] immigration as an "invasion" of the [[country]], distinct from [[Europe|European]] immigration due to historical claims to the Southwestern [[United States]].

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:38:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed that the [[United States|U.S]]. should return to the national origins quota immigration policy under which his own [[Mexican]] ancestors entered the [[country]].

(02:41:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified himself as "Castizo," meaning of predominantly Spanish descent from the [[Latin]] world, to distinguish his ethnicity from the broader [[Mexican]] or [[Hispanic]] categories.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:43:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the definition of "white" has expanded over [[time]] to include [[Italian Republic|Italians]], [[Ireland|Irish]], [[Germany|Germans]], and [[Spaniards]], showing that the [[concept]] of [[United States|American]] identity can grow.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:52:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Hispanic]] enclaves in cities like [[Los Angeles]] do not integrate, citing Spanish-language signs and bilingual schools as evidence, despite data showing rising English proficiency.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:54:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the ongoing demographic transformation of states like [[State of California|California]], [[State of Texas|Texas]], [[Arizona]], and [[State of New Mexico|New Mexico]] into [[Hispanic]]-majority regions is a problem because the [[United States|U.S]]. took that land from [[Mexico]] in the 1840s, inspiring regionalist sentiment.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(02:57:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes defined core [[United States|American]] values from 1607 to 1965 as the [[Christianity|Christian]] religion, [[Protestant]] values and moralism, the [[Protestant]] work ethic, the [[English]] language, [[United Kingdom|British]] traditions of law and limits on government, and the legacy of [[Europe|European]] art, literature, philosophy, and music.

### AF 2017-11-08 0049
(03:00:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that being [[Christianity|Christian]] was a core part of the [[United States|American]] character from 1607 to 1965, and that preserving the [[Christianity|Christian]] religion is a key goal.

(01:59:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized [[Donald Trump]]'s proposed tax plan, which included reducing corporate tax rates, consolidating income tax brackets, raising the standard deduction, increasing the child tax credit, and eliminating the [[state]] and local tax deduction.

(00:13:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the establishment's primary rule was to forbid advocacy for white people, using [[Robert Mercer]]'s defunding of [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] after his association with Richard Spencer as a definitive example.

(00:15:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged [[Robert Mercer]]'s stated commitment to free speech, asking why free speech protections did not extend to himself or Richard Spencer when discussing topics like [[Israel]] or white demographic displacement.

Bush|Jeb Bush]] and [[Marco Rubio]] attacked each other heavily, as an example of how fierce internal competition can produce a strong general election candidate like [[Donald Trump]].

Bush|Jeb Bush]] and [[Marco Rubio]].

(00:52:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the easy, "fun" activism of attending rallies and listening to edgy podcasts with the difficult, unglamorous work of knocking on doors, running for office, and moderating messages for political gain.

(00:10:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported receiving phone calls from business connections stating they could not work with him due to his comments about [[Israel]] and [[Jews|Jewish]] influence.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:12:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a statistic showing [[Jews|Jewish people]] gave evangelicals a favorability rating of around 30 out of 100, indicating low warmth towards that religious group.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:13:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that in 1933, international [[Jews|Jewry]] declared a boycott of [[Germany]] after [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]] was appointed Chancellor, and that Zionists later established a working economic relationship with the [[Nazi government]] to facilitate [[Jews|Jewish]] emigration to [[Palestine]].

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:15:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes described a Zionist program where [[Jews]] in [[Germany]] sold their assets and then used the funds to buy [[Germany|German]] manufactured goods for import to [[Palestine]], in cooperation with the [[Nazi government]].

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:16:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Israel]] should be treated like any other [[country]] and that he opposes [[Israel|Israeli]], [[China|Chinese]], [[Saudi Arabia|Saudi]], [[Russian]], and [[France|French]] influence in [[United States|America]] with equal vigor.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:17:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes discussed a [[The New York Times|New York Times]] story about [[U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement|ICE]] detaining a 10-year-old girl with cerebral palsy after emergency gallbladder surgery, which was paid for by [[Medicaid]].

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:18:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the girl's family entered the [[United States]] illegally from [[Mexico]] a decade prior because they believed they could not afford adequate healthcare in [[Mexico]], which already has a [[universal]] system.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:19:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Medicaid]] funding comes from [[United States|U.S.]] taxpayers, not an abstract program, and that the story highlights [[Mexico]]'s failing healthcare system and economy.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:21:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that illegal immigrants [[who]] are on [[Medicaid]] are likely net consumers of government resources rather than contributors to the economy through taxes.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:23:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the narrative that immigration primarily benefits the immigrants, arguing it is a slap in the face to the ancestors [[who]] built [[United States|America]] and that the [[country]] does not need people [[who]] contribute "social strife, cultural stagnation, [and] economic backwardness."

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:25:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected the "[[Ron Paul|Paul]] Ryan myth" that immigrants are all potential entrepreneurs like [[Steve Jobs]], stating they often create language and cultural barriers and transform the [[country]]'s demographic makeup.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:26:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that in a debate, he argued against immigration not on economic grounds but on the principle that [[United States|America]] should remain [[United States|American]], regardless of whether immigrants contribute economically.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:27:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes said the [[United States]] leads the world in immigration with 43 million people per year, while the next largest recipient takes in 11 million, and noted that [[African continent|Africa]] and [[Latin America]] accept very [[little]] immigration.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:28:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if immigrants were so beneficial, other countries like [[Mexico]] and [[China]] would eagerly accept them, but they do not.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:32:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted being in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] when [[Chuck Schumer]], [[Bernie Sanders]], [[Nancy Pelosi]], and [[Tammy Duckworth]] led a protest against the [[United States|American]] Health Care Act, and saw a reporter ask [[Chuck Schumer]] about a bill making it illegal to boycott [[Israel]].

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:33:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that in [[France]], it is illegal to deny the [[United States Holocaust Memorial Museum|Holocaust]] but legal to deny the Armenian genocide, as courts struck down a law against the latter on free expression grounds.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel]] has been the number one recipient of [[United States|U.S.]] foreign aid since 1976 or 1978, receiving the most money under the most generous terms compared to any other [[country]].

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:35:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that [[United States|U.S.]] aid to [[Israel]] is given in the form of loans rather than grants to avoid [[United States|U.S.]] military oversight, and these loans are effectively forgiven, constituting grants in all but name.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:36:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes said [[Israel]] receives its annual $3.8 billion aid package in a lump sum within the first 30 days of the fiscal year, then invests it in [[United States|U.S.]] [[Treasury]] bonds to earn approximately $150 million in interest.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:37:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the next largest recipients of [[United States|U.S.]] aid are [[Jim Jordan|Jordan]] and [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]], which each receive about $1 billion annually, largely to maintain peace with [[Israel]].

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:38:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]] disregards [[United States|U.S.]] interests by building civilian settlements in the [[Gaza Strip|West Bank]], which are illegal under international law and opposed by every [[United States|U.S.]] [[president]] since 1967.

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:39:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Israel]] of flying missions against [[Syria]], illegally retaining control of the [[Israel|Golan Heights]], aggressively spying on the [[United States]], and selling advanced [[United States|U.S.]] military technology to countries like [[China]] and [[Iran]].

### AF 2017-10-27 0041
(00:39:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel]] has stolen uranium and nuclear secrets from [[Germany]] and other [[Europe|European]] countries.

### AF 2017-10-26 0040
(00:05:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced a special Halloween episode for Tuesday, which would feature pumpkin carving and a call-in show format.

### AF 2017-10-26 0040
(00:11:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes engaged in a public argument with [[Hunter Wallace]], the head of the [[League of the South]], over the inclusion of groups like the [[National Socialist Movement]] in the "White Lives Matter" rally.

### AF 2017-10-26 0040
(00:16:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Mike Cernovich|Mike Enoch]] for associating with the "White Lives Matter" rally and its participating organizations, despite finding him intelligent and funny.

### AF 2017-10-26 0040
(00:17:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes scheduled [[Mike Cernovich|Mike Enoch]] to appear on his show the following Wednesday for a discussion on movement optics and rhetoric.

### AF 2017-10-26 0040
(00:18:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes invited Richard Spencer to appear on the "Nationalist Review" podcast for that Wednesday, though Spencer had not yet confirmed.

### AF 2017-10-26 0040
(00:27:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Jordan Peterson]] as a successful model for the movement, noting his influence, his Patreon earnings of approximately $80,000 per month, and his message of personal responsibility, marriage, and family.

### AF 2017-10-26 0040
(00:36:37) [[Twitter]] locked numerous alt-right and right-wing accounts, including those of Nicholas J. Fuentes, [[Peter Sweden]], and [[American Renaissance]], for alleged automated activity, requiring phone verification to restore access.

### AF 2017-10-26 0040
(00:41:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that a friend in [[Dominion of Canada|Canada]] was arrested for hate speech after stating that [[Islam]] is a religion of war, and that [[Peter Sweden]]'s parents in the [[United Kingdom]] were questioned by a police hate speech unit over his online comments.

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(00:23:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced the launch of a new midweek episode of his podcast, [[Nationalist Review]], following the achievement of a $500 threshold on [[Patreon]].

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(02:13:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes highlighted the ongoing investigation into the [[Las Vegas]] shooting, noting that authorities had not established a motive or a consistent timeline three and a half weeks after the incident.

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(03:43:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that federal authorities and the [[Clark County]] Sheriff's Department were withholding information about the [[Las Vegas]] shooting.

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(22:33:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced a controversy involving [[Donald Trump]] and Representative [[Frederica Wilson]] regarding a call to a Gold Star family.

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(43:52:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he worked with people in the office of [[Jim Durkin]], the [[Republican Party|Republican]] minority leader of the [[Illinois House of Representatives]].

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(51:40:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced plans to build an independent website to host his content and mitigate reliance on [[Google|YouTube]].

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(52:57:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the original design of the [[U.S. Constitution|United States Constitution]] was closer to fascist principles than the current system, citing amendments like the 16th (income tax), 17th (direct election of senators), and the establishment of the [[Federal Reserve|Federal Reserve System]].

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(53:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Founding Fathers of the United States]] were opposed to democracy and were troubled by the excesses of the [[French Revolution]].

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(54:16:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed belief in the [[U.S. Constitution|United States Constitution]] and [[federalism]], describing the [[U.S. Constitution|Constitution]] as the ideal form of government for the [[United States|American]] people and a perfection of the Anglo-[[United States|American]] liberal tradition.

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(58:52:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Alex Jones]]'s style as a deliberate "news entertainment brand" designed to be entertaining and engaging.

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(01:00:25:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented that he listened to a podcast by [[Mike Cernovich|Mike Enoch]] of [[The Daily Shoah]] and found it smart and entertaining, but stated their projects had different goals and styles.

### AF 2017-10-25 0039
(01:04:18:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes's show, America First with Nicholas J. Fuentes, airs Monday through Friday, while [[James Allsup]]'s [[America First Overdrive]] airs on Tuesdays and Thursdays.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(00:05:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a key political litmus test should be whether a policy or message would be palatable to a blue-collar [[Catholic]] white voter in [[Wisconsin]], a demographic he identified as the most significant electoral bloc for future nationalist growth in states like [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]], [[State of Ohio|Ohio]], [[Iowa]], and [[Minnesota]].

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(00:30:10) Richard Spencer responded to Nicholas J. Fuentes's political test by arguing that blue-collar [[Catholic]] Wisconsinites had not influenced culture or social norms for a century and should not be the primary audience for their movement.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(00:48:84) [[Mike Cernovich|Mike Enoch]], participating in the same online discussion as Richard Spencer, stated that his [[aim]] was a broad cultural transformation rather than concrete electoral politics, focusing on appealing to young people and shifting the Overton window.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(01:04:64) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the 2016 election as evidence for his strategy, noting that [[Donald Trump]] won by securing the votes of white blue-collar workers in [[Wisconsin]], [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[State of Ohio|Ohio]], [[Iowa]], and [[North Carolina]], which prevented policies like the [[Trans-Pacific Partnership|TPP]] trade deal, higher taxes, nationalized healthcare, and the legalization of millions of illegal immigrants.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(01:45:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that while Richard Spencer's post-[[United States|American]] identitarian project was being executed well for its own aims, he believed their goals and methods were largely separate, with Spencer focused on long-term cultural change and himself on immediate electoral and policy victories.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(02:26:74) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Jeff Flake's]] retirement speech as dishonest, arguing his decision was driven by an inability to win re-election against a [[Steve Bannon]]-backed challenger rather than principled opposition to [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(03:14:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Flake's]] admission that a "traditional [[Republican Party|Republican]]" [[who]] was pro-free trade and pro-immigration could not win in [[Arizona]] demonstrated how [[Donald Trump]] had shifted the political center for white [[United States|Americans]] and [[Republican Party|Republicans]].

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(04:05:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted betting $100 on [[Donald Trump]] winning the presidency after the [[Republican Party|GOP]] primary in July 2016, another $100 after [[Hillary Clinton]] fainted on [[September 11 attacks|September 11]], 2016, and an additional $150 after the second presidential debate in October 2016.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(04:20:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed several policy predictions he made about [[Donald Trump]] that he claimed were vindicated, including the appointment of conservative [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] justices, avoiding war in [[Syria]], not enshrining [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] into law, and implementing travel bans.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(04:56:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to the large crowds drawn by [[Ben Shapiro]] and [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] on their speaking tours as evidence of their significant cultural influence, despite his personal disagreements with them.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(05:24:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the ascendancy of [[Alex Jones|InfoWars]], [[Paul Joseph Watson]], [[Milo Yiannopoulos]], [[Ben Shapiro]], and [[Mike Cernovich]] as examples of successful cultural movements, contrasting them with what he viewed as the failed mass appeal of events like the [[Charlottesville]] rally.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(06:25:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes described a speech he gave titled "Putting [[America First]]" at a [[U.S. Inc.]] event as an example of tactful rhetoric that implicitly advanced alt-right ideas while being palatable to a broad audience, including minorities and mainstream [[Republican Party|Republicans]].

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(07:28:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled defending [[Donald Trump]] during a September 2017 controversy over a potential [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] deal with [[Chuck Schumer]] and [[Nancy Pelosi]], arguing that no concrete legislation had been agreed upon and that [[Donald Trump|Trump's]] long-held platform made a sudden reversal unlikely.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(08:30:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that [[Stephen Miller]]'s role in shooting down a [[Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals|DACA]] immigration deal was likely aligned with [[Donald Trump]]'s own opposition, and that [[the president's]] actions serviced the political ascendancy of [[Steve Bannon]] and [[Roy Moore]] in [[Alabama]].

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(08:31:74) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused mainstream media outlets like [[Washington Post|The Hill]] of misleading coverage by reporting the resumption of the [[United States|U.S]]. refugee program while omitting [[Donald Trump]]'s simultaneous ban on refugees from 11 specific countries.

### AF 2017-10-24 0038
(09:27:76) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]]'s suitability for the presidency came from his experience as a billionaire executive of a private real estate company, a skill set he viewed as parallel to running a [[country]], unlike that of a celebrity like [[Kid Rock]].

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(01:25:650) Richard Spencer held a rally in [[Gainesville]] last Wednesday, which reignited internal debates about movement optics and strategy.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(13:22:670) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the tightly controlled [[Generation Identity]] operation avoided the chaos seen at events like [[Gainesville]], where individuals showed up with [[Germany|Nazi]] tattoos.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(15:10:990) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Generation Identity]]'s [[Europe|European]]-style symbolism and tactics were not directly transferable or ideal for activism in the [[United States]].

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(17:09:050) Nicholas J. Fuentes proposed that effective activism in the [[United States]] should use [[United States|American]] flags and slogans like "Put [[America First]], Resist Multiculturalism."

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(18:02:110) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the 2017 [[Charlottesville]] "Unite the Right" rally suffered from a confusing and incoherent message, mixing themes of Confederate monument preservation, white identity, and right-wing unity.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(21:06:530) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the arrest of three individuals for attempted homicide after the [[Gainesville]] rally as an example of damaging optics that would repel potential recruits.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(23:28:970) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[American Identity Movement|Identity Europa]] for using alien branding and poorly thought-out rhetoric in its campus flyer campaigns.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(24:01:570) [[American Renaissance]] distributed a poster at [[Boston College]] featuring Uncle Sam and the message "Don't be ashamed for being white," which Nicholas J. Fuentes cited as effective, accessible outreach.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(26:18:450) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that users on the forum [[Politically Incorrect]] (Pol) were engaged in intense debates about him and [[James Allsup]] of [[America First Media]].

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(26:42:550) Nicholas J. Fuentes denied vying for leadership of the movement and asked people to stop comparing him to Richard Spencer.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(28:20:970) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he had met and gotten along with movement figures like Richard Spencer, [[Jared Taylor]], [[Peter Brimelow]], and [[Eli Mosley]].

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(30:34:350) Nicholas J. Fuentes planned to discuss a story about [[Steve Bannon]] disavowing [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] after a [[BuzzFeed]] expose.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(31:04:210) Richard Spencer was present in a video where [[Milo Yiannopoulos]] sang "[[United States|America]] the Beautiful" to a crowd giving Roman salutes.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(31:12:830) Richard Spencer launched a [[Twitter]] thread arguing that [[Steve Bannon]] was an enemy of the movement and a "cuck."

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(33:20:810) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Paul Joseph Watson]] had 800,000 [[Twitter]] followers, [[Alex Jones]] had a million, and figures like [[Mike Cernovich]] and [[Stefan Molyneux]] had a combined massive reach.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(38:58:210) Nicholas J. Fuentes acknowledged that Richard Spencer had founded the alt-right movement and built institutions like the [[National Policy Institute]], [[Radix Journal]], and [[AltRight.com]].

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(39:12:450) Richard Spencer and [[Eli Mosley]] were instrumental in the [[organization]] [[American Identity Movement|Identity Europa]].

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(46:57:950) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that securing reliably [[Republican Party|Republican]] votes in [[Wisconsin]], [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]], and [[New Hampshire]] could temporarily counteract the [[Democratic Party]]'s long-term demographic advantage in southwestern states.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(47:15:230) [[Donald Trump]]'s 2016 campaign success in the Midwest and New [[United Kingdom|England]] served as a prototype for a potential anti-immigration political movement, according to Nicholas J. Fuentes.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(49:07:030) Richard Spencer was mentored by [[Jews|Jewish]] writer [[Paul Gottfried]], [[who]] coined the term "alt-right."

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(01:00:13:350) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that reading [[Curtis Yarvin|Mencius Moldbug]] and other Neo-Reactionary (NRX) literature made him more skeptical of libertarianism and democracy for its own sake.

### AF 2017-10-23 0037
(01:03:55:150) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed his top three [[United States|U.S]]. presidents as [[Donald Trump]], [[Andrew Jackson]], and [[John F. Kennedy]].

### AF 2017-10-20 0036
(00:11:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that a subversive element within the New Right, which he identified as [[Laura Loomer]], was causing significant problems for the movement through duplicitous behavior, lying, and alleged blackmail.

### AF 2017-10-20 0036
(00:59:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported hearing rumors that [[Laura Loomer]] possessed compromising sexual information about figures within the Alt-Light movement, including [[Jack Posobiec]] and [[Ali Akbar]], which she allegedly used as leverage.

### AF 2017-10-20 0036
(01:13:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he had established a temporary truce with [[Ali Akbar]] after calling into his [[Twitter|Periscope]] AMA to air grievances, agreeing to cease name-calling.

### AF 2017-10-20 0036
(01:25:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted his efforts over the past year to secure a debate with [[Ben Shapiro]], including outreach through [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]], [[Kassy Dillon|Cassie Dillon]], and [[Elliott Hamilton]], all of which were ignored or blocked by [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]]'s associates.

### AF 2017-10-20 0036
(01:26:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Ben Shapiro]] as a "Zionist supremacist" [[who]] believes [[Jews|Jewish people]] are different and better, which Fuentes argued is why [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]] avoids engaging with him on his program.

### AF 2017-10-20 0036
(01:35:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the term "Judeo-[[Christianity|Christian]]" is a fabricated [[concept]] invented in the 1940s to reduce anti-Semitism by creating a false sense of shared identity and values between [[Christianity|Christians]] and [[Jews]], which he finds fundamentally contradictory.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes opened his program by acknowledging the day's major event, a speech by Richard Spencer in [[Gainesville]], which had been preceded by exclusive interviews with [[Enoch Spencer]] and [[Eli Mosley]] on a live stream hosted by [[James Allsup|James Alsup]].

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes commended the courage and organizational effort of Richard Spencer, [[Eli Mosley]], and [[Enoch Spencer]] for holding an event in a hostile environment at a university, facing threats of doxing and violence from groups like [[Antifa]].

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:03:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the activism of Richard Spencer and the [[NPI]] lacked clearly defined short-term and long-term political objectives, making it difficult to evaluate whether events like the [[Gainesville]] speech were a net positive or negative for their movement.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:05:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that the organizers of the [[Gainesville]] event paid ten thousand dollars for the facility, while the campus spent five hundred thousand dollars on security, attracting nationwide media coverage.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:06:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that Richard Spencer and [[American Identity Movement|Identity Europa]] had successfully co-opted the free speech movement, a tactic he viewed as strategically smart because it leveraged a popular, quintessentially [[United States|American]] issue that appealed to a broad audience.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:08:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that events like the [[Gainesville]] speech demonstrated the "insanity of [[the left]]" and the "bankruptcy of the right wing," providing good optics by showcasing a "disgusting leftist unwashed mob" on television, which could drive traditionalist voters away from the [[Democratic Party]].

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:09:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that a third positive of the [[Gainesville]] event was the exposure it provided for Richard Spencer's ideas to a new audience, a valuable asset for any political movement.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:11:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the [[Gainesville]] event for its poor audio and video recording, aside from a stream by [[Ruptly]], arguing that failing to professionally capture the message was a major logistical flaw for an event designed to spread ideas.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:12:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the stage optics of the [[Gainesville]] event, noting the lack of a podium, poor lighting, and the awkward positioning of [[Eli Mosley]] and [[Enoch Spencer]] on stage, contrasting this with the professional event training provided by organizations like the [[Leadership Institute]] and [[Turning Point USA]].

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:14:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Gainesville]] venue was too large for the tightly vetted audience, suggesting that a smaller, packed space would have created better optics than a sparsely filled room, a basic principle of event [[organization]] taught by groups like [[Young Americans for Liberty]].

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:16:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized Richard Spencer's "bitter, defensive, resentful tone" during the [[Gainesville]] speech, particularly when calling protesters "morons," arguing that a more humble, likable, and empathetic tone would have greater mass appeal, especially to older generations.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:18:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes proposed that Richard Spencer should adopt an "Americana" aesthetic—wearing [[United States|American]] flag pins, using patriotic imagery, and discussing [[United States|American]] authors and founders—instead of a [[Europe|European]] identitarian style, to make his message more palatable and effective for a mainstream [[United States|American]] audience.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:21:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes described Richard Spencer, [[Eli Mosley]], and [[Enoch Spencer]] as "pioneers" and "trailblazers" in [[United States|American]] politics, stating his criticisms were offered constructively to help refine their activism, not to attack their ideology.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:23:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes praised [[Eli Mosley]] as a "workhorse" [[who]] had overhauled his [[organization]] and had a good short-term strategy, describing his efforts as "God's work."

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:25:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes mocked a [[Turning Point USA]] demonstration at [[Kent State University]] where a member wore a diaper and crawled on the ground to protest safe spaces, calling it a "clown show" that made the conservative movement look unserious and perverted.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:29:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the 1970 [[Kent State University]] shootings, where students were killed while seriously protesting the [[Cambodia]] incursion ordered by [[Richard Nixon]], with the contemporary "diaper boy" protest, lamenting the decline from a "serious [[country]]" to a "clownish" one.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:30:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Charlie Kirk]] of dedicating "Zionist supremacist money" to advocating for lower corporate taxes while ignoring demographic changes like white [[United States|Americans]] becoming a minority and [[State of Texas|Texas]] turning into a blue [[state]].

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:32:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the post-2016 election media landscape led to an oversaturation of incompetent political commentators like [[Milo Yiannopoulos]], [[Mike Cernovich]], [[Ali Akbar]], and [[Jack Posobiec]], [[who]] lacked philosophical foundations and needed to be "bred out" of the market.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:36:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes participated in a [[Twitter|Periscope]] broadcast with [[Lucian Wintrich]] and noted that, unlike other "alt-light" figures, [[Lucian Wintrich]] had a coherent, thought-out political philosophy and was willing to engage in serious discussion, even with figures like [[Millennial Matt]] [[who]] waved a [[Germany|Nazi]] flag.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:38:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed a desire for a more articulate and engaging "alt-light" flank, suggesting that if figures like [[Dave Rubin]] were serious thinkers [[who]] hosted people like Richard Spencer, it would sharpen arguments and benefit both sides of the discourse.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:40:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized a "purity spiral" within the alt-right that excluded anyone [[who]] wasn't an "unironic Hitlerian," arguing for broader cooperation on shared goals like being anti-war, anti-legal immigration, and against bad trade deals, which he viewed as implicitly pro-white.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:40:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented on [[Ali Akbar]] (Ollie), describing him as a "nobody" with poor social media engagement and a fascinating specimen of a confused right-winger [[who]] was half-[[black]], half-Arab, and possibly a closeted homosexual.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:43:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed to have evidence from several sources that [[Ali Akbar]] was a "legitimate homosexual" [[who]] was "gay for the alt-right," which he found ironically humorous given [[Ali Akbar]]'s attacks on others.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:47:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he had a contentious but ultimately respectful direct message exchange with [[Mike Cernovich]], whom he found charming and entertaining, and would be willing to interact with in [[person]].

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:50:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes declined to debate Richard Spencer, citing respect for his clout and the potential negative consequences of publicly challenging a movement leader, but expressed eagerness to debate "alt-light" figures to attract their audience.

### AF 2017-10-19 0035
(00:54:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he never read [[The Daily Stormer]] because its style was too abrasive and shock-value-oriented, preferring a gentlemanly approach to politics exemplified by figures like [[Oswald Mosley]].

### AF 2017-10-19 0035

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:01:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a debate with [[Wilt Chamberlain|Will Chamberlain]] on the question of whether the [[United States]] should end all military aid to [[Israel]].

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:02:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[United States]] should end all military aid to [[Israel]] to return to an "[[America First]]" foreign policy.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:03:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited Article 1, Section 8 of the [[U.S. Constitution]], which grants [[Congress]] the power to collect taxes to provide for the common defense and general welfare, as the sole constitutional basis for foreign aid.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:04:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]] does not serve [[United States|America]]'s geopolitical interests and was the first nation in the [[Middle East]] to acquire nuclear weapons almost 50 years ago.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:04:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the Lavon Affair, the Apollo Affair, and a record of [[Israel|Israeli]] espionage against the [[United States]] as evidence that [[Israel]] behaves as a rogue [[state]] rather than a trusted ally.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:12:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]]'s military budget is more than five times the [[United States|U.S]]. aid amount and that $2 billion in annual private contributions from the [[United States]] could be increased to offset the loss of government aid.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:13:15) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited an instance where [[Donald Trump]] told [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Netanyahu]] to stop building [[Gaza Strip|West Bank]] settlements, but the [[Israel|Knesset]] later approved an expansion, demonstrating that [[United States|U.S]]. aid does not restrain [[Israel]].

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:13:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]] lacks the manpower and money to administer conquered territories and that potential invaders like [[Syria]] and [[Iraq]] are militarily weakened.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:14:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Israel]] was found to have been selling [[United States|American]] military technology to [[China]] for decades, with disclosures as recent as 2013.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:15:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that during the 1991 Gulf War, the [[United States]] could not use [[Israel|Israeli]] troops because it would have disbanded the 400,000-strong coalition of Arab partners.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:15:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a report from the [[United States|U.S]]. intelligence community stating that in 95% of contingencies in the Persian Gulf, [[Israel]] would be useless militarily.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:16:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the 1973 oil embargo demonstrated that [[Israel]] is a liability to [[United States|U.S]]. strategic interests, not an asset.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:17:30) [[Wilt Chamberlain|Will Chamberlain]] argued that Nicholas J. Fuentes failed to articulate a single positive change for [[United States|American]] lives resulting from cutting aid to [[Israel]], while conceding a risk of major war.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:22:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a report that [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi Netanyahu]] warned [[United States|U.S]]. senators in 2002 that [[Saddam Hussein]] was developing nuclear weapons, and that [[Israel]] [[fed]] alarming reports about [[Iraq]]'s WMD program to the [[United States]].

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:23:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that both the [[Israel|Knesset]] and the [[U.S. Senate]] intelligence committees later found the intelligence on [[Iraq|Iraqi]] [[Weapons of Mass Destruction|WMDs]] to be false.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:32:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that intelligence sharing with [[Israel]] could continue without foreign aid, as it does with [[European Union]] countries, and that [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Netanyahu]] had previously promised to phase out [[United States|U.S]]. aid.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:33:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited *The [[Israel Lobby]]* by [[John Mearsheimer]] and [[Stephen Walt]], quoting a former [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] official [[who]] called [[Israel Defense Forces|Israeli military intelligence]] "lousy" and "gossip stuff mostly."

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:33:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Stephen Walt]] stating that [[Israel]] provided faulty or misleading intelligence on several occasions to encourage the [[United States]] to take actions [[Israel]] wanted.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:36:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[United States|U.S]].-[[Israel]] memorandum of understanding for $3.8 billion annually was struck in 2016, and that [[Donald Trump]] and [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Netanyahu]] had both expressed openness to phasing out aid.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:37:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes reiterated that [[Israel]] was useless as a forward base in the 1991 Gulf War because Arab partners would not cooperate with [[Israel|Israeli]] troops, and questioned why bases in [[Qatar]], [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]], or [[Turkey]] were insufficient.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:38:11) [[Wilt Chamberlain|Will Chamberlain]] challenged Nicholas J. Fuentes to articulate a single positive change in [[United States|American]] lives resulting from cutting military aid to [[Israel]].

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:40:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[United States]] has given [[Israel]] approximately $150 billion since 1948, not including aid to [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]] and [[Jim Jordan|Jordan]] for peace agreements, or the influence on the wars in [[Iraq]] and [[Afghanistan]].

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:41:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that ending aid to [[Israel]] would reduce terrorism, citing [[Ramzi Yousef]]'s 1993 World Trade Center bombing and [[Osama bin Laden]]'s 1996 fatwa, which listed [[United States|U.S]]. support for [[Israel]] as a primary grievance.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:42:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the [[September 11 attacks|9/11]] Commission report that [[Osama bin Laden]] wanted to accelerate the [[September 11 attacks|9/11 attacks]] to coincide with [[Ariel Sharon]]'s visit to the Temple Mount.

(00:44:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited an [[Israeli defense minister]] and chief intelligence officer [[who]] stated [[Israel]] would prefer [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] to [[Iran]] in [[Syria]], showing [[Israel]] does not help fight the principal actor of terrorism against the [[Kanye West|West]].

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:45:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Iran]] has sponsored no terror attacks against the [[United States]] since the Islamic Revolution, whereas [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] has sponsored attacks in San Bernardino and [[Orlando]].

(00:45:46) [[Wilt Chamberlain|Will Chamberlain]] argued that Arab countries' desire to wipe [[Israel]] off the map is the prime motivation for terrorism related to [[Israel]], creating a tension with Nicholas J. Fuentes's argument that cutting aid would not cause a war.

(00:46:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]] has the most powerful conventional military in the [[Middle East]], a nuclear deterrent (the "Samson Option"), and that potential invaders like [[Syria]] and [[Iraq]] are too weak to pose a threat.

### AF 2017-10-11 0029
(00:47:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Iran]] is the only significant threat to [[Israel]], but would have to invade through [[Iraq]] and could not ally with Arab rivals like [[Saudi Arabia]] and [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]] due to [[Shiite|Shia]]-[[Sunni]] and Persian-Arab divisions.

(00:50:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified the debate was about ending foreign direct assistance, not weapons sales, intelligence sharing, the [[United States|U.S]]. security guarantee, or uncontested diplomatic support in the [[United Nations]].

(00:51:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that more than half of all [[United States|U.S]]. vetoes in the [[United Nations]] during the Cold War were against anti-[[Israel]] resolutions.

(00:51:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that deterrence, as with [[Ukraine]] or [[North Atlantic Treaty Organization|NATO]] members, comes from security guarantees and nuclear deterrence, not from aid, making the "war of all against all" scenario a hard sell.

Fuentes summarized that [[Israel]] is a rogue [[state]], citing the Lavon Affair, theft of [[United States|U.S]]. nuclear secrets, sale of military tech to [[China]], aggressive espionage, and lobbying for [[United States|U.S]]. wars in [[Iraq]] and [[Afghanistan]] that served [[Israel|Israeli]] interests.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(53:24:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented on the [[2017 Catalan independence referendum]], noting the irony of [[European Union]] leaders championing democracy while Spanish authorities used force to suppress the vote and seize ballot boxes.

(56:20:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Ben Sasse]] must be primaried in 2020 and called him a "cuck."

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(58:33:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes commented on the arrest of the streamer [[Destiny]] for public drunkenness, expressing a degree of pity for him due to his stature and life circumstances.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(59:08:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the nationalist right needs to improve its aesthetics and build community and brotherhood before holding more rallies, citing the infighting within the alt-right movement as a fundamental problem.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:00:34:87) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed satisfaction that he and figures like [[James Allsup]] are "red-pilling" members of Generation Z with a substantive message, contrasting it with what he called the cheap, sensational message of [[Milo Yiannopoulos]].

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:01:52:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that his political views were shaped by a combination of independent research and discussions with a friend named [[Steve Chatterson]] [[who]] introduced him to alternative theories.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:02:24:63) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that, as [[president]], he would not allow [[Dominion of Canada|Canadian]] provinces to join the [[United States]] even if they legally seceded, calling it a bad precedent.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:04:32:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes dismissed [[Ben Sasse]]'s argument that an [[United States|American]] cannot be a white nationalist, citing the Naturalization Act of 1790, writings by [[John Jay]], [[Thomas Jefferson]], [[George Washington]], and others to argue that [[United States|America]] was founded as a white nation.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:06:44:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated it was premature to create an organized sub-party movement to take over the [[Republican Party|GOP]], arguing that a cultural movement must come first and that individuals need to build families and communities.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:07:30:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump]] is not at fault for tax breaks favoring the wealthy because he does not write the laws, which are shaped by congressional campaign donors.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:09:49:87) When asked to name his favorite historical "bad guy," Nicholas J. Fuentes declined to label historical figures as such but mentioned [[Oswald Mosley]] as "pretty cool" while condemning his anti-Semitism.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:11:05:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Ben Shapiro]]'s argument that the present is better than the 1950s because of greater consumption, implying [[Ben Shapiro|Shapiro]]'s view is biased by the existence of [[Israel]].

(01:11:33:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes recommended reading authors such as [[Julius Evola]], [[Sam Francis]], [[Patrick Buchanan|Pat Buchanan]], [[Samuel Huntington|Samuel P. Huntington]], [[Oswald Mosley]], [[Oswald Spengler]], [[Thomas Aquinas]], [[Augustine]], [[Fyodor Dostoevsky]], [[Leo Tolstoy]], and [[Friedrich Nietzsche]].

(01:14:30:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he had not read the works of [[Christopher Lasch]].

(01:15:55:91) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected an idea to create a community forum on his website, telling viewers to get off the internet and meet people in real life through church or other local communities, though he mentioned an existing Discord server run by [[James Allsup]].

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:21:41:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed five main policy goals for a hypothetical [[America First]] Nationalist party: restricting immigration, implementing [[fair]] trade policies, enacting pro-natal tax policies, abolishing the [[Federal Reserve]] to restore sound money, and ending foreign wars.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:23:08:99) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced [[Charles Murray]]'s analysis of the meritocratic elite, where high-IQ individuals cluster in cities after college, leading to a lack of social mobility and geographic polarization.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:28:45:85) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that making transgender hormone therapy or pornography illegal would not fix underlying societal problems, criticizing a legislative approach as a lazy substitute for personal and communal action.

(01:33:51:63) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to [[Russia]]'s cultural and demographic rebound following the collapse of the [[Soviet Union]] as evidence that a traditionalist revival is possible and not naive.

### AF 2017-10-02 0022
(01:36:00:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized identitarian and [[Generation Identity]] movements as out of touch and "LARPy," arguing that a successful political movement in the [[United States]] must use local [[United States|American]] aesthetics like the [[United States|American]] flag rather than [[Europe|European]] symbols.

(01:40:30:87) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed moral reservations about human cloning but conceded it might be necessary for national competition if other countries pursued it.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a debate on [[North Korea]] on his program, featuring a guest identified as Vendetta.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:11:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes responded that while the guest provided a sober analysis of the costs of war, the long-term cost of a nuclear-armed [[North Korea]] was worse, citing risks of regime instability, proliferation to other rogue states, and the unacceptable contingency of a future strike on the [[United States|U.S]]. mainland.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:13:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[North Korea]]'s regime is on the brink of collapse and reliant on [[China]] for crude oil, suggesting the [[country]] could implode within three months if that support were cut off.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:14:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[Kim regime]] in [[North Korea]] is full of rational decision-makers [[who]] want to survive, and he conceded they would not immediately strike the [[United States|U.S]]. mainland upon acquiring an ICBM capability.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:15:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified proliferation as a key risk, noting [[North Korea]]'s history of sharing nuclear technology and infrastructure with [[Pakistan]] and [[Iran]], and the potential for spread to terrorists or other rogue states.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:17:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[North Korea]]'s circumstance was exceptional, as it pursued nuclear weapons from a position of extreme desperation and isolation, unlike [[China]], which had the support of the [[Soviet Union]] and Eastern Bloc during its development.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:18:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[China]] is tightening economic pressure on [[North Korea]], complying with a recent [[United Nations]] Security Council resolution to restrict textile trade and energy exports, which could cripple the [[North Korea]]n economy.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:20:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that achieving nuclear capability would not bring stability to [[North Korea]] because the [[country]] remains economically isolated and fragile, with its survival dependent on [[China]]'s energy exports.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:21:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the [[China|Chinese]] Foreign Ministry declared its desire to avoid war on the [[Korean Peninsula]] at all costs that week.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:23:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the world should [[aim]] for fewer unstable nuclear regimes, not more, and that [[Pakistan]]'s instability actually supports the case for preventing [[North Korea]]'s proliferation.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:24:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed it would have been preferable to address [[North Korea]]'s nuclear program during the [[Bill Clinton]] administration, but argued the current choice is between a horrible war and accepting the long-term risk of a nuclear-armed rogue regime.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:24:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that in 50 to 100 years, the [[United States]] would be held hostage by a nuclear-armed [[North Korea]], relying on the goodwill of [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]] and his commanders for safety.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:26:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified he was not arguing [[North Korea]] would launch a suicidal strike, but that an Intercontinental Ballistic Missile (ICBM) capability grants them parity and creates an unacceptable long-term risk of regime collapse or proliferation over decades.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:27:32) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that if [[South Korea]] saw a nuclear-armed [[North Korea]] and a non-interventionist [[United States|U.S]]., it might seek its own nuclear arsenal, potentially triggering a cascade of proliferation in [[Saudi Arabia]], [[Arab Republic of Egypt|Egypt]], and [[Turkey]].

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:28:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[North Korea]] lacks the intrinsic stability of established nuclear powers like [[Russia]] or [[China]], having no legitimate government, commerce, or infrastructure to ensure the secure command of its arsenal.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:29:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he would be more comfortable with [[Iran]] having a nuclear weapon than [[North Korea]], because [[Iran]] has trading partners and is not a totalitarian [[state]].

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:30:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes countered that [[China]] and [[Russia]] have less to fear from [[North Korea]]n collapse or proliferation, as any missiles would [[target]] [[United States|U.S]]. rivals, and technology would likely be shared with their allies like [[Iran]].

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:31:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenged the guest to accept the risk of a nuclear strike on the [[United States|U.S]]. homeland from a collapsing [[North Korea]] or from terrorists they armed, arguing isolationists do not properly weigh these contingencies.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:34:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes rejected the analogy of accepting risk from [[China]]'s existing arsenal, arguing it is different to trust [[Beijing]]'s rationality than to trust its competence and swiftness to bail out a collapsing [[Pyongyang]] and secure loose nukes.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:36:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested [[China]] has been stalling and watering down [[United Nations]] sanctions, and might tacitly support a nuclear [[North Korea]] as a deterrent to [[United States|U.S]]. action on the peninsula, as proliferation would primarily harm [[United States|U.S]]. interests.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:37:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Donald Trump]]'s policy as a game of chicken to see whether [[Kim Jong Un|Kim Jong-un]] collapses or denuclearizes first, with [[China]] less willing to play due to the consequences of collapse.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:40:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes promoted an upcoming new show, "[[America First]] Overdrive," hosted by [[James Allsup|James Alsup]], debuting the following Tuesday on his channel.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:41:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced he would be campaigning for [[Paul Nehlen]] in [[Wisconsin]]'s 1st congressional district in August 2018.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:42:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized the focus on criticizing [[Islam]], noting that while attacks on [[Christianity]] and [[Islam]] are common in media, criticism of the [[Talmud]] and certain facts about [[Judaism]] are forbidden, which he presented as an observable double standard.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:43:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced passages from the [[Talmud]] that disparage [[Jesus Christ]], stating these are facts not discussed in mainstream media despite open criticism of other religions.

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:44:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a survey indicating evangelical [[Christianity|Christians]] have warm feelings toward [[Jews]], while [[Jews]] have very cold feelings toward evangelicals, despite evangelical political support for [[Israel]].

### AF 2017-09-29 0021
(00:45:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes mentioned a rumor that the original working title for Stanley Kubrick's film *Eyes Wide Shut* was *The Jaded Eyes Wide Shut*.

(03:48:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[C.J. Pearson]] as an example of a young [[black]] conservative [[who]] gained internet fame for supporting [[Donald Trump]].

(11:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Ben Shapiro]], [[Ben Sasse]], and other establishment figures avoid debating the alt-right on issues of race and immigration.

(12:05:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[North Korea]] and tax policy as current events topics for the show.

### AF 2017-09-28 0020
(23:15:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes's mother interrupted the show to tell him to turn his microphone down.

### AF 2017-09-28 0020
(27:48:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that economic factors, including stagnant wages and high costs, are a primary reason why white and middle-class people in the [[United States]] are not having children.

### AF 2017-09-28 0020
(28:49:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the post-World War II GI Bill as a policy that enabled returning soldiers to buy homes, start families, and get good-paying jobs, leading to a baby boom.

### AF 2017-09-28 0020
(29:33:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that birth rates are stabilizing in [[State of Japan|Japan]], the [[Kingdom of the Netherlands|Netherlands]], [[Israel|Poland]], and [[Russia]] due to pro-natal economic policies.

### AF 2017-09-28 0020
(30:06:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that addressing the low native birth rate in the [[United States]] through pro-family economic policy could slow demographic change and buy political [[time]].

### AF 2017-09-28 0020
(32:05:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized [[Donald Trump]]'s proposed tax policy as pro-natal and pro-family, which could be supported by [[Paul Ryan]], [[Mitch McConnell]], evangelicals, and others [[who]] oppose white nationalism.

(35:19:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes disputed the claim that [[Donald Trump]] was "memed" into office, arguing his victory was due to grassroots campaigning, door-knocking, phone banking, and donations.

### AF 2017-09-28 0020
(37:03:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he believes [[Adolf Hitler|Hitler]] was a bad and reprehensible guy, but noted that even his movement required political party [[organization]] and grunt work, not just propaganda.

(41:06:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the [[North Korea]] crisis as a race between the [[country]] developing a miniaturized nuclear warhead for an ICBM and the [[United States]] and [[China]] collapsing its economy through sanctions.

(42:33:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[North Korea]]'s core foreign policy objective is to obtain a nuclear weapon to deter the [[United States]], while the [[United States]]'s post-Cold War objective is to prevent nuclear proliferation in rogue states.

(44:47:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Iran]] maintains a latent nuclear capability to deter [[United States]] intervention but that invading [[Iran]] is not justified.

(48:44:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that if diplomacy and sanctions can force [[North Korea]] to give up its nuclear weapons, war would be avoidable; otherwise, conflict would be justified due to irreconcilable strategic interests.

(01:12:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that many right-wing pundits, including [[Mike Cernovich]], [[Lucian Wintrich]], and [[Milo Yiannopoulos]], were motivated by money and fame rather than principle, leading them to avoid engaging with the alt-right.
